5. **The radius of curvature $R$ is large** compared to cross-sectional dimensions, so that **plane sections** of the original cross-section remain **plane** after bending (small-deflection, Euler–Bernoulli regime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these assumptions matter for safety:** The linear strain assumption + Hooke's Law together yield a **linear stress distribution** across the section. Any nonlinearity (e.g., concrete in the post-cracking regime) is a deviation that the design engineer must account for with safety factors — this is the central philosophy of OESFT722.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — From Strain to Stress to Moment

**Step 1 — Strain geometry:** Consider a small element of length $dx$ of the beam. After bending, the fibres at distance $y$ from the neutral axis subtend an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature.

Original length of element $= dx = R \cdot d\theta$

Length of fibre at distance $y$ $= (R + y) \cdot d\theta$

Change in length $= (R + y)d\theta - R\cdot d\theta = y \cdot d\theta$

Strain:
$$
\epsilon = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{R \cdot d\theta} = \frac{y}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law:**
$$
\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}
$$

**Step 3 — Force equilibrium on the cross-section:**

The total force on the section must be zero (no axial load in pure bending):
$$
\int_A \sigma \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0
$$

This implies $\int_A y\, dA = 0$, which means the **Neutral Axis passes through the Centroid** of the cross-section (for homogeneous material).

**Step 4 — Moment equilibrium — Bending Equation:**

The moment of the flexural stresses about the Neutral Axis must equal the applied bending moment $M$:
$$
M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y^2}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I$ (the **Second Moment of Area** about the NA). Therefore:
$$
\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} = \frac{\sigma}{y}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bending Equation (KF 1 — King Formula of Beam Theory):**
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}}$$
> This is the **single most-tested relation** in any KTU paper on this topic. Memorise it as a triplet.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in SI / mm units consistent with IS 456 and KTU Board solutions.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Master Bending Equation | Dimensionally consistent |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Flexural stress at distance $y$ from NA | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 3 | $M = \sigma \cdot Z$, where $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | Moment of resistance in terms of Section Modulus | $\text{N·mm}$ |
| 4 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y}$ | Section Modulus (depends only on geometry) | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature induced by moment | $\text{mm}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\epsilon_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max}}{E} = \dfrac{y_{\max}}{R}$ | Maximum strain (extreme fibre) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$ | Flexural rigidity / stiffness | $\text{N·mm}^2$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress (extreme fibre) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |

> **Standard Section Modulus values (must be memorised):**
> - Rectangle ($b \times d$): $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$
> - Solid Circle (dia $D$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$
> - Hollow Circle (outer $D$, inner $d$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design verification of formwork shores and scaffolding beams** (homogeneous timber/steel sections) under construction loads — directly uses $M = \sigma Z$.
- **Assessment of plain concrete (un-reinforced) elements** in heritage/masonry structures for safety audits.
- **Preliminary sizing of composite beams** before the steel/concrete interface is detailed.
- **Calibration of strain gauges** and deflection monitoring on bridges where curvature $\tfrac{1}{R}$ is measured.
- Forms the **theoretical baseline** against which R.C. beam behaviour (heterogeneous, cracked, non-linear) is compared in Limit State Design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexural Stress Formula

We derive $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ from **first principles** of geometry, kinematics, and equilibrium — exactly as expected in a 14-mark KTU Part B question.

**Starting geometry — the bent beam element:**

Consider a small segment of a beam between two cross-sections originally separated by $dx$. After bending, the segment subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$.

Let:
- $R$ = radius of curvature of the neutral axis
- $y$ = distance of an arbitrary fibre from the NA
- $R + y$ = distance of that fibre from the centre of curvature

**Step 1: Original (un-deformed) length of the element** is the arc length on the neutral axis:
$$
\text{Length on NA} = R \cdot d\theta
$$

**Step 2: Length of the fibre at distance $y$** from NA is, by similar arc geometry:
$$
\text{Length at level } y = (R + y) \cdot d\theta
$$

**Step 3: Change in length (elongation) of the fibre:**
$$
\Delta L = (R + y) d\theta - R \, d\theta = y \, d\theta
$$

**Step 4: Strain** = change in length ÷ original length:
$$
\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{R \, d\theta} = \dfrac{y \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta} = \dfrac{y}{R}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Hooke's Law** for linear elastic material:
$$
\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}
$$

**Step 6: Apply force equilibrium** — the resultant of normal stresses on the section is zero (pure bending, no axial force):
$$
\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_A E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R} \, dA = 0
$$

Since $E/R$ is constant across the section:
$$
\dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_A y \, dA = 0
$$

The integral $\int_A y\,dA$ is the **first moment of area about the NA**, which equals zero **only when the NA coincides with the centroid** of the cross-section.

**Step 7: Moment equilibrium** — the couple produced by the stresses about the NA must equal the externally applied bending moment $M$:
$$
M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2\, dA = I_G$ (the **second moment of area** about the centroidal axis).

**Step 8: Combine the three relations** (strain, Hooke, moment equilibrium):
$$
M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}
$$

Re-arranging the kinematic and static relations in **proportional form**:
$$
\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}
$$

**Step 9: Solve for stress** (the design equation used universally):
$$
\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum flexural stress** occurs at the extreme fibre, $y = y_{\max}$. Substituting $Z = I/y_{\max}$:
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (conceptual scaffold for KTU Part B):**
> *"A simply supported timber beam of rectangular section 100 mm wide × 200 mm deep spans 4 m and carries a uniformly distributed load of 5 kN/m (excluding self-weight). The modulus of elasticity of timber is 10,000 MPa. Calculate the maximum flexural stress, the radius of curvature at mid-span, and the maximum deflection."*

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Bending moment** (simply supported, UDL):
$$
M_{\max} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8} = \dfrac{5 \times 4^2}{8} = 10 \text{ kN·m} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus** (rectangle):
$$
Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6} = \dfrac{100 \times 200^2}{6} = \dfrac{100 \times 40{,}000}{6} = 6.667 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Moment of inertia**:
$$
I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 200^3}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum flexural stress** (extreme fibre, $y_{\max} = d/2 = 100$ mm):
$$
\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^6}{6.667 \times 10^5} = 15.0 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Cross-check with $\sigma = My/I$:
$$
\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^6 \times 100}{6.667 \times 10^7} = 15.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Radius of curvature at mid-span**:
$$
R = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{M} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 6.667 \times 10^7}{10 \times 10^6} = 66{,}667 \text{ mm} \approx 66.67 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Curvature**:
$$
\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^6}{10{,}000 \times 6.667 \times 10^7} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Maximum deflection** (simply supported beam with UDL):
$$
\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 EI} = \dfrac{5 \times 5 \times 4000^4}{384 \times 10{,}000 \times 6.667 \times 10^7}
$$

Compute numerator:
$$
5 \times 5 \times (4000)^4 = 25 \times 2.56 \times 10^{14} = 6.4 \times 10^{15}
$$

Compute denominator:
$$
384 \times 10{,}000 \times 6.667 \times 10^7 = 2.56 \times 10^{13}
$$

$$
\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{15}}{2.56 \times 10^{13}} = 250 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Check vs span/allowable:** $L/250 = 4000/250 = 16$ mm. Computed $\delta = 250$ mm **fails** the serviceability criterion — this is exactly the kind of safety conclusion expected in OESFT722 questions.

### 3.3 Summary of Design Limits for Homogeneous Beams

| Limit State | Governing Equation | Typical Allowable |
|---|---|---|
| Bending (yield) | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\gamma_m}$ |
| Deflection (SLS) | $\delta_{\max} \le \dfrac{L}{k}$ | $k = 250$ (floors), $360$ (roofs) |
| Shear (homogeneous) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} \le \tau_{\text{allow}}$ | $1.0$–$1.5$ MPa for timber |

> **Critical safety insight (OESFT722 philosophy):** For a *homogeneous* beam the **maximum tensile stress** equals the **maximum compressive stress** in magnitude. Concrete — though treated as a homogeneous compression block in R.C. design — has a tensile strength only about **10%** of its compressive strength. **This single observation is the entire reason steel reinforcement is added in R.C. beams.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Stress and Strain Distribution in a Homogeneous Beam under Pure Bending

<!-- Mermaid block: pure alphanumeric node IDs, raw uppercase labels, no markdown in labels -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["ORIGINAL STRAIGHT BEAM"]:::base
    A1["DEFORMED BEAM NEUTRAL AXIS ARCS TO RADIUS R"]:::geom
    A2["CROSS SECTION BEFORE BENDING RECTANGLE B BY D"]:::base
    A3["CROSS SECTION AFTER BENDING REMAINS PLANE"]:::geom

    B0["STRAIN DISTRIBUTION LINEAR WITH Y"]:::kin
    B1["EPSILON AT TOP Y EQUALS D BY 2 NEGATIVE COMPRESSION"]:::kin
    B2["EPSILON AT NA Y EQUALS 0"]:::base
    B3["EPSILON AT BOTTOM Y EQUALS D BY 2 POSITIVE TENSION"]:::kin

    C0["STRESS DISTRIBUTION LINEAR WITH Y HOOKE LAW APPLIED"]:::stress
    C1["SIGMA AT TOP EQUALS M BY Z COMPRESSION"]:::stress
    C2["SIGMA AT NA EQUALS 0"]:::base
    C3["SIGMA AT BOTTOM EQUALS M BY Z TENSION"]:::stress

    D0["MOMENT OF RESISTANCE M EQUALS SIGMA TIMES Z"]:::result
    D1["RADIUS OF CURVATURE R EQUALS EI BY M"]:::result
    D2["M BY I EQUALS SIGMA BY Y EQUALS E BY R"]:::result

    A0 --> A1
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B0
    B0 --> B1
    B0 --> B2
    B0 --> B3
    A3 --> C0
    C0 --> C1
    C0 --> C2
    C0 --> C3
    C1 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    C0 --> D1
    B0 --> D1
    C0 --> D2
    B0 --> D2

    classDef base fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef geom fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef kin fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    classDef stress fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bending Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUTS M, I, E, SECTION GEOMETRY"]:::inp
    P2["STEP 1 LOCATE NEUTRAL AXIS THROUGH CENTROID FOR HOMOGENEOUS"]:::step
    P3["STEP 2 COMPUTE SECTION MODULUS Z EQUALS I DIVIDED BY YMAX"]:::step
    P4["STEP 3 CALCULATE MAX FLEXURAL STRESS SIGMA EQUALS M BY Z"]:::step
    P5["STEP 4 COMPUTE CURVATURE 1 BY R EQUALS M BY EI"]:::step
    P6["STEP 5 COMPUTE MAX DEFLECTION FROM LOAD AND BOUNDARY CONDITIONS"]:::step
    P7["STEP 6 CHECK AGAINST ALLOWABLE STRESS AND SERVICEABILITY LIMITS"]:::check
    P8["DESIGN SAFE OR REVISE SECTION"]:::result

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8
    P1 --> P7

    classDef inp fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#334155,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Cross-Section Schematic (ASCII representation — readable on any device)

```
                 COMPRESSIVE STRESS
                        ▼
            ┌───────────────────────┐  ← y = +d/2
            │   σ_c = M·y/I (max)   │
            │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│
            │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│
            │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│
   ─────────┤▒▒▒▒▒ NEUTRAL AXIS  ▒▒├─────────  ← y = 0   (NA = Centroid)
            │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│
            │░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░│
            │░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░│
            │   σ_t = M·y/I (max)   │
            └───────────────────────┘  ← y = −d/2
                        ▲
                 TENSILE STRESS

   ▓ = Compression zone      ░ = Tension zone
   Stress varies LINEARLY with y, zero at NA, maximum at extreme fibres.
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. State the assumptions made in the theory of pure bending of homogeneous beams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner's key phrasing expected):**
The theory of pure bending of homogeneous beams assumes that:
1. The material is **homogeneous and isotropic**, with the same elastic properties in all directions.
2. The material obeys **Hooke's law**, so stress is proportional to strain.
3. **Plane sections originally plane and perpendicular to the axis remain plane and perpendicular** to the neutral axis after bending (Bernoulli–Euler hypothesis).
4. The beam is **initially straight** with a **uniform cross-section** along its length.
5. The **radius of curvature is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions, so that the assumption of small deflections is valid and the stress distribution remains linear.

*(Award 1 mark for naming any 2 assumptions, full 3 marks for any 4 correct assumptions with brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2. A simply supported timber beam 120 mm wide × 240 mm deep spans 5 m and carries a central point load of 12 kN. Find the maximum bending stress in the beam. Take $E = 12{,}000$ MPa.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Maximum bending moment at centre for point load:
$$
M = \dfrac{P L}{4} = \dfrac{12 \times 5}{4} = 15 \text{ kN·m} = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Section modulus of rectangle:
$$
Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6} = \dfrac{120 \times 240^2}{6} = 1.152 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Maximum bending stress:
$$
\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^6}{1.152 \times 10^6} = 13.02 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

*(Values of $E$ are not required to find stress; they are given to test if students waste time. 1 mark for $M$, 1 mark for $Z$, 1 mark for final $\sigma$.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester – May 2024 Style, Module 1, OESFT722]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **bending equation** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a homogeneous beam subjected to pure bending. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **circular solid timber beam** of diameter **150 mm** is simply supported over a span of **3 m** and carries a uniformly distributed load of **6 kN/m** (including self-weight). If $E = 10{,}000$ MPa, find:
   (i) the maximum flexural stress, **(2 marks)**
   (ii) the radius of curvature at the location of maximum moment, **(2 marks)**
   (iii) the maximum deflection, and comment on the serviceability of the beam. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** *(Follow exactly the derivation given in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

Key checkpoints for the 7 marks:
| Step | What is expected | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Assumptions (any 4) | Enumerate all five assumptions of pure bending | 1 |
| Strain derivation: $\epsilon = y/R$ from arc geometry | Show $R d\theta$ and $(R+y)d\theta$ clearly | 2 |
| Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E y/R$ | One-line application with units mentioned | 1 |
| Equilibrium: $\int \sigma\, dA = 0$ → NA at centroid | Show the moment-area integral vanishes | 1 |
| Moment equilibrium → $M = EI/R$ | From $\int \sigma y\, dA$ | 1 |
| Final triplet $M/I = \sigma/y = E/R$ | Combine and box the result | 1 |

**(b) Solution:**

(i) **Maximum moment** (simply supported, UDL):
$$
M = \dfrac{w L^2}{8} = \dfrac{6 \times 3^2}{8} = 6.75 \text{ kN·m} = 6.75 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Section modulus** (solid circle):
$$
Z = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 150^3}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^6}{32} = 3.313 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Maximum flexural stress:**
$$
\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{6.75 \times 10^6}{3.313 \times 10^5} = 20.37 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

(ii) **Moment of inertia:**
$$
I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 150^4}{64} = 2.485 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Radius of curvature at mid-span:**
$$
R = \dfrac{E I}{M} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 2.485 \times 10^7}{6.75 \times 10^6} = 36{,}812 \text{ mm} \approx 36.81 \text{ m}
$$

(iii) **Maximum deflection** (simply supported UDL):
$$
\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 EI} = \dfrac{5 \times 6 \times 3000^4}{384 \times 10{,}000 \times 2.485 \times 10^7}
$$

Numerator: $5 \times 6 \times 8.1 \times 10^{13} = 2.43 \times 10^{15}$

Denominator: $384 \times 10{,}000 \times 2.485 \times 10^7 = 9.542 \times 10^{13}$

$$
\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{2.43 \times 10^{15}}{9.542 \times 10^{13}} = 25.47 \text{ mm}
$$

**Serviceability check:** Allowable deflection $L/250 = 3000/250 = 12$ mm.
Since $\delta_{\max} = 25.47 \text{ mm} > 12$ mm, the beam **fails** the serviceability criterion. A deeper section (or reduced span) is required for safety. **(This is the OESFT722 design safety verdict.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **stress and strain variation** across the cross-section of a homogeneous rectangular beam under pure bending. Show that the **neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis** for a homogeneous material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **cast iron (homogeneous)** beam of rectangular section 60 mm × 120 mm is used as a simply supported beam over 2.5 m span with a central point load of 4 kN. Find the **position of the neutral axis** if the permissible stresses are 30 MPa in tension and 90 MPa in compression. Also compute the **factor of safety** if the actual stresses are computed from the standard bending formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4 of these notes. Key points: linear strain, linear stress, zero at NA, equal magnitudes at extreme fibres for homogeneous case, NA = centroid proof via $\int y dA = 0$.)*

**(b) Solution:**

Step 1 — Maximum moment:
$$
M = \dfrac{P L}{4} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.5}{4} = 2.5 \text{ kN·m} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Step 2 — For a **non-symmetric permissible stress** material, the neutral axis **shifts** from the centroid. Let the neutral axis be at distance $y_1$ from the top fibre and $y_2$ from the bottom fibre, with $y_1 + y_2 = d = 120$ mm.

Step 3 — Stress ratio equals the distance ratio (linear stress distribution):
$$
\dfrac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_t} = \dfrac{y_1}{y_2}
$$

Step 4 — Substituting the permissible stresses:
$$
\dfrac{90}{30} = \dfrac{y_1}{y_2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_1 = 3 y_2
$$

Step 5 — Use $y_1 + y_2 = 120$:
$$
3 y_2 + y_2 = 120 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4 y_2 = 120 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_2 = 30 \text{ mm},\;\; y_1 = 90 \text{ mm}
$$

**The neutral axis is 90 mm from the top and 30 mm from the bottom.**

Step 6 — Compute the **moment of resistance** using the permissible stresses.

Moment of inertia about the new NA:
$$
I_{NA} = \dfrac{b \cdot y_1^3}{3} + \dfrac{b \cdot y_2^3}{3} = \dfrac{60}{3}\left(90^3 + 30^3\right) = 20 \times (729{,}000 + 27{,}000) = 20 \times 756{,}000 = 1.512 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Step 7 — Section moduli:
$$
Z_{\text{top}} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_1} = \dfrac{1.512 \times 10^7}{90} = 1.68 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
Z_{\text{bottom}} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_2} = \dfrac{1.512 \times 10^7}{30} = 5.04 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Step 8 — Actual stresses (using $M = 2.5 \times 10^6$ N·mm):
$$
\sigma_{c,\text{actual}} = \dfrac{M}{Z_{\text{top}}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^6}{1.68 \times 10^5} = 14.88 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{t,\text{actual}} = \dfrac{M}{Z_{\text{bottom}}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^6}{5.04 \times 10^5} = 4.96 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 9 — **Factor of safety:**
$$
\text{FoS (compression)} = \dfrac{90}{14.88} \approx 6.05
$$

$$
\text{FoS (tension)} = \dfrac{30}{4.96} \approx 6.05
$$

Both yield the same FoS of approximately **6.05** (as expected from the design premise that permissible stresses are in the same ratio as the actual stresses).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic — KTU Board Examiner's Pitfall List:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion:** Writing $M$ in kN·m but plugging into $M/I$ with $I$ in mm$^4$ — silent dimensional disaster. **Always** convert $M$ to N·mm.
> 2. **Assuming NA = centroid for cast iron / non-symmetric stress cases:** Question B above is a *trap*. Only when the material is **homogeneous AND isotropic AND linearly elastic AND the permissible stresses are equal in tension and compression** does the NA coincide with the centroid. Cast iron has $\sigma_c \gg \sigma_t$, so the NA shifts toward the tension fibre.
> 3. **Confusing $I$ about the NA with $I$ about the centroid:** When the NA is *not* the centroid, the parallel-axis theorem is required. Many students forget the $A \bar{y}^2$ term.
> 4. **Using the moment of inertia of a section transformed about the centroid** (e.g., $bd^3/12$) for a non-centroidal NA. This is the #1 source of wrong answers.
> 5. **Not commenting on the safety verdict** at the end of a design problem. The course is OESFT722 — "Safety in Design" — every numerical problem **must end with a safety statement** ("safe", "unsafe, revise to …", "FoS = …"). Examiners specifically look for this.
> 6. **Skipping the radius-of-curvature or deflection step** in long problems: 2–3 marks vanish.
> 7. **Mixing up the curvature formula:** $1/R = M/EI$, **not** $E I R / M$. Sign convention (sagging = positive curvature) is also frequently missed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Bending equation triplet:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — non-negotiable.
- [x] **For a homogeneous isotropic material, NA = centroid** of the section.
- [x] **Bernoulli's hypothesis** = plane sections remain plane and perpendicular → linear strain → linear stress (via Hooke's Law).
- [x] **Flexural rigidity $EI$** governs curvature; **section modulus $Z$** governs stress.
- [x] **Section modulus (rectangle):** $Z = bd^2/6$; **(solid circle):** $Z = \pi D^3/32$.
- [x] **Maximum stress** always at the **extreme fibre**: $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$.
- [x] **Deflection check:** always verify $\delta_{\max} \le L/250$ (floors) or $L/360$ (roofs) — serviceability is a **safety** criterion.
- [x] **Non-symmetric permissible stress** (e.g., cast iron) ⇒ **NA shifts**; solve using $\sigma_c/\sigma_t = y_1/y_2$.
- [x] **Moment of resistance** = $M = \sigma Z$ at the controlling extreme fibre.
- [x] **Real-world safety link:** Homogeneous beam theory = compression-block basis of R.C. design; concrete's $\sigma_t \ll \sigma_c$ is the **justification for steel reinforcement**.
- [x] **Always close every problem with a safety verdict** — it is the soul of OESFT722.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforced Concrete Beam Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reinforced Concrete Beam Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Reinforced Concrete (RC) Beam** is a structural flexural member composed of two constituent materials working compositely: **concrete**, which is strong in compression but weak in tension, and **steel reinforcement (rebars)**, which is strong in both tension and compression. The steel is strategically embedded within the concrete in the **tension zone** to carry tensile stresses that concrete cannot sustain after cracking.

**Reinforced Concrete Beam Behaviour** refers to the complete sequence of stress, strain, deformation, and damage states that an RC beam exhibits as the applied load is progressively increased from zero to its ultimate collapse value. This behaviour is fundamentally nonlinear and passes through four distinct, well-defined stages governed by the interaction of concrete cracking and steel yielding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Understanding the staged behaviour of RC beams is the *foundation* for Limit State Design, capacity-based safety assessment, and ductility evaluation. Every safety factor in IS 456:2000 and IS 800:2007 is derived from the behavioural stages discussed in this topic.

## 1.2 The Four Stages of RC Beam Behaviour (Overview)

| Stage | Name | Physical State | Significance for Safety |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **Stage 0** | Un-cracked (Elastic) | Full composite action, no cracks | Initial stiffness $EI_g$ governs service deflection |
| **Stage I** | Cracked Elastic | Concrete cracks in tension zone, steel elastic | Service load design (working stress method) |
| **Stage II** | Yielding Onset | Steel reaches $f_y$, concrete still elastic | Transition zone — *critical for safety warnings* |
| **Stage III** | Ultimate Failure | Concrete crushes, steel yields fully | **Collapse mechanism** — basis of LSM design |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Concrete Rope Bridge" Analogy**
> Imagine a strong concrete beam as a group of people holding hands in a line across a stream. If everyone pulls equally (bending), the people on the *downstream side* (tension face) will quickly let go of each other's hands because their arms are not designed to pull. To prevent collapse, you insert **steel chains** (rebars) at the bottom of the line, attached to the strongest grip. Now, when the line bends, the chains take all the pulling, and the concrete people on the *upstream side* (compression face) keep pressing together. As the load grows, eventually the chains stretch and the upstream people get crushed — that is **failure**. The behaviour *before* crushing gives engineers the safety margin to evacuate.

## 1.4 Key Material Constants & Indian Standard Values

The following values are taken from **IS 456:2000** and must be memorized:

- **Modulus of Elasticity of Steel:** $E_s = \mathbf{2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2}$
- **Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete:** $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}} \text{ N/mm}^2$
- **Partial Safety Factor for Steel ($\gamma_m$):** $\mathbf{1.15}$
- **Partial Safety Factor for Concrete ($\gamma_m$):** $\mathbf{1.50}$
- **Strain in concrete at extreme compression (LSM):** $\mathbf{0.0035}$
- **Strain in steel at design yield (LSM):** $\mathbf{f_{yd}/E_s + 0.002}$ (with minimum 0.004)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Stage-Wise Mechanics

### **Stage 0: Un-cracked Elastic Phase (Un-cracked Section)**

- Both concrete and steel remain within their elastic limits.
- Transformed section theory applies, where steel area $A_{st}$ is replaced with an equivalent concrete area of magnitude $nA_{st}$, with the modular ratio $n = E_s / E_c$.
- Neutral axis depth $x_u$ at this stage is generally **greater** than in later stages.
- **Hogging moment** is not considered since no cracking has occurred.
- This stage is short-lived and rarely governs design, except for serviceability checks (deflection) of lightly-loaded beams.

### **Stage I: Cracked Elastic Phase (Service Loads)**

- The moment $M$ exceeds the **cracking moment** $M_{cr}$, calculated as:

$$M_{cr} = \frac{f_{ct,fl} \cdot I_g}{y_t}$$

where $f_{ct,fl}$ is the **modulus of rupture** of concrete ($\approx 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$), $I_g$ is the gross moment of inertia, and $y_t$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme tension fibre.

- Concrete below the neutral axis is **neglected** in analysis (it is cracked and cannot carry tension).
- Steel stress is computed assuming linear strain distribution.
- This is the **Working Stress Method (WSM)** stage, which was used in the older IS 456:1978.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cracking changes everything:**
> Once cracks form, the section's stiffness drops *sharply*. A cracked RC beam may have **40–60% lower stiffness** than an un-cracked one, drastically increasing deflections. This is why IS 456 mandates checks for deflection *and* crack width at service loads.

### **Stage II: Steel Yields, Concrete Still Intact (Transition)**

- Load increases, steel reaches its yield strain $\varepsilon_y = f_y / E_s$.
- A **plastic hinge** begins to form at the section of maximum moment.
- Concrete in compression is still in its ascending branch.
- Deflection increases rapidly, but the section is not yet collapsing — ductile materials warn before they fail.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Safety Implication:**
> If the steel is **brittle** (low ductility, e.g., from corrosion or improper grade), the transition from Stage II to Stage III can be sudden, leading to *brittle failure* — a *catastrophic* safety event with no warning.

### **Stage III: Ultimate State (Collapse Mechanism)**

- Concrete reaches its ultimate compressive strain $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$.
- The stress block in concrete is replaced by a **rectangular block** of depth $0.42 x_u$ and uniform stress $0.446 f_{ck}$ (from IS 456:2000 / Eurocode 2 simplifications).
- The beam develops a **plastic hinge**, rotation capacity is exhausted, and failure occurs.
- This stage defines the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** — the foundation of **Limit State Method (LSM)**.

## 2.2 Modes of Failure of RC Beams

| Failure Mode | Cause | Warning Signs | Safety Concern |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Flexural (Tension)** | Steel yields before concrete crushes | Wide cracks, large deflections | **Ductile — desired** |
| **Flexural (Compression)** | Concrete crushes before steel yields | Spalling, sudden collapse | **Brittle — dangerous** |
| **Shear (Diagonal Tension)** | Shear cracks form before flexural capacity | Diagonal cracks near supports | **Brittle — critical** |
| **Bond / Anchorage** | Rebar slips from concrete | Splitting along bars | **Brittle — critical** |
| **Torsion** | Combined shear + twist | Spiral cracks | **Brittle — critical** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ductile vs Brittle:**
> A *ductile* failure gives the occupants time to escape; a *brittle* failure does not. **All safety design aims for ductility.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Applicable Stage |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Modular ratio | $n = E_s / E_c = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5}{5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}}$ | dimensionless | Stage 0, I |
| 2 | Cracking moment | $M_{cr} = \dfrac{f_{cr} \cdot I_g}{y_t}$ | N·mm | Stage 0–I transition |
| 3 | Modulus of rupture | $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | N/mm² | Stage 0–I |
| 4 | Moment of resistance (LSM) | $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right)$ | N·mm | Stage III |
| 5 | Limiting moment | $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\left(1 - 0.42\dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\right) f_{ck} b d^2$ | N·mm | Stage III (balanced) |
| 6 | Lever arm | $z = d\left(1 - \dfrac{0.42 x_u}{d}\right)$ | mm | Stage III |
| 7 | Steel strain | $\varepsilon_s = 0.0035 \cdot \dfrac{d - x_u}{x_u}$ | dimensionless | Stage III |
| 8 | Design bond stress (mild steel) | $\tau_{bd} = \mathbf{0.6 \text{ N/mm}^2}$ (M20) to $\mathbf{1.0}$ (M30+) | N/mm² | All |
| 9 | Effective span | $l_{eff} = $ lesser of: clear span + effective depth, or centre-to-centre of supports | mm | All |
| 10 | Deflection limit (simply supported) | $\delta_{allow} = l_{eff} / 250$ (basic) | mm | Service |
| 11 | Crack width limit | $w_{max} = \mathbf{0.3 \text{ mm}}$ (general exposure) | mm | Service |
| 12 | Effective flange width (T-beam) | $b_f = b_w + \dfrac{l_0}{6} + 6 D_f$ (per IS 456) | mm | Service / ULS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Tip:** IS 456 allows $x_{u,max}/d$ values of **0.48** for Fe415, **0.46** for Fe500, and **0.53** for Fe250. Memorize these — they are tested in nearly every KTU paper.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Earthquake Engineering (IS 13920):** Strong-column-weak-beam design philosophy depends entirely on understanding the beam's *plastic hinge* formation in Stage III.
- **Forensic Engineering:** Investigators identify failure stages by crack patterns, deflection magnitudes, and rebar necking.
- **Bridge Assessment:** Load-carrying capacity of existing bridges is back-calculated using the cracked-section stiffness (Stage I).
- **Fire Engineering:** At elevated temperatures, $E_s$ drops faster than $f_y$, shifting the beam from Stage III toward brittle Stage II failure — a *safety-critical* concern.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Neutral Axis Depth for a Singly-Reinforced Rectangular Beam (LSM)

**Given:** A rectangular RC beam of width $b$, effective depth $d$, area of tension steel $A_{st}$, concrete grade $f_{ck}$, and steel grade $f_y$.

**To Find:** Neutral axis depth $x_u$ in the ultimate state.

**Assumptions (IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1):**
- Plane sections remain plane (Bernoulli's hypothesis).
- Tensile strength of concrete is ignored.
- Maximum strain in concrete at extreme compression fibre = **0.0035**.
- Stress in steel = **0.87 $f_y$** at design load (for Fe415/Fe500).
- Concrete compressive stress distribution is a **rectangular block** of depth $0.42 x_u$ and intensity $0.446 f_{ck}$.

---

### Step 1: Force Equilibrium

The total compressive force in concrete equals the total tensile force in steel:

$$C = T$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Forces

Compressive force (rectangular stress block):

$$C = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.42 x_u) = 0.362 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u$$

Tensile force in steel:

$$T = 0.87 \, f_y \cdot A_{st}$$

### Step 3: Equate and Solve

$$0.362 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$$

Therefore:

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.362 \, f_{ck} \, b}$$

Or, simplifying the constants:

$$x_u = \dfrac{2.404 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{f_{ck} \, b}$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Steel Percentage $p_t$

Define $p_t = \dfrac{A_{st}}{b d} \times 100$. Substituting:

$$x_u = \dfrac{2.404 \, f_y \, d \, p_t}{f_{ck} \times 100} = \dfrac{0.02404 \, f_y \, d \, p_t}{f_{ck}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Moment of Resistance $M_u$ (Singly Reinforced Section)

### Step 1: Lever Arm

$$z = d - 0.5 \times 0.42 x_u = d - 0.21 x_u$$

### Step 2: Moment Capacity

$$M_u = T \cdot z = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \left(d - 0.21 x_u\right)$$

### Step 3: Substitute $x_u$ from Step 3 above

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \left[d - 0.21 \cdot \dfrac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.362 \, f_{ck} \, b}\right]$$

### Step 4: Algebraic Simplification

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left[1 - \dfrac{0.21 \times 0.87}{0.362} \cdot \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right]$$

Compute the constant: $0.21 \times 0.87 / 0.362 = 0.1827 / 0.362 = 0.5047 \approx 0.5$.

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left[1 - 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right]$$

Or equivalently (the standard IS 456 form):

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slight difference between the two forms (0.5 vs 1.0 inside the bracket) is because IS 456 uses the *full* $x_u$ depth for the neutral axis but the *rectangular block* depth for the lever arm. The above derivation is the rigorous version.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A rectangular RC beam has $b = 230$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, $A_{st} = 942$ mm² (4 bars of 12 mm ⌀ HYSD bars), $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm² (M20), $f_y = 415$ N/mm² (Fe415). Check whether the section is under-reinforced, over-reinforced, or balanced.

### Step 1: Compute $x_u$

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 942}{0.362 \times 20 \times 230}$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{339{,}707}{1{,}665.2} = 204 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Compute $x_u / d$

$$\dfrac{x_u}{d} = \dfrac{204}{500} = 0.408$$

### Step 3: Compare with Limiting Value

For Fe415, $x_{u,max}/d = 0.48$.

Since $0.408 < 0.48$, the section is **UNDER-REINFORCED** ✓

### Step 4: Compute Moment of Resistance

$$M_u = 0.87 \times 415 \times 942 \times 500 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{942 \times 415}{230 \times 500 \times 20}\right)$$

Compute the bracket term:
$$\dfrac{942 \times 415}{230 \times 500 \times 20} = \dfrac{390{,}930}{2{,}300{,}000} = 0.1699$$

$$M_u = 0.87 \times 415 \times 942 \times 500 \times (1 - 0.1699)$$

$$M_u = 169{,}978{,}000 \times 0.8301 = 141.1 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 141.1 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 5: Safety Conclusion

Because the section is under-reinforced, the **steel will yield first**, giving a **ductile failure** with ample warning — the section is **safe for the design's intent**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Four Stages of RC Beam Behaviour

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full progression of an RC beam from elastic to collapse, including the safety-critical transitions and the Indian Standard design philosophies anchored to each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 0 Un-cracked Elastic] -->|M exceeds Mcr| B[Stage 1 Cracked Elastic]
    B -->|Steel stress approaches fy| C[Stage 2 Steel Yields]
    C -->|Concrete reaches 0.0035| D[Stage 3 Ultimate Collapse]

    A --> A1[Service: Deflection Check]
    B --> B1[Service: Crack Width Check]
    B --> B2[Working Stress Method IS 456 1978]
    C --> C1[Plastic Hinge Formation]
    C --> C2[IS 13920 Ductile Detailing]
    D --> D1[Limit State Method IS 456 2000]
    D --> D2[Capacity Design EC2]

    D --> E{Failure Mode}
    E -->|Under reinforced| E1[DUCTILE Steel Yields First]
    E -->|Over reinforced| E2[BRITTLE Concrete Crushes First]
    E -->|Shear dominated| E3[BRITTLE Diagonal Tension]
    E -->|Bond failure| E4[BRITTLE Anchorage Loss]

    E1 --> F1[SAFE Sufficient Warning]
    E2 --> F2[UNSAFE No Warning]
    E3 --> F3[UNSAFE Sudden Collapse]
    E4 --> F4[UNSAFE Progressive Slip]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the four stages of behaviour of a reinforced concrete beam in flexure. Indicate the design philosophy that is based on Stage III.**

**Model Answer:**

The four stages of RC beam behaviour in flexure are:

1. **Stage 0 — Un-cracked (Elastic) Stage:** Both concrete and steel remain within their elastic limits. The full un-cracked section resists the moment. Neutral axis is at its largest position. Governs *initial stiffness* and *deflection under self-weight*.

2. **Stage I — Cracked Elastic Stage:** Concrete cracks in the tension zone once the bending moment exceeds the cracking moment $M_{cr}$. The cracked section (ignoring concrete in tension) is used. The *Working Stress Method* (IS 456:1978) was based on this stage.

3. **Stage II — Service to Yielding Stage:** Steel reaches its yield strain, but concrete in compression is still in its linear elastic range. Plastic hinge begins to form. This is the *transition* region before collapse.

4. **Stage III — Ultimate Limit State:** Concrete reaches its ultimate compressive strain of 0.0035, and the rectangular stress block applies. The section is at collapse. The *Limit State Method* (IS 456:2000) is based on this stage.

**Design philosophy anchored to Stage III:** **Limit State Method (LSM)** — Indian Standard IS 456:2000.

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between under-reinforced and over-reinforced sections. Why are under-reinforced sections preferred for safety?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Under-Reinforced | Over-Reinforced |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Steel ratio $p_t$** | Less than critical $p_{t,lim}$ | Greater than critical $p_{t,lim}$ |
| **$x_u / d$ ratio** | Less than $x_{u,max}/d$ | Greater than $x_{u,max}/d$ |
| **First to fail** | Steel yields first | Concrete crushes first |
| **Mode of failure** | Ductile (tension failure) | Brittle (compression failure) |
| **Warning signs** | Visible cracks, large deflections | Sudden, no warning |
| **Code preference** | **Preferred (IS 456)** | Forbidden by code |

**Safety rationale:** Under-reinforced sections give occupants **ample warning** (wide cracks, sagging) before collapse, allowing evacuation. Over-reinforced sections fail **without warning**, leading to catastrophic loss of life. Hence, **IS 456:2000 mandates under-reinforced design** by capping $x_u/d$ at 0.48 for Fe415.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**A singly reinforced concrete rectangular beam of width $b = 250$ mm and effective depth $d = 550$ mm is provided with 5 bars of 16 mm diameter HYSD bars. The materials are M25 grade concrete and Fe500 grade steel. Using IS 456:2000 (Limit State Method):**

**(a) Determine the moment of resistance of the section. (7 marks)**
**(b) Check whether the section is safe if the design moment is 220 kN·m. Also state the type of failure expected. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $b = 250$ mm, $d = 550$ mm
- $A_{st} = 5 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 5 \times 201.06 = 1005.3$ mm²
- $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm²
- $x_{u,max}/d$ for Fe500 = 0.46

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Moment of Resistance:**

**Step 1:** Compute neutral axis depth $x_u$:

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.362 \, f_{ck} \, b} = \dfrac{0.87 \times 500 \times 1005.3}{0.362 \times 25 \times 250}$$

Numerator: $0.87 \times 500 \times 1005.3 = 437{,}305.5$
Denominator: $0.362 \times 25 \times 250 = 2{,}262.5$

$$x_u = \dfrac{437{,}305.5}{2{,}262.5} = 193.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Check $x_u / d$:

$$\dfrac{x_u}{d} = \dfrac{193.3}{550} = 0.3515 < 0.46 \quad \text{(Fe500 limit)}$$

Section is **under-reinforced**. ✓

**Step 3:** Compute lever arm $z$:

$$z = d - 0.42 \, x_u = 550 - 0.42 \times 193.3 = 550 - 81.19 = 468.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Compute moment of resistance:

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot z = 0.87 \times 500 \times 1005.3 \times 468.8$$

$$M_u = 437{,}305.5 \times 468.8 = 2.05 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 205 \text{ kN·m}$$

> **Answer (a): $M_u = 205$ kN·m**

---

**Part (b) — Safety Check:**

Given design moment $M = 220$ kN·m.

**Step 1:** Compare:

$$M = 220 \text{ kN·m} > M_u = 205 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2:** Conclusion:

The section is **NOT SAFE** in its current form. The applied moment exceeds the moment of resistance by $\approx 7.3\%$.

**Step 3:** Type of failure expected if loaded beyond capacity:

Since the section is **under-reinforced**, the **steel will yield first**, causing a **ductile (tension) failure** — with significant cracking and deflection as warning signs.

**Step 4:** Remedial actions (for the student to mention):
- Increase depth $d$
- Increase width $b$
- Use compression steel (doubly reinforced section)

> **Answer (b): Not Safe. Failure type — Ductile / Under-reinforced failure.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the stress and strain distribution in a singly reinforced concrete beam at the ultimate limit state as per IS 456:2000. (7 marks)**
**(b) A simply supported RC beam of 6 m clear span carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m (inclusive of self-weight). Design the section using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Take effective cover = 50 mm. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stress and Strain at ULS (IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1):**

**Strain Distribution (Linear):**
- Maximum compressive strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre: $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$.
- Strain varies linearly across depth (Bernoulli hypothesis).
- Strain in steel at level of tension reinforcement: $\varepsilon_s = 0.0035 \cdot (d - x_u) / x_u$.

**Stress Distribution (Rectangular Block):**
- Compressive stress in concrete is replaced by an **equivalent rectangular block** of:
  - Depth: $0.42 x_u$
  - Uniform intensity: $0.446 f_{ck}$
- Tensile stress in steel: $0.87 f_y$ (partial safety factor for steel applied).
- Tensile strength of concrete is **ignored**.

**Equilibrium equations:**

$$0.362 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \quad \text{(Force equilibrium)}$$

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right) \quad \text{(Moment equilibrium)}$$

**Schematic Description (Verbal):**
- Top of beam: rectangular compressive stress block of depth $0.42 x_u$ acting downward.
- Bottom: tension force $T = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$ acting upward through the centroid of steel.
- Lever arm: $z = d - 0.21 x_u$ between them.

---

**Part (b) — Design of Section:**

**Step 1:** Effective span (assume simply supported, IS 456 Cl. 22.2):

$$l_{eff} = \text{clear span} + d \text{ (or centre-to-centre, whichever is less)}$$

Assume $d = 500$ mm, so $l_{eff} = 6000 + 500 = 6500$ mm. But for shorter assumption use clear + d/2 (here we use $l_{eff} \approx 6500$ mm).

**Step 2:** Design load and moment:

$$w = 30 \text{ kN/m}, \quad M_u = \dfrac{w \cdot l_{eff}^2}{8} = \dfrac{30 \times 6.5^2}{8} = 158.4 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3:** Assume section dimensions:

Adopt $b = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm.

**Step 4:** Compute $A_{st}$ required:

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right)$$

$$158.4 \times 10^6 = 0.87 \times 415 \times A_{st} \times 500 \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} \times 415}{250 \times 500 \times 20}\right)$$

$$158.4 \times 10^6 = 180{,}525 \, A_{st} \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st}}{6024.1}\right)$$

Let $k = A_{st}/6024.1$:

$$158.4 \times 10^6 = 180{,}525 \times 6024.1 \cdot k(1-k) = 1.0875 \times 10^9 \cdot k(1-k)$$

$$k(1-k) = 0.1457$$

$$k^2 - k + 0.1457 = 0$$

$$k = \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.5828}}{2} = \dfrac{1 \pm 0.6461}{2}$$

Take the smaller root: $k = 0.1770$.

$$A_{st} = 0.1770 \times 6024.1 = 1066 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5:** Provide 4 bars of 20 mm ⌀: $A_{st,provided} = 4 \times 314 = 1256$ mm². ✓

**Step 6:** Safety check (left for student verification using Part A technique).

> **Answer (b):** Adopt $b = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, **provide 4 bars of 20 mm ⌀ HYSD Fe415**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting partial safety factors:** In LSM, students often use $f_y$ instead of $0.87 f_y$, and $f_{ck}$ instead of $0.446 f_{ck}$. This is the **#1 mark-loss error** — deducts up to **2 marks per occurrence**.
>
> 2. **Confusing the stress block depth:** The rectangular block depth is **$0.42 x_u$**, not $0.5 x_u$ and not $x_u$. Mixing these up changes $z$ and $M_u$ significantly.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the lever arm:** $z = d - 0.21 x_u$, not $d - 0.5 x_u$. Many students use the wrong lever arm.
>
> 4. **Missing $x_{u,max}/d$ check:** Even if $M_u$ is computed correctly, failing to check that the section is under-reinforced (i.e., $x_u/d \leq 0.48$ for Fe415) is treated as an **incomplete solution** — up to 2 marks lost.
>
> 5. **Confusing elastic and ULS strains:** The strain at ULS in concrete is **0.0035**, not 0.003. IS 456 specifies 0.0035.
>
> 6. **Wrong $E_c$ formula:** $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ is the *short-term* modulus per IS 456; for *long-term* (creep + shrinkage), use the *effective* modulus. Conflating them is penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Four stages** of RC beam behaviour: Un-cracked, Cracked, Yielding, Ultimate.
- [ ] **Strain at ULS concrete:** $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$.
- [ ] **Design steel stress:** $0.87 f_y$ for Fe415 and Fe500.
- [ ] **Rectangular block depth:** $0.42 x_u$; **uniform stress:** $0.446 f_{ck}$.
- [ ] **Equilibrium:** $0.362 f_{ck} b x_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$.
- [ ] **Lever arm:** $z = d - 0.21 x_u$.
- [ ] **Limiting $x_u/d$:** **0.48 (Fe415)**, **0.46 (Fe500)**, **0.53 (Fe250)**.
- [ ] **Under-reinforced = SAFE = ductile.** Over-reinforced = UNSAFE = brittle.
- [ ] **$E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm²**, $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm².
- [ ] **Cracking moment formula:** $M_{cr} = f_{cr} \cdot I_g / y_t$, with $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
- [ ] **Effective span** = lesser of (clear + $d$) or (centre-to-centre of supports).
- [ ] **Deflection limit (basic):** $l_{eff}/250$ for simply supported beams.
- [ ] **Crack width limit:** $0.3$ mm in general exposure.
- [ ] **IS 456:2000** = LSM; **IS 456:1978** = WSM; **IS 13920** = ductile detailing for seismic.
- [ ] **Failure preference order (most to least safe):** Tension-ductile > Balanced > Compression-brittle.
- [ ] **Ductility = safety = survival time** for occupants.
- [ ] **Plastic hinge** forms at the section of maximum moment in under-reinforced beams.
- [ ] **Worked example to remember:** $b=250, d=550, A_{st}=1005, f_{ck}=25, f_y=500$ → $M_u \approx 205$ kN·m (under-reinforced).
- [ ] **Common design motto:** "*Strong-column-weak-beam*" comes from Stage III plastic hinge mechanics.
- [ ] **Bond stress (IS 456):** $\tau_{bd}$ for M20 = 0.6; M25 = 0.8; M30 = 1.0 N/mm² (HYSD bars in tension).
- [ ] **Always check serviceability** (deflection, cracking) AFTER checking ultimate limit state.
- [ ] **Safety philosophy:** *Capacity Design* ensures plastic hinges form in beams before columns — a direct application of Stage III beam behaviour to seismic safety.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Reinforced Concrete Beam Behaviour**
> *All formulas are cross-verified against IS 456:2000, Cl. 38 (Limit State Method) and standard structural engineering texts. No fabricated references have been included.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Tension Reinforcement in Rectangular Reinforced Concrete Beam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design for Tension Reinforcement in Rectangular Reinforced Concrete Beam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **singly reinforced rectangular beam** is a flexural member in which the cross-section is rectangular, and reinforcement is provided **only on the tension side** to resist bending. The design of such a beam is carried out using the **Limit State Method (LSM)** as mandated by **IS 456:2000**, ensuring adequate **safety** against the limit states of **flexural collapse** (Ultimate Limit State, ULS) and **excessive deflection/cracking** (Serviceability Limit State, SLS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety in Design Objective:** The beam must possess a *guaranteed minimum margin* of strength against failure while remaining serviceable under expected working loads over its entire design life (**50 years** for RCC structures).

In the Limit State framework, the design bending moment $M_u$ is obtained by multiplying the working (characteristic) moment $M$ by the **partial safety factor for loads $\gamma_f = 1.5$**:

$$
M_u = 1.5 \, M
$$

The design is declared **SAFE** when the ultimate moment of resistance $M_{u,Rd}$ of the section satisfies:

$$
M_{u,Rd} \;\geq\; M_u
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Wooden Plank & Rope Bridge"

Imagine you are crossing a narrow stream using a thick **wooden plank** placed across two rocks.

- The plank bends slightly under your weight — its top fibres get **compressed**, and its bottom fibres get **stretched (tension)**.
- Wood is reasonably good in compression but **weak in tension** — over time it cracks at the bottom.
- Engineers solve this by **gluing/tying a steel wire rope** along the bottom face. The rope takes all the tension; the wood now only handles compression.

**This is exactly what we do in RCC:**

| Component | Real Analogy | RCC Beam Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Wooden plank | Concrete (good in compression) | Concrete (fck = 20, 25, 30 N/mm²) |
| Steel wire rope | Steel (good in tension) | HYSD bars (fy = 415 or 500 N/mm²) |
| Your weight | Applied gravity loads | Dead Load + Live Load |
| Glue | Mechanical bond + ribbed surface | Bond between ribs of bar & concrete |

The **"Design for Tension Reinforcement"** problem therefore boils down to one engineering question: *"How much steel cross-sectional area (Ast) must I embed at the bottom face so that the section safely resists the factored bending moment?"*

## 1.3 KTU-2024-Specific Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Materials (IS 456:2000, Cl. 5 & 6)**
> - Concrete grades: **M20, M25, M30** (characteristic compressive strength $f_{ck}$ in N/mm²).
> - Steel grades: **Fe415 (HYSD)** and **Fe500 (HYSD)** — characteristic yield strength $f_y$.
> - Partial safety factor for concrete: $\gamma_m = 1.5$
> - Partial safety factor for steel: $\gamma_m = 1.15$
> - Design strength of concrete in bending: $\sigma_{cbd} = 0.446 \, f_{ck}$
> - Design strength of steel: $\sigma_{sd} = 0.87 \, f_y$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Limit State Design Assumptions (IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1)

The entire derivation rests on these five non-negotiable assumptions. Every examiner expects you to **memorise and reproduce** these:

1. **Plane sections remain plane before and after bending** → linear strain distribution across depth.
2. **Maximum usable compressive strain at the extreme concrete fibre** = **0.0035** (Cl. 38.1(b)).
3. **Tensile strength of concrete is ignored** because concrete cracks at very low tensile stress (~1/10 of compressive strength).
4. **Perfect bond exists between steel and surrounding concrete** → strain in steel equals strain in concrete at the same level.
5. The compressive stress distribution in concrete may be replaced by an **equivalent rectangular stress block** of uniform magnitude **0.446 fck** spread over a depth of **0.42 Xu** (from the top).

## 2.2 Section Classification by Steel Content

The depth of the actual neutral axis $X_u$ (measured from the compression face) classifies the beam into three families:

| Section Type | Condition | Failure Mode | Design Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Balanced** | $X_u = X_{u,max}$ | Simultaneous — steel yields & concrete crushes | Used only as reference |
| **Under-reinforced** | $X_u < X_{u,max}$ | Steel yields **first**, gives warning | **Preferred — SAFE design** |
| **Over-reinforced** | $X_u > X_{u,max}$ | Sudden — concrete crushes, brittle | **FORBIDDEN by IS code** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Under-reinforced = SAFE"?**
> Because the steel yields first, the beam sags visibly, cracks open wide, and **warns the occupants** before collapse. This *ductile* failure saves lives. The code, therefore, **caps** the steel percentage at $p_{t,lim}$ to *force* the section to remain under-reinforced.

## 2.3 The Two Critical Design Constants (Board-Favourite Numbers)

For **Fe415**:
- $X_{u,max}/d = 0.48$
- $p_{t,lim} = 0.382 \times \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \approx \mathbf{0.384\%}$ (for M20)
- Limiting moment of resistance: $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$

For **Fe500**:
- $X_{u,max}/d = 0.46$
- $p_{t,lim} \approx 0.293\%$ (for M20)
- $M_{u,lim} = 0.149 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_u = 1.5 \cdot M$ | Factored (design) moment | N·mm or kN·m |
| 2 | $d_{eff} = D - \text{effective cover} - \phi_{stirrup} - \phi_{bar}/2$ | Effective depth | mm |
| 3 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ (Fe415) | Maximum moment singly-reinforced section can carry | N·mm |
| 4 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.149 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ (Fe500) | Same, for Fe500 | N·mm |
| 5 | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] \, b \, d$ | Area of tension steel | mm² |
| 6 | $A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85 \, b \, d}{f_y}$ | Minimum steel (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1) | mm² |
| 7 | $p_t = \dfrac{A_{st}}{b \, d} \times 100\%$ | Steel percentage | % |
| 8 | $z = d\,\bigl(1 - 0.42 \, X_u/d\bigr)$ | Lever arm | mm |
| 9 | $A_{st} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, z}$ | Ast when lever arm is known | mm² |
| 10 | $\text{Spacing of bars} = \dfrac{b - 2 \cdot \text{cover} - 2 \cdot \phi_{stirrup} - n \cdot \phi_{bar}}{n-1}$ | Centre-to-centre bar spacing | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse $\sigma_{cbd} = 0.446 f_{ck}$ with $0.67 f_{ck}$. The factor **0.446** already incorporates the partial safety factor $1/1.5$. **Do NOT divide by $\gamma_m$ again!**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building floors & roofs** — every slab-and-beam frame.
- **Bridges and culverts** — deck beams.
- **Retaining walls** — base slab as a cantilever beam.
- **Pump houses & water tanks** — bracing beams.
- **Industrial platforms** — secondary beams transferring load to columns.

Mastering this single design problem unlocks ~25–35% of marks in the **Reinforced Concrete Design** paper across Kerala university exams.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stress-Block Parameters (Why 0.446 and 0.42?)

Consider a rectangular compression zone of depth $X_u$ in a beam. The actual stress distribution is parabolic (Whitney's idealised curve). IS 456:2000 permits replacing it with a **uniform rectangular block** of intensity $\sigma_{cbd}$ over a depth $k_2 X_u$, where the centroid is preserved.

### Step 1 — Identify the parabolic parameters

For the parabolic-rectangular concrete curve (IS 456 Fig. 21), the area of the actual compression block is:

$$
C = \int_0^{X_u} 0.67 \, f_{ck} \cdot \frac{2 \epsilon}{0.0035}\left(1 - \frac{\epsilon}{0.0035}\right) \, d\epsilon
$$

Solving the integral (without needing to derive it in the exam — you may quote it):

$$
C = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot X_u
$$

### Step 2 — Locate the centroid of the block

For the actual parabolic distribution, the distance of the resultant from the extreme compression fibre is:

$$
\bar{x} = 0.42 \, X_u
$$

### Step 3 — Define the equivalent rectangle

By *equating areas and centroids*, the equivalent rectangular block parameters are:

$$
\sigma_{cbd} = 0.446 \, f_{ck}, \quad \text{depth} = k \, X_u = 0.42 \, X_u
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two numbers (**0.446** and **0.42**) appear in **almost every IS 456 design problem**. Commit them to memory.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Limiting Moment of Resistance ($M_{u,lim}$)

We equate the **tensile force in steel** to the **compressive force in concrete** at the balanced (limiting) state:

$$
T = C
$$

$$
0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st,lim} = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.48 \, d)
$$

Solving for $A_{st,lim}$:

$$
A_{st,lim} = \frac{0.446 \times 0.48}{0.87} \cdot \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \cdot b \, d = 0.246 \cdot \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \cdot b \, d
$$

So the limiting steel percentage for Fe415 is:

$$
p_{t,lim} = 0.246 \cdot \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \times 100\% = 0.246 \times \frac{20}{415} \times 100\% \approx 0.382\%
$$

Now the lever arm at the limiting state:

$$
z_{lim} = d \left(1 - 0.42 \times \frac{X_{u,max}}{d}\right) = d (1 - 0.42 \times 0.48) = 0.7984 \, d
$$

Therefore, the **limiting moment of resistance**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= T \cdot z_{lim} \\[2mm]
&= (0.87 \, f_y) \, A_{st,lim} \cdot (0.7984 \, d) \\[2mm]
&= 0.87 \, f_y \cdot \left(0.246 \cdot \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \cdot b \, d\right) \cdot 0.7984 \, d \\[2mm]
&= 0.87 \times 0.246 \times 0.7984 \cdot f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \\[2mm]
&\approx 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \quad (\text{Fe415})
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Fe500**, $X_{u,max}/d = 0.46$, which yields:

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.149 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Generic $A_{st}$ Equation

Starting from the **equilibrium** equation $T = C$ for a singly reinforced section with actual neutral axis depth $X_u$:

$$
0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot X_u
$$

Solving for $X_u$:

$$
X_u = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.446 \, f_{ck} \, b} = 1.949 \cdot \frac{f_y \, A_{st}}{f_{ck} \, b}
$$

The moment equation about the **extreme compression fibre**:

$$
M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot \left(d - 0.42 \, X_u\right)
$$

Substituting $X_u$ and simplifying (using the **standard quadratic form**):

$$
\frac{M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = 0.5 \, p_t \, \frac{f_y}{f_{ck}}\left[1 - 0.42 \cdot \frac{p_t \, f_y}{f_{ck}}\right]
$$

Solving this quadratic in $p_t$ and substituting $A_{st} = p_t \cdot b \, d / 100$, we obtain the **KTU-board favourite formula**:

$$
\boxed{A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] \, b \, d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **4.6** is the inverse of $\frac{1}{0.87 \times 0.7984 / 1.949} \approx 4.6$. It is the universal coefficient that magically combines all the rectangular stress-block constants.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** Design a singly reinforced RCC beam for the following data:

- Span $L = 6\,\text{m}$
- Working live load = $15\,\text{kN/m}$
- Working dead load (excluding self-weight) = $10\,\text{kN/m}$
- Materials: M20 concrete, Fe415 steel
- Overall depth D = 500 mm, breadth $b$ = 250 mm
- Effective cover = 50 mm, $\phi_{stirrup} = 8\,\text{mm}$, $\phi_{bar} = 20\,\text{mm}$

### Step 1 — Effective depth

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= D - \text{cover} - \phi_{stirrup} - \phi_{bar}/2 \\
&= 500 - 50 - 8 - 10 \\
&= 432 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Self-weight of beam

$$
w_{sw} = 0.25 \times 0.5 \times 25 = 3.125 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### Step 3 — Total working load and bending moment

$$
w = 15 + 10 + 3.125 = 28.125 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
M = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{28.125 \times 6^2}{8} = 126.56 \text{ kN·m}
$$

### Step 4 — Factored design moment

$$
M_u = 1.5 \times 126.56 = 189.84 \text{ kN·m} = 189.84 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Limiting moment of resistance

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 432^2 \times 10^{-6} = 128.85 \text{ kN·m}
$$

### Step 6 — Check the section type

Since $M_u = 189.84 \text{ kN·m} > M_{u,lim} = 128.85 \text{ kN·m}$, the section is **over-reinforced** as a singly reinforced member. **We must either:**
- (a) Increase $b$ or $d$, OR
- (b) Provide a **doubly reinforced** section, OR
- (c) Use a higher grade of concrete.

Let us **revise dimensions**: Take $b = 300\,\text{mm}$, $D = 600\,\text{mm}$. Recalculate:

$$
d = 600 - 50 - 8 - 10 = 532 \text{ mm}, \quad w_{sw} = 0.30 \times 0.60 \times 25 = 4.5 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
w = 15 + 10 + 4.5 = 29.5 \text{ kN/m}, \quad M = \frac{29.5 \times 36}{8} = 132.75 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
M_u = 1.5 \times 132.75 = 199.125 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 532^2 \times 10^{-6} = 234.43 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Now $M_u < M_{u,lim}$ → **Singly reinforced section is sufficient.** ✓

### Step 7 — Calculate $A_{st}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 199.125 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times 532^2}}\right] \times 300 \times 532 \\[2mm]
&= 0.2409\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{915.97 \times 10^6}{1.698 \times 10^9}}\right] \times 159600 \\[2mm]
&= 0.2409 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.5394}\right] \times 159600 \\[2mm]
&= 0.2409 \times 0.3190 \times 159600 \\[2mm]
&= 12268 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Check minimum steel

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.85 \times 300 \times 532}{415} = 326.9 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$A_{st} > A_{st,min}$ → **OK** ✓

### Step 9 — Select bars

Area of one 20 mm $\phi$ bar $= \pi \times 20^2/4 = 314.16\,\text{mm}^2$.

Number of bars $n = 12268 / 314.16 \approx 39.05$. This is **far too many**.

**Revise to higher grade concrete** — try **M30** with Fe415.

Recompute $M_{u,lim}$:

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 30 \times 300 \times 532^2 \times 10^{-6} = 351.65 \text{ kN·m} \;>\; M_u
$$

Now compute $A_{st}$:

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 30}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 199.125 \times 10^6}{30 \times 300 \times 532^2}}\right] \times 300 \times 532
$$

$$
= 0.3614 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.3596}\right] \times 159600 = 0.3614 \times 0.2016 \times 159600 = 11631 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Try **20 mm $\phi$ bars, 5 nos.** on the tension face (2 rows: 3 + 2).

$$
A_{st,provided} = 5 \times 314.16 = 1570.8 \text{ mm}^2 \;\; \text{INSUFFICIENT!}
$$

Try **25 mm $\phi$ bars**: area each = $490.87\,\text{mm}^2$.

Need $n = 11631/490.87 = 23.7$ bars — still too many.

**Final practical solution:** Use **doubly reinforced** OR **pre-tensioned prestressed beam**. This shows how *trial-and-revision* is the real engineering workflow.

> [!NOTE]
> Real board questions usually pre-dimension the section so that $A_{st}$ is realistic. The above example illustrates that **safety requires iteration** — exactly the spirit of NEP 2020 design thinking.

```mermaid
        cForce["C = 0.446 fck . b . Xu\nResultant force in concrete"]
        cBlock["Equivalent rectangular block\ndepth = 0.42 Xu\nintensity = 0.446 fck"]
    end

    subgraph neutral["NEUTRAL AXIS"]
        na["X-axis: distance Xu from top\nplane sections remain plane\nstrains vary linearly"]
    end

    subgraph tension["TENSION ZONE (Bottom)"]

    end

        leverArm["z = d - 0.42 Xu\nTHIS is where the\nmoment is resisted"]
    end

    cBlock -->|"acts at 0.42 Xu from top"| na
    na -->|"strain in steel = 0.0035 (Xu / d minus 1)"| tSteel

```

## 4.2 Section Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Given M, b, d, fck, fy"] --> stepB["Compute Mu = 1.5 M"]
    stepB --> stepC["Compute Mulim\n0.138 fck b d squared for Fe415"]
    stepC --> decision1{"Is Mu greater than Mulim?"}
    decision1 -- "YES" --> branchA["Increase depth OR\nprovide doubly reinforced OR\nraise concrete grade"]
    decision1 -- "NO" --> stepD["Section OK for single\nreinforcement"]
    stepD --> stepE["Compute Ast using\n0.5 fck over fy times bracket"]
    stepE --> stepF["Check Ast >= Ast min"]
    stepF --> decision2{"Ast adequate?"}
    decision2 -- "YES" --> stepG["Select bar size\nand number"]
    decision2 -- "NO" --> branchA
    stepG --> stepH["Check spacing and\ncrack width limits"]
    stepH --> finish["SAFE DESIGN\ncomplete"]
    branchA --> finish
```

