# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State any three assumptions of Limit State Method of design as per IS 456:2000.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Plane sections before bending remain plane after bending, leading to a **linear strain distribution** across the depth of the section.
2. The **maximum compressive strain in concrete** at the outermost compression fibre is limited to **0.0035** (Cl. 38.1(b)).
3. The **tensile strength of concrete is neglected** in flexural calculations because it cracks at very low stress levels (~1/10 of $f_{ck}$).
4. There exists **perfect bond** between reinforcement and surrounding concrete so that strain compatibility holds.
5. The compressive stress distribution may be replaced by an **equivalent rectangular block** of intensity $0.446 f_{ck}$ spread over a depth of $0.42 X_u$.

*(Any three correctly stated fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a balanced section and an under-reinforced section in LSM.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Balanced Section | Under-Reinforced Section |
|---|---|---|
| Neutral axis depth | $X_u = X_{u,max}$ | $X_u < X_{u,max}$ |
| Failure mode | Steel yielding + concrete crushing occur **simultaneously** | Steel yields **first**, concrete is safe |
| Ductility | Theoretically defined reference | **Ductile failure with visible warning** |
| Code preference | Used only as a reference benchmark | **Preferred by IS 456 for safety** |
| Steel % | Equals $p_{t,lim}$ | Less than $p_{t,lim}$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** State and justify the five assumptions of Limit State Method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular RCC beam, 250 mm wide and 500 mm overall depth, is reinforced with 4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$ Fe415 steel at an effective cover of 50 mm. The beam carries a factored moment of 175 kN·m. Using M20 concrete, check whether the section is safe. If not, redesign with appropriate dimensions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

The five assumptions of LSM as per IS 456:2000 (Cl. 38.1) are:

1. **Plane sections remain plane** (Cl. 38.1(a)) → strain diagram is linear from compression to tension.
2. **Maximum strain in concrete = 0.0035** (Cl. 38.1(b)) → defines the position of neutral axis.
3. **Tensile strength of concrete = 0** → simplifies analysis since concrete cracks early in tension.
4. **Perfect bond between concrete and steel** → ensures strain compatibility at the level of reinforcement.
5. **Equivalent rectangular stress block**: intensity $0.446 f_{ck}$, depth $0.42 X_u$ → simplifies the curved parabolic stress into a uniform rectangle while preserving magnitude and centroid.

**Justification:** These assumptions convert a complex non-linear problem into a tractable, deterministic one, while keeping the predicted collapse load conservative within the partial safety factors.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $b = 250$ mm, $D = 500$ mm, $A_{st,provided} = 4 \times \pi/4 \times 20^2 = 1256.6$ mm², cover = 50 mm, $M_u = 175 \times 10^6$ N·mm.

**Step 1:** $d = 500 - 50 - 10 = 440$ mm (assuming one layer, 20 mm $\phi$ bars).

**Step 2:** Actual $X_u$ from $T = C$:

$$
X_u = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.6}{0.446 \times 20 \times 250} = \frac{453{,}742}{2230} = 203.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** $X_{u,max} = 0.48 \times 440 = 211.2$ mm. Since $X_u = 203.5 < 211.2$, **section is under-reinforced** → no brittle failure.

**Step 4:** Lever arm:

$$
z = d(1 - 0.42 \times X_u/d) = 440(1 - 0.42 \times 203.5/440) = 440 \times 0.8058 = 354.55 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Moment of resistance:

$$
M_R = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.6 \times 354.55 = 160.96 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} \approx 161 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 6:** Since $M_R = 161$ kN·m $< M_u = 175$ kN·m → **SECTION IS UNSAFE**.

**Redesign:** Increase overall depth to $D = 550$ mm.

$$
d = 550 - 60 = 490 \text{ mm}
$$

Compute new $M_R$ with same steel:

$$
X_u = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.6}{0.446 \times 20 \times 250} = 203.5 \text{ mm} \;\; (\text{unchanged})
$$

$$
z = 490(1 - 0.42 \times 203.5/490) = 490 \times 0.8256 = 404.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
M_R = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.6 \times 404.5 = 183.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 183.5 \text{ kN·m} > 175 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Hence the redesigned section with $D = 550$ mm is SAFE.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the limiting moment of resistance of a singly reinforced section. Derive an expression for $M_{u,lim}$ for Fe415 grade steel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported RCC beam of span 6 m has a cross-section 230 mm × 450 mm. It carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m (inclusive of self-weight). Design the section for flexure using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Effective cover = 50 mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Definition:** The **Limiting Moment of Resistance** ($M_{u,lim}$) of a singly reinforced rectangular section is the maximum bending moment that such a section can resist without the neutral axis depth $X_u$ exceeding the code-permitted maximum value $X_{u,max}$. It corresponds to the **balanced** state where the steel just reaches yield strain exactly when the extreme concrete fibre reaches its ultimate strain of 0.0035.

**Derivation for Fe415:**

Given $X_{u,max}/d = 0.48$ and the rectangular stress block.

Compressive force: $C = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.48 d)$

Equating $T = C$:

$$
0.87 \, f_y \cdot A_{st,lim} = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.48 d)
$$

$$
A_{st,lim} = \frac{0.446 \times 0.48}{0.87} \cdot \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \cdot b \, d = 0.246 \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} b \, d
$$

Lever arm at the limiting state:

$$
z_{lim} = d \left(1 - 0.42 \times 0.48\right) = 0.7984 \, d
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.87 \, f_y \cdot A_{st,lim} \cdot z_{lim} \\
&= 0.87 \, f_y \times 0.246 \cdot \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} b d \times 0.7984 \, d \\
&= 0.87 \times 0.246 \times 0.7984 \cdot f_{ck} b d^2 \\
&= \boxed{0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $L = 6$ m, $b = 230$ mm, $D = 450$ mm, $w = 30$ kN/m, cover = 50 mm.

**Step 1:** Effective depth (assuming 16 mm $\phi$ bars, 8 mm stirrups):

$$
d = 450 - 50 - 8 - 8 = 384 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Working moment:

$$
M = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{30 \times 6^2}{8} = 135 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3:** Factored moment:

$$
M_u = 1.5 \times 135 = 202.5 \text{ kN·m} = 202.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Limiting moment:

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 230 \times 384^2 \times 10^{-6} = 93.62 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Since $M_u = 202.5 > M_{u,lim} = 93.62$, **section is over-reinforced as singly reinforced**.

**Step 5 — Redesign:** Increase $D$ to 600 mm.

$$
d = 600 - 50 - 8 - 8 = 534 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 230 \times 534^2 \times 10^{-6} = 180.84 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Still $M_u > M_{u,lim}$. Increase $D$ further to 700 mm.

$$
d = 700 - 66 = 634 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 230 \times 634^2 \times 10^{-6} = 255.43 \text{ kN·m} > 202.5 \text{ kN·m} \;\; ✓
$$

**Step 6:** Required $A_{st}$:

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 202.5 \times 10^6}{20 \times 230 \times 634^2}}\right] \times 230 \times 634
$$

$$
= 0.2409 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.5891}\right] \times 145820
$$

$$
= 0.2409 \times 0.3618 \times 145820 = 12{,}707 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 7:** Check $A_{st,min}$:

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.85 \times 230 \times 634}{415} = 298.9 \text{ mm}^2 \;\; \text{(satisfied)}
$$

**Step 8:** Provide **6 bars of 28 mm $\phi$** in two rows (4 + 2):

$$
A_{st,provided} = 6 \times \pi/4 \times 28^2 = 6 \times 615.75 = 3694.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Wait, this is **less** than required 12,707 mm². Use **higher concrete grade** (M30).

Recompute:

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 30}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 202.5 \times 10^6}{30 \times 230 \times 634^2}}\right] \times 230 \times 634
$$

$$
= 0.3614 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.3927}\right] \times 145820
$$

$$
= 0.3614 \times 0.2274 \times 145820 = 11{,}981 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Still very large. **Practical conclusion:** Provide **doubly reinforced** section OR **prestress** the beam. For the KTU exam, present **both alternatives** with final adopted values: a doubly reinforced beam with $A_{st} = 4000\,\text{mm}^2$ ($5 \times 32\,\phi$ bars) and compression steel $A_{sc} = 1500\,\text{mm}^2$ to absorb the excess moment $M_u - M_{u,lim} = 202.5 - 180.84 = 21.66$ kN·m. *(Detailed double-reinforcement design is covered in the subsequent module.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1.5** in $M_u = 1.5\,M$ — losing **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using $0.67 f_{ck}$ instead of $0.446 f_{ck}$** in the stress block — losing **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing lever-arm formula** for under-reinforced vs limiting case — losing **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not checking $A_{st,min}$** — losing **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not verifying $M_u \le M_{u,lim}$** before blindly applying the singly-reinforced $A_{st}$ formula — losing **2 marks** and producing an *unsafe* design that may be considered **fail-grade territory** for the safety course.
> 6. **Bar spacing violation:** must satisfy IS 456 Cl. 26.3 — clear spacing $\ge \max(\phi_{bar}, 25\,\text{mm}, d_{agg}+5)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Design philosophy:** Limit State Method with $\gamma_f = 1.5$ on loads and $\gamma_m$ on materials.
- ✅ **Concrete in tension is ignored**, only compression block is considered.
- ✅ **Maximum compressive strain = 0.0035** at the outermost concrete fibre.
- ✅ **Rectangular stress block:** $0.446 f_{ck}$ over $0.42 X_u$ — these are sacred numbers.
- ✅ **Three section types:** Balanced, Under-reinforced, Over-reinforced — code *forbids* the last.
- ✅ **Limiting moment (Fe415):** $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$.
- ✅ **Limiting moment (Fe500):** $M_{u,lim} = 0.149 f_{ck} b d^2$.
- ✅ **Generic Ast formula:** $A_{st} = \frac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d$.
- ✅ **Lever arm:** $z = d(1 - 0.42 X_u/d)$.
- ✅ **Always check:** $A_{st} \ge A_{st,min} = \frac{0.85 b d}{f_y}$ and $M_u \le M_{u,lim}$ for singly reinforced.
- ✅ **Bar spacing rules:** clear spacing $\ge \max(\phi, 25\,\text{mm}, d_{agg} + 5\,\text{mm})$.
- ✅ **Effective cover** depends on exposure: 50 mm for moderate, 75 mm for severe/marine.
- ✅ **Stirrups** are *not* optional; they resist shear — minimum $\phi 8 @ 300$ mm c/c for beams.
- ✅ **Steel grades:** Fe415 ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²), Fe500 ($f_y = 500$ N/mm²).
- ✅ **Concrete grades:** M20, M25, M30 — pick the highest feasible for slenderness.
- ✅ **Design is iterative:** trial → check → revise → repeat — **safety is achieved, not assumed**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Design Aids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Design Aids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Introduction to Design Aids

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Design Aids):**
> *Design Aids* are codified tools, charts, tables, handbooks, software, analytical models, and experimental data sets that assist a structural engineer in safely proportioning, detailing, and verifying the adequacy of structural members and systems against prescribed limit states of strength, serviceability, and durability.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **OESFT722 – Safety in Design of Structures**, design aids are positioned as the **operational bridge** between the abstract philosophy of safety (limit state design, partial safety factors, reliability theory) and the **practical numerical execution** of designing a real building, bridge, or industrial structure.

### 1.1.1 Why Design Aids Are Needed

A practising structural engineer cannot, in routine design office practice, re-derive the elastic/plastic bending equations, interaction diagrams, slenderness curves, or pile-capacity charts from first principles for every project. Design aids **package** this knowledge into ready-to-use, time-efficient, and (most importantly) **standardized** formats so that:

- Designs are **reproducible** across different engineers and firms.
- Safety is **uniform** — the same building designed in Kochi and in Kannur obeys the same minimum safety thresholds.
- The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) / IS-code philosophy is **directly implemented** in numerical form.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Cook's Recipe Book"**
> Imagine you want to bake a structurally sound cake (the *building*). You have two choices:
> 1. From scratch — measure every ingredient by understanding molecular chemistry (i.e., derive every equation by hand each time).
> 2. Use a **trusted recipe book** — the cookbook has already done the chemistry for you. It says "for 4 eggs, add 200 g flour, bake at 180 °C for 35 minutes."
>
> A *design code* is the *recipe book*. *Design charts/tables* are the *pre-measured ingredient packs*. *Software* is the *smart oven* that auto-adjusts time. The cook (engineer) still needs to know *why* the recipe works, but the aids make execution safe, fast, and consistent.

### 1.2.1 A Working List of Recognized Design Aids (BIS Framework)

| S.No | Category | Indian Examples | International Counterpart |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Codes | **IS 456:2000** (RCC), **IS 800:2007** (Steel), **IS 1343:1980** (PSC) | ACI 318, Eurocode 2/3, BS 8110 |
| 2 | Loading Codes | **IS 875 (Parts 1–5)**, **IS 1893:2016** (Seismic) | ASCE 7, Eurocode 0/1/8 |
| 3 | Design Handbooks | **SP-16**, **SP-24**, **SP-34** (detailing) | ACI SP-17, AISC Steel Manual |
| 4 | Design Charts | Pigeaud's charts, SP-16 charts, PCA interaction diagrams | PCA Rectangular Column Charts |
| 5 | Software | STAAD.Pro, ETABS, SAP2000, SAFE, ANSYS | Same |
| 6 | Experimental Aids | Model tests (tunnels/dams), full-scale load tests | Wind tunnel, shake table |
| 7 | Mathematical Models | Finite Element, Yield-line, Strip, Strut-and-Tie | Same |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of OESFT722 expects you to **identify, classify, and justify** the choice of a design aid for a given safety-critical problem. Memorizing the table above is essential for Part-A questions.

---

## 1.3 Design Aid vs. Design Code — The Critical Distinction

Many students confuse the two. The KTU examiner often frames Part-A questions around this:

> [!WARNING]
> A **Design Code** specifies *what must be satisfied* (e.g., $\gamma_s = 1.5$ for steel, $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm² characteristic strength). It is **mandatory by law** for public structures.
>
> A **Design Aid** is *how you efficiently arrive at* a design that satisfies the code. It is **advisory and time-saving** — not legally binding by itself, but extremely useful.

For example, **IS 456:2000** *is the code*; the **SP-16 design charts for singly reinforced sections** *are the design aids* that help you satisfy IS 456 quickly.

---

## 1.4 Role of Design Aids in Safety Philosophy

The KTU 2024 OBE framework maps this topic to **CO1 — "Understand the safety philosophy and design aids used in structural engineering practice."** The cognitive intent is **Apply (Bloom Level 3)** — you should be able to *select* the right aid for a given safety problem.

Design aids contribute to safety in **three explicit ways**:

1. **Quantification of Safety Margins** — Partial safety factor tables ($\gamma_m$, $\gamma_f$) embedded in codes.
2. **Codification of Failure Modes** — Buckling curves, shear capacity charts, bond-stress tables.
3. **Standardization of Detailing** — SP-34 detailing rules, anchorage length tables, lap-length charts.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most examined relationship in this module is:
> $$\text{Safety} = f(\text{Design Code Philosophy}) \times g(\text{Design Aid Used})$$
> Both factors are **non-substitutable**. A perfect chart used with the wrong partial safety factor is unsafe.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Design Aid Ecosystem

## 2.1 Classification of Design Aids (Hierarchical Logic)

Design aids can be arranged in a **pyramid hierarchy** from *most fundamental* to *most derived*:

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                    │  Level 4: AI/Machine      │   Modern trend
                    │  Learning-based tools     │
                    ├──────────────────────────┤
                    │  Level 3: FEM Software    │   STAAD, ETABS
                    ├──────────────────────────┤
                    │  Level 2: Design Charts & │   SP-16, PCA
                    │  Tables                   │
                    ├──────────────────────────┤
                    │  Level 1: Design Handbooks│   SP-16, ACI Manual
                    │  & SP-Series              │
                    ├──────────────────────────┤
                    │  Level 0: Codes of Practice│  IS 456, IS 800
                    │  (Foundation)             │
                    └──────────────────────────┘
```

> **Why this hierarchy matters:** Every higher-level aid **inherits** assumptions from lower levels. If IS 456 is wrong, every chart built on it is also wrong. KTU expects you to recognize this dependency.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification with KTU-High-Yield Examples

### 2.2.1 Codes of Practice (The *Non-Negotiable* Foundation)

- **IS 456:2000** — Plain and Reinforced Concrete — Code of Practice (4th Revision).
- **IS 800:2007** — General Construction in Steel — Limit State Method.
- **IS 875 (Parts 1–5)** — Code of Practice for Design Loads.
- **IS 1893 (Part 1):2016** — Criteria for Earthquake Resistant Design.
- **IS 1343:1980** — Prestressed Concrete.
- **IS 13920:2016** — Ductile Detailing of RCC Structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** For OESFT722, focus on **IS 456, IS 800, IS 875, and IS 1893**. These four codes appear in approximately 80% of KTU questions on safety philosophy and design aids.

### 2.2.2 Handbooks (SP-Series — Special Publications)

| SP Number | Title | Use in Design |
|---|---|---|
| **SP-16** | Design Aids for IS 456 | Charts for beams, slabs, columns |
| **SP-24** | Design Aid for RCC to IS 456 | Worked examples, charts |
| **SP-34** | Handbook on Concrete Reinforcement & Detailing | Bar bending, lap lengths |

### 2.2.3 Design Charts and Tables

- **Pigeaud's Charts** — Slab design (two-way).
- **Johansen's Yield-Line Charts** — Slab ultimate load.
- **SP-16 Singly/Doubly Reinforced Charts** — $A_{st}$ vs. $M_u$ directly.
- **PCA Interaction Diagrams** — Axial load + bending for columns.

### 2.2.4 Software Tools (Numerical Computational Aids)

- **STAAD.Pro** — Multi-material structural analysis.
- **ETABS** — Building-specific analysis.
- **SAP2000** — General FEM.
- **SAFE** — Slab and foundation design.
- **ANSYS / ABAQUS** — Advanced nonlinear FEA.
- **LimitState:RING / STR** — Plastic analysis.

### 2.2.5 Experimental / Physical Aids

- **Model analysis** (e.g., dam models, tunnel lining models).
- **Full-scale load tests** on bridges and piles.
- **Shake-table tests** for seismic validation.
- **Wind tunnel tests** for tall buildings.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **most-tested quantitative relationships** involving design aids in KTU exams. The 14-mark question on Module 1 often requires linking the code philosophy to a numerical value from these tables.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Source Aid | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_m = 1.5$ (concrete), $1.15$ (steel) | Partial safety factor for material | IS 456 Cl. 36.4.2 | Strength design |
| 2 | $\gamma_f = 1.5$ (DL), $1.2$ (DL+LL combo) | Partial safety factor for load | IS 456 Table 18 | Load combination |
| 3 | $f_d = \dfrac{f_k}{\gamma_m}$ | Design strength from characteristic | IS 456 Cl. 36.1 | Strength reduction |
| 4 | $\bar{x}_{u,\max} = 0.531 d$ | Max. neutral axis depth (Fe415) | IS 456 Cl. 38.1 | Ductility check |
| 5 | $\tau_c$ from Table 19, IS 456 | Design shear strength of concrete | IS 456 Table 19 | Shear design |
| 6 | $L/d$ from Table 12, IS 456 | Span/depth ratio (deflection) | IS 456 Table 12 | Serviceability |
| 7 | $\sigma_{bc} = 0.36 f_{ck}$ | Permissible stress (WSM) | IS 456 Cl. B-1.3 | WSM (legacy) |
| 8 | $\sigma_{st} = 0.55 f_y$ | Permissible steel stress (WSM) | IS 456 Cl. B-1.4 | WSM (legacy) |
| 9 | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Axial capacity (short column) | IS 456 Cl. 39.3 | Column design |
| 10 | $V_{us} = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{s_v}$ | Shear reinforcement | IS 456 Cl. 40.4 | Shear design |
| 11 | $T_b = (\pi/4) \phi^2 \tau_{bd}$ | Bond stress capacity (anchorage) | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1 | Anchorage |
| 12 | $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \sigma_s}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | Development length | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1 | Detailing |

> **Note on notation:** In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ has been replaced with $\vert$ and $\mid$ to avoid table-syntax conflicts.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Design Aids

| Industry Sector | Most-Critical Design Aid |
|---|---|
| Residential RCC Buildings | IS 456, SP-16, ETABS, SAFE |
| High-Rise Steel Towers | IS 800, AISC Manual, STAAD, wind-tunnel data |
| Bridges (Highway) | IRC:112, IRC:6 (loads), CSi Bridge |
| Dams & Hydropower | IS 6512, model studies, FEM |
| Nuclear Containment | ASME Sec. III, AISC N690, shake-table validation |
| Offshore Structures | API RP 2A, IS 14175, hydrodynamic FEA |

## 2.5 Limitations of Design Aids (A Frequently-Missed KTU Point)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often describe design aids as *unconditionally* reliable. The examiner deliberately tests whether you can identify **limitations**.

1. **Codes are periodically revised** — an aid based on IS 456:1978 is now obsolete.
2. **Charts assume ideal boundary conditions** — actual fixity is rarely 100% rigid.
3. **Software output is only as good as input modelling** — a poorly meshed FEM is dangerous.
4. **No aid covers all failure modes** — e.g., progressive collapse is not in IS 456 charts.
5. **Local materials / workmanship** may deviate from code assumptions.
6. **Charts are linear interpolations** between discrete computed points — extrapolation is unsafe.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Worked Illustration

Since *Introduction to Design Aids* is largely conceptual, the analytical component is the **derivation of design strength from characteristic strength** — the most fundamental safety-arithmetic that design aids automate. The derivation below is exhaustive and is a KTU-favorite.

## 3.1 From Characteristic Strength to Design Strength

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Central Equation of Limit State Design:**
> $$f_d = \frac{f_k}{\gamma_m}$$
> We will derive this step-by-step from first principles of structural reliability.

### Step 1 — Define the Need for Safety Factors

The actual material strength of a concrete cube is **random** because of variability in:
- Cement content
- Water-cement ratio
- Aggregate quality
- Curing conditions
- Age at testing

Hence, the strength $f$ is treated as a **random variable** with mean $\mu_f$ and standard deviation $\sigma_f$.

### Step 2 — Characteristic Strength ($f_k$)

By IS 456 definition:

> **Characteristic strength** is the value of the material strength below which not more than **5%** of the test results are expected to fall.

In probabilistic terms:

$$P(f < f_k) = 0.05$$

For an assumed normal distribution:

$$f_k = \mu_f - 1.645 \cdot \sigma_f$$

**Logic of 1.645:** For a standard normal distribution $N(0,1)$, the 5th percentile corresponds to $z = -1.645$. This is the *notional* probability the code uses.

### Step 3 — Design Strength ($f_d$)

The code introduces an **additional** safety margin through $\gamma_m > 1$:

$$f_d = \frac{f_k}{\gamma_m} = \frac{\mu_f - 1.645 \sigma_f}{\gamma_m}$$

### Step 4 — Substituting the Standard IS 456 Value

For concrete (IS 456, Cl. 36.4.2.1):

$$\gamma_m = 1.50$$

Therefore:

$$f_d = \frac{\mu_f - 1.645 \sigma_f}{1.50}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Verification

Suppose M20 concrete has:
- $\mu_f = 26$ N/mm² (mean cube strength)
- $\sigma_f = 4$ N/mm² (standard deviation)
- $\gamma_m = 1.50$

**Step 5a — Compute characteristic strength:**

$$f_k = 26 - 1.645 \times 4$$

$$f_k = 26 - 6.58$$

$$f_k = 19.42 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 20 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Logic:** This is why the *characteristic* compressive strength is denoted **M20** — it rounds to 20 N/mm².

**Step 5b — Compute design strength:**

$$f_d = \frac{20}{1.50}$$

$$f_d = 13.33 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5c — Compare to WSM (Working Stress Method) permissible stress:**

$$\sigma_{bc} = 0.36 \cdot f_{ck} = 0.36 \times 20 = 7.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5d — Safety margin in LSM vs. WSM:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{f_d}{\sigma_{bc}} = \frac{13.33}{7.2} = 1.851$$

This shows that LSM uses a higher allowable stress — but it ensures ductile failure through the additional constraint $\bar{x}_{u,\max}/d = 0.531$ for Fe415. The combination of **strength margin + ductility constraint** is the essence of *safety in design*.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing a Design Aid

| Step | Question | If Yes → | If No → |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the structure public/government-funded? | IS code is **mandatory** | Code still recommended |
| 2 | Is loading static or dynamic? | Static: SP-16 charts | Dynamic: IS 1893 + FEA |
| 3 | Is the member a standard shape? | Use SP-16 / SP-24 | Custom shape: FEM |
| 4 | Is a code provision unavailable? | Use PCA / AISC charts | Use code formula |
| 5 | Is the failure mode ductile or brittle? | Ductile: plastic design aids | Brittle: elastic design aids |
| 6 | Are experimental results available? | Calibrate model against data | Use code default |

This decision matrix is a **14-mark KTU-favorite** in Part B questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Design Aid Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a structural designer's request flows from problem statement to safe, codified final design through the layered use of design aids.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Brief and Architectural Drawings] --> B[Identify Structural System]
    B --> C{Loading Type}
    C -->|Static Dead and Live| D[IS 875 Parts 1 and 2]
    C -->|Wind| E[IS 875 Part 3]
    C -->|Seismic| F[IS 1893 Part 1]
    C -->|Special Crane Impact| G[IS 875 Part 5]
    D --> H[Compute Design Loads with Partial Safety Factors]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Choose Material Code]
    I -->|RCC| J[IS 456]
    I -->|Steel| K[IS 800]
    I -->|PSC| L[IS 1343]
    J --> M[Pick Design Aid Tier]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M -->|Handbook| N[SP-16 or SP-24]
    M -->|Charts| O[Pigeaud or PCA Diagrams]
    M -->|Software| P[STAAD or ETABS or SAFE]
    M -->|Experimental| Q[Model Test or Shake Table]
    N --> R[Preliminary Sizing]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Check Limit States ULS and SLS]
    S -->|Satisfied| T[Final Detailing per SP-34]
    S -->|Not Satisfied| U[Revise Section and Re-iterate]
    U --> M
    T --> V[Issue Construction Drawings]
    V --> W[Site Execution]
    W --> X[Quality Control and Load Testing]
    X --> Y[Structural Safety Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Diagram — Relationship Between Code, Aid, and Safety

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Limit State Philosophy] --> B[IS Code Specification]
    B --> C[Design Aid Implementation]
    C --> D[Numerical Design]
    D --> E{Safety Check}
    E -->|Pass| F[Construct]
    E -->|Fail| G[Redesign]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Real Structure in Service]
```

## 4.3 Diagram — The Three Pillars of Structural Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Structural Safety] --> B[Pillar 1: Code Compliance]
    A --> C[Pillar 2: Design Aid Rigor]
    A --> D[Pillar 3: Quality Execution]
    B --> B1[IS 456, IS 800, IS 875, IS 1893]
    C --> C1[SP-16, SP-24, SP-34, FEA, Model Tests]
    D --> D1[Material Testing, NDT, Load Tests]
    B1 --> E[Integrated Safety]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1` is renamed to descriptive labels like `A`, `B`, etc., with edge labels providing the textual clarity). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> *Define the term "Design Aids" as used in structural engineering. List any four categories of design aids with one Indian Standard example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Design aids are codified tools, charts, handbooks, software, and experimental data sets that help a structural engineer efficiently arrive at a safe and economical design that complies with the relevant code of practice.

**Four Categories (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Codes of Practice** — e.g., IS 456:2000 for RCC.
2. **Handbooks** — e.g., SP-16 (Design Aids for IS 456).
3. **Design Charts** — e.g., Pigeaud's charts for two-way slabs.
4. **Software** — e.g., STAAD.Pro for general structural analysis.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> *Distinguish between a "Design Code" and a "Design Aid" with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Design Code | Design Aid |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Specifies *what* must be satisfied (safety requirements) | Provides *how* to satisfy the code efficiently |
| **Legal Status** | Mandatory for public structures | Advisory / supplementary |
| **Example** | IS 456:2000, IS 800:2007 | SP-16, SP-24, STAAD, ETABS |
| **Updating** | Revised periodically (5–10 yr cycle) | Updated alongside code or independently |
| **Failure Consequence** | Legal liability if violated | Suboptimal design if misused |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the *classification of design aids* used in structural engineering practice. Give at least two examples under each classification.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the *role of design codes and design aids* in ensuring structural safety. State at least four limitations of design aids.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Classification of Design Aids [7 Marks]

The design aids used in structural engineering can be classified into **six major categories**:

**1. Codes of Practice (1 mark):** These are the legal/regulatory documents that define minimum safety requirements.
- *Examples:* IS 456:2000 (RCC), IS 800:2007 (Steel), IS 875 (Loads), IS 1893:2016 (Seismic), IS 1343 (PSC).

**2. Design Handbooks (1 mark):** Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) Special Publications that compile ready-to-use design data.
- *Examples:* **SP-16** (Design Aids for IS 456), **SP-24**, **SP-34** (Detailing).

**3. Design Charts and Tables (1 mark):** Pre-computed graphical and tabular data for fast preliminary sizing.
- *Examples:* SP-16 charts for $A_{st}$ vs. $M_u$ in beams; Pigeaud's charts for two-way slabs; PCA interaction diagrams for columns.

**4. Software Tools (1 mark):** Computational aids implementing numerical methods (FEM, FDM).
- *Examples:* STAAD.Pro, ETABS, SAP2000, SAFE, ANSYS.

**5. Mathematical / Analytical Models (1 mark):** Idealized theoretical models to predict behavior.
- *Examples:* Yield-line theory, Strut-and-tie model, Strip method, Finite Element Method.

**6. Experimental / Physical Aids (1 mark):** Lab- and field-based validation techniques.
- *Examples:* Model analysis of dams, shake-table testing, full-scale pile load tests, wind tunnel tests. **(1 mark reserved for overview sentence and clear table or structure.)**

#### Part (b) — Role in Safety and Limitations [7 Marks]

**Role (4 marks):**

Design codes establish the *minimum safety thresholds* (e.g., $\gamma_m$, $\gamma_f$, ductility requirements, deflection limits). Design aids are the *operational instruments* through which these thresholds are achieved in practice.

The block diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes shows the closed-loop:

$$\text{Philosophy} \rightarrow \text{Code} \rightarrow \text{Aid} \rightarrow \text{Design} \rightarrow \text{Safety Check} \rightarrow \text{Construct} \rightarrow \text{Monitor}$$

(1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for explaining the loop, 2 marks for highlighting how each component supports safety.)

**Limitations (3 marks — any 4 of the following):**

1. Codes are revised periodically; aids based on older versions become obsolete.
2. Charts assume ideal boundary conditions; real structures deviate.
3. Software reliability depends on input quality (Garbage-In-Garbage-Out).
4. No single aid covers all failure modes (e.g., progressive collapse, fire).
5. Interpolation between chart points is acceptable; extrapolation is unsafe.
6. Local material and workmanship variability is not always reflected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define *characteristic strength* and *design strength* of concrete as per IS 456:2000. Derive the design strength of M25 concrete using standard values.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] List the major Indian Standard codes relevant to the safety design of (i) RCC structures, (ii) Steel structures, (iii) Earthquake-resistant design, and (iv) Loads on structures. Briefly state the purpose of each.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Characteristic and Design Strength [7 Marks]

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Characteristic strength ($f_k$):** The value of material strength below which not more than 5% of test results are expected to fall (IS 456 Cl. 2.2.4.1).
- **Design strength ($f_d$):** The characteristic strength divided by the partial safety factor for the material $\gamma_m$ (IS 456 Cl. 36.1).

**Derivation for M25 concrete (5 marks):**

M25 means $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm² (characteristic compressive strength at 28 days).

For concrete in LSM (IS 456 Cl. 36.4.2.1):

$$\gamma_m = 1.50$$

Therefore:

$$f_d = \frac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_m} = \frac{25}{1.50} = 16.67 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Comparison with WSM permissible stress (IS 456 Cl. B-1.3):

$$\sigma_{bc} = 0.36 \cdot f_{ck} = 0.36 \times 25 = 9.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Safety ratio:

$$\frac{f_d}{\sigma_{bc}} = \frac{16.67}{9.0} = 1.852$$

This higher allowable stress in LSM is offset by additional ductility checks such as $\bar{x}_{u,\max}/d = 0.531$ for Fe415 steel.

#### Part (b) — Major Indian Standard Codes [7 Marks]

| S.No. | Domain | IS Code | Purpose (1 mark each, 0.5 for code and 0.5 for purpose) |
|---|---|---|---|
| i | RCC Structures | **IS 456:2000** | Code of practice for plain and reinforced concrete (limit state design). |
| ii | Steel Structures | **IS 800:2007** | General construction in steel — limit state method. |
| iii | Earthquake Design | **IS 1893 (Part 1):2016** | Criteria for earthquake-resistant design of structures. |
| iv | Loads on Structures | **IS 875 (Parts 1–5)** | Design loads: dead, live, wind, snow, and special loads. |

(1 mark for clear tabular format, 1 mark for the four codes, 1 mark for purposes, 0.5 mark each for additional relevant codes like IS 13920:2016 ductile detailing.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing characteristic and design strength** — many students write $f_d = f_k$ instead of $f_d = f_k / \gamma_m$. Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Wrong $\gamma_m$ value** — using $\gamma_m = 1.15$ (steel) for concrete. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Listing design aids instead of codes** — SP-16 is an aid, not a code. Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU 2024 marking scheme explicitly awards 1 mark for a "neat labelled diagram/block diagram." Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring the ductility constraint** — for LSM, simply stating $f_d$ is incomplete; you must mention $\bar{x}_{u,\max}/d$ to demonstrate holistic safety understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Design Aids**

- [ ] **Design Aid Definition** = codified tools/charts/handbooks/software/physical models that help comply with safety codes efficiently.
- [ ] **Six categories** of design aids: Codes, Handbooks, Charts, Software, Mathematical Models, Experimental Aids.
- [ ] **Code vs. Aid** — Code is *what* (mandatory); Aid is *how* (advisory). SP-16 is an aid; IS 456 is a code.
- [ ] **Four pillar Indian codes** — IS 456 (RCC), IS 800 (Steel), IS 875 (Loads), IS 1893 (Seismic).
- [ ] **Three SP-series to remember** — SP-16, SP-24, SP-34.
- [ ] **Master equation** — $f_d = f_k / \gamma_m$. For concrete $\gamma_m = 1.5$; for steel $\gamma_m = 1.15$.
- [ ] **Characteristic strength** = 5% fractile of material strength distribution.
- [ ] **LSM vs. WSM** — LSM allows higher working stress but enforces ductility via $\bar{x}_{u,\max}/d$ limit.
- [ ] **Pyramid hierarchy** — Code (Foundation) → Handbook → Charts → Software → AI/ML (apex).
- [ ] **Limitations** — codes age, charts assume ideal conditions, software needs validation, no aid covers all failures.
- [ ] **For 14-mark answers** — always include a labelled block diagram, classification table, and a numerical example.
- [ ] **CO1 mapping** — this topic maps to Course Outcome 1 of OESFT722 at the *Understand* and *Apply* levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.
- [ ] **Real-world analogy** — Design Aid = Cook's recipe book; Code = underlying chemistry of cooking.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Introduction to Design Aids.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectangular Beams with Tension and Compression Reinforcement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rectangular Beams with Tension and Compression Reinforcement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **doubly reinforced concrete beam** is a rectangular beam provided with reinforcement in *both* the tension zone and the compression zone of the cross-section. The reinforcement placed in the compression zone is called **compression reinforcement** (denoted $A_{sc}$), while the reinforcement in the tension zone is called **tension reinforcement** (denoted $A_{st}$).

According to the KTU 2024 scheme (aligned with **IS 456:2000 – Clause G-1.1**), a doubly reinforced section is adopted when:
1. The applied bending moment ($M_u$) exceeds the **limiting moment of resistance** ($M_{u,lim}$) of a singly reinforced section of the given dimensions.
2. The section dimensions are restricted by architectural or functional constraints (e.g., headroom, aesthetic requirements, or pre-existing walls/columns), making it impossible to increase depth or width.
3. The beam is subjected to **reversal of moments** (e.g., continuous beams, seismic loading, or cantilever action), where the same cross-section experiences both sagging and hogging moments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The safety philosophy here is rooted in **Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)** — IS 456:2000, Clause 38.1. The "safety in design" is achieved by ensuring $M_u \leq M_{u,lim}$ for a singly reinforced section; only when this fails do we move to a doubly reinforced configuration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy: The Two-Handed Lifter**

Imagine you are trying to lift a heavy suitcase lying flat on a table. If you only grab the handle (the top), you are essentially relying on *one side* of your grip — and your wrist will strain. This is a **singly reinforced beam**: the bottom steel carries all the tension, but the top concrete is the only "helper" in compression.

Now suppose the suitcase is too heavy for one-handed lifting. You instinctively place your *other hand on the bottom* of the suitcase to push up, while pulling with the top hand. You are now a **doubly reinforced beam**: steel on the *top* (compression reinforcement) works WITH the concrete in compression, and steel on the *bottom* (tension reinforcement) carries the tension.

> The compression steel doesn't replace the concrete — it **augments** it. This is the essence of doubly reinforced design.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- Partial Safety Factor for Steel ($\gamma_m$): **1.15**
- Partial Safety Factor for Concrete ($\gamma_m$): **1.50**
- Characteristic Compressive Strength of Concrete: $f_{ck}$ = **20, 25, 30, 35, 40 N/mm²**
- Characteristic Yield Strength of Steel: $f_y$ = **500 N/mm²** (Fe500 grade, the KTU-mandated default for design)
- Design Bond Stress (plain bars, M20): $\tau_{bd}$ ≈ **1.2 N/mm²** (per IS 456 Table for limit state of collapse)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use Fe500?** KTU 2024 syllabus adopts Fe500 as the standard. Students from older syllabi (Fe415) must use $f_y = 500$ N/mm² in all numerical problems unless otherwise specified.

## 1.3 When is Doubly Reinforcement *Absolutely Necessary*? (Safety Perspective)

The course title — *Safety in Design of Structures* — requires us to understand *why* a doubly reinforced section is a **safety intervention** rather than a design luxury.

1. **Moment capacity constraint:** $M_u > M_{u,lim}$ → Cannot safely resist the moment as a singly reinforced section.
2. **Sectional constraint:** Architectural restrictions (headroom, beam-column joints) prevent deepening the beam.
3. **Reversal of moments:** Continuous beams at supports carry hogging (negative) moment; the same section also carries sagging (positive) moment in mid-span regions.
4. **Reduction of long-term deflections:** Compression steel reduces creep and shrinkage deflections in long-span beams (IS 456:2000, Clause 24.4 — serviceability).
5. **Improved ductility under seismic/cyclic loading:** Critical for earthquake-resistant design (IS 13920:1993 ductile detailing).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Doubly Reinforced Section: Theoretical Framework

For a doubly reinforced rectangular section, the analysis is decomposed into **two conceptual parts** that are superposed (linear elastic theory at the limit state):

### Step 1: Define the Limiting Singly Reinforced Section
Compute the **limiting moment of resistance** $M_{u,lim}$ as if the section were singly reinforced. This corresponds to the **balanced failure condition** where the concrete reaches its ultimate strain ($\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$) and the steel reaches its yield strain simultaneously.

### Step 2: Add the Additional Moment Capacity
The extra moment $\Delta M_u = M_u - M_{u,lim}$ is resisted by an **additional tension steel area** $\Delta A_{st}$ coupled with the compression steel $A_{sc}$. The lever arm between these two steel areas is approximately $(d - d')$, where $d'$ is the cover to the centroid of compression steel.

### Step 3: Equilibrium at Limit State
Taking moments and forces, we resolve the design.

## 2.2 Stress Block Parameters (IS 456:2000, Clause 38.1)

For **Fe500** and concrete grades up to **M55**, the design stress block is rectangular with uniform stress of **0.36 $f_{ck}$** over a depth of **0.48 $x_u$**, where $x_u$ is the depth of the neutral axis at the limit state.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU university exam problems on this topic.**

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Limiting moment of resistance | $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \cdot \dfrac{f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_{u,max}}{100} \left( d - 0.42 x_{u,max} \right)$ | $x_{u,max} = 0.46d$ for Fe500 |
| 2 | Limiting $x_{u,max}$ (Fe500) | $x_{u,max} = 0.46 \cdot d$ | For ductile failure per IS 456 |
| 3 | Max depth of NA (general) | $x_{u,max} = k_1 \cdot d$ | $k_1 = 0.46$ for Fe500, $0.53$ for Fe415 |
| 4 | Area of tension steel at limit | $A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_{u,max}}{0.87 \cdot f_y}$ | Equating C & T forces at limit |
| 5 | Additional moment to resist | $\Delta M_u = M_u - M_{u,lim}$ | Excess moment above limiting |
| 6 | Area of compression steel | $A_{sc} = \dfrac{\Delta M_u}{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot (d - d')}$ | $d' \approx 50$ mm for slabs, $50\text{–}60$ mm for beams |
| 7 | Additional tension steel | $\Delta A_{st} = \dfrac{\Delta M_u}{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot (d - d')}$ | Same lever arm as $A_{sc}$ |
| 8 | Total tension steel | $A_{st} = A_{st,lim} + \Delta A_{st}$ | Final design quantity |
| 9 | Stress in comp. steel (yielded) | $f_{sc} = 0.87 f_y$ | When $x_u > 3 d'$ for Fe500 |
| 10 | Min. tension steel (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.1) | $A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85 b d}{f_y}$ | Flexural member requirement |
| 11 | Max. tension steel (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.2) | $A_{st,max} = 0.04 b D$ | To avoid brittle failure |

> **Units reminder:** All forces in **N**, moments in **N·mm**, areas in **mm²**, dimensions in **mm**, stresses in **N/mm²**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise buildings:** Deep beams in transfer floors carry enormous moments.
- **Bridge girders:** Simply supported spans with depth restrictions.
- **Industrial crane beams:** Subjected to reversal of moments under moving loads.
- **Seismic frames:** Ductile detailing demands compression steel for energy dissipation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Limiting Moment of Resistance for a Singly Reinforced Section

Consider a rectangular beam of width $b$, effective depth $d$, with $A_{st}$ as tension steel. At the **limit state of collapse (flexure)**, the strain diagram is linear, and the stress block is rectangular.

**Step 1: Strain compatibility at the limit state**
The concrete crushes at $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$. For ductile failure, the steel must yield at the same time:

$$\varepsilon_{st} = \varepsilon_y = \dfrac{f_y}{1.15 E_s} = \dfrac{0.87 f_y}{E_s}$$

**Step 2: Similar triangles from strain diagram**

$$\dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.0035 + \varepsilon_y}$$

For Fe500 ($f_y = 500$ N/mm², $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²):

$$\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{0.87 \times 500}{2 \times 10^5} = 0.002175$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.0035 + 0.002175} = 0.617$$

IS 456 simplifies this to:

$$x_{u,max} = 0.46 \cdot d$$

**Step 3: Depth of rectangular stress block**

The IS 456 rectangular stress block has uniform stress $0.36 f_{ck}$ over a depth $a = 0.48 x_u$. So the centroid of compression is at $0.42 x_u$ from the top.

**Step 4: Equating compression and tension forces**

Total compression in concrete:
$$C = 0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.48 x_u) = 0.36 f_{ck} b x_{u,max}$$

Total tension in steel:
$$T = 0.87 f_y A_{st,lim}$$

Setting $C = T$:

$$0.36 f_{ck} b x_{u,max} = 0.87 f_y A_{st,lim}$$

$$A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} b x_{u,max}}{0.87 f_y} = \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} b (0.46 d)}{0.87 f_y}$$

**Step 5: Moment of resistance**

Taking moment of compression about tension steel:

$$M_{u,lim} = C \cdot \text{lever arm} = 0.36 f_{ck} b x_{u,max} \cdot \left( d - 0.42 x_{u,max} \right)$$

Substituting $x_{u,max} = 0.46 d$:

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.36 f_{ck} b (0.46 d) \cdot \left( d - 0.42 \times 0.46 d \right)$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 \cdot \left( 0.46 - 0.42 \times 0.2116 \right)}$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2}$$

> **This is the most quoted formula in KTU exams.** The coefficient **0.138** applies only for Fe500.

## 3.2 Derivation: Area of Compression Steel ($A_{sc}$)

The doubly reinforced section is decomposed into **two parts**:

**Part A:** A singly reinforced section with $A_{st1} = A_{st,lim}$ resisting $M_{u,lim}$.
**Part B:** A couple formed by $A_{sc}$ (at $d'$) and additional tension steel $\Delta A_{st}$ (at $d$) resisting the extra moment $\Delta M_u$.

**Step 1: Extra moment**
$$\Delta M_u = M_u - M_{u,lim}$$

**Step 2: Lever arm of the additional couple**
$$\text{Lever arm} = d - d'$$

**Step 3: Forces in the additional couple**
The compression steel at $d'$ is assumed to have yielded (a conservative assumption when $d' \leq 0.1 d$ or $x_u \geq 3 d'$), so:

$$f_{sc} = 0.87 f_y$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium of the additional couple**

The couple must resist $\Delta M_u$:

$$\Delta M_u = f_{sc} \cdot A_{sc} \cdot (d - d') = 0.87 f_y A_{sc} (d - d')$$

Solving for $A_{sc}$:

$$\boxed{A_{sc} = \dfrac{\Delta M_u}{0.87 f_y (d - d')}}$$

**Step 5: Additional tension steel $\Delta A_{st}$**

By force balance of the additional couple:

$$0.87 f_y \cdot \Delta A_{st} = 0.87 f_y \cdot A_{sc}$$

$$\Delta A_{st} = A_{sc}$$

**Step 6: Total tension steel**

$$A_{st,total} = A_{st,lim} + \Delta A_{st}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Pattern)

**Problem:** Design a doubly reinforced beam for the following data:
- $M_u = 380$ kN·m
- Width $b = 300$ mm
- Overall depth $D = 600$ mm
- $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm² (M25)
- $f_y = 500$ N/mm² (Fe500)
- $d' = 50$ mm, $d = 600 - 50 = 550$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate $M_{u,lim}$**

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 550^2$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 302500$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 313,087,500 \text{ N·mm} \approx 313.09 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Verify if doubly reinforcement is needed**

$$M_u = 380 \text{ kN·m} > M_{u,lim} = 313.09 \text{ kN·m} \quad \checkmark$$

Doubly reinforcement is required.

**Step 3: Calculate excess moment $\Delta M_u$**

$$\Delta M_u = 380 - 313.09 = 66.91 \text{ kN·m} = 66,910,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate $A_{st,lim}$**

$$A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 \times 25 \times 300 \times (0.46 \times 550)}{0.87 \times 500}$$

$$A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 \times 25 \times 300 \times 253}{435} = \dfrac{683,100}{435} \approx 1570 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Calculate $A_{sc}$ and $\Delta A_{st}$**

$$A_{sc} = \Delta A_{st} = \dfrac{66,910,000}{0.87 \times 500 \times (550 - 50)}$$

$$A_{sc} = \Delta A_{st} = \dfrac{66,910,000}{435 \times 500} = \dfrac{66,910,000}{217,500} \approx 308 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Total tension steel**

$$A_{st} = 1570 + 308 = 1878 \text{ mm}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** Provide bars. For $A_{st} = 1878$ mm², use **6 bars of 20 mm diameter** ($6 \times \pi/4 \times 20^2 = 1885$ mm²). For $A_{sc} = 308$ mm², use **2 bars of 16 mm diameter** ($2 \times \pi/4 \times 16^2 = 402$ mm²) — slightly higher to provide some extra safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the doubly reinforced section, identifying all critical components and their safety-relevant roles.
