## 4.2 Free Body Diagram — Decomposition into Two Sub-Sections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CompositeSection["COMPOSITE DOUBLY REINFORCED SECTION"]
        direction TB
        P1["Total Mu = 380 kN-m"]
        P2["Total Ast = 1878 sq mm"]
        P3["Total Asc = 308 sq mm"]
    end
    
    P1 --> Q1["Singly Reinforced Sub-Section Part A"]
    P1 --> Q2["Additional Couple Sub-Section Part B"]
    
    Q1 --> R1["Mu lim = 313.09 kN-m"]
    Q1 --> R2["Ast lim = 1570 sq mm"]
    Q1 --> R3["Concrete Block Full"]
    
    Q2 --> S1["delta Mu = 66.91 kN-m"]
    Q2 --> S2["delta Ast = 308 sq mm"]
    Q2 --> S3["Asc = 308 sq mm"]
    Q2 --> S4["Lever Arm d minus d-prime = 500 mm"]
    
    R1 --> T["Superposition = Total Capacity"]
    S1 --> T
    T --> U["Mu total = 380 kN-m CHECK"]
    
    style CompositeSection fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:3px
    style P1 fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style R1 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style S1 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style T fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style U fill:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Stress & Strain Block Schematic Description

The rectangular stress block (IS 456, Clause 38.1) for the doubly reinforced section has the following architecture:

| **Component** | **Location** | **Stress/Force** | **Role** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Concrete block | $0.48 x_u$ from top | $0.36 f_{ck}$ (uniform) | Primary compression |
| Compression steel | At $d'$ from top | $0.87 f_y$ (if yielded) | Supplementary compression |
| Tension steel | At $d$ from top | $0.87 f_y$ | Primary tension |
| Effective depth $d$ | Distance to centroid of $A_{st}$ | — | Lever-arm basis |
| Cover $d'$ | To centroid of $A_{sc}$ | — | Bond and fire protection |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three situations where doubly reinforced sections are preferred over singly reinforced sections.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. When the applied factored moment $M_u$ exceeds the limiting moment of resistance $M_{u,lim}$ of a singly reinforced section of given dimensions. (1 mark)
2. When the section dimensions are restricted due to architectural or functional requirements (headroom constraints, pre-existing walls). (1 mark)
3. When the beam is subjected to **reversal of bending moments** (continuous beams, cantilever action, seismic loading), where the same cross-section must resist both sagging and hogging moments. (1 mark)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of providing compression reinforcement in beams?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Compression reinforcement increases the **moment of resistance** of the section beyond $M_{u,lim}$, allowing the beam to safely resist moments higher than the balanced moment. (1 mark)
2. It reduces **long-term deflections** due to creep and shrinkage, improving serviceability as per IS 456, Clause 24.4. (1 mark)
3. It enhances **ductility** under cyclic/seismic loading, critical for earthquake-resistant design. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper 2]`

A doubly reinforced concrete beam of rectangular section has width $b = 250$ mm, overall depth $D = 550$ mm, effective cover to tension steel $d = 500$ mm, and cover to compression steel $d' = 50$ mm. The materials used are M25 grade concrete and Fe500 grade steel. The beam is to resist a factored bending moment $M_u = 320$ kN·m.

**(a) Check whether the section can be designed as singly reinforced. If not, design the section as a doubly reinforced beam, computing $A_{st}$ and $A_{sc}$. (7 marks)**

**(b) Sketch the cross-section, showing reinforcement details, and verify the design satisfies IS 456 codal provisions for minimum and maximum steel. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a): Singly vs Doubly Reinforced Check**

**Step 1: Limiting moment of resistance**

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 250 \times (500)^2$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 250 \times 250{,}000 = 215{,}625{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,lim} = 215.63 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 2: Comparison**

$$M_u = 320 \text{ kN·m} > M_{u,lim} = 215.63 \text{ kN·m}$$

The section **cannot be designed as singly reinforced**. Doubly reinforced design is required.

**Step 3: Excess moment**

$$\Delta M_u = 320 - 215.63 = 104.37 \text{ kN·m} = 104{,}370{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Limiting tension steel area $A_{st,lim}$**

$$A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_{u,max}}{0.87 \cdot f_y}$$

$$A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 \times 25 \times 250 \times 0.46 \times 500}{0.87 \times 500}$$

$$A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 \times 25 \times 250 \times 230}{435} = \dfrac{517{,}500}{435} \approx 1190 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Additional steel areas**

$$A_{sc} = \Delta A_{st} = \dfrac{\Delta M_u}{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot (d - d')}$$

$$A_{sc} = \Delta A_{st} = \dfrac{104{,}370{,}000}{0.87 \times 500 \times (500 - 50)}$$

$$A_{sc} = \Delta A_{st} = \dfrac{104{,}370{,}000}{217{,}500} \approx 480 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Total tension steel**

$$\boxed{A_{st} = A_{st,lim} + \Delta A_{st} = 1190 + 480 = 1670 \text{ mm}^2}$$

**Part (b): Detailing and Codal Verification**

**Step 7: Bar selection**
- For $A_{st} = 1670$ mm² → use **4 bars of 25 mm $\phi$** (provides $4 \times 490.87 = 1963$ mm²).
- For $A_{sc} = 480$ mm² → use **2 bars of 18 mm $\phi$** (provides $2 \times 254.47 = 509$ mm²).

**Step 8: Minimum tension steel check (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.1)**

$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85 \cdot b \cdot d}{f_y} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 250 \times 500}{500} = 212.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provided $A_{st} = 1963$ mm² $> A_{st,min} = 212.5$ mm² $\checkmark$

**Step 9: Maximum steel check (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.2)**

$$A_{st,max} = 0.04 \cdot b \cdot D = 0.04 \times 250 \times 550 = 5500 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provided $A_{st} = 1963$ mm² $< A_{st,max} = 5500$ mm² $\checkmark$

**Step 10: Minimum compression steel check**

When the section is doubly reinforced, IS 456 (Cl. 26.5.1.4) requires:
$$A_{sc,min} = 0.04 \cdot \text{web width} \cdot \text{distance between compression face and centroid of tension steel} / 2$$
More commonly, the minimum is to be such that the area of compression steel is not less than **8% of the area of tension steel** (per IS 13920 for seismic) or as per general practice, at least **2 bars of 12 mm $\phi$**.

Provided $A_{sc} = 509$ mm² satisfies the minimum requirement. $\checkmark$

**Step 11: Sketch — Cross-Section Description**
- Effective span cross-section: 250 mm × 550 mm.
- Top: 2 bars of 18 mm $\phi$ at $d' = 50$ mm from top.
- Bottom: 4 bars of 25 mm $\phi$ at $d = 500$ mm from top.
- 8 mm $\phi$ lateral ties at 150 mm spacing (for ductility).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> 1. **Most common error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to check the singly-reinforced condition first. Students often jump directly to computing $A_{sc}$ and $\Delta A_{st}$ without verifying that $M_u > M_{u,lim}$.
> 2. **Forgotten codal check (–1 mark):** Not verifying $A_{st} \geq A_{st,min}$ and $A_{st} \leq A_{st,max}$. KTU examiners strictly check this.
> 3. **Unit error (–1 mark):** Reporting areas in cm² instead of mm². Always use **mm²** in limit state design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A reinforced concrete beam of rectangular section, 300 mm wide and 650 mm overall depth, is to be designed as a doubly reinforced section. The effective cover is 50 mm, and cover to compression reinforcement is also 50 mm. Concrete grade is **M30**, and steel grade is **Fe500**. The beam is subjected to a factored moment of **425 kN·m**.

**(a) Compute the area of tension and compression reinforcement required. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the same section were to be designed as a singly reinforced section, what would be the maximum moment of resistance it can safely carry? Comment on the safety implications. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a): Doubly Reinforced Design**

**Step 1: Given data**
- $b = 300$ mm, $D = 650$ mm, $d = 650 - 50 = 600$ mm, $d' = 50$ mm
- $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm²
- $M_u = 425 \times 10^6$ N·mm

**Step 2: Limiting moment of resistance**

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 = 0.138 \times 30 \times 300 \times (600)^2$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 30 \times 300 \times 360{,}000 = 447{,}120{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,lim} = 447.12 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 3: Check for doubly reinforcement**

$$M_u = 425 \text{ kN·m} < M_{u,lim} = 447.12 \text{ kN·m}$$

The section can be designed as **singly reinforced** with the given $M_u$. However, the problem explicitly asks for doubly reinforced design. The doubly reinforced design will, in this case, provide **redundant safety** but with non-zero compression steel.

**Step 4: Doubly reinforced design (as per problem statement)**

$$\Delta M_u = M_u - M_{u,lim} = 425 - 447.12 = -22.12 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $\Delta M_u$ is **negative**, the section is technically under-reinforced. To force a doubly reinforced design, a minimum compression steel of **2 bars of 12 mm $\phi$** is provided.

$$A_{sc,min} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 12^2 = 226 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Tension steel for singly reinforced equivalent**

$$A_{st} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot z}$$

For an under-reinforced section, $x_u$ needs to be found by solving:

$$0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_u \cdot (d - 0.42 x_u) = M_u$$

$$0.36 \times 30 \times 300 \times x_u \times (600 - 0.42 x_u) = 425 \times 10^6$$

$$3240 \cdot x_u \cdot (600 - 0.42 x_u) = 425 \times 10^6$$

This is a quadratic in $x_u$. Iterating:
- Try $x_u = 250$ mm: $3240 \times 250 \times (600 - 105) = 3240 \times 250 \times 495 = 400{,}950{,}000$ N·mm (slightly low)
- Try $x_u = 270$ mm: $3240 \times 270 \times 486.6 = 425{,}549{,}000$ N·mm ≈ $M_u$ ✓

So $x_u \approx 270$ mm.

$$A_{st} = \dfrac{0.36 \times 30 \times 300 \times 270}{0.87 \times 500} = \dfrac{874{,}800}{435} \approx 2011 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **5 bars of 22 mm $\phi$** ($5 \times 380 = 1900$ mm²) — slightly inadequate. Use **4 bars of 25 mm $\phi$** ($4 \times 490.87 = 1963$ mm²) — still slightly low. **Use 5 bars of 25 mm $\phi$** ($5 \times 490.87 = 2454$ mm²) for adequate safety.

**Final Design:**
- $A_{st} = 2454$ mm² (5 bars of 25 mm $\phi$)
- $A_{sc} = 226$ mm² (2 bars of 12 mm $\phi$)

**Part (b): Maximum Moment of Resistance as Singly Reinforced**

The maximum moment that the singly reinforced section can resist is $M_{u,lim} = 447.12$ kN·m.

**Safety Implications:**
1. The applied moment $M_u = 425$ kN·m is **less than $M_{u,lim}$**, so a singly reinforced section is **structurally safe** but lacks the redundancy and improved ductility of a doubly reinforced section.
2. The doubly reinforced design with nominal compression steel provides **enhanced safety** against moment reversal (seismic loads, alternate loading patterns) and reduced long-term deflections.
3. From a **safety philosophy** perspective, the additional cost of compression steel is justified in critical structures (hospitals, schools, high-rise buildings) where redundancy is non-negotiable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Misjudging the case (–2 marks):** Students often wrongly conclude $\Delta M_u > 0$ here. Always compute $M_{u,lim}$ FIRST and compare. If $M_u < M_{u,lim}$, the doubly reinforced design is optional, not mandatory.
> 2. **Quadratic equation mishandling (–2 marks):** Part (a) requires solving for $x_u$ iteratively. Students frequently skip this step and assume $x_u = x_{u,max}$, which is **only valid for limiting section** — not for under-reinforced.
> 3. **Forgetting safety commentary (–1 mark):** Part (b) explicitly asks for "safety implications." Marks are reserved for the discussion on redundancy, ductility, and economic trade-off.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **When is doubly reinforcement mandatory?** When $M_u > M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (for Fe500).
- **Why use 0.138?** It is the simplified coefficient for Fe500 derived from $0.36 \times 0.46 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.46) = 0.138$. For Fe415, the coefficient is **0.149**.
- **Master the formula:** $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ — quoted in nearly every KTU problem.
- **The "superposition trick":** Doubly reinforced = Singly reinforced ($M_{u,lim}$) + Additional couple ($\Delta M_u$).
- **Lever arm is $d - d'$**, NOT $d$ alone.
- **$A_{sc} = \Delta A_{st}$** (numerically equal) when both have yielded — students often forget this.
- **Total $A_{st} = A_{st,lim} + \Delta A_{st}$**.
- **Codal checks (always):** $A_{st,min} = 0.85 b d / f_y$ and $A_{st,max} = 0.04 b D$.
- **Cover $d' = 50$ mm** for beams, **$d' = 30$–50 mm** for slabs (as per problem data).
- **Units:** kN·m for moments, mm² for steel, mm for dimensions, N/mm² for stresses.
- **Don't forget lateral ties** on compression steel to prevent buckling (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.2).
- **Sketch the cross-section** in every problem — it earns 1–2 easy marks.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU ESE (End Semester Exam), Part B questions on this topic will **always** be 14 marks, split as 7+7. They will require numerical computation + codal verification. Practice at least **3–4 numerical problems** before the exam for confidence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Slabs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Slabs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TYPES OF SLABS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slab (as per IS 456:2000, Cl. 2.6):** A structural member, generally horizontal (or near horizontal), whose thickness is small compared to its span, and which is loaded primarily by out-of-plane (transverse) loads acting perpendicular to its plane. Slabs are the primary horizontal load-carrying elements in Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) framed buildings.

A slab is classified as a **shallow structural element** when its effective depth (`d`) is less than about **1/10 of its smaller span**. The structural action of a slab depends on the **ratio of its longer span (Ly) to its shorter span (Lx)** and the **support conditions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wooden Bridge"

Imagine a **rectangular thin plywood sheet** supported at the four corners on four pillars. If you gently push down on the sheet:

- If the sheet is **much longer in one direction** (say, 3 m × 1 m), the load "travels" primarily along the **shorter direction** to the supports — it acts like a *one-way* bridge.
- If the sheet is **nearly square** (say, 3 m × 3 m), the load "splits" and travels **in both directions** to reach the nearest support — it acts like a *two-way* basket.

This intuitive "load travel path" logic is exactly how structural engineers classify slabs in design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Significance:** The *type* of slab dictates the entire design philosophy — bending moment distribution, reinforcement layout, depth selection, deflection control, and even safety factors as mandated in IS 456:2000 and SP-16 design tables.

### 1.3 Primary Classification of Slabs

Slabs are broadly classified based on **structural action**, **geometry**, and **support conditions**:

**A. Based on Structural Action (Load Transfer Path):**
- One-way Slab
- Two-way Slab

**B. Based on Geometry & Section:**
- Solid Slab (Flat Plate / Conventional)
- Ribbed / Trough Slab
- Waffle Slab
- Hollow Block / Filler Slab

**C. Based on Support Conditions:**
- Simply Supported Slab
- Continuous Slab
- Cantilever Slab
- Flat Slab (with column drops / capitals)

**D. Special Slabs:**
- Slab on Grade (Ground Slab)
- Folding Slab
- Dome / Shell Slab
- Prestressed Slab

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 One-Way Slab

A slab is designed as a **one-way slab** when the ratio of the longer span to the shorter span is **greater than 2** (i.e., `Ly/Lx > 2`).

- Bending occurs **predominantly in one direction** (the short span).
- Main reinforcement is provided **along the short span** (perpendicular to the supports on the short sides).
- Distribution steel is provided along the long span (secondary, constructional).
- The slab behaves essentially as a **series of independent beams** of unit width.

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 456:2000 — Cl. 24.1:** For slabs spanning in one direction, the slabs shall be designed as rectangular beams of unit width, taking the span equal to the centre-to-centre distance between the supports (effective span).

**Typical Use:** Floor slabs of long, narrow buildings; verandah slabs; chajjas.

## 2.2 Two-Way Slab

A slab is designed as a **two-way slab** when `Ly/Lx ≤ 2`.

- Bending occurs **significantly in both directions**.
- Reinforcement is provided in **both directions** (main steel in both `Lx` and `Ly`).
- Load is transferred to all four supporting edges.

### 2.2.1 Design Methods (as per IS 456:2000, Cl. 24.4)

| Method | Application |
|---|---|
| **Rankine–Grashoff Method** | When the slab is simply supported on all four sides with corners held down (rare in practice) |
| **IS Code Coefficient Method (Cl. 26.5, Table 12, 26)** | Continuous / restrained slabs — most commonly used in KTU exams |
| **Yield Line Theory** | Ultimate load analysis, irregular panels |
| **Equivalent Frame Method** | Complex multi-bay, multi-storey frames |

### 2.2.2 IS 456 Coefficients for Two-Way Slabs (Cl. 26.5.3.1)

The design bending moments for a two-way restrained slab are obtained by multiplying the **maximum design moment per unit width** (M) by **tabulated coefficients** (αx and αy):

$$M_x = \alpha_x \cdot M \quad \text{and} \quad M_y = \alpha_y \cdot M$$

where the **maximum design moment per metre width**:

$$M = \frac{w \cdot L_x^2}{12}$$

The coefficients αx and αy depend on the **type of panel** (interior, edge, corner) and the **ratio Ly/Lx**.

## 2.3 Flat Slab

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flat Slab (IS 456:2000, Cl. 31):** A RCC slab supported directly on columns without beams. Load transfer occurs through two-way bending and punching shear around the column heads.

**Components of a Flat Slab (Fig. concept):**
- **Drop Panel** — Thickened portion around the column to resist punching shear.
- **Column Head / Capital** — Enlarged top of column (optional in modern construction).
- **Panel** — Slab area bounded by column centrelines.
- **Column Strip** — Middle strip across the column line (width = half the panel on each side).
- **Middle Strip** — Central strip between two column strips.

## 2.4 Ribbed / Waffle / Hollow Slabs

These are slabs where concrete in the **tension zone (below neutral axis)** is replaced by voids to reduce self-weight.

- **Ribbed Slab:** One-way ribs running in one direction. Provided with **hollow blocks / filler blocks** between ribs.
- **Waffle Slab:** Two-way grid of ribs, also called **coffered slab**. Effective for large spans and auditoriums.
- **Hollow Block Slab:** Precast hollow blocks (terracotta or concrete) placed between ribs.

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 456:2000, Cl. 30:** Ribbed / hollow slabs are designed as T-beams or L-beams. The minimum flange thickness and rib geometry are specified to prevent local buckling/collapse.

## 2.5 Slab on Grade

A slab cast directly on the ground to serve as a floor for warehouses, pavements, parking areas, and industrial floors.
- Designed as **plain concrete** or **lightly reinforced** depending on load.
- The soil subgrade acts as the support.
- **Safety consideration:** proper compaction, DPC (Damp Proof Course), and control joints are critical.

## 2.6 Cantilever Slab

A slab projecting freely with **one edge fixed** and the **other edge free** (no support).
- Examples: Chajja, canopy, balcony projecting slab.
- Treated as a **one-way slab** with maximum bending moment and shear at the fixed support.

## 2.7 Continuous Slab

A slab that is **continuous over multiple supports** (intermediate beams/walls).
- **Negative moments** develop over supports, **positive moments** at mid-span.
- Coefficients from **IS 456:2000 Table 12** are used for design.

## 2.8 Special Slabs — Safety Perspective

| Slab Type | Critical Safety Check | Failure Mode Avoided |
|---|---|---|
| Flat Slab | **Punching Shear** at column | Brittle column-slab joint failure |
| One-Way Slab | **Main steel curtailment** | Flexural collapse at mid-span |
| Two-Way Slab | **Corner reinforcement** | Corner lifting / cracking |
| Cantilever Slab | **Top steel at fixed end** | Top-fibre tension failure |
| Waffle / Ribbed | **Rib shear** | Diagonal tension failure |
| Slab on Grade | **Differential settlement** | Cracking due to subgrade voids |

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | IS Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-Way / Two-Way classification | $\dfrac{L_y}{L_x} \leq 2$ → Two-way ; $> 2$ → One-way | — | IS 456:2000, Cl. 24.1 |
| 2 | Effective span (simply supported) | $L_{eff} = L_n + d$ or $c/c$ distance, whichever is **less** | m | Cl. 22.2(a) |
| 3 | Effective span (continuous) | $L_{eff} = c/c$ distance between supports | m | Cl. 22.2(c) |
| 4 | One-way slab: max BM per m width | $M = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$ (simply supported) | kN·m/m | Cl. 24.1 |
| 5 | Two-way slab: max BM (IS method) | $M = \dfrac{w L_x^2}{12}$ | kN·m/m | Cl. 26.5.2.1 |
| 6 | Moment coefficient tables | $\alpha_x, \alpha_y$ from Table 12 & Table 26 of SP-16 | — | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 7 | Effective depth (for deflection control) | $\dfrac{L}{d}$ basic = $20 \times \dfrac{M}{F_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2}$ | m | Cl. 23.2.1 |
| 8 | Min. slab thickness (one-way) | $\dfrac{L}{d}_{basic} = 20$ (S.S.) to $26$ (continuous) | m | Cl. 26.2.1 |
| 9 | Min. slab thickness (two-way) | $\dfrac{L}{d}_{basic} = 26$ (S.S.) to $32$ (continuous) | m | Cl. 26.2.1 |
| 10 | Min. reinforcement (HYSD bars) | $A_{s,min} = \dfrac{0.12 \cdot b \cdot D}{100}$ | mm² | Cl. 26.5.2.2 |
| 11 | Max. diameter of bar (to control cracking) | $\phi_{max}$ as per Table 15 of IS 456 | mm | Cl. 26.3.2 |
| 12 | Max. spacing of main bars | $3d$ or $300 \text{ mm}$, whichever is less | mm | Cl. 26.3.3(b)(1) |
| 13 | Max. spacing of distribution bars | $5d$ or $450 \text{ mm}$, whichever is less | mm | Cl. 26.3.3(b)(2) |
| 14 | Flat slab: minimum drop depth | $t_d \geq \dfrac{L}{10}$ of the span | m | Cl. 31.2.1 |
| 15 | Flat slab: effective diameter for punching shear | $b_d = c + 3d$ (for interior columns) | m | Cl. 31.6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are the *daily tools* of a structural design engineer — used in RCC building design software (ETABS, SAFE, STAAD) hand-calibration, site supervision, and quality control. The IS 456 limits on `L/d` and minimum steel are **safety-based empirical envelopes** that prevent serviceability and collapse failures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — One-Way Slab Maximum Bending Moment

### Setup
A one-way slab of unit width (1 m) supported on two parallel walls, treated as a simply supported beam.

Let:
- $L$ = effective span of slab (m)
- $w$ = total factored load (kN/m²) × 1 m = total load per metre width (kN/m)
- $M$ = maximum bending moment per metre width (kN·m/m)
- $R_A, R_B$ = reactions at supports (kN)

### Step 1 — Equilibrium
For a simply supported beam with uniformly distributed load $w$ per metre:

By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$

### Step 2 — Bending Moment at Mid-Span
The bending moment at a section `x` from support A is:

$$M_x = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Moment Location
To find where $M_x$ is maximum, differentiate and equate to zero:

$$\dfrac{dM_x}{dx} = R_A - w x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \dfrac{R_A}{w} = \dfrac{L}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $x = L/2$

$$M_{max} = R_A \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{w}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} \quad \text{per metre width}}$$

This is the classical **simply supported beam formula**, applied per metre of slab width.

### Step 5 — Design Moment Using Load Factors (IS 456:2000, Cl. 18.2.1)
The **factored** load $w_u = 1.5 (DL + LL)$ is used for ultimate limit state design:

$$M_u = \dfrac{w_u L^2}{8} = \dfrac{1.5 (DL + LL) \cdot L^2}{8}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Two-Way Slab Moment Coefficient (IS 456, Cl. 26.5)

### Concept
IS 456 provides **moment coefficients** (αx, αy) that are empirical values derived from elastic plate theory (Westergaard/Marcus) and validated through experiments. They give the **negative and positive moments** at critical sections of a continuous two-way slab panel.

### Step 1 — Define the Maximum Moment per Unit Width

$$M = \dfrac{w \cdot L_x^2}{12}$$

This is the **base moment** for a two-way slab simply supported on all edges with `Ly = Lx` and uniformly distributed load.

> The factor `12` (instead of `8` as in one-way) arises because the load is shared by bending in **two directions** — so each direction carries roughly **2/3** of the total moment.

### Step 2 — Multiply by Coefficients
For any panel type (interior, edge, corner) and any `Ly/Lx` ratio, the design moments are:

$$M_{x} = \alpha_x \cdot M \qquad M_{y} = \alpha_y \cdot M$$

where αx and αy are read from **SP-16 Table 26** (or IS 456 Table 12 for continuous slabs).

### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation of Coefficients
- αx, αy **decrease** as the panel becomes more restrained (continuous > simply supported).
- αy (long span) is always **less than** αx (short span) because the long direction is **less stiff** and carries less moment.
- The sum of relevant coefficients obeys the **static equilibrium check** within the panel.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU 14-Mark Style

### Problem Statement
> A simply supported **one-way slab** has a clear span of **3.5 m** and effective span of **4.0 m**. It carries a live load of **3 kN/m²** and floor finish of **1 kN/m²**. Slab thickness = **150 mm** with 20 mm cover. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Design the slab for flexure.

### Given Data
- Span $L_{eff} = 4.0$ m
- Slab thickness $D = 150$ mm
- Cover = 20 mm; assume 10 mm Ø bars → $d = 150 - 20 - 5 = 125$ mm = 0.125 m
- $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm²; $f_y = 415$ N/mm²
- $LL = 3$ kN/m²; Floor finish (FF) = 1 kN/m²

### Step 1 — Load Calculation (per m²)

$$\text{Self-weight (SW)} = 0.150 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{Dead Load (DL)} = \text{SW} + \text{FF} = 3.75 + 1.0 = 4.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{Total Load (TL)} = DL + LL = 4.75 + 3.0 = 7.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Factored Load

$$w_u = 1.5 \times 7.75 = 11.625 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

For 1 m width, $w_u = 11.625$ kN/m.

### Step 3 — Design Bending Moment (Simply Supported)

$$M_u = \dfrac{w_u \cdot L_{eff}^2}{8} = \dfrac{11.625 \times 4.0^2}{8} = \dfrac{186.0}{8} = 23.25 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

### Step 4 — Check Effective Depth (Deflection, IS 456 Cl. 23.2.1)

Basic L/d for simply supported = **20** (from Table 5, IS 456).

Modification factor $\tau_s$ depends on $P_t$ (percentage of steel); assume $P_t \approx 0.5\%$ → $\tau_s \approx 1.4$ (from Fig. 4, IS 456).

$$\dfrac{L}{d}_{\text{allowable}} = 20 \times 1.4 = 28$$

$$d_{required} = \dfrac{L}{\text{allowable ratio}} = \dfrac{4000}{28} = 142.9 \text{ mm}$$

Provided $d = 125$ mm < 142.9 mm → **DEFLECTION GOVERNS.** Increase depth.

> [!WARNING]
> **Deflection always governs** in most one-way slab designs. Students often skip this check and lose 2–3 marks.

### Step 5 — Revised Section
Let us use $D = 175$ mm → $d = 175 - 25 = 150$ mm.

$$\dfrac{L}{d}_{provided} = \dfrac{4000}{150} = 26.67 < 28 \quad \checkmark \text{ OK}$$

### Step 6 — Design Flexural Reinforcement

$$M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} \cdot d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} \cdot f_y}{b \cdot d \cdot f_{ck}}\right)$$

Solving quadratic for $A_{st}$ with $b = 1000$ mm, $d = 150$ mm:

$$A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2}}\right] b d$$

$$\dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2} = \dfrac{4.6 \times 23.25 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 150^2} = \dfrac{1.0695 \times 10^8}{4.5 \times 10^8} = 0.2377$$

$$A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.2377}\right] \times 1000 \times 150$$

$$A_{st} = 0.0241 \times \left[1 - 0.8730\right] \times 150000 = 0.0241 \times 0.1270 \times 150000$$

$$\boxed{A_{st} = 459 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}}$$

### Step 7 — Select Bar Spacing
Provide **10 mm Ø bars** (area of one bar = 78.5 mm²):

$$\text{Spacing} = \dfrac{1000 \times 78.5}{459} = 171 \text{ mm} \approx 160 \text{ mm c/c}$$

Provide **10 mm Ø @ 160 mm c/c** as main reinforcement.

### Step 8 — Distribution Steel (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.2)
Minimum = $\dfrac{0.12 \times 1000 \times 175}{100} = 210$ mm²/m

Provide **8 mm Ø @ 240 mm c/c** (area = 209 mm²/m) — distribution steel along the long span.

### Step 9 — Checks Summary

| Check | Required | Provided | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective depth (deflection) | 142.9 mm | 150 mm | ✓ |
| Main reinforcement | 459 mm²/m | 491 mm²/m | ✓ |
| Min. reinforcement | 210 mm²/m | 210 mm²/m | ✓ |
| Max. spacing of main bars | 3d = 450 mm or 300 mm | 160 mm | ✓ |
| Max. spacing of dist. bars | 5d = 750 mm or 450 mm | 240 mm | ✓ |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Slab Type (Decision Flow)

| Condition / Span | Slab Type Recommended | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Span < 3.5 m, simply supported | Solid one-way | Economical, simple detailing |
| 3.5 m < Span < 6 m, continuous | Two-way solid | Reduced mid-span moment, good stiffness |
| Span 6 – 9 m, heavy load | Ribbed / Waffle | Reduced self-weight, longer span capability |
| Span > 9 m, no intermediate columns | Waffle / Prestressed | Optimal for large open areas |
| Commercial / Parking with flat soffit | **Flat slab with drop** | Clear headroom, no beams |
| Industrial floor on ground | Slab on grade | Direct support by soil, low cost |
| Chajja / canopy | Cantilever slab | Free projection, fixed at one end |
| Auditorium roof, special shape | Dome / Folding / Shell | Aesthetic, structural efficiency |

---

    A --> C["Geometry"]
    A --> D["Support Condition"]
    A --> E["Special Slabs"]

    S1["Start Identify Ly by Lx Ratio"] --> S2{"Ly by Lx less or equal 2"}
    S2 -- No --> S3["Design as One Way Slab"]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4["Identify Panel Type Interior Edge Corner"]
    S4 --> S5["Compute Factored Load wu"]
    S5 --> S6["Compute Max Moment M equals wu Lx squared by 12"]
    S6 --> S7["Read alpha x and alpha y from SP 16 Table 26"]
    S7 --> S8["Compute Mx and My"]
    S8 --> S9["Check L by d for Deflection"]
    S9 --> S10["Compute Ast from Mu"]
    S10 --> S11["Select Bar Diameter and Spacing"]
    S11 --> S12["Check Ast min and Spacing Max"]
    S12 --> S13["Check Shear and Detailing"]
    S13 --> S14["Provide Corner Reinforcement"]

```

    P5 --> P6["Min Steel Check"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between a one-way and a two-way slab. State the IS 456:2000 criterion for the classification.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Way Slab:** A slab that bends primarily in one direction (the short span) and transfers the load to the two parallel supporting beams/walls.
> **Two-Way Slab:** A slab that bends in both perpendicular directions and transfers the load to all four supporting edges.

**IS 456:2000 Criterion (Cl. 24.1):**
A slab is designed as **one-way** when the ratio of longer span to shorter span `Ly/Lx > 2`, and as **two-way** when `Ly/Lx ≤ 2`.

**Key differences:**

| Parameter | One-Way | Two-Way |
|---|---|---|
| Span ratio (Ly/Lx) | > 2 | ≤ 2 |
| Bending | In one direction | In both directions |
| Main steel direction | Short span only | Both directions |
| Bending moment coeff. | wL²/8 (per m) | wLx²/12 (per m) + coefficients |
| Analysis method | Beam analogy | Plate theory / IS 456 coefficients |

*(3 marks — Distinguish + criterion correctly stated)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List the components of a flat slab and state the function of a drop panel.**

**Model Answer:**

The main components of a flat slab (IS 456, Cl. 31) are:
1. **Slab panel** — the main flat surface
2. **Drop panel** — thickened portion around the column
3. **Column capital** — enlarged top of column (optional)
4. **Column strip** — middle strip across the column line
5. **Middle strip** — central region between column strips

**Function of Drop Panel:**
The drop panel **increases the effective depth of the slab near the column**, thereby:
- Increasing the **punching shear capacity** (shear perimeter becomes deeper).
- Reducing the **negative moment** in the column strip.
- Improving the **stiffness and crack control** at the column-slab joint.

*(3 marks — Components listed + function of drop panel clearly stated)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> Internal choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

A simply supported **one-way RCC slab** is provided for a residential building with the following data:

- Clear span = 3.0 m; effective span = 3.2 m
- Slab thickness = 130 mm; cover = 20 mm
- Live Load (LL) = 3.0 kN/m²; Floor Finish (FF) = 1.0 kN/m²
- Materials: M20 concrete, Fe415 steel

**a)** Compute the **factored design moment** per metre width. (7 Marks)
**b)** Design the **main reinforcement** in the slab. Check for deflection and minimum steel. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Given:** $L_{eff} = 3.2$ m, $D = 130$ mm, cover = 20 mm, assume 10 mm Ø bars → effective depth $d = 130 - 20 - 5 = 105$ mm = 0.105 m.

### Part (a) — Factored Design Moment

**Step 1 — Load per m²**

$$\text{Self-weight} = 0.130 \times 25 = 3.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{Dead Load (DL)} = 3.25 + 1.0 = 4.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{Total Load} = 4.25 + 3.0 = 7.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Factored Load** (IS 456, Cl. 18.2.1)

$$w_u = 1.5 \times 7.25 = 10.875 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

For 1 m strip: $w_u = 10.875$ kN/m.

**Step 3 — Maximum BM (Simply Supported)**

$$\boxed{M_u = \dfrac{w_u L^2}{8} = \dfrac{10.875 \times 3.2^2}{8} = \dfrac{111.36}{8} = 13.92 \text{ kN·m/m}}$$

### Part (b) — Design of Main Reinforcement

**Step 1 — Check Deflection (Cl. 23.2.1)**
For simply supported: basic L/d = 20. Assume $P_t \approx 0.3\%$ → $\tau_s \approx 1.6$ (IS 456 Fig. 4).

$$\dfrac{L}{d}_{allow} = 20 \times 1.6 = 32$$

$$\dfrac{L}{d}_{provided} = \dfrac{3200}{105} = 30.5 < 32 \quad \checkmark \text{ OK}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Ast**

$$\dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} = \dfrac{4.6 \times 13.92 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 105^2} = 0.2901$$

$$A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.2901}\right] \times 1000 \times 105$$

$$A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.1428 \times 105000 = 361.5 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Check Minimum Steel (Cl. 26.5.2.2)**

$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 1000 \times 130}{100} = 156 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

Since 361.5 > 156 → design steel governs.

**Step 4 — Select Bar Spacing**
Using 8 mm Ø bars (area = 50.3 mm²):

$$\text{Spacing} = \dfrac{1000 \times 50.3}{361.5} = 139 \text{ mm} \approx 130 \text{ mm c/c}$$

**Provide 8 mm Ø @ 130 mm c/c** as main reinforcement.

**Step 5 — Spacing Check (Cl. 26.3.3)**
- Max spacing of main bars = $3d = 315$ mm or 300 mm → 130 mm ✓
- Use distribution steel 8 mm Ø @ 250 mm c/c (parallel to supports).

**Step 6 — Curtailment**
Curtail 50% of main bars at 0.1L from the support (subject to anchorage).

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

A two-way restrained slab has clear dimensions **4.0 m × 6.0 m** with the longer edges discontinuous and shorter edges continuous. Slab thickness = 175 mm; Live Load = 4 kN/m²; Floor Finish = 1.5 kN/m². Use M25 concrete and Fe500 steel.

**a)** Classify the slab and compute the **design moments** at mid-span and at the continuous edge using the IS code coefficient method. (7 Marks)
**b)** Design the **reinforcement** for the short-span direction and check for deflection. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Given:** $L_x = 4.0$ m (short span), $L_y = 6.0$ m (long span), $D = 175$ mm, $LL = 4$ kN/m², $FF = 1.5$ kN/m², $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm².

### Part (a) — Classification & Design Moments

**Step 1 — Classification**

$$\dfrac{L_y}{L_x} = \dfrac{6.0}{4.0} = 1.5 \leq 2 \Rightarrow \text{Two-way slab}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Spans**
Assuming support width 230 mm and effective depth d ≈ 150 mm:

$$L_{x,eff} = 4.0 + \dfrac{0.23}{2} + \dfrac{0.15}{2} \approx 4.19 \text{ m (or simply use clear span)}$$

For design, use $L_x = 4.0$ m, $L_y = 6.0$ m.

**Step 3 — Load Calculation**

$$\text{Self-weight} = 0.175 \times 25 = 4.375 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{DL} = 4.375 + 1.5 = 5.875 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{Factored load } w_u = 1.5 \times (5.875 + 4.0) = 14.81 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Base Moment**

$$M = \dfrac{w_u L_x^2}{12} = \dfrac{14.81 \times 4.0^2}{12} = 19.75 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Step 5 — Coefficients (SP-16 Table 26, for Slab Type 3: long edges discontinuous, short edges continuous)**

For $L_y/L_x = 1.5$:
- Negative moment coefficient at continuous short edge: $\alpha_x = 0.054$, $\alpha_y = 0.038$
- Positive moment coefficient at mid-span: $\alpha_x = 0.043$, $\alpha_y = 0.031$

**Step 6 — Design Moments**

| Location | Moment | Calculation | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mid-span (Short) | $M_{x,+}$ | $0.043 \times 19.75$ | **0.85 kN·m/m** |
| Mid-span (Long) | $M_{y,+}$ | $0.031 \times 19.75$ | **0.61 kN·m/m** |
| Continuous edge (Short) | $M_{x,-}$ | $0.054 \times 19.75$ | **1.07 kN·m/m** |
| Continuous edge (Long) | $M_{y,-}$ | $0.038 \times 19.75$ | **0.75 kN·m/m** |

### Part (b) — Reinforcement Design (Short-Span)

**Step 1 — Deflection Check (Cl. 23.2.1)**
For two-way continuous slab, basic L/d = 32. With $P_t \approx 0.4\%$, $\tau_s \approx 1.4$:

$$\dfrac{L_x}{d}_{allow} = 32 \times 1.4 = 44.8$$

$$d_{required} = \dfrac{4000}{44.8} = 89.3 \text{ mm}$$

Provided $d = 150$ mm > 89.3 mm → **Deflection OK.**

**Step 2 — Design for $M_{x,-} = 1.07$ kN·m/m at the continuous edge** (this is the larger moment in x-direction)

$$\dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} = \dfrac{4.6 \times 1.07 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 150^2} = 0.00874$$

$$A_{st,x} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 25}{500} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.00874}\right] \times 1000 \times 150$$

$$A_{st,x} = 0.025 \times 0.00438 \times 150000 = 16.4 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

This is very small → **minimum steel governs.**

**Step 3 — Minimum Steel (Cl. 26.5.2.2)**

$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 1000 \times 175}{100} = 210 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

**Provide 8 mm Ø @ 220 mm c/c** → 230 mm²/m in both directions.

**Step 4 — Corner Reinforcement (Cl. 26.5.3.2)**
At the **discontinuous corners**, provide top and bottom mesh reinforcement of 8 mm Ø @ 200 mm c/c, extending a distance equal to **1/5 of the short span** in each direction from the corner.

**Final Detailing:**
- Main steel (short span, top & bottom): 8 mm Ø @ 220 mm c/c
- Distribution steel (long span): 8 mm Ø @ 220 mm c/c
- Corner reinforcement: 8 mm Ø @ 200 mm c/c mesh extending 0.8 m from each corner

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the load factor 1.5** → Use $w_u = 1.5 \times (DL + LL)$ always. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Skipping deflection (L/d) check** → IS 456 mandates it; missing = –2 marks.
> 3. **Not checking $A_{st,min}$** → Minimum steel prevents sudden brittle failure.
> 4. **Wrong moment coefficient table** → Use SP-16 Table 26 for two-way; not the same as Table 12.
> 5. **Forgetting corner reinforcement in two-way slab** → IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.2 is mandatory.
> 6. **Curtailment without anchorage length** → Always provide full anchorage beyond the theoretical cut-off point.
> 7. **Confusing effective span for simply supported vs continuous** → Use $L_n + d$ for SS and c/c for continuous.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Slabs**

### A. Classification
- **One-way:** `Ly/Lx > 2` → designed as beam of unit width.
- **Two-way:** `Ly/Lx ≤ 2` → IS 456 / SP-16 coefficients.
- **Flat slab:** no beams; columns only; check punching shear.
- **Ribbed/Waffle:** reduce dead load; span > 6 m.
- **Cantilever:** top steel at fixed end; main steel at top.
- **Slab on grade:** soil-supported; minimum 100 mm thick; proper joints.

### B. Critical Numbers to Memorize (IS 456:2000)
- One-way simply supported L/d basic = **20**
- Two-way continuous L/d basic = **32**
- Min. steel (HYSD) = `0.12 bD / 100`
- Max. main bar spacing = `3d or 300 mm` (whichever is less)
- Max. dist. bar spacing = `5d or 450 mm` (whichever is less)
- Load factor for ULS = **1.5**
- Drop panel depth ≥ **L/10** for flat slab

### C. Common Design Path (Always Follow)
1. Compute loads (DL, LL) → Factored load $w_u = 1.5(DL+LL)$.
2. Compute design moment $M_u$ per m width.
3. **Check deflection first** (L/d ratio).
4. Compute $A_{st}$ from $M_u$.
5. Compare with $A_{st,min}$.
6. Choose bar diameter and spacing.
7. Check shear, development length, curtailment.
8. Provide corner reinforcement (two-way slabs).

### D. Safety-Critical Items (Most Often Forgotten)
- ✅ **Corner reinforcement** in two-way slabs (Cl. 26.5.3.2).
- ✅ **Punching shear** check in flat slabs (Cl. 31.6).
- ✅ **Torsion steel** at edge panels.
- ✅ **Anchorage** of main bars at simple supports.
- ✅ **Cover** to fire and durability requirements (Cl. 26.4).
- ✅ **Crack width** check using Table 15, IS 456 (Cl. 26.3.2).

### E. Keywords for KTU 14-Mark Answers
- "One-way slab designed as **rectangular beam of unit width**." (Cl. 24.1)
- "**Deflection governs the depth**, not bending." (Always quote Cl. 23.2.1)
- "**Minimum steel** prevents sudden brittle failure in cracked section." (Cl. 26.5.2.2)
- "**Corner reinforcement** prevents lifting of corners due to torsion." (Cl. 26.5.3.2)

> **End of Topic: Types of Slabs — Module 2**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Safety in Design of Structures (OESFT722).*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of One-Way Slabs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of One-Way Slabs # 🏗️ SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU/IS 456 Terminology) A **one-way slab** is a structural floor element, generally horizontal, of uniform thickness, whose plan aspect ratio is such that the load transfer occurs primarily in **one direction** (the shorter span direction) to the supporting beams or walls. As per the widely accepted classification aligned with IS 456:2000 (Clause 24.1), a slab behaves as a **one-way slab** when the ratio of the longer span to the shorter span (Ly/Lx) is **greater than or equal to 2.0**. The flexural reinforcement is therefore provided primarily along the short-span direction, while a secondary mesh known as **distribution steel** is placed in the transverse (long) direction to resist shrinkage, temperature effects, and to distribute concentrated loads. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Definition (IS 456, Cl. 24.1):** When Ly/Lx ≥ 2, the slab is designed as a one-way slab spanning in the X-direction (short span). Bending moments in the Y-direction are negligible and only nominal distribution steel is provided perpendicular to the main reinforcement. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wooden Plank on Two Walls" 🍽️ Imagine a **wooden plank** resting freely on **two parallel brick walls** in your kitchen, with empty space on both long sides. When you place a heavy utensil of, say, **50 kg** in the middle of the plank: 1. The plank **bends in only one direction** (perpendicular to the walls). 2. The load **travels along the shorter span** and reaches the two walls as reactions. 3. The plank does **NOT** bend sideways even though it has length on both sides — because the walls provide the shortest load path. This is exactly how a one-way slab behaves: - **Plank → One-way slab** - **Walls → Supporting beams / walls** - **Shorter span direction → Direction of primary bending & reinforcement** In contrast, a **two-way slab** would be like a tabletop resting on **all four edges** — the load splits and travels along **both** directions to all four edges. ## 1.3 Classification Criterion (Geometric Decision Rule) $$L_y / L_x \geq 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{One-Way Slab}$$ $$L_y / L_x < 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Two-Way Slab}$$ Where: - $L_x$ = **shorter span** (centre-to-centre distance between supports, in metres) - $L_y$ = **longer span** (centre-to-centre distance between supports, in metres) ## 1.4 Why Safety Demands a Different Design Approach for Slabs Slabs are the **first line of structural defence** against vertical loads in any RCC building. A failure here is catastrophic because: - Slabs support **all superimposed live loads** (occupants, furniture, equipment). - They form the **diaphragm** that transmits lateral (seismic/wind) forces to vertical elements. - They provide **fire separation** between floors — fire resistance is governed by minimum thickness and cover (Table 16A, IS 456). Hence, design must satisfy: - **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** — strength and stability. - **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** — deflection, cracking, vibration. - **Durability & Fire Safety** — minimum cover, minimum grade of concrete, minimum thickness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Safety Precept:** A slab is designed using the **Limit State Method** with partial safety factors — γf = **1.5** for both DL and LL (when combined), γm = **1.5** for concrete and **1.15** for steel. These multipliers convert characteristic loads/resistances into **design values**, building in the safety margin. --- # 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Design Philosophy — Limit State Method (Cl. 35, IS 456) The Limit State Method requires that the structure should satisfy two fundamental criteria: **State 1 — Limit State of Collapse (Strength):** $$\text{Design Action} \leq \text{Design Resistance}$$ $$\gamma_f \times \text{Load} \leq \frac{\text{Characteristic Strength}}{\gamma_m \times \text{Reduction Factor}}$$ **State 2 — Limit State of Serviceability:** The structure must remain functional under service loads — deflection ≤ span/250, crack width ≤ 0.3 mm (general), vibration limits, etc. ## 2.2 Design Assumptions (Cl. 22, IS 456) 1. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending (Bernoulli's hypothesis). 2. Maximum strain in concrete at the outermost compression fibre = **0.0035**. 3. **Tensile strength of concrete is ignored.** 4. The design stress-strain curve for steel is **bilinear** with characteristic yield stress $f_y$ and modulus $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². 5. The compressive stress distribution in concrete is **rectangular-parabolic** simplified to an **equivalent rectangular block** with depth = 0.42x and stress = 0.67$f_{ck}$ (for fck ≤ 50 MPa). ## 2.3 Span-to-Depth (L/d) Requirements for Deflection Control (Cl. 23.2.1) The **basic L/d ratio** governs minimum depth to control short-term and long-term deflection: | **Support Condition** | **Fe250** | **Fe415** | **Fe500** | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Simply Supported | 35 | **20** | 17 | | Continuous | 45 | **26** | 22 | | Cantilever | 12 | **7** | 6 | These values are then **modified** using modification factors from Fig. 4 (IS 456), based on: - Tension reinforcement percentage ($p_t$) - Service stress in steel ($\sigma_{st} = 0.58 f_y \times A_{st,req}/A_{st,prov}$) **Allowable Span/Effective Depth:** $$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{allow} = \left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{basic} \times k_1 \times k_2 \times k_3 \times k_4$$ Where $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$ are modification factors. ## 2.4 Effective Span (Cl. 22.2, IS 456) $$l_{eff} = \min\!\left(l_{clear} + d, \;\; l_{c/c}\right)$$ Where: - $l_{clear}$ = clear span (face-to-face of supports) - $d$ = effective depth of slab - $l_{c/c}$ = centre-to-centre distance between supports > For end-bearing conditions on masonry/concrete, $l_{eff}$ is taken as the clear span plus the effective depth OR the centre-to-centre of bearing — whichever is **smaller**. ## 2.5 Loading — Design Strip Concept A 1-metre wide strip (b = **1000 mm**) is considered for design: | **Load Component** | **Calculation** | **Unit** | |:---|:---|:---:| | Self-weight (slab) | $25 \times D_{total}$ | kN/m² | | Floor Finish (FF) | $\rho_{FF} \times t_{FF}$ | kN/m² | | Live Load (LL) | From IS 875 Part 2 (table) | kN/m² | | **Design Load, w** | $1.5 \times (DL + LL)$ | kN/m² | Where $D_{total}$ = overall depth of slab (in metres), **25 kN/m³** is unit weight of RCC. > [!NOTE] > **For the design strip of 1 m width, the loads (in kN/m²) act as UDL (kN/m) because they are multiplied by the strip width of 1 m.** This simplifies bending moment and shear force calculation. ## 2.6 Bending Moment & Shear Force Coefficients (Cl. 22.5, Table 12) For **simply supported slabs:** $$M_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}^{\,2}}{8}, \quad V_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}}{2}$$ For **continuous slabs**, Table 12 of IS 456 provides coefficients. The DL and LL are treated **separately** to obtain the worst-case envelope (pattern loading): ### Coefficients for Bending Moment (w·L²/x) | **End Condition** | **DL: +ve (near mid-span)** | **DL: -ve (at support)** | **LL: +ve (near mid-span)** | **LL: -ve (at support)** | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Simply supported | 1/8 | 0 | 1/8 | 0 | | Interior span | 1/12 | -1/24 | 1/10 | -1/10 | | End span, discontinuous end unrestrained | 1/11 | -1/10 | 1/9 | -1/9 | | End span, discontinuous end partially restrained | 1/12 | -1/12 | 1/10 | -1/10 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pattern Loading:** To find the **maximum positive moment** at any section, place the LL only on **alternate spans** (or the span under consideration). To find the **maximum negative moment**, place the LL on **adjacent spans**. This produces the design envelope. ### Coefficients for Shear Force (w·L) | **End Condition** | **At End Support** | **At Continuous End** | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Simply supported | 0.4 to 0.6 | — | | End span, discontinuous end unrestrained | 0.45 | 0.6 | | End span, discontinuous end restrained | 0.5 | 0.6 | ## 2.7 Design of Main Reinforcement (Flexure) The **cl. 38.1, IS 456** formula for balanced/under-reinforced rectangular section (no compression steel): $$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left(1 - \frac{A_{st} f_y}{f_{ck} \, b \, d}\right)$$ Solving for $A_{st}$: $$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] b \, d$$ Or, in coefficient form using Table 12 (SP-16 design aids): $$A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, \left(d - k \cdot \frac{a}{2}\right)}$$ where $a = \frac{A_{st} f_y}{0.36 f_{ck} b}$ (depth of NA block), $k = 0.42$. ### 2.7.1 Limiting Moment of Resistance $$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \;\; (\text{for Fe415})$$ If $M_u > M_{u,lim}$, **increase depth** — slabs are not provided with compression steel. ### 2.7.2 Minimum Reinforcement (Cl. 26.5.2.1) $$A_{st,min} = \frac{0.85 \, b \, d}{f_y} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{For Fe415: } 0.12\% \text{ of } b d$$ This minimum ensures the section is ductile and prevents sudden brittle failure. ### 2.7.3 Maximum Spacing of Main Bars (Cl. 26.3.3) $$s_{max} = \min(3d, \; 300 \text{ mm})$$ ## 2.8 Shear Design (Cl. 40, IS 456) Nominal shear stress: $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \, d}$$ Design shear strength of concrete (no shear reinforcement, Table 19): $$\tau_c = f(p_t, \; f_{ck}) \quad \text{where} \quad p_t = \frac{100 \, A_{st}}{b \, d}$$ **Slab Design Rule (Cl. 40.2):** - If $\tau_v \leq \tau_c$ → **No shear reinforcement needed** (slab is safe in shear). - If $\tau_v > \tau_c$ → **Increase depth** (since shear reinforcement is not provided in slabs). > [!WARNING] > **Maximum Shear Stress (Table 20, IS 456):** $\tau_{v,max}$ for M20 concrete = **2.8 N/mm²**. If $\tau_v$ exceeds this, the section dimensions must be increased regardless of $\tau_c$. ## 2.9 Distribution Reinforcement (Cl. 26.5.2.2) For slabs with main reinforcement parallel to the span: $$A_{sd,min} = 0.12\% \text{ of gross cross-sectional area (for Fe415)}$$ - For Fe500: 0.15% - For Fe250: 0.20% **Maximum spacing:** $\min(5d, \; 450 \text{ mm})$ ## 2.10 Nominal Cover & Fire Resistance (Cl. 26.4 & Table 16A) | **Exposure Condition** | **Nominal Cover** | |:---|:---:| | Mild | 20 mm | | Moderate | **30 mm** | | Severe | 45 mm | | Very Severe | 50 mm | | Extreme | 75 mm | For fire resistance of **1 hour** in slabs, the effective cover to main steel ≥ **20 mm** (and additional thickness-based requirements apply). ## 2.11 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet 📋 | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Reference** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | Effective Span | $l_{eff} = \min(l_{clear}+d, \; l_{c/c})$ | Cl. 22.2 | | 2 | Basic L/d (Simply Sup., Fe415) | 20 | Cl. 23.2.1 | | 3 | Basic L/d (Continuous, Fe415) | 26 | Cl. 23.2.1 | | 4 | Design Load | $w_u = 1.5(DL + LL)$ | Cl. 35.3 | | 5 | Max BM (Simply Sup.) | $w_u L^2/8$ | Cl. 22.5 | | 6 | Max SF (Simply Sup.) | $w_u L/2$ | Cl. 22.5 | | 7 | Limiting $M_u$ (Fe415) | $0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ | SP-16 | | 8 | Area of Steel | $0.5 \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y}[1-\sqrt{1-4.6M_u/(f_{ck}bd^2)}]bd$ | Cl. 38.1 | | 9 | Min $A_{st}$ (Fe415) | $0.85 b d/f_y$ | Cl. 26.5.2.1 | | 10 | Nominal Shear Stress | $\tau_v = V_u/(bd)$ | Cl. 40.1 | | 11 | Max Spacing (main bars) | $\min(3d, \; 300 \text{ mm})$ | Cl. 26.3.3 | | 12 | Distribution Steel (Fe415) | 0.12% of gross area | Cl. 26.5.2.2 | | 13 | Max Spacing (dist. bars) | $\min(5d, \; 450 \text{ mm})$ | Cl. 26.5.2.2 | --- # 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Worked Numerical Solution ## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Span The **effective span** is the span over which bending moment is computed. IS 456 specifies it as follows: $$l_{eff} = \min\!\left(l_{clear} + d, \;\; l_{c/c}\right)$$ **Physical reasoning:** - The slab bends like a beam supported on the two bearing faces. - The bending moment curve is essentially zero just beyond the face of support, so adding **one effective depth (d)** on each side gives a reasonable span estimate. - However, if supports are far apart (centre-to-centre larger than clear+d), the simplified engineering model uses the c/c distance to prevent over-design. ## 3.2 Derivation of Area of Steel (Cl. 38.1) Starting from the force and moment equilibrium of a rectangular singly-reinforced section under flexure: **Force equilibrium** (compressive force = tensile force): $$0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$$ Solving for the neutral axis depth: $$x_u = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b} = \frac{2.417 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{f_{ck} \, b}$$ **Moment equilibrium** (taking moments about the centroid of tension steel): $$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot \left(d - 0.42 \, x_u\right)$$ Substituting $x_u$: $$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot d \left(1 - \frac{0.42 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{f_{ck} \, b \, d}\right)$$ Let $p = \frac{A_{st}}{b d}$. Rearranging: $$\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = \frac{p \, f_y}{f_{ck}}\left[2 - \frac{2 p \, f_y}{f_{ck}}\right]$$ Setting $Q = p f_y / f_{ck}$ and solving the quadratic $Q^2 - Q + \frac{2.3 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} = 0$: $$\boxed{\;A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] b \, d\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The term under the square root must be **positive and non-zero**; otherwise, the section is **over-reinforced**, requiring depth increase. ## 3.3 Curtailment of Bars (Cl. 26.2.3.2) **50% of bars** may be curtailed at a distance of $d$ or $L/10$ (whichever is greater) from the face of the support. This safety provision prevents: - Sudden shear failure at supports. - Excess reinforcement in regions of low bending moment (saving steel). - Concentration of cracks. ## 3.4 Lap Length & Anchorage (Cl. 26.2.5, 26.2.1) - **Tension lap length** = $L_d$ (development length) = $\frac{\phi \, \sigma_s}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ where $\tau_{bd}$ is bond stress (Table 26). - **For slabs in tension**, lap length = $L_d$ (min). - Anchorage: bars must extend by $L_d / 3$ beyond the critical section at simple supports. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark):** > Design a simply supported one-way slab for a residential building with the following data: > - Clear span: **3.5 m**, support width = **230 mm** > - Live load = **3 kN/m²**, floor finish = **1 kN/m²** > - Materials: M20 concrete, Fe415 steel > - Exposure: Moderate ### Step 1 — Slab Classification Assume $L_x \approx 3.5 \text{ m} + 0.23 = 3.73 \text{ m}$, and if $L_y \geq 2 \times 3.73 = 7.46 \text{ m}$, then **one-way slab**. ### Step 2 — Adopt Depth & Effective Span Try $D = 120 \text{ mm}$ (assumed). Effective cover = **30 mm** (moderate exposure, assuming 8 mm bars). $$d = 120 - 30 - 4 = 86 \text{ mm}$$ Effective span: $$l_{eff} = \min(l_{clear} + d, \; l_{c/c}) = \min(3500 + 86, \; 3500 + 230) = \min(3586, 3730)$$ $$\boxed{l_{eff} = 3586 \text{ mm} = 3.586 \text{ m}}$$ ### Step 3 — Deflection Check (Cl. 23.2.1) $$\frac{L}{d} = \frac{3586}{86} = 41.7$$ Basic value for Fe415 simply supported = **20**. With modification factors $k_1 \approx 1.2$, $k_2 \approx 1.0$, $k_3 \approx 1.0$: $$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{allow} = 20 \times 1.2 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 24$$ Since $41.7 > 24$, depth is **INSUFFICIENT**. Increase depth. ### Step 4 — Revised Depth Try $D = 160 \text{ mm}$, $d = 160 - 30 - 4 = 126 \text{ mm}$: $$\frac{L}{d} = \frac{3586}{126} = 28.46$$ $$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{allow} \approx 28 \text{ (with k}_1 = 1.4\text{)}$$ Acceptable — adopt **D = 160 mm, d = 126 mm**. ### Step 5 — Load Calculation (per 1 m strip) | **Component** | **Calculation** | **Value (kN/m²)** | |:---|:---|:---:| | Self-weight (slab) | $25 \times 0.16$ | 4.00 | | Floor finish | Given | 1.00 | | **Total DL** | | **5.00** | | **LL** | Given | **3.00** | | **Working Load** | $DL + LL$ | 8.00 | | **Design Load** $w_u$ | $1.5 \times 8$ | **12.00** | ### Step 6 — Bending Moment $$M_u = \frac{w_u \, l_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{12 \times 3.586^2}{8} = 19.29 \text{ kN·m}$$ ### Step 7 — Check Limiting Moment $$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 126^2 \times 10^{-6} = 43.83 \text{ kN·m}$$ Since $19.29 < 43.83$, section is **safe and under-reinforced** ✓ ### Step 8 — Area of Main Steel $$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 19.29 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 126^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 126$$ Computing term under square root: $$\frac{4.6 \times 19.29 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 126^2} = \frac{88.74 \times 10^6}{317.52 \times 10^6} = 0.2795$$ $$A_{st} = \frac{10}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.2795}\right] \times 126000 = 0.0241 \times \sqrt{0.7205} \times 126000$$ $$\sqrt{0.7205} \approx 0.849 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 1 - 0.849 = 0.151$$ $$A_{st} = 0.0241 \times 0.151 \times 126000 \approx 458 \text{ mm}^2$$ ### Step 9 — Minimum Steel Check $$A_{st,min} = \frac{0.85 \times 1000 \times 126}{415} = 258 \text{ mm}^2$$ Since $458 > 258$, the design $A_{st}$ governs. ### Step 10 — Spacing of 10 mm Bars Area of one 10 mm bar = $\pi/4 \times 10^2 = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2$ $$\text{Spacing} = \frac{1000 \times 78.54}{458} = 171.5 \text{ mm}$$ Provide **10 mm @ 165 mm c/c** (rounded down to standard spacing). Check max spacing: $\min(3 \times 126, 300) = 300 \text{ mm}$. OK ✓ ### Step 11 — Shear Check $$V_u = \frac{w_u l_{eff}}{2} = \frac{12 \times 3.586}{2} = 21.52 \text{ kN}$$ $$\tau_v = \frac{21.52 \times 1000}{1000 \times 126} = 0.171 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $p_t = \frac{100 \times A_{st}}{b d} = \frac{100 \times 458}{1000 \times 126} = 0.363\%$ For M20, $p_t = 0.36\%$: $\tau_c \approx 0.39 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (interpolated from Table 19) Since $\tau_v = 0.171 < \tau_c = 0.39 < \tau_{c,max} = 2.8$, **shear is safe** ✓ — No shear reinforcement needed. ### Step 12 — Distribution Steel $$A_{sd,min} = 0.12\% \text{ of } b \times D = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 160 = 192 \text{ mm}^2$$ Provide **8 mm @ 250 mm c/c** (parallel to longer span). - Area of one 8 mm bar = $50.27 \text{ mm}^2$ - Spacing = $\frac{1000 \times 50.27}{192} = 261 \text{ mm}$, use **250 mm c/c** - Max spacing check: $\min(5 \times 126, 450) = 450 \text{ mm}$ → OK ✓ ### Step 13 — Detailing & Safety Provisions - **Cover**: 30 mm (moderate exposure) - **Curtailment**: 50% bars may be curtailed at $L/5$ or $d$ from support face. - **Development length**: Bars must extend at least $L_d/3$ into supports. - **Fire resistance**: Cover ≥ 20 mm satisfies 1-hour fire rating for slabs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Detailing:** Main steel: 10 mm ϕ @ 165 mm c/c (bottom, along short span); Distribution steel: 8 mm ϕ @ 250 mm c/c (top/bottom). Concrete grade M20, Fe415, cover 30 mm. --- # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — One-Way Slab Load Path ```mermaid flowchart TD A[VERTICAL LOADS
Dead Load + Live Load] --> B[ONE WAY SLAB

AND SECONDARY EFFECTS] C --> E[SUPPORTING BEAM 1
Along Ly direction] C --> F[SUPPORTING BEAM 2
Along Ly direction] E --> G[COLUMNS / WALLS] F --> G G --> H[FOUNDATION] G --> I[GROUND TRANSFER] ``` ## 4.2 Design Workflow — Limit State Method ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[START: Geometry and Material Selection] --> S2{Determine Ly/Lx Ratio} S2 -->|Ly greater than or equal to 2 Lx| S3[One Way Slab Confirmed] S2 -->|Ly less than 2 Lx| STOP1[Two Way Slab Design Out of Scope] S3 --> S4[Assume Overall Depth D] S4 --> S5[Compute Effective Depth d] S5 --> S6[Calculate Effective Span Leff] S6 --> S7[Compute Loads: DL LL] S7 --> S8[Design Load Wu = 1.5 DL plus LL] S8 --> S9[Compute Mu using Table 12 Coefficients] S9 --> S10[Check L/d Ratio for Deflection] S10 -->|L/d exceeds limit| S4 S10 -->|L/d OK| S11[Check Mu less than Mu lim] S11 -->|Exceeds| S4 S11 -->|Within| S12[Compute Ast Main Steel] S12 --> S13[Check Ast greater than Ast min] S13 --> S14[Check Shear: tau v less than tau c] S14 -->|Exceeds| S4 S14 -->|OK| S15[Compute Distribution Steel] S15 --> S16[Detailing: Curtailment Anchorage Cover Fire] S16 --> S17[FINAL SLAB DESIGN] ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Reinforcement Layout (Schematic) ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:slab L["Clear Span Leff Lx"] end block:reinforcement M["MAIN BARS: 10 mm @ 165 mm c/c

Larger Support Moments] ``` ## 4.5 Limit State Design Flowchart — Safety Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR LS1[Limit State of Collapse] --> LSA[Flexural Strength Mu] LS1 --> LSB[Shear Strength tau c] LS1 --> LSC[Bond and Anchorage Ld] LS2[Limit State of Serviceability] --> LSD[Deflection L less than d] LS2 --> LSE[Crack Width less than 0.3 mm] LS2 --> LSF[Vibration Limits] LS3[Durability] --> LSG[Concrete Grade and Cover] LS3 --> LSH[Minimum Cement Content] LS4[Fire Safety] --> LSI[Cover Table 16A] LS4 --> LSJ[Minimum Thickness] ``` --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Style]** ### Question 1: Define a one-way slab and state the criterion for its classification. Mention the role of distribution steel. **Model Answer:** A one-way slab is a structural element whose plan aspect ratio satisfies $L_y/L_x \geq 2$, so that the load is transferred predominantly along the shorter span $L_x$ to the supporting beams. Distribution steel (perpendicular to main reinforcement) is provided with minimum area = **0.12% of gross cross-sectional area for Fe415** (Cl. 26.5.2.2) to resist shrinkage, temperature effects, and to distribute concentrated loads. *(3 marks — Cl. 24.1 and 26.5.2.2, IS 456)* --- ### Question 2: List the basic span-to-depth ratios for one-way slabs per IS 456, for Fe415 steel. **Model Answer:** Per Cl. 23.2.1, IS 456 (for Fe415): | **Support Condition** | **Basic L/d Ratio** | |:---|:---:| | Cantilever | 7 | | Simply supported | **20** | | Continuous | **26** | These are further modified using factors from Fig. 4 (IS 456) based on $p_t$ and $\sigma_{st}$. *(3 marks — Cl. 23.2.1, IS 456)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Pattern, Module 2]** ### Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented Problem **(a)** *Design a simply supported one-way slab for a room with clear span of 4 m supported on 230 mm wide masonry walls. Take live load = 4 kN/m², floor finish = 1.5 kN/m². Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Assume moderate exposure.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Check the slab designed in part (a) for deflection and shear per IS 456, and provide the reinforcement detailing with curtailment and anchorage details.* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Question A(a) **Step 1 — Depth Assumption & Effective Span** Assume $D = 150 \text{ mm}$, with 10 mm bars and 30 mm cover → $d = 150 - 30 - 5 = 115 \text{ mm}$. $$l_{eff} = \min(4000 + 115, \; 4000 + 230) = 4115 \text{ mm} \approx 4.115 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Loads** - DL: Self-weight = $25 \times 0.15 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$; FF = $1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$ → **Total DL = 5.25 kN/m²** - LL = $4 \text{ kN/m}^2$ - Design load $w_u = 1.5 \times (5.25 + 4) = 13.875 \text{ kN/m}^2$ **Step 3 — Bending Moment** $$M_u = \frac{13.875 \times 4.115^2}{8} = 29.37 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4 — Limiting Moment** $$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 115^2 \times 10^{-6} = 36.50 \text{ kN·m}$$ Since $29.37 < 36.50$, section is safe and under-reinforced. **Step 5 — Main Steel** $$\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 29.37 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 115^2} = 0.510$$ $$A_{st} = \frac{10}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.510}\right] \times 1000 \times 115$$ $$\sqrt{0.490} = 0.700 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 - 0.700 = 0.300$$ $$A_{st} = 0.0241 \times 0.300 \times 115000 = 832 \text{ mm}^2$$ Check $A_{st,min} = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 115 / 415 = 235 \text{ mm}^2$. OK. **Step 6 — Spacing of 12 mm Bars** Area of one 12 mm bar = $113.1 \text{ mm}^2$ $$s = \frac{1000 \times 113.1}{832} = 136 \text{ mm}$$ **Provide 12 mm ϕ @ 130 mm c/c.** #### Model Solution — Question A(b) **Step 7 — Deflection Check** $$\frac{L}{d} = \frac{4115}{115} = 35.78$$ Basic value = 20 (simply supported). With $p_t = \frac{100 \times 832}{1000 \times 115} = 0.723\%$ and $k_1 \approx 1.6$ (from Fig. 4): $$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{allow} = 20 \times 1.6 = 32$$ Since $35.78 > 32$, **depth is INSUFFICIENT** — revise to $D = 170 \text{ mm}$, $d = 135 \text{ mm}$. **Step 8 — Revised Calculations** $L/d = 4115/135 = 30.5$, allow $\approx 30$ → Acceptable. $M_u = 13.875 \times 4.215^2 / 8 = 30.83 \text{ kN·m}$ $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 135^2 \times 10^{-6} = 50.30 \text{ kN·m}$ → OK. $A_{st} = 870 \text{ mm}^2$ → Provide **12 mm ϕ @ 130 mm c/c**. **Step 9 — Shear Check** $V_u = 13.875 \times 4.215 / 2 = 29.24 \text{ kN}$ $\tau_v = 29240 / (1000 \times 135) = 0.217 \text{ N/mm}^2$ $\tau_c \approx 0.41 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (for $p_t = 0.64\%$, M20). $\tau_c,max = 2.8 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Since $\tau_v < \tau_c$, **no shear reinforcement required** ✓ **Step 10 — Distribution Steel** $A_{sd,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 170 = 204 \text{ mm}^2$ Provide **8 mm ϕ @ 240 mm c/c** ($A_{sd} = 209 \text{ mm}^2$). **Step 11 — Detailing Summary** - **Main Steel:** 12 mm ϕ @ 130 mm c/c (bottom, short span) - **Distribution Steel:** 8 mm ϕ @ 240 mm c/c (top, long span) - **Cover:** 30 mm (moderate exposure) - **Curtailment:** Curtail 50% alternate bars at $L/5 = 0.843 \text{ m}$ from support face - **Anchorage:** Bars extend 75 mm into support with 90° hook (≥ $L_d/3$) - **Lap Length:** 50ϕ = 600 mm (alternatively staggered, not at midspan) - **Fire Resistance:** Cover ≥ 20 mm → Satisfies 1-hour fire rating. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + Design Alternative **(a)** *Explain the bending moment and shear force coefficients for continuous one-way slabs as per Table 12 of IS 456. Discuss pattern loading and its importance in design.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Design a continuous one-way slab over three equal spans of 4 m each, supported on 300 mm wide beams. Take live load = 3 kN/m², floor finish = 1 kN/m². Use M25 concrete and Fe415 steel.* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Question B(a) — Outline 1. **Definition of Table 12 coefficients** — span moment and shear coefficients based on end conditions (Cl. 22.5, IS 456). 2. **Five standard cases:** simply supported, end span with discontinuous end unrestrained, end span with discontinuous end restrained, interior span, cantilever. 3. **Coefficients tabulated** for DL (all spans loaded) and LL (pattern loaded). 4. **Pattern Loading:** - For **maximum positive moment** at a span → load that span and alternate spans. - For **maximum negative moment** at a support → load spans on **both sides**. - Live load is **moved** to produce worst-case envelope. 5. **Engineering Importance:** Reduces over-design by capturing true envelope; ensures safe but economical design. *(7 marks — Cl. 22.5, IS 456)* #### Model Solution — Question B(b) — Sketch For 3 spans × 4 m, end span (one end continuous, one discontinuous unrestrained), and middle interior span (continuous on both ends): **Step 1 — Adopt Depth** $l_{eff}$ for interior span = $\min(4000 + d, \; 4000 + 300) \approx 4000 + d$ For end span = $\min(4000 + d, \; 4000 + 300) \approx 4000 + d$ Try $D = 175 \text{ mm}$, $d = 140 \text{ mm}$. $l_{eff} = 4140 \text{ mm} = 4.14 \text{ m}$. **Step 2 — Loads** - DL (slab) = $25 \times 0.175 = 4.375 \text{ kN/m}^2$ - FF = $1.00 \text{ kN/m}^2$ - **DL total = 5.375 kN/m²**, LL = 3 kN/m² - $w_u = 1.5 \times (5.375 + 3) = 12.56 \text{ kN/m}^2$ **Step 3 — Maximum Moments (using Table 12 coefficients)** For end span: - Max +ve moment (LL on end + alternate) = $w_u L^2 / 9 = 12.56 \times 4.14^2 / 9 = 23.95 \text{ kN·m}$ - Max −ve at continuous end = $w_u L^2 / 9 = 23.95 \text{ kN·m}$ For interior span: - Max +ve = $w_u L^2 / 10 = 12.56 \times 4.14^2 / 10 = 21.53 \text{ kN·m}$ - Max −ve at supports = $w_u L^2 / 10 = 21.53 \text{ kN·m}$ **Design moment** = max of above = **23.95 kN·m**. **Step 4 — Ast Calculation** $M_{u,lim}$ (M25, Fe415) = $0.138 \times 25 \times 1000 \times 140^2 \times 10^{-6} = 67.62 \text{ kN·m}$ → OK. $$\frac{4.6 \times 23.95 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 140^2} = 0.225$$ $$A_{st} = \frac{12.5}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.225}\right] \times 1000 \times 140$$ $$\sqrt{0.775} = 0.880 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 - 0.880 = 0.120$$ $$A_{st} = 0.0301 \times 0.120 \times 140000 = 506 \text{ mm}^2$$ Provide **10 mm ϕ @ 150 mm c/c** at midspan (positive moment zone). At continuous supports (negative moment), provide additional top bars: **10 mm ϕ @ 200 mm c/c** with adequate anchorage beyond point of inflection. **Step 5 — Deflection Check** $L/d = 4140/140 = 29.6$; basic = 26 (continuous); $k_1 \approx 1.2$ → allow = 31.2. **OK** ✓ **Step 6 — Shear Check** $V_u$ at end support = $0.4 w_u L = 0.4 \times 12.56 \times 4.14 = 20.80 \text{ kN}$ $\tau_v = 20800/(1000 \times 140) = 0.149 \text{ N/mm}^2$ → Safe ✓ **Step 7 — Distribution Steel** $A_{sd,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 175 = 210 \text{ mm}^2$ Provide **8 mm ϕ @ 230 mm c/c**. *(7 marks — Full IS 456 design procedure)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas in One-Way Slab Design:** > 1. **Effective span calculation** — students often use $l_{c/c}$ directly without considering $l_{clear} + d$; remember to take the **smaller** value. > 2. **Deflection check ignored** — even if $A_{st}$ is sufficient, $L/d$ must be checked separately; this is a **serviceability** check, not a strength check. > 3. **Pattern loading for continuous slabs** — forgetting that DL and LL are **separately** applied leads to under-estimation of negative moments at continuous supports. > 4. **Distribution steel** — minimum 0.12% of gross area is frequently missed; this causes **durability failure** even in a "safe" slab. > 5. **Curtailment rules** — curtailment at $L/5$ (or $d$, whichever is greater) is often violated; bars continued past midspan lead to inefficient design. > 6. **Lap length in tension** — full lap length $L_d$ must be provided, not the compressed lap. Stagger laps, avoid midspan. > 7. **Cover for fire & durability** — students sometimes use 20 mm blindly; **moderate exposure = 30 mm**, severe = 45 mm. Fire safety mandates Table 16A compliance. > 8. **No shear reinforcement in slabs** — students add shear stirrups; the correct approach is to **increase depth** if $\tau_v > \tau_c$. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember | **#** | **Key Point** | **Reference** | |:---:|:---|:---:| | 1 | One-way slab when $L_y / L_x \geq 2$ | Cl. 24.1 | | 2 | $l_{eff} = \min(l_{clear} + d, \; l_{c/c})$ | Cl. 22.2 | | 3 | Basic L/d for Fe415 = 20 (SS) / 26 (Cont.) | Cl. 23.2.1 | | 4 | Design load: $1.5(DL + LL)$ | Cl. 35.3 | | 5 | DL and LL applied **separately** for envelope | Cl. 22.5 | | 6 | Max spacing of main bars = $\min(3d, \; 300 \text{ mm})$ | Cl. 26.3.3 | | 7 | Min $A_{st} = 0.85 bd/f_y$ (Fe415) = 0.12% bd | Cl. 26.5.2.1 | | 8 | Distribution steel = 0.12% of gross area (Fe415) | Cl. 26.5.2.2 | | 9 | Distribution bar spacing $\leq \min(5d, \; 450 \text{ mm})$ | Cl. 26.5.2.2 | | 10 | Curtail 50% bars at $L/5$ or $d$ from support | Cl. 26.2.3.2 | | 11 | Anchorage length ≥ $L_d/3$ into simple supports | Cl. 26.2.1 | | 12 | Slab shear design → increase depth, NOT stirrups | Cl. 40.2 | | 13 | Nominal cover (moderate exposure) = 30 mm | Cl. 26.4 | | 14 | Fire resistance ≥ 1 hr → cover ≥ 20 mm (slab) | Table 16A | | 15 | Pattern loading to obtain max +ve / −ve moments | Cl. 22.5 | | 16 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe415) | SP-16 | | 17 | $\tau_{c,max}$ for M20 = 2.8 N/mm² | Table 20 | | 18 | Section must be **under-reinforced** for ductility | Cl. 38.1 | > **Final Safety Mantra:** *A one-way slab, though simple in geometry, is a critical safety element. Every parameter — from effective span and depth to curtailment and cover — must be computed with rigor, because a slab failure has direct consequences on life safety, fire integrity, and structural robustness.* ---

topics.title: Temperature and Shrinkage Reinforcement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Temperature and Shrinkage Reinforcement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Temperature and Shrinkage Reinforcement (T&S Steel)** is a category of *secondary or distribution reinforcement* provided in reinforced concrete (RCC) members — particularly in slabs, walls, floors, roofs, and pavements — to resist and control cracking caused by **volumetric changes** in concrete arising from thermal movements and moisture-related shrinkage.

Per **IS 456:2000, Clause 26.5**, this reinforcement is mandatory in *all directions* where structural analysis does not specifically require tension steel, and is designed on the basis of:
- **Minimum area of steel** as a percentage of gross cross-sectional area
- **Maximum permissible spacing** of bars to ensure crack width control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (IS 456:2000, Cl. 26.5.1 & 26.5.1.1):**
> The minimum reinforcement in *each direction* of a slab shall be:
> - **0.12%** of the gross cross-sectional area for **HYSD bars** (Fe415, Fe500)
> - **0.15%** of the gross cross-sectional area for **Mild Steel bars** (Fe250)
>
> This rule applies *irrespective* of whether the member is a structural slab, a non-structural slab, or even a roof slab carrying no calculated load.

### 1.2 Why Does Concrete Crack Due to T & S?

Concrete, although strong in compression, is **weak in tension** (approximately 1/10th of its compressive strength). Two phenomena generate **tensile stresses inside concrete**:

| Phenomenon | Cause | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal movement** | Temperature rise/fall on restrained members | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ |
| **Drying shrinkage** | Loss of free water during hydration and drying | $\varepsilon_{sh} \approx 200\text{ to } 1000 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| **Autogenous shrinkage** | Internal consumption of water during cement hydration | $\varepsilon_{au} \approx 40 \text{ to } 100 \times 10^{-6}$ |

When these strains are **restrained** (by adjoining members, foundations, or reinforcement itself), tensile stresses develop. If the stress exceeds the **modulus of rupture** ($f_{cr} \approx 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$), concrete cracks.

### 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — The "Tea-Cup Effect"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Train Track Principle**
>
> Imagine a long stretch of **railway track** laid continuously without gaps. On a hot summer day, the rails expand. Since they are restrained at the ends, they **buckle sideways** (the famous "sun kink" derailment).
>
> To prevent this, railway engineers deliberately **cut small gaps** (expansion joints) at regular intervals — but they *also* place **side anchors** that hold the rails firmly so any small expansion is dissipated as controlled stress rather than a violent buckle.
>
> **T&S reinforcement in concrete works exactly like these anchors.** The concrete wants to shrink and contract; the steel holds the cracks *closed* and *narrow*, distributing the strain over many hairline cracks instead of one large, dangerous crack.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Material Properties

- **Coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete, $\alpha_c$** $\approx$ **$10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C** (with aggregate-dependent variation of 6 to 12 × 10⁻⁶)
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion of steel, $\alpha_s$** $\approx$ **$12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C** (very close to concrete → compatible behaviour)
- **Modulus of elasticity of concrete** $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm²
- **Drying shrinkage strain** of plain concrete: **$300 \text{ to } 700 \times 10^{-6}$** (typical value **$500 \times 10^{-6}$**)
- **Modulus of rupture of concrete** $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm²

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Principal Sources of Volume Change

#### A. Temperature-Induced Strain
A change in temperature causes a **free thermal strain** in the member. If the member is **fully restrained**, the induced thermal stress is:

$$\sigma_T = E_c \cdot \alpha_c \cdot \Delta T$$

**Example:** For M25 concrete, $E_c = 5000\sqrt{25} = 25{,}000$ N/mm², with $\Delta T = 20°C$:

$$\sigma_T = 25{,}000 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 = 5.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This is **greater** than the tensile strength of M25 concrete ($f_{cr} = 3.5$ N/mm²) → **cracking is inevitable without reinforcement**.

#### B. Shrinkage-Induced Strain
As concrete cures and dries, it loses water. The cement paste contracts. Aggregate and reinforcement restrain this contraction, generating **tensile stresses in the paste and bond stresses at the steel interface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Distribution Steel Must Run in BOTH Directions**
