> Shrinkage occurs **isotropically** (in all directions) — so the restraint force acts in *all* directions. Hence, T&S reinforcement is required in **two orthogonal directions** for slabs, and both **horizontally and vertically** for walls.

### 2.2 Logic of Minimum Reinforcement (Conceptual Steps)

The **why** behind 0.12% of gross area is a classic **equilibrium + crack-control logic**:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the cracked region:** When concrete cracks, it transfers its tensile force to the steel at the crack location.
2. **Step 2 — Equate force before and after cracking:** Force in uncracked concrete $\approx$ Force in steel at the crack.
3. **Step 3 — Bound the steel stress:** Limit $f_s$ to a safe value (≈ 0.8 $f_y$) so that crack width remains within serviceable limits.
4. **Step 4 — Solve for minimum steel area** required to absorb the released tensile force over the entire gross section.
5. **Step 5 — Empirically calibrate** the result against extensive experimental data → IS Code adopts **0.12% (HYSD)** as the codified minimum.

### 2.3 IS 456:2000 Provisions — Comprehensive Summary

| Member Type | Code Clause | Min. Reinforcement (HYSD) | Min. Reinforcement (Mild Steel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Slabs (each direction)** | 26.5.1.1 | **0.12%** of gross c/s area | **0.15%** of gross c/s area |
| **Beams (web / side face)** | 26.5.1.3 | 0.12% of web area | 0.15% of web area |
| **Walls (vertical)** | 32.7.1 | 0.12% of gross c/s area | 0.15% of gross c/s area |
| **Walls (horizontal)** | 32.7.1 | **0.20%** of gross c/s area | **0.25%** of gross c/s area |

### 2.4 Maximum Spacing Rules (IS 456:2000, Cl. 26.3.3)

| Type of Bar | Maximum Spacing |
|---|---|
| Main reinforcement in slabs | $\min(3d, \text{ 300 mm})$ |
| Secondary / distribution steel in slabs | $\min(5d, \text{ 450 mm})$ |

Where $d$ = effective depth of the member.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \cdot b \cdot D$ | Min. T&S steel (HYSD) for slabs | mm² |
| 2 | $A_{st,min} = 0.0015 \cdot b \cdot D$ | Min. T&S steel (Mild Steel) for slabs | mm² |
| 3 | $A_{sv} = 0.0020 \cdot b \cdot D$ | Horizontal wall steel (HYSD) | mm² |
| 4 | $s = \dfrac{1000 \cdot a_{bar}}{A_{st,req}}$ | Spacing for required area per metre width | mm |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha_c \cdot \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | — |
| 6 | $\sigma_T = E_c \cdot \alpha_c \cdot \Delta T$ | Fully-restrained thermal stress | N/mm² |
| 7 | $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Modulus of rupture of concrete | N/mm² |
| 8 | $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Modulus of elasticity of concrete | N/mm² |
| 9 | $a_{bar} = \dfrac{\pi \phi^2}{4}$ | Area of one bar of diameter $\phi$ | mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** T&S reinforcement is critical in:
> - **Roof slabs and exposed floors** of industrial buildings
> - **Mass concrete structures** (dams, raft foundations) where heat of hydration causes high internal temperatures
> - **Pavements and runways** subjected to direct solar radiation
> - **Chimneys, silos, water tanks** with exposed surfaces

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why 0.12% of Gross Area?

We consider a **unit strip** (1 m wide) of a slab of overall depth $D$. Assume the member is **fully restrained** and a uniform tensile stress equal to the modulus of rupture, $f_{cr}$, exists in the concrete just before cracking.

**Step 1** — Total tensile force in the concrete *just before* cracking (per 1 m width):

$$T_c = f_{cr} \cdot b \cdot D = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}} \cdot 1000 \cdot D$$

**Step 2** — When a crack forms, this entire force must be transferred to the steel crossing the crack:

$$T_s = A_{st,min} \cdot f_s$$

where $f_s$ is the **service stress in steel** (limited to a safe value to control crack width).

**Step 3** — Equating forces and rearranging for $A_{st,min}$:

$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}} \cdot 1000 \cdot D}{f_s}$$

**Step 4** — Substituting typical values for **M20 concrete** ($f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm²) and HYSD bars with $f_s$ limited to 0.5 $f_y$ (i.e., 207.5 N/mm²) to keep crack widths within limits:

$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.7 \times \sqrt{20} \times 1000 \times D}{207.5} = \dfrac{0.7 \times 4.472 \times 1000 \cdot D}{207.5}$$

$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{3130.5 \cdot D}{207.5} \approx 15.1 \cdot D \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 5** — Compare with the IS Code empirical value:

$$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times D = 1.2 \cdot D \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

The Code's value is **conservative** (about 12× lower) because:
- Concrete does not remain fully elastic in tension
- Shrinkage and thermal stresses are *not* additive to full magnitude simultaneously
- Partial cracking is *acceptable* — the goal is **crack control**, not crack elimination

> [!NOTE]
> The empirical 0.12% reflects a **calibrated compromise** between structural safety, economy, and acceptable crack widths under service conditions — a standard engineering Code-philosophy in limit-state design.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Design of T&S Steel in a Solid Slab

**Problem Statement:**
> Design the **temperature and shrinkage reinforcement** for a one-way solid RCC roof slab of clear dimensions **3.5 m × 6.0 m**, with an overall depth $D = 150$ mm, using **Fe415 HYSD bars**. Effective cover = 20 mm. The slab is exposed to atmospheric conditions.

**Given Data:**
- Overall depth: $D = 150$ mm
- Effective cover: $c = 20$ mm
- Effective depth: $d = D - c - \phi/2 \approx 150 - 20 - 4 = 126$ mm (assuming 8 mm bars)
- Grade of steel: Fe415 → **HYSD bar** → Use **0.12%** of gross area (per IS 456 Cl 26.5.1.1)
- Strip width: $b = 1000$ mm (per metre width)

**Step 1 — Compute minimum T&S steel required per metre width:**

$$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times b \times D$$

$$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 150 = 180 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Choose trial bar diameter and compute spacing:**

Try **8 mm diameter** bars (area $a_{bar} = \dfrac{\pi \times 8^2}{4} = 50.27$ mm²)

$$s = \dfrac{1000 \cdot a_{bar}}{A_{st,min}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 50.27}{180}$$

$$s = 279.3 \text{ mm} \approx 275 \text{ mm c/c}$$

**Step 3 — Verify maximum spacing (IS 456 Cl 26.3.3):**

For *secondary* (distribution) reinforcement:

$$s_{max} = \min(5d, 450 \text{ mm}) = \min(5 \times 126, 450) = \min(630, 450) = 450 \text{ mm}$$

Since provided spacing 275 mm $<$ 450 mm ✓ **SAFE**

For *main* reinforcement:

$$s_{max} = \min(3d, 300 \text{ mm}) = \min(3 \times 126, 300) = \min(378, 300) = 300 \text{ mm}$$

Since 275 mm $<$ 300 mm ✓ **SAFE**

**Step 4 — Adopt design:**

Provide **8 mm $\phi$ HYSD bars at 275 mm c/c** in *both* directions (perpendicular to main steel and parallel to it). This satisfies the *minimum* T&S requirement even if the main flexural steel is in one direction only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The T&S reinforcement must be provided **in both directions of the slab** — including the direction that is *not* the principal span direction — to control shrinkage and thermal cracking.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Grade of Steel on Spacing

| Grade of Steel | $f_y$ (N/mm²) | $A_{st,min}$ (% of gross) | For D=150 mm, $A_{st,min}$ (mm²/m) | 8 mm @ spacing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fe250 (Mild) | 250 | 0.15% | **225** | 220 mm c/c |
| Fe415 (HYSD) | 415 | 0.12% | **180** | 275 mm c/c |
| Fe500 (HYSD) | 500 | 0.12% | **180** | 275 mm c/c |

**Observation:** Using HYSD bars (Fe415/Fe500) **reduces the required T&S steel by 20%** compared to mild steel, which is one of the key practical advantages cited in KTU design problems.

### 3.4 Verification of Crack-Width Bound (Additional Insight)

To demonstrate the design is *safe* for crack control, we verify the **service stress in steel** $f_s$ at the cracked section:

$$f_s = \dfrac{T_c}{A_{st,provided}}$$

For our adopted design: $A_{st,provided} = \dfrac{1000 \times 50.27}{275} = 182.8$ mm²/m

$$f_s = \dfrac{0.7\sqrt{20} \times 1000 \times 150}{182.8} = \dfrac{3130.5 \times 150}{182.8}$$

$$f_s \approx 2570 \text{ N/mm (force per metre)} \rightarrow \text{stress} = \dfrac{2570}{182.8} = 14.1 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This is **well below** $0.5 f_y = 207.5$ N/mm² → confirms the section is **crack-controlled** ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Design Process for T&S Reinforcement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Member Type] --> B{Member is a Slab?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply IS 456 Cl 26.5.1.1]
    B -- No --> D{Member is a Wall?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply IS 456 Cl 32.7.1]
    D -- No --> F{Member is a Beam Web?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply IS 456 Cl 26.5.1.3]
    F -- No --> H[Custom Design Required]
    C --> I{Grade of Steel?}
    E --> I
    G --> I
    I -- Fe250 Mild Steel --> J[Min Steel = 0.15 percent of Gross Area]
    I -- Fe415 Fe500 HYSD --> K[Min Steel = 0.12 percent of Gross Area]
    I -- Fe550 Fe600 HYSD --> L[Min Steel = 0.12 percent of Gross Area]
    J --> M[Compute Ast per metre width]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Choose Trial Bar Diameter]
    N --> O[Compute Spacing s = 1000 abar divided by Ast]
    O --> P{Check Max Spacing}
    P -- Main Bars --> Q[Max = min 3d 300 mm]
    P -- Secondary Bars --> R[Max = min 5d 450 mm]
    Q --> S{Spacing OK?}
    R --> S
    S -- Yes --> T[Adopt Spacing Provide Bars Both Directions]
    S -- No --> U[Reduce Spacing or Increase Bar Dia]
    U --> O
    T --> V[End: Final T and S Design]
    H --> V
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow — Cause–Effect Chain of T & S Cracking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Concrete Hardens] --> B[Heat of Hydration Released]
    A --> C[Water Evaporates]
    B --> D[Internal Temperature Rises]
    D --> E[Thermal Expansion restrained by base or supports]
    E --> F[Tensile Stress Induced]
    C --> G[Drying Shrinkage Strain]
    G --> F
    F --> H{Tensile Stress greater than fcr?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Cracks Form]
    H -- No --> J[No Cracking Service Safe]
    I --> K[T and S Reinforcement Activated]
    K --> L[Crack Width Controlled within Limits]
    L --> M[Serviceable and Durable Structure]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Role of T & S Steel in a Slab Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SlabCrossSection[Slab Cross Section]
        TOP[Top Cover 20 mm]
        BOT[Bottom Cover 20 mm]
        T1[Top Distribution Steel T and S 0.12 percent]
        T2[Bottom Distribution Steel T and S 0.12 percent]
        M1[Main Flexural Steel As designed for bending]
        CORE[Plain Concrete Core 150 mm thick]
    end
    M1 --> CORE
    T1 --> CORE
    T2 --> CORE
    TOP --> T1
    BOT --> M1
    BOT --> T2
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**

- The **main flexural steel** at the bottom resists bending moments and is designed from flexural analysis.
- The **top and bottom distribution steel** (running perpendicular to main bars) constitutes the **temperature and shrinkage reinforcement** — sized per **0.12% (HYSD)** or **0.15% (Fe250)** of gross area.
- Both layers work together: the main steel carries flexure; the distribution steel carries the **tensile force released by thermal and shrinkage cracks** and *bridges* them to keep them narrow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model QP]**
*What is the purpose of providing temperature and shrinkage reinforcement in RCC slabs? Mention the IS 456 provision for minimum reinforcement in slabs using Fe415 HYSD bars.*

**Model Answer (5 lines):**

Temperature and shrinkage (T&S) reinforcement is provided in RCC slabs to **resist tensile stresses** developed due to **thermal expansion/contraction** and **drying shrinkage** of concrete. It controls the **width and spacing of cracks** arising from these volumetric changes, ensuring serviceability and durability. Per **IS 456:2000, Clause 26.5.1.1**, the minimum T&S reinforcement in slabs (each direction) using **Fe415 HYSD bars** is **0.12% of the gross cross-sectional area**. For mild steel (Fe250), this is increased to **0.15%** of the gross area.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model QP]**
*State the maximum spacing rules for main and secondary reinforcement in one-way slabs as per IS 456:2000.*

**Model Answer (4 lines):**

As per **IS 456:2000, Clause 26.3.3**, the maximum spacing rules in slabs are:

- **Main reinforcement:** $\min(3d, 300 \text{ mm})$, where $d$ is the effective depth
- **Secondary (distribution) reinforcement:** $\min(5d, 450 \text{ mm})$

This ensures the steel is closely spaced enough to **control crack width** within serviceable limits.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model QP]**

A **two-way solid RCC slab** for a commercial building has clear dimensions **4.0 m × 5.0 m**, supported on all four edges. The slab has an overall depth of **180 mm**, with effective cover of 20 mm. Concrete grade is M25, and steel is Fe415 HYSD.

**(a)** Compute the **minimum temperature and shrinkage reinforcement** required per metre width in each direction.
**(b)** Choose a suitable bar diameter, compute the spacing, and verify it against the **maximum spacing criteria of IS 456:2000**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Minimum T&S Steel per metre width:**

For Fe415 HYSD bars (IS 456 Cl 26.5.1.1):

$$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times b \times D = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 180$$

$$\boxed{A_{st,min} = 216 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}}$$

**Part (b) — Bar selection and spacing check:**

Trial bar: **10 mm $\phi$ HYSD** (area $a_{bar} = 78.54$ mm²)

$$s = \dfrac{1000 \times 78.54}{216} = 363.6 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt spacing: **10 mm $\phi$ @ 350 mm c/c** in *both* directions.

**Verification of provided area:**

$$A_{st,provided} = \dfrac{1000 \times 78.54}{350} = 224.4 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} > 216 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Effective depth:** $d = 180 - 20 - 5 = 155$ mm

**Max spacing for main bars:**

$$s_{max,main} = \min(3 \times 155, 300) = \min(465, 300) = 300 \text{ mm}$$

Since 350 mm $>$ 300 mm → **Does not satisfy main bar spacing rule!**

**Correction:** For the **main reinforcement direction**, use closer spacing. Switch to **10 mm @ 300 mm c/c** for the main direction.

$$A_{st,main,provided} = \dfrac{1000 \times 78.54}{300} = 261.8 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} > 216 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \quad \checkmark$$

For the **secondary direction**:

$$s_{max,sec} = \min(5 \times 155, 450) = \min(775, 450) = 450 \text{ mm}$$

Since 350 mm $<$ 450 mm → **OK** ✓

**Final Design:** Provide **10 mm $\phi$ Fe415 HYSD bars at 300 mm c/c in the main direction** and **at 350 mm c/c in the secondary direction**.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks - Alternative]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model QP]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **mechanism of shrinkage cracking** in RCC slabs. How does temperature and shrinkage reinforcement control such cracks?
**(b)** A one-way slab has clear span 3.0 m, overall depth 130 mm, and is reinforced with Fe415 HYSD bars. Compute and detail the **distribution steel** required. Cover = 20 mm.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Shrinkage Cracking:**

Shrinkage cracking in RCC slabs develops through the following sequence:

1. **Hydration begins:** As cement hydrates, the paste volume decreases and water migrates to the surface → drying shrinkage strain ($\varepsilon_{sh} \approx 500 \times 10^{-6}$).
2. **Restrained contraction:** The reinforcement, supporting beams, columns, and friction at the base all **restrain** this free contraction.
3. **Tensile stress buildup:** Restrained shrinkage generates tensile stresses given approximately by $\sigma_{sh} = E_c \cdot \varepsilon_{sh}$.
4. **Crack initiation:** When $\sigma_{sh} > f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$, the weakest plane (perpendicular to restraint direction) cracks.
5. **T&S reinforcement role:** The closely-spaced distribution steel **bridges the crack**, absorbing the released tensile force, limiting crack width, and ensuring multiple fine cracks replace one wide crack.

**Part (b) — Distribution Steel Calculation:**

Per IS 456 Cl 26.5.1.1 (HYSD, Fe415):

$$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 130 = 156 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

Trial: **8 mm $\phi$** (area = 50.27 mm²)

$$s = \dfrac{1000 \times 50.27}{156} = 322 \text{ mm}$$

**Effective depth:** $d \approx 130 - 20 - 4 = 106$ mm

**Max spacing for secondary (distribution) bars:**

$$s_{max,sec} = \min(5 \times 106, 450) = \min(530, 450) = 450 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt: **8 mm $\phi$ @ 320 mm c/c** in the distribution direction ✓

**Provided area check:** $A_{st,provided} = 1000 \times 50.27 / 320 = 157.1$ mm²/m $> 156$ mm²/m ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the grade of steel distinction (0.12% vs 0.15%):** Many students apply 0.15% even for HYSD bars, leading to uneconomical (over-reinforced) designs. **Check steel grade first!**
> 2. **Ignoring maximum spacing rules:** Just meeting the *area* requirement is not enough. Always verify spacing with **3d/300 mm (main)** and **5d/450 mm (secondary)**.
> 3. **One-direction mistake:** A common error is providing T&S steel only in the *secondary* direction. The Code mandates it in *both* directions of slabs.
> 4. **Confusing "gross" with "effective" cross-section:** The 0.12%/0.15% is based on **$b \times D$** (overall depth), not $b \times d$. Wrong depth → wrong answer.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversions:** Watch for mm² vs cm² — IS Code uses mm²; cm² in some textbooks. KTU expects **N/mm² and mm** throughout.
> 6. **Forgetting to check crack-width bounding:** In design-oriented 14-mark questions, examiners often award a 2-mark bonus for verifying $f_s$ remains below service-stress limits.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Temperature and Shrinkage Reinforcement**

- [x] **Definition:** Secondary reinforcement to control cracking from thermal movement and drying shrinkage.
- [x] **IS 456:2000 Cl. 26.5.1.1** is the controlling clause for slabs.
- [x] **Minimum % for HYSD (Fe415/Fe500):** **0.12%** of gross cross-sectional area.
- [x] **Minimum % for Mild Steel (Fe250):** **0.15%** of gross cross-sectional area.
- [x] **Gross area = $b \times D$** (overall depth, NOT effective depth).
- [x] **Maximum spacing — main bars:** $\min(3d, 300 \text{ mm})$.
- [x] **Maximum spacing — secondary bars:** $\min(5d, 450 \text{ mm})$.
- [x] **Walls (Cl. 32.7.1):** Vertical = 0.12% HYSD; Horizontal = **0.20%** HYSD.
- [x] **Beams (web / side face, Cl. 26.5.1.3):** 0.12% HYSD of web area.
- [x] **T&S steel is provided in BOTH orthogonal directions** of a slab.
- [x] **Coefficient of thermal expansion, $\alpha_c \approx 10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C** — close to that of steel (compatibility).
- [x] **Tensile strength of concrete:** $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm².
- [x] **Thermal stress in fully restrained member:** $\sigma_T = E_c \cdot \alpha_c \cdot \Delta T$.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Roof slabs, mass concrete, chimneys, water tanks, runways, exposed floors.
- [x] **Analogy:** "Train-rail side anchors" — the steel holds cracks *closed* and *narrow*.
- [x] **Common student pitfall:** Confusing "minimum flexural steel" with "minimum T&S steel" — both exist, but T&S governs *each direction independently*.
- [x] **Code philosophy:** The 0.12% is *empirically calibrated*, not derived from first principles — it is a *safe lower bound*, not an exact stress-balance value.

---

**End of Notes — Module 2 — Temperature and Shrinkage Reinforcement**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R.C. Columns: Design of Short Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** R.C. Columns: Design of Short Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: R.C. COLUMNS — DESIGN OF SHORT COLUMNS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IS 456:2000 Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.1.1):**
> A **Compression Member** is a structural element subjected primarily to axial compressive forces. In reinforced concrete construction, this is termed a **Reinforced Concrete (R.C.) Column**, designed to transfer loads from beams and slabs to the foundation.

A column is classified as a **Short Column** when its **slenderness ratio (λ)** is less than or equal to **12** for both unbraced and braced conditions, irrespective of the cross-sectional shape. For short columns, the failure is governed by **material crushing (compressive yielding of concrete and yielding of steel)** rather than by buckling instability.

$$\boxed{\lambda = \dfrac{l_{eff}}{D} \leq 12 \quad \text{(Short Column)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the design of structures, **Safety** dictates that columns—the primary load-transfer elements—must be designed with adequate redundancy. For short columns, the safety margin is achieved through the **partial safety factors for materials** (γ_m = 1.5 for concrete, 1.15 for steel) and the **load factor** (γ_f = 1.5) prescribed by IS 456:2000, ensuring a factored design strength lower than the characteristic strength.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stacked Pillars" Intuition

Imagine you stack a row of identical wooden pillars (blocks) vertically and place a heavy stone on top. As long as the stack is **short and stubby**, the failure mode is **crushing** of the wood at the bottom—you'll hear a crunch as the fibres are squeezed. The failure is sudden, local, and governed by the **material's intrinsic strength**.

Now imagine the same stack made **tall and slender**. Long before the wood crushes, the column will **bow outwards (buckle)** sideways. The failure is sudden, global, and governed by **geometry and stiffness**, not just material strength.

**The Design Lesson:** For short columns (stubby), we must check **material capacity (concrete crushing + steel yielding)**. For long columns (slender), we must also check **buckling capacity (Euler-type instability)**.

### 1.3 Classification of Columns (By Slenderness Ratio)

| Classification | Effective Length to Least Lateral Dimension (leff/D) | Governing Failure Mode |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Short Column (Axially Loaded)** | $\lambda \leq 12$ | Compression / Crushing |
| **Short Column (With Eccentricity)** | $\lambda \leq 12$ | Compression + Biaxial Bending |
| **Long Column** | $12 < \lambda \leq 60$ | Combined Crushing + Buckling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Code Parameters Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **γ_m (Concrete)** = **1.5** (Partial Safety Factor, IS 456 Table 5)
> - **γ_m (Steel)** = **1.15** (Partial Safety Factor, IS 456 Table 5)
> - **γ_f (Dead Load)** = **1.5**, **γ_f (Live Load)** = **1.5** (IS 456 Table 4)
> - **Minimum Eccentricity (e_min)** = **l/500 + D/30, ≥ 20 mm** (IS 456 Cl. 25.4)
> - **Minimum Longitudinal Steel (p_min)** = **0.8% of Gross Cross-Section (Ag)**
> - **Maximum Longitudinal Steel (p_max)** = **6% of Ag** (up to 4% may be lapped at one location)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Minimum Eccentricity? — The "Perfectly Axial" Myth

In **idealized theory**, an axially loaded column has the load passing through the **centroid** of the cross-section. However, in **real construction**:

1. **Imperfections in construction** — columns are never perfectly vertical.
2. **Loading uncertainties** — beams frame into columns causing moment transfer.
3. **Material heterogeneity** — concrete strength varies across the section.

Therefore, IS 456 mandates a **minimum design eccentricity (e_min)** that EVERY column must be designed for, even if calculations show it as purely axial.

$$\boxed{e_{min} = \dfrac{l}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}}$$

Where:
- $l$ = unsupported length of column (mm)
- $D$ = lateral dimension in the bending direction (mm)

## 2.2 Effective Length (leff) — End Condition Dependent

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.3 (Effective Length):**
> The effective length of a compression member is calculated based on the rotational and translational restraints at its ends. For a braced column (no sway), it depends on relative stiffness of beams and columns; for unbraced columns (sway frame), it's larger.

### Effective Length Factors for Braced Columns

| End Condition | End Condition 2 | leff (Braced) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Fixed | Fixed | 0.65 L |
| Fixed | Hinged | 0.80 L |
| Fixed | Free (Cantilever) | 1.20 L |
| Hinged | Hinged | 1.00 L |

> For KTU exam purposes, **braced columns with both ends effectively held in position (typical building frames)**, $l_{eff} = 0.65L$ to $1.0L$ depending on rotational restraint.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — every short column design problem uses these.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Reference (IS 456) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = l_{eff} / D$ | Cl. 25.1.2 |
| 2 | Short Column Limit | $\lambda \leq 12$ | Cl. 25.1.2 |
| 3 | Minimum Eccentricity | $e_{min} = l/500 + D/30 \geq 20$ mm | Cl. 25.4 |
| 4 | Axial Load Capacity (Short, Helical) | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Cl. 25.4.1 |
| 5 | Axial Load Capacity (Short, Tied) | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Cl. 25.4.1 |
| 6 | Design Load (Factored) | $P_u = 1.5 (D.L. + L.L.)$ | Table 4 |
| 7 | Gross Area | $A_g = b \times D$ | — |
| 8 | Net Concrete Area | $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ | — |
| 9 | Min. Longitudinal Steel | $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 A_g$ | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 10 | Max. Longitudinal Steel | $A_{sc,max} = 0.06 A_g$ | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 11 | Min. Diameter of Bar | 12 mm | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 12 | Min. Number of Bars (Rectangular) | 4 (one at each corner) | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 13 | Min. Number of Bars (Circular) | 6 | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 14 | Tie Diameter (for $\leq$ 32 mm bars) | $\geq \phi_{main}/4$ | Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 15 | Tie Diameter (for $>$ 32 mm bars) | 12 mm | Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 16 | Tie Spacing | $\leq$ least lateral dimension | Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 17 | Tie Spacing (Max) | $\leq 300$ mm | Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 18 | Nominal Cover (Column) | 40 mm (or as per exposure) | Cl. 26.4 |

> **Critical Note:** The expression $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ has the constants **0.4** and **0.67** already incorporating the partial safety factors (γ_m). Therefore, **P_u is the FACTORED (design) load capacity**, NOT the characteristic capacity.

## 2.4 Why 0.4 and 0.67? — The Safety Logic Explained

Let us derive why these numerical constants appear in IS 456:

$$\text{Concrete Contribution} = \dfrac{0.67 \times f_{ck}}{\gamma_{mc}} = \dfrac{0.67 \times f_{ck}}{1.5} = 0.4467 f_{ck} \approx 0.4 f_{ck}$$

$$\text{Steel Contribution} = \dfrac{0.67 \times f_y}{\gamma_{ms}} = \dfrac{0.67 \times f_y}{1.15} = 0.5826 f_y \approx 0.67 f_y \text{ (already used as design)}$$

> The **0.67 factor** is the **modular ratio equivalence factor** (compliance factor) and accounts for the **difference in stress-strain behaviour between concrete and steel** at ultimate load.

## 2.5 Tied vs Helical Columns — The Lateral Confinement Choice

| Aspect | Tied Column (Lateral Ties) | Helical Column (Spiral) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lateral Reinforcement | Discrete rectangular ties @ spacing | Continuous helix along column |
| Confinement | Local at corners | Continuous radial confinement |
| Permissible Load (IS 456) | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | **1.05 times** the tied capacity |
| Design Strength | Lower (no bonus) | **5% bonus** for spiral |
| Application | Most buildings | High seismic zones, heavy loads |

$$\boxed{P_{u,helical} = 1.05 \times \left[ 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc} \right]}$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Frames**: Every column in a multi-storey building (typical λ < 12) is a short column governed by this module's content.
- **Bridge Piers**: Massive piers with low slenderness ratios use the same axial capacity formula.
- **Industrial Structures**: Columns supporting heavy machinery with low eccentricity use spiral reinforcement for additional ductility.
- **Earthquake Engineering**: Short columns are vulnerable to **shear failure** and require **capacity design** principles — making the safety aspect of their design critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Axial Capacity Equation from First Principles

Starting from the fundamental assumption of **strain compatibility** in a composite R.C. section at ultimate load:

**Step 1: Equilibrium of Forces**

At the ultimate limit state, the total factored axial load $P_u$ is resisted by concrete in compression and steel in compression:

$$P_u = P_{c} + P_{s}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Material Design Stresses**

For concrete, design stress in compression = $\dfrac{0.67 f_{ck}}{\gamma_{mc}} = \dfrac{0.67 f_{ck}}{1.5}$

For steel, design stress = $\dfrac{0.67 f_y}{\gamma_{ms}}$ where $0.67 f_y$ is the **limiting compressive stress in steel** to prevent buckling of longitudinal bars (IS 456, Cl. 25.4.1 Note).

**Step 3: Form the Design Equation**

$$\boxed{P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}}$$

Where:
- $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ (concrete area after deducting steel)
- $f_{ck}$ = characteristic compressive strength of concrete
- $f_y$ = characteristic yield strength of steel
- $A_{sc}$ = area of longitudinal steel
- $A_g$ = gross cross-sectional area

## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Design of a Short Axially Loaded Column

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Model Question Pattern (14 Marks):** A typical design problem requires students to determine column dimensions, calculate required steel, check all IS 456 code provisions, and detail the reinforcement layout.

### Problem Statement

**Design a short, axially loaded R.C. column to carry a factored axial load of $P_u = 1500$ kN. The column is 3.5 m long, effectively held in position at both ends but not restrained against rotation. Use M20 grade concrete and Fe415 grade steel. Assume the column is exposed to a mild environment. Adopt a square cross-section.**

**Given:**
- Factored Load $P_u = 1500$ kN $= 1500 \times 10^3$ N
- Column Length $L = 3500$ mm
- $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm²
- $f_y = 415$ N/mm²
- Exposure: Mild → Nominal Cover = **40 mm**

---

### Step 1: Determine Effective Length

For both ends held in position but not restrained against rotation (typical braced building):
$$l_{eff} = 1.0 L = 1.0 \times 3500 = 3500 \text{ mm}$$

(Conservative assumption for a first-iteration design.)

### Step 2: Assume Column Dimensions

Try a square column: $b = D = 400$ mm

$$\text{Gross Area } A_g = 400 \times 400 = 160000 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3: Check Slenderness Ratio (Confirm Short Column)

$$\lambda = \dfrac{l_{eff}}{D} = \dfrac{3500}{400} = 8.75$$

Since $\lambda = 8.75 < 12$, the column is classified as a **SHORT COLUMN**. ✓ (Verification confirmed.)

### Step 4: Calculate Required Steel Area

Using the design equation:
$$P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

Substitute $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$:
$$P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} (A_g - A_{sc}) + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

Rearrange to solve for $A_{sc}$:
$$P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_g - 0.4 f_{ck} A_{sc} + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

$$P_u - 0.4 f_{ck} A_g = A_{sc} (0.67 f_y - 0.4 f_{ck})$$

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{P_u - 0.4 f_{ck} A_g}{0.67 f_y - 0.4 f_{ck}}$$

**Substitute numerical values:**

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3 - 0.4 \times 20 \times 160000}{0.67 \times 415 - 0.4 \times 20}$$

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{1500000 - 1280000}{278.05 - 8.00}$$

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{220000}{270.05}$$

$$A_{sc} = 814.66 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 5: Check Against Minimum and Maximum Steel Limits

$$A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times A_g = 0.008 \times 160000 = 1280 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{sc,max} = 0.06 \times A_g = 0.06 \times 160000 = 9600 \text{ mm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL OBSERVATION:** The required steel $A_{sc} = 814.66$ mm² is LESS than the minimum $A_{sc,min} = 1280$ mm² mandated by IS 456 (Cl. 26.5.3.1). Therefore, **we must provide 1280 mm² of steel** as the governing requirement.

### Step 6: Select Reinforcement Bars

Try **8 numbers of 16 mm diameter bars**:

$$A_{sc,provided} = 8 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 8 \times 201.06 = 1608.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Check:** $A_{sc,provided} = 1608.5 > 1280 = A_{sc,min}$ ✓ (OK)

**Check maximum:** $1608.5 < 9600$ ✓ (OK)

### Step 7: Verify Revised Column Capacity

$$P_{u,capacity} = 0.4 \times 20 \times (160000 - 1608.5) + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1608.5$$

$$P_{u,capacity} = 8 \times 158391.5 + 278.05 \times 1608.5$$

$$P_{u,capacity} = 1267132 + 447403.4$$

$$P_{u,capacity} = 1714535.4 \text{ N} = 1714.5 \text{ kN}$$

> Since $P_{u,capacity} = 1714.5$ kN $> P_{u,required} = 1500$ kN ✓ **Column is SAFE.**

### Step 8: Design of Lateral Ties (Transverse Reinforcement)

**Tie Diameter (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2):**

$$\phi_{tie} \geq \dfrac{\phi_{main}}{4} = \dfrac{16}{4} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

Provide **8 mm diameter ties** (standard practice).

**Tie Spacing:**

$$s_{tie} \leq \text{least lateral dimension} = 400 \text{ mm}$$

$$s_{tie} \leq 16 \times \phi_{main} = 16 \times 16 = 256 \text{ mm}$$

$$s_{tie} \leq 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Governing spacing: $s_{tie} = 256$ mm** → Adopt **250 mm c/c** (conservative round-down).

### Step 9: Check Minimum Eccentricity

$$e_{min} = \dfrac{l}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} = \dfrac{3500}{500} + \dfrac{400}{30} = 7 + 13.33 = 20.33 \text{ mm}$$

Since $e_{min} = 20.33 > 20$ mm ✓, **but this is small relative to dimensions**, the column is treated as axially loaded. (For $e_{min}/D > 0.05$, biaxial moment design would be required.)

### Step 10: Final Detailing

**Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Column Size | 400 mm × 400 mm |
| Longitudinal Steel | 8 nos. of 16 mm $\phi$ |
| Lateral Ties | 8 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c |
| Concrete Grade | M20 |
| Steel Grade | Fe415 |
| Cover | 40 mm |
| Slenderness Ratio | 8.75 (Short) |
| Design Capacity | 1714.5 kN $>$ 1500 kN ✓ **SAFE** |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Variations

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to identify the design path for any given problem.**

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Design Path |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Find Dimensions | $P_u$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, λ, L | b, D, $A_{sc}$ | Calculate $A_g$ from $P_u = 0.45 f_{ck} A_g + 0.6 f_y A_{sc,min}$ using $p_{min}$ |
| Find Steel Only | $P_u$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, b, D | $A_{sc}$ | Direct substitution in design equation |
| Check Capacity | All dimensions and steel | $P_u$ capacity | Substitute into design equation, compare to applied |
| Helical Column | Same as tied but spiral | $P_u$ | Multiply tied capacity by 1.05 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Design Workflow for Short Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Design a Short R.C. Column] --> B[Step 1: Gather Inputs: Pu, fck, fy, L, Exposure]
    B --> C[Step 2: Assume Effective Length leff based on End Conditions]
    C --> D[Step 3: Assume Cross-Section Dimensions b x D]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Slenderness Ratio lambda = leff / D]
    E --> F{Step 5: Is lambda less than or equal to 12?}
    F -- No --> G[STOP: This is a Long Column. Use SP-16 Charts or Buckling Analysis]
    F -- Yes --> H[Step 6: Calculate Required Steel Area Asc]
    H --> I[Step 7: Check Asc min and Asc max Limits]
    I --> J{Asc required greater than Asc min?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Step 8: Select Bar Diameter and Number]
    J -- No --> L[Step 8: Adopt Asc min as Design Steel]
    K --> M[Step 9: Verify Revised Capacity greater than Applied Pu]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Step 10: Design Lateral Ties: Diameter and Spacing]
    N --> O[Step 11: Check Minimum Eccentricity e min]
    O --> P[Step 12: Final Detailing and Drawing]
    P --> Q[END: SAFE DESIGN COMPLETE]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: Cross-Section & 3D Reinforcement Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Column Plan View - 400 mm x 400 mm]
        A1[Corner Bar 16 mm at NW] --> A2[Corner Bar 16 mm at NE]
        A2 --> A3[Corner Bar 16 mm at SE]
        A3 --> A4[Corner Bar 16 mm at SW]
        A4 --> A1
        A5[Intermediate Bar 16 mm at N Mid] --> A6[Intermediate Bar 16 mm at E Mid]
        A6 --> A7[Intermediate Bar 16 mm at S Mid]
        A7 --> A8[Intermediate Bar 16 mm at W Mid]
        A8 --> A5
    end
    subgraph B[Column Elevation View]
        B1[Lateral Tie 8 mm at Top] --> B2[Lateral Tie 8 mm at 250 mm c/c]
        B2 --> B3[Lateral Tie 8 mm at Mid]
        B3 --> B4[Lateral Tie 8 mm at Bottom]
        B4 --> B1
        B5[Main Bar 16 mm Full Length]
    end
    subgraph C[Concrete Cover]
        C1[Cover 40 mm All Sides]
    end
    A --> C
    B --> C
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow: Force Transfer Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Factored Axial Load Pu] --> P2[Applied at Column Top]
    P2 --> P3[Load Splits into Two Paths]
    P3 --> P4[Path 1: Concrete Core in Compression]
    P3 --> P5[Path 2: Longitudinal Steel Bars in Compression]
    P4 --> P6[Concrete Contribution: 0.4 fck times Ac]
    P5 --> P7[Steel Contribution: 0.67 fy times Asc]
    P6 --> P8[Equilibrium: Pu equals Sum of Contributions]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Load Transferred to Foundation via Base Plate]
    P9 --> P10[SAFE LOAD PATH CLOSED]
    P11[Helical or Tied Lateral Reinforcement] --> P12[Provides Confinement and Prevents Bar Buckling]
    P12 --> P4
    P12 --> P5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Short Column as per IS 456:2000. [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**

According to IS 456:2000 (Cl. 25.1.2), a compression member may be classified as a short column when the ratio of its effective length to its least lateral dimension (slenderness ratio, λ) is less than or equal to **12**. For such members, the failure mode is governed by **material crushing** rather than buckling. The design is based on the axial capacity equation $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$, without considering slenderness reduction factors.

---

### Question 2: What is Minimum Eccentricity? State IS 456 Provisions. [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**

Minimum eccentricity accounts for unavoidable imperfections in construction and ensures that all columns are designed for at least a small bending moment in addition to axial load. As per IS 456 (Cl. 25.4), the minimum eccentricity shall not be less than:

$$e_{min} = \dfrac{l_{unsupported}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

This ensures the column has adequate resistance against unintended moments due to construction tolerances, loading uncertainties, and material heterogeneity.

---

## PART B — Design Problems (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Design Problem [Internal Choice Option 1]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Dec 2024 Pattern]**

Design a short, axially loaded R.C. column to carry a service dead load of 800 kN and a service live load of 600 kN. The column is 4 m long with both ends fixed against translation but partially restrained against rotation. Use M25 grade concrete and Fe500 grade steel. Assume mild exposure conditions. Sketch the reinforcement details.

#### Sub-Part (a): Determine Column Dimensions and Longitudinal Steel [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Factored Load**
$$P_u = 1.5 (D.L. + L.L.) = 1.5 \times (800 + 600) = 2100 \text{ kN} = 2100 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Effective Length** (both ends held in position, partially restrained → assume $l_{eff} = 0.8L$)
$$l_{eff} = 0.8 \times 4000 = 3200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Assume Square Column Section.** Using the formula with assumed steel ratio of ~1.5%:
$$A_g = \dfrac{P_u}{0.45 f_{ck} + 0.75 p f_y} \approx \dfrac{2100 \times 10^3}{0.45 \times 25 + 0.015 \times 415}$$

$$A_g = \dfrac{2100000}{11.25 + 6.225} = \dfrac{2100000}{17.475} = 120172 \text{ mm}^2$$

Adopt **350 mm × 350 mm** column ($A_g = 122500$ mm²).

**Step 4: Slenderness Check**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{3200}{350} = 9.14 < 12 \quad \checkmark \text{ Short Column}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Steel Area**
$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{P_u - 0.4 f_{ck} A_g}{0.67 f_y - 0.4 f_{ck}} = \dfrac{2100000 - 0.4 \times 25 \times 122500}{0.67 \times 500 - 0.4 \times 25}$$

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{2100000 - 1225000}{335 - 10} = \dfrac{875000}{325} = 2692.3 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Code Limits**
- $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times 122500 = 980$ mm²
- $A_{sc,max} = 0.06 \times 122500 = 7350$ mm²
- Required: 2692.3 mm² ✓ (within limits)

**Step 7: Select Bars.** Try 20 mm $\phi$ bars: Area of one bar = $\pi/4 \times 20^2 = 314.16$ mm²
$$n = \dfrac{2692.3}{314.16} = 8.57 \rightarrow \text{Provide 9 bars of 20 mm} \phi$$
$$A_{sc,provided} = 9 \times 314.16 = 2827.4 \text{ mm}^2 > 2692.3 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

#### Sub-Part (b): Design Lateral Ties and Verify [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 8: Tie Diameter** (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2)
$$\phi_{tie} \geq \dfrac{\phi_{main}}{4} = \dfrac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ mm}$$
Provide **8 mm $\phi$ ties**.

**Step 9: Tie Spacing**
$$s_{tie} \leq \text{least lateral dimension} = 350 \text{ mm}$$
$$s_{tie} \leq 16 \phi_{main} = 16 \times 20 = 320 \text{ mm}$$
$$s_{tie} \leq 300 \text{ mm}$$
**Adopt: 300 mm c/c.**

**Step 10: Minimum Eccentricity Check**
$$e_{min} = \dfrac{4000}{500} + \dfrac{350}{30} = 8 + 11.67 = 19.67 \text{ mm} < 20 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, use **$e_{min} = 20$ mm** (governing value).

Since $e_{min}/D = 20/350 = 0.057 > 0.05$, technically the column should be designed for uniaxial bending. However, for the short-column classification, the axial capacity check is sufficient.

**Step 11: Verify Revised Capacity**
$$P_{u,cap} = 0.4 \times 25 \times (122500 - 2827.4) + 0.67 \times 500 \times 2827.4$$
$$= 10 \times 119672.6 + 335 \times 2827.4$$
$$= 1196726 + 947179 = 2143905 \text{ N} = 2143.9 \text{ kN}$$
$$P_{u,cap} = 2143.9 \text{ kN} > P_u = 2100 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE DESIGN}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Design Problem [Internal Choice Option 2]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Dec 2024 Pattern]**

A short circular column of 450 mm diameter supports a factored axial load of 1800 kN. The column is 3 m long, hinged at both ends. Design the column using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel with helical reinforcement. Provide complete reinforcement details including ties/spirals.

#### Sub-Part (a): Longitudinal Steel Design [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Gross Area**
$$A_g = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 450^2 = 159043 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Slenderness Check** (hinged-hinged, $l_{eff} = 1.0L$)
$$\lambda = \dfrac{3000}{450} = 6.67 < 12 \quad \checkmark \text{ Short Column}$$

**Step 3: Steel Area for Helical Column**

For helical columns, IS 456 (Cl. 25.4.1) permits 1.05 times the tied column capacity:
$$P_{u,helical} = 1.05 \times [0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}]$$

Using $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ and solving for $A_{sc}$:
$$\dfrac{P_u}{1.05} = 0.4 f_{ck}(A_g - A_{sc}) + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

$$\dfrac{1800 \times 10^3}{1.05} = 0.4 \times 20 \times (159043 - A_{sc}) + 0.67 \times 415 \times A_{sc}$$

$$1714286 = 1272344 + A_{sc}(-8 + 278.05)$$

$$1714286 - 1272344 = 270.05 A_{sc}$$

$$A_{sc} = \dfrac{441942}{270.05} = 1636.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Code Limits** (Circular column: minimum 6 bars)
- $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times 159043 = 1272.3$ mm²
- $A_{sc,max} = 0.06 \times 159043 = 9542.6$ mm²
- Required: 1636.5 mm² ✓

**Step 5: Select Bars.** Use 20 mm $\phi$ bars (area = 314.16 mm²):
$$n = \dfrac{1636.5}{314.16} = 5.21 \rightarrow \text{Provide 6 bars of 20 mm} \phi$$
$$A_{sc,provided} = 6 \times 314.16 = 1885 \text{ mm}^2 > 1636.5 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$
(Minimum 6 bars satisfied for circular column.)

#### Sub-Part (b): Helical (Spiral) Reinforcement Design [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 6: Spiral Pitch** (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3)

The pitch of the helical reinforcement shall not exceed:
- 75 mm
- $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of core diameter = $\dfrac{450 - 2 \times 40 - 2 \times 8}{6} = \dfrac{354}{6} = 59$ mm

**Governing pitch: 59 mm → adopt 50 mm pitch** (conservative).

**Step 7: Spiral Bar Size**
- Minimum diameter of helical bar = $\dfrac{1}{4} \times \phi_{main} = \dfrac{20}{4} = 5$ mm
- Provide **8 mm $\phi$ helical bar** at **50 mm pitch**.

**Step 8: Minimum Eccentricity Check**
$$e_{min} = \dfrac{3000}{500} + \dfrac{450}{30} = 6 + 15 = 21 \text{ mm} \geq 20 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 9: Capacity Verification**
$$P_{u,cap} = 1.05 \times [0.4 \times 20 \times (159043 - 1885) + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1885]$$
$$= 1.05 \times [8 \times 157158 + 278.05 \times 1885]$$
$$= 1.05 \times [1257264 + 524124]$$
$$= 1.05 \times 1781388 = 1870457 \text{ N} = 1870.5 \text{ kN}$$
$$P_{u,cap} = 1870.5 \text{ kN} > P_u = 1800 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE DESIGN}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.4 and 0.67** — Many students incorrectly use $f_{ck}$ and $f_y$ directly without the code-prescribed reduction factors. This leads to **2-3 mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Not checking $A_{sc}$ against $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 A_g$** — IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.1 mandates minimum 0.8% steel regardless of load calculation. If calculated $A_{sc}$ < 1280 mm² for a 400×400 column, the **minimum governs**. Students lose **1-2 marks** by ignoring this.
>
> 3. **Incorrect effective length assumption** — The $l_{eff}$ depends on end conditions. For exam purposes, use $l_{eff} = L$ (hinged-hinged, conservative) unless explicitly stated.
>
> 4. **Skimping on tie detailing** — Tie diameter and spacing are 1-2 mark questions separately. Provide explicit values, not just "provide 8 mm ties".
>
> 5. **Not drawing the cross-section** — KTU board valuation awards **1-2 marks** specifically for the neat sketch with bar layout and tie arrangement.
>
> 6. **Missing helical column bonus (1.05×)** — For spiral columns, students forget the 5% capacity enhancement, under-designing the section.
>
> 7. **Forgetting minimum cover (40 mm for mild exposure)** — Failing to account for cover in the cross-section calculation reduces effective dimensions.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section — it captures every high-yield point from this module.**

- ✅ **Definition:** A column with slenderness ratio $\lambda = l_{eff}/D \leq 12$ is a **Short Column**.
- ✅ **Design Equation (Master):** $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ (with $\gamma_m$ factors already included).
- ✅ **Helical Bonus:** Helical/spiral columns get **1.05× the capacity** of tied columns.
- ✅ **Minimum Eccentricity:** $e_{min} = l/500 + D/30 \geq 20$ mm — Always check this.
- ✅ **Steel Limits:** $p_{min} = 0.8\%$ of $A_g$ and $p_{max} = 6\%$ of $A_g$ (Cl. 26.5.3.1).
- ✅ **Minimum Bars:** **4** for rectangular (one at each corner); **6** for circular.
- ✅ **Minimum Bar Diameter:** **12 mm** for longitudinal steel.
- ✅ **Tie Diameter:** $\phi_{tie} \geq \phi_{main}/4$ (for main bars ≤ 32 mm).
- ✅ **Tie Spacing:** **LEAST** of: (i) least lateral dimension, (ii) $16\phi_{main}$, (iii) **300 mm**.
- ✅ **Cover:** **40 mm** for mild exposure (IS 456 Cl. 26.4).
- ✅ **Safety Factors:** $\gamma_m = 1.5$ (concrete), $1.15$ (steel); $\gamma_f = 1.5$ (loads).
- ✅ **Partial Safety Factors in Code Equation:** Already built into 0.4 and 0.67 — do **NOT** re-divide by $\gamma_m$.
- ✅ **0.67 Derivation:** Concrete contribution $0.67 f_{ck}/1.5 = 0.447 \approx 0.4 f_{ck}$. Steel stress $0.67 f_y$ accounts for stress-strain compatibility at ultimate.
- ✅ **Safety Philosophy:** Short columns have **no slenderness reduction** — they are designed for full axial capacity because their failure is by crushing (ductile, predictable), unlike long columns (brittle buckling).

> **End of Module 2 Notes — R.C. Columns: Design of Short Columns** 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axial Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Axial Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Axial Compression** is a structural loading condition in which a force (or a system of forces) is applied along the longitudinal centroidal axis of a straight structural member, directed inward toward the cross-section, thereby inducing a state of uniaxial compressive stress. The member subjected to this loading is commonly called a **column** (in buildings/frames) or a **strut** (in trusses/bracing systems).

Mathematically, the induced average compressive stress is given by:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A}$$

where:
- $P$ = magnitude of the axial compressive load (in **N** or **kN**)
- $A$ = gross cross-sectional area of the member (in **mm²** or **m²**)
- $\sigma_c$ = axial compressive stress (in **N/mm²** = **MPa**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A member loaded in pure axial compression is rarely governed by *crushing* of the material. The governing failure mode for most practical columns is **instability (buckling)**, not material strength. This is the central safety concern in the design of compression members.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on an empty aluminium soda can by pressing straight down on the top rim with your hand. The can does not "crush" instantly. Instead, the thin cylindrical wall suddenly **kinks sideways** with a sharp *crack* — even though the load you applied is far below the material's true crushing strength. That sudden sideways snap is **buckling under axial compression**.

- A **short, fat can** (low slenderness) would require enormous force to buckle — it would crush.
- A **tall, thin can** (high slenderness) buckles almost effortlessly — failure is by instability.
- This is exactly the same behaviour we see in **steel columns, wooden props, bamboo scaffolding, and even human bones** under compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** Strength of material alone is *not* a sufficient design criterion for compression members. **Stiffness and geometry** (length, end restraints, cross-sectional shape) are equally — sometimes more — important. This is the essence of "Safety in Design of Structures."

### 1.3 Classification of Compression Members (by Slenderness)

| Type | Slenderness Ratio $(\lambda = L_e/r)$ | Governing Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Short Column / Strut** | $\lambda \leq \lambda_c$ (very low) | Pure Crushing (Material Yielding) |
| **Intermediate Column** | Moderate range | Combined Crushing + Buckling |
| **Long / Slender Column** | $\lambda \geq \lambda_c$ (very high) | Pure Elastic Buckling (Euler) |

where $\lambda_c$ is the **critical slenderness ratio** that demarcates elastic from inelastic behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Slenderness Ratio — The Master Parameter

The single most important non-dimensional number governing the behaviour of a compression member is the **Slenderness Ratio**, defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}$$

where:
- $L_e$ = **effective length** of the column (depends on end conditions), in mm
- $r$ = **radius of gyration** of the cross-section about the buckling axis, $r = \sqrt{I/A}$, in mm

> [!NOTE]
> A *larger* slenderness ratio means a *more slender* column, which means a *lower* buckling capacity. Designers always strive to **minimise $\lambda$** by choosing a compact cross-section or by providing lateral restraint.

## 2.2 Effective Length ($L_e$) for Standard End Conditions

The effective length is the length of an equivalent *pin-ended* column that has the same buckling load as the actual column with its given end conditions.

| End Condition Sketch | Effective Length $L_e$ |
|---|---|
| Both ends **pin-jointed** (hinged) | $L_e = 1.00\,L$ |
| Both ends **fixed** | $L_e = 0.65\,L$ |
| One end **fixed**, other end **free** (cantilever) | $L_e = 2.00\,L$ |
| One end **fixed**, other end **pinned** | $L_e = 0.80\,L$ |
| One end **fixed**, other end **guided (translation prevented, rotation free)** | $L_e = 1.00\,L$ |
| Both ends **fixed against translation, free to rotate** | $L_e = 1.00\,L$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "*Pin-Pin = 1, Fixed-Fixed = 0.65, Fixed-Free = 2*". These are the three most frequently tested values in KTU exams.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Name | Applicability / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$ | **Euler's Critical Load** | Long, slender, elastic columns (Euler region) |
| 2 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | **Euler's Critical Stress** | Long, slender, elastic columns |
| 3 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\left(L_e/r\right)^2}$ | Critical Stress in terms of $\lambda$ | Elastic buckling |
| 4 | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a\left(L_e/r\right)^2}$ | **Rankine's Formula** | Intermediate columns (empirical) |
| 5 | $\lambda_c = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}}$ | **Critical Slenderness Ratio** | Boundary between long & short |
| 6 | $P = \sigma_{ac} \cdot A$ | Axial Capacity (short column) | Crushing governs |
| 7 | $\sigma_{ac} = 0.6\,f_y$ | Permissible comp. stress (IS 800) | Steel short column |
| 8 | $FOS = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{P_{applied}}$ | Factor of Safety | Stability safety check |
| 9 | $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ | Radius of Gyration | Cross-section property |
| 10 | $\sigma_c = P/A$ | Average Compressive Stress | Stress calculation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU Formula Sheet** above, *vertical bars* are written as $\vert x \vert$ using LaTeX `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always remember to convert $P_{cr}$ to *working load* by dividing by the appropriate **Factor of Safety (FOS)** before using in design.

## 2.4 Why Axial Compression is a "Safety" Topic

In tension members, failure is *ductile, gradual, and preceded by visible yielding* (necking, elongation). A tension failure gives ample warning.

In **compression members**, however, buckling is:
- **Sudden** (no warning),
- **Catastrophic** (entire structure may collapse),
- **Sensitive to small imperfections** (initial crookedness, load eccentricity, material variability).

Hence, *Safety in Design of Structures* gives special attention to axial compression — to ensure that the predicted critical load $P_{cr}$ is comfortably above any realistic service load, with adequate FOS.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil:** Building columns, bridge piers, transmission towers.
- **Mechanical:** Piston rods, hydraulic press columns, screw jacks.
- **Aerospace:** Stringers and longerons in aircraft fuselage.
- **Marine:** Hull stiffeners, mast columns of ships.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3 START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Buckling Load ($P_{cr}$)

**Assumptions:**
1. The column is initially perfectly straight and the load is truly axial.
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law (linear elastic).
3. The self-weight of the column is negligible compared to the applied load.
4. The cross-section is uniform throughout the length.
5. End joints are frictionless (ideal pin/fixed).
6. Failure occurs by **bending only** (no torsion, no lateral-torsional coupling).

### Step 1 — Consider the Deflected Shape

Let the column be a pin-ended member of length $L$ subjected to an axial compressive load $P$. At a distance $x$ from the bottom support, the lateral deflection is $y$. The bending moment at this section is:

$$M_x = -P \cdot y$$

The negative sign indicates that the moment is *restoring* (it tries to reduce the deflection).

### Step 2 — Apply Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation

The governing differential equation for elastic flexural buckling is:

$$E I \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M_x = -P \cdot y$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{E I}\, y = 0$$

### Step 3 — Define a Substitute Constant

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. The ODE becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

The general solution of this second-order linear homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients is:

$$y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions (Pin-Pin Case)

For a pin-ended column:
- At $x = 0$: $y = 0$ → $A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0$
- At $x = L$: $y = 0$ → $A \cos(kL) + B \sin(kL) = 0$

Since $A = 0$, the second condition reduces to:

$$B \sin(kL) = 0$$

### Step 5 — Trivial vs. Non-Trivial Solution

For a non-trivial (physical) buckling deformation, $B \neq 0$, therefore:

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the Critical Load

The smallest buckling load corresponds to $n = 1$ (first mode of buckling):

$$kL = \pi \;\Rightarrow\; k^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

Recall that $k^2 = P / EI$, therefore:

$$\frac{P}{EI} = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

Solving for $P$:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}}$$

This is **Euler's Critical Buckling Load** for a pin-ended column. The corresponding deflected shape is a half-sine wave: $y = B \sin(\pi x / L)$.

### Step 7 — Extend to General End Conditions

For a column of actual length $L$ but with effective length $L_e$ (incorporating the end-fixity factor), the general Euler formula is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Slenderness Ratio $\lambda_c$

Euler's formula is valid only while the material is elastic, i.e., $\sigma_{cr} \le \sigma_y$ (yield stress). The *limiting* condition occurs when $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y$:

$$\frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \sigma_y \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2} \cdot A} = \sigma_y$$

Substituting $I = A r^2$:

$$\frac{\pi^2 E A r^2}{L_e^{\,2} \cdot A} = \sigma_y \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\pi^2 E r^2}{L_e^{\,2}} = \sigma_y$$

Rearranging for $L_e / r = \lambda$:

$$\lambda_c^2 = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}$$

Therefore, the **critical slenderness ratio** is:

$$\boxed{\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_y}}}$$

### Interpretation
- If $\lambda \ge \lambda_c$ → **Euler's formula applicable** (elastic buckling).
- If $\lambda < \lambda_c$ → **Euler's formula is NOT valid**; use Rankine's formula, Johnson's formula, or IS code curves.

## 3.3 Derivation of Rankine's Formula

Rankine proposed an empirical rational formula that smoothly transitions from crushing (for short columns) to buckling (for long columns), covering the *entire* range of slenderness ratios.

**Form hypothesis:** The total unit axial deformation is the sum of the unit crushing deformation and the unit buckling deformation.

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_E}$$

where:
- $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ (crushing load)
- $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$ (Euler buckling load)

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{\sigma_c A} + \frac{L_e^{\,2}}{\pi^2 E I}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator of the second term by $A$ (using $I = A r^2$):

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{\sigma_c A} \left[1 + \frac{\sigma_c L_e^{\,2}}{\pi^2 E r^2}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma_c A}\left[1 + \frac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}\,\lambda^2\right]$$

Defining Rankine's constant $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$, we get:

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{\sigma_c A}\left[1 + a\,\lambda^2\right]$$

Inverting:

$$\boxed{P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{r}\right)^2}}$$

where $a$ is a constant that depends on the material (e.g., for mild steel, $a \approx 1/7500$).

### 3.3.1 Limiting Behaviour Check

- For **short column** ($\lambda \to 0$): $P_R \to \sigma_c A$ ✓ (crushing governs)
- For **long column** ($\lambda \to \infty$): $P_R \to \sigma_c A / (a\lambda^2) = \pi^2 E I / L_e^{\,2}$ ✓ (Euler governs)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Classification of Compression Members

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Compression Load P applied on member] --> B[Compute Slenderness Ratio]
    B --> B1[lambda equals L_e divided by r]
    B1 --> C{Compare lambda with lambda_c}
    C -->|lambda greater than lambda_c| D[LONG / SLENDER COLUMN]
    C -->|lambda less than or equal to lambda_c| E[SHORT / INTERMEDIATE COLUMN]
    D --> D1[Use Euler Buckling Formula]
    D1 --> D2[P_cr equals pi squared E I over L_e squared]
    D2 --> D3[Failure Mode Elastic Instability]
    E --> E1[Short Column]
    E --> E2[Intermediate Column]
    E1 --> E3[Use Crushing Stress Criterion]
    E3 --> E4[P equals sigma_c times A]
    E4 --> E5[Failure Mode Material Yielding]
    E2 --> E6[Use Rankine or IS Code Curve]
    E6 --> E7[P_R equals sigma_c A divided by 1 plus a lambda squared]
    E7 --> E8[Failure Mode Combined Buckling and Crushing]
    D3 --> F[Apply Factor of Safety]
    E5 --> F
    E8 --> F
    F --> G[Permissible Load equals P_cr or P_R divided by FOS]
    G --> H[Final Safe Design Capacity]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — End Conditions and Effective Length

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compression Member End Conditions] --> A1[Pin Pin]
    A --> A2[Fixed Fixed]
    A --> A3[Fixed Free Cantilever]
    A --> A4[Fixed Pinned]
    A1 --> B1[L_e equals 1.00 L]
    A2 --> B2[L_e equals 0.65 L]
    A3 --> B3[L_e equals 2.00 L]
    A4 --> B4[L_e equals 0.80 L]
    B1 --> C[Compute Euler Buckling Load P_cr]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[P_cr equals pi squared E I over L_e squared]
    D --> E[Check against Actual Applied Load]
    E --> F{P_cr greater than FOS times P_applied}
    F -->|YES| G[DESIGN IS SAFE]
    F -->|NO| H[REDESIGN Increase cross section or add bracing]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Apply?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given: Column with L, A, I, E, End Conditions, Material] --> Step1[Compute r equals sqrt of I over A]
    Step1 --> Step2[Determine L_e from end condition table]
    Step2 --> Step3[Compute lambda equals L_e over r]
    Step3 --> Step4[Compute lambda_c equals pi times sqrt of E over sigma_y]
    Step4 --> Step5{Is lambda greater than lambda_c}
    Step5 -->|Yes| UseEuler[Apply Euler P_cr equals pi squared E I over L_e squared]
    Step5 -->|No| CheckIntermediate{Is lambda between lambda_min and lambda_c}
    CheckIntermediate -->|Yes| UseRankine[Apply Rankine P_R equals sigma_c A over 1 plus a lambda squared]
    CheckIntermediate -->|No| UseCrushing[Apply Crushing P equals sigma_c times A]
    UseEuler --> Output[Critical Load P_cr or P_R]
    UseRankine --> Output
    UseCrushing --> Output
    Output --> FOS[Divide by Factor of Safety]
    FOS --> Final[Permissible Safe Load]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in exams:** Mermaid flowcharts are not just illustrations — they are the *exact sequence* your solution should follow. Examiners reward clearly-stated "Given → Compute → Compare → Decide" logic with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define **slenderness ratio** and state its significance in the design of axially loaded compression members.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The slenderness ratio is defined as the ratio of the *effective length* of a compression member to the *radius of gyration* of its cross-section about the buckling axis: $\lambda = L_e / r$.
> **Significance:**
> (i) It is the master non-dimensional parameter that classifies a column as short, intermediate, or long.
> (ii) It directly governs the *failure mode* — low $\lambda$ → crushing, high $\lambda$ → elastic buckling.
> (iii) The Euler critical load is inversely proportional to $\lambda^2$, so a small increase in slenderness drastically reduces buckling capacity.
> (iv) It is used to select the appropriate design formula (Euler / Rankine / IS 800 curve).

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Differentiate between **crushing failure** and **buckling failure** in compression members.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Crushing Failure | Buckling Failure |
> |---|---|---|
> | Cause | Material reaches yield/crushing stress | Structural instability (lateral deflection) |
> | Slenderness | Short, stocky columns ($\lambda$ low) | Long, slender columns ($\lambda$ high) |
> | Stress at failure | Equals $\sigma_c$ | Far less than $\sigma_c$ (e.g., $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E/\lambda^2$) |
> | Nature | Ductile, gradual | Sudden, catastrophic |
> | Governing formula | $P = \sigma_c A$ | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ |
> | Prevention | Increase area, use higher-grade material | Increase $I$, reduce $L_e$, add lateral bracing |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — On Euler's Buckling Theory

**(a)** State the assumptions of Euler's theory and derive the expression for the **critical buckling load** of a pin-ended column. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **mild steel column** of **circular hollow section** has outer diameter **100 mm**, inner diameter **80 mm**, and length **4 m**. Both ends are *fixed*. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** and factor of safety = **3**. Find the **safe axial compressive load** using Euler's formula. (7 Marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Assumptions and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions of Euler's Theory** *(2 marks)*:
1. Column is initially perfectly straight, and the load is truly axial (concentric).
2. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law throughout.
3. Self-weight of the column is negligible.
4. Cross-section is uniform and the column buckles in a single curvature mode.
5. End conditions are ideal (frictionless pins or perfect fixity).
6. Failure is by **flexural buckling only** — no torsion, no local buckling.

**Derivation** *(5 marks)*: See the complete step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student is expected to write:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{EI}\,y = 0$$

with solution $y = A\cos(kx) + B\sin(kx)$ where $k^2 = P/EI$, apply boundary conditions $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$ → $\sin(kL) = 0$ → $kL = \pi$ → $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L^2$.

> Extend to general end conditions by replacing $L$ with $L_e$:
> $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Outer diameter $D = 100$ mm → $D_o = 100$ mm
- Inner diameter $d = 80$ mm → $D_i = 80$ mm
- Length $L = 4$ m $= 4000$ mm
- Both ends fixed → $L_e = 0.65\,L = 0.65 \times 4000 = 2600$ mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- FOS = 3

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area $A$**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(100^2 - 80^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}(10000 - 6400) = \frac{\pi}{4}(3600)$$

$$A = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Moment of Inertia $I$**

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(100^4 - 80^4\right)$$

$$100^4 = 10^8 = 100{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$80^4 = 4096 \times 10^4 = 40{,}960{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(100{,}000{,}000 - 40{,}960{,}000\right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 59{,}040{,}000$$

$$I = 924{,}375\,\pi \approx 2{,}903{,}036 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Euler's Critical Load $P_{cr}$**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2{,}903{,}036}{(2600)^2}$$

Numerator:
$$\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2{,}903{,}036 = 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2{,}903{,}036$$
$$= 9.8696 \times 5.806 \times 10^{11} \approx 5.7309 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:
$$(2600)^2 = 6{,}760{,}000 = 6.76 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{5.7309 \times 10^{12}}{6.76 \times 10^6} \approx 8.477 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 847.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Safe Load**

$$P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{FOS} = \frac{847.7}{3} \approx 282.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{P_{safe} \approx 282.6 \text{ kN}}$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — On Rankine's Formula and IS Code (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain why Euler's formula is *not valid* for short columns. Derive **Rankine's formula** and state where it is applicable. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **short steel column** of cross-section **200 mm × 200 mm** and length **1.5 m** carries an axial load. Both ends are *fixed*. Using **Rankine's formula**, find the **safe load** with a factor of safety of **3**. Take $\sigma_c = 320$ **N/mm²**, $a = 1/7500$, and $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²**. (7 Marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Limitations of Euler & Derivation of Rankine (7 Marks)

**Why Euler's formula fails for short columns** *(3 marks)*:
Euler's formula assumes that the column material remains within the *elastic limit* during buckling, i.e., $\sigma_{cr} \le \sigma_y$. For a short column, $\lambda$ is small, so $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$ becomes very large — typically exceeding the yield stress of the material. Hence the assumption of elastic behaviour is violated and the formula gives *unrealistically high* (and unsafe) predictions. Physically, the short column fails by *crushing/yielding*, not by buckling.

**Derivation of Rankine's Formula** *(4 marks)*: See **Section 3.3** of these notes for the full derivation. Key result:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{r}\right)^2}$$

**Applicability:** Valid for both short and long columns, smoothly transitioning between crushing-dominated and buckling-dominated behaviour. Most accurate for *intermediate slenderness* ranges.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Cross-section: 200 mm × 200 mm (square)
- Length $L = 1.5$ m $= 1500$ mm
- Both ends fixed → $L_e = 0.65 \times 1500 = 975$ mm
- $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm², $a = 1/7500$
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², FOS = 3

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area $A$**

$$A = 200 \times 200 = 40{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Moment of Inertia and Radius of Gyration $r$**

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 200^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^9}{12} = 1.333 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.333 \times 10^8}{40{,}000}} = \sqrt{3333.33} \approx 57.735 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Slenderness Ratio $\lambda$**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{975}{57.735} \approx 16.88$$

**Step 4: Rankine's Critical Load $P_R$**

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a\,\lambda^2} = \frac{320 \times 40{,}000}{1 + \dfrac{1}{7500}\,(16.88)^2}$$

Numerator: $320 \times 40{,}000 = 12{,}800{,}000$ N $= 12{,}800$ kN

Denominator term: $\dfrac{(16.88)^2}{7500} = \dfrac{284.93}{7500} \approx 0.03799$

$$P_R = \frac{12{,}800}{1 + 0.03799} = \frac{12{,}800}{1.03799} \approx 12{,}332 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Safe Load**

$$P_{safe} = \frac{P_R}{FOS} = \frac{12{,}332}{3} \approx 4110.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{P_{safe} \approx 4111 \text{ kN}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Students often forget to convert $L$ into $L_e$ using the end-condition factor. Using $L$ instead of $L_e$ will give **wrong slenderness ratio** and can lead to **unsafe design** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Unit trap:** $E$ is in N/mm² and $I$ in mm⁴ — keep all dimensions in **mm** to avoid mixing m and mm.
> 3. **FOS placement:** Factor of safety must be applied to the *critical load*, not to the stress. Always write "$P_{safe} = P_{cr} / \text{FOS}$" as the final step.
> 4. **Formula selection:** Using Euler's formula on a short column (or vice-versa) without checking the slenderness ratio is a guaranteed **3-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Forgetting $r$:** Many students compute $I$ but forget to compute $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ before finding $\lambda$ — examiners watch for this specifically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Axial Compression (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Axial compression** induces uniaxial compressive stress: $\sigma_c = P/A$.
- ☐ **Column / Strut** = member under axial compression; failure can be by *crushing* OR *buckling*.
- ☐ **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_e / r$ is the master parameter governing failure mode.
- ☐ **Effective length** $L_e$ depends on end conditions: Pin-Pin = $1.0\,L$, Fixed-Fixed = $0.65\,L$, Fixed-Free = $2.0\,L$, Fixed-Pinned = $0.8\,L$.
- ☐ **Euler's formula** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ applies only when $\lambda \ge \lambda_c$ (long/elastic columns).
- ☐ **Critical slenderness ratio** $\lambda_c = \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$ demarcates elastic from inelastic buckling.
- ☐ **Rankine's formula** $P_R = \sigma_c A / \left[1 + a(L_e/r)^2\right]$ applies to *intermediate* and *short* columns.
- ☐ **Crushing load** $P_c = \sigma_c A$ governs very short, stocky columns.
- ☐ **Factor of Safety (FOS)** is mandatory — buckling failures are *sudden* and *catastrophic* with no warning.
- ☐ **Increase buckling capacity by:** increasing $I$ (use hollow/box sections), reducing $L_e$ (better end fixity/bracing), or reducing $L$ (add intermediate supports).
- ☐ **Safety philosophy:** A compression member is *safe* only if $P_{applied} \le P_{cr}/\text{FOS}$ — never ignore this inequality.
- ☐ **KTU answer-writing tip:** Always draw a *free-body diagram* with end conditions, *list given data*, *state the chosen formula* with justification based on $\lambda$, then *plug in numbers* — this structure fetches full marks.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Axial Compression**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lateral Ties and Spirals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lateral Ties and Spirals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lateral ties** are closed-loop transverse reinforcement bars placed perpendicular (or at an angle) to the longitudinal reinforcement in a reinforced concrete (RC) column. Their primary structural role is to **confine the concrete core**, **prevent lateral buckling (bulging outward)** of the longitudinal bars under compression, and **resist shear forces** and diagonal tension cracks induced by lateral loads (seismic/wind).

**Spirals** (also called *helical reinforcement*) are continuous transverse reinforcement wound in the form of a helix around the longitudinal bars of a circular or rectangular column. Spirals provide **continuous, uniform, and superior confinement** compared to discrete lateral ties because the confining pressure acts along the entire length of the column without interruption at tie-spacing intervals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Both lateral ties and spirals are **mandatory ductility-detailing provisions** prescribed under **IS 456:2000 (Plain and Reinforced Concrete – Code of Practice)** and **IS 13920:2016 (Ductile Design and Detailing of Reinforced Concrete Structures)**. They are the *backbone of column safety design* in seismic and high-load zones.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bundle of drinking straws held in your hand. If you press the bundle from the top (compression), the straws will simply **bulge out sideways and buckle** because they have no lateral support. Now wrap a rubber band tightly around the bundle. The rubber band keeps the straws in place, prevents them from splaying outward, and the bundle can now carry much more load before failing.

- **Longitudinal bars + concrete core** = the bundle of straws (axial compression members)
- **Lateral ties / Spirals** = the rubber band (transverse restraint)
- **Bulging/buckling failure** = the catastrophic lateral expansion we want to prevent

The **tie spacing** is analogous to how tightly you wind the rubber band — closer spacing means stronger confinement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition (Exam-ready):**
> Lateral ties and spirals are **transverse steel reinforcements** placed around the main longitudinal bars of a column to (1) hold them in position during construction, (2) prevent buckling under compression, (3) confine the concrete core to enhance compressive strength and ductility, and (4) resist lateral shear forces.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum tie diameter | $\phi_t$ | $\mathbf{\ge \dfrac{1}{4}}$ of largest longitudinal bar diameter |
| Maximum tie spacing | $s_t$ | **Least of:** $300\ \text{mm}$, $16\phi_{main}$, $48\phi_{tie}$ |
| Minimum spiral ratio | $\rho_s$ | $\mathbf{\ge 0.36 \dfrac{A_g}{A_c} - 0.24}$ (for circular sections) |
| Yield strength of steel | $f_y$ | **Fe415 / Fe500** (commonly used) |
| Concrete grade | $f_{ck}$ | $\mathbf{M20}$ minimum (M25 for seismic zones) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Breakdown of Lateral Ties

### A. Functions of Lateral Ties (Step-by-step Logic)

1. **Holding Longitudinal Bars in Position**
   - During concrete pouring and vibration, longitudinal bars tend to displace.
   - Ties act as a **cage framework**, ensuring proper cover and bar alignment.
   - *Why:* Misaligned bars reduce effective depth and bonding, compromising strength.

2. **Preventing Buckling of Longitudinal Bars**
   - Long slender bars under compression tend to **buckle outward** between supports.
   - Ties act as **intermediate lateral supports**, reducing the unsupported length.
   - *How:* Slenderness ratio of longitudinal bar $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r} \le 100$ (acceptable), where $L_{eff}$ ≈ tie spacing.

3. **Confinement of Concrete Core**
   - Concrete under axial compression expands laterally (Poisson effect).
   - Ties apply **lateral confining pressure** $f_l$, enhancing core compressive strength.
   - Enhanced strength: $f'_{cc} = f'_{co} + k_1 \cdot f_l$ (Mander's model — simplified).

4. **Shear Resistance**
   - Ties contribute to **transverse shear capacity** of the column.
   - Shear reinforcement term: $V_s = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{s_t}$

### B. Types of Lateral Ties (IS Code Nomenclature)

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Tie / Single Hoop** | One continuous closed loop around perimeter | Small/rectangular columns |
| **Double Tie / Double Hoop** | Two overlapping closed loops (interlocking) | Larger columns with multiple bar layers |
| **Diamond / Cross-tie (Cross-tie)** | A tie anchored into the core by 90° + 135° hooks engaging peripheral hoops | Intermediate restraint for middle bars |
| **Supplementary Crossties** | Inner ties provided when **bar spacing > 75 mm** (face-to-face) | Required under IS 13920 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every corner and alternate longitudinal bar (when spacing > 75 mm c/c) **must have lateral support** by the corner of a tie with an included angle ≤ 135°. This is a critical KTU 2024 examination point.

### C. IS Code Provisions for Tie Spacing (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2)

The **pitch/spacing** of lateral ties is the **least** of:
