- $16$ times the diameter of the smallest longitudinal bar ($16\phi_{min}$)
- $48$ times the diameter of the tie bar ($48\phi_t$)
- The **least lateral dimension** of the column
- **300 mm**

$$\boxed{s_t = \min(16\phi_{min},\ 48\phi_t,\ B_{min},\ 300\ \text{mm})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **seismic zones (IS 13920)**, tie spacing is more stringent: at potential plastic hinge regions (top and bottom of column over $l_0$ or longer of $D_{col}$, $\dfrac{M}{V}$, $\dfrac{l_{clear}}{6}$):
> $s_t = \min\left(\dfrac{B_{min}}{4},\ 6\phi_{main},\ 100\ \text{mm}\right)$

## 2.2 Functional Breakdown of Spirals

### A. Why Spirals Are Superior to Ties

- **Continuous confinement**: No weak zones between tie levels (unlike discrete ties).
- **Triaxial stress state**: Confines core into a true **3D triaxial compression state**.
- **Enhanced ductility**: Columns can undergo large plastic deformations before failure — critical for seismic safety.
- **Higher axial capacity**: Effective core area is more uniformly loaded.

### B. Spiral Pitch (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3)

The **pitch of spiral** $p$ shall not exceed:
- **75 mm**
- $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of the **core diameter** $D_c$
- $3 \times $ spacing required for lateral ties (i.e., $3 \times s_t$)

$$\boxed{p = \min\left(75\ \text{mm},\ \dfrac{D_c}{6},\ 3s_t\right)}$$

### C. Minimum Spiral Reinforcement Ratio (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3)

The **volumetric ratio** of spiral reinforcement to concrete core is:

$$\boxed{\rho_s = \dfrac{V_{spiral}}{V_{core}} = \dfrac{4 a_s}{D_c \cdot p} \ge 0.36 \dfrac{A_g}{A_c} - 0.24}$$

Where:
- $a_s$ = cross-sectional area of spiral bar ($\text{mm}^2$)
- $D_c$ = core diameter (centre-to-centre of spiral, mm)
- $p$ = pitch of spiral (mm)
- $A_g$ = gross area of column section
- $A_c$ = area of concrete core (measured to outside of spiral)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet for KTU Board Exams** — memorize these expressions and their limit conditions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Limit / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tie spacing (IS 456) | $s_t = \min(16\phi_{min},\ 48\phi_t,\ B_{min},\ 300\ \text{mm})$ | mm |
| 2 | Tie spacing (IS 13920, hinge zone) | $s_t = \min(B_{min}/4,\ 6\phi_{main},\ 100\ \text{mm})$ | mm |
| 3 | Min. tie diameter | $\phi_t \ge \phi_{max}/4$ | mm |
| 4 | Spiral pitch | $p = \min(75,\ D_c/6,\ 3s_t)$ | mm |
| 5 | Min. spiral ratio (volumetric) | $\rho_s \ge 0.36\,A_g/A_c - 0.24$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Spiral ratio in terms of $a_s$ | $\rho_s = 4a_s/(D_c \cdot p)$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Confined core strength | $f'_{cc} = f'_{co} + k_1 f_l$ | MPa |
| 8 | Confining pressure (spiral) | $f_l = \dfrac{2 a_s f_y}{D_c \cdot s_t}$ | MPa |
| 9 | Shear contribution of ties | $V_s = 0.87 f_y A_{sv} d / s_t$ | N |
| 10 | Hook length of tie | $\ge 9\phi_t$ or $75$ mm (whichever greater) | mm |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise buildings**: Spirals in circular columns of lift cores and parking decks — improves ductility and resists multi-axial loads.
- **Bridges and flyovers**: Piers use spirals to absorb seismic and impact loads.
- **Industrial chimneys**: Circular hollow columns with spirals handle wind-induced lateral loads.
- **Earthquake-resistant design**: IS 13920 mandates closely-spaced ties at plastic hinge locations — saves lives by allowing the column to undergo ductile deformation instead of brittle shear failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Spiral Reinforcement Ratio

This is the **most critical derivation** in this topic — frequently asked in KTU Board Exams (14-mark level).

### Problem Statement
Derive the expression for the minimum volumetric ratio of spiral reinforcement in a circular column such that, on spalling of the outer concrete shell, the spiral can carry the entire vertical load that was previously carried by the concrete shell.

### Given / Assumed
- Column diameter (gross) = $D_g$, core diameter (to outside of spiral) = $D_c$
- Concrete cover spalls off → spiral now carries additional axial load
- Spiral steel area = $a_s$, pitch = $p$, yield strength = $f_y$
- Concrete strength = $f_{ck}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Area of concrete shell that spalls off**

The outer shell area that loses load-carrying capacity after spalling:

$$A_{shell} = A_g - A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_g^2 - D_c^2\right)$$

**Step 2 — Vertical load carried by shell (before failure)**

$$P_{shell} = f_{ck} \cdot A_{shell} = f_{ck} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_g^2 - D_c^2\right)$$

**Step 3 — Volume of spiral in one pitch length**

In one pitch length $p$, the spiral makes exactly **one turn** around the core. Its length along the helix is $\approx \pi D_c$ (for closely pitched spirals). Thus volume of spiral:

$$V_{spiral} = a_s \cdot \pi D_c \quad (\text{per pitch } p)$$

**Step 4 — Volume of concrete core (per pitch p)**

$$V_{core} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_c^2 \cdot p$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric spiral ratio**

$$\rho_s = \dfrac{V_{spiral}}{V_{core}} = \dfrac{a_s \cdot \pi D_c}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_c^2 \cdot p} = \dfrac{4 a_s}{D_c \cdot p}$$

**Step 6 — Equilibrium of spiral as a helix under axial load**

Consider a horizontal free-body of the spiral cut at one location. The spiral carries a **vertical force component** $F_v$ and a **horizontal radial force** $F_h$. From geometry (helix angle $\alpha \approx \tan\alpha = p/(\pi D_c)$, very small):

$$F_v = F \sin\alpha \approx F \cdot \dfrac{p}{\pi D_c}$$

$$F_h = F \cos\alpha \approx F$$

The vertical component of spiral force (per turn) must equal $f_y \cdot a_s$:

$$F_v = f_y \cdot a_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \dfrac{f_y \cdot a_s \cdot \pi D_c}{p}$$

**Step 7 — Vertical load carrying capacity of spiral per pitch**

The spiral's contribution to vertical load per pitch = $F_v = f_y a_s$ (directly from yield).

**Step 8 — Apply safety requirement**

The spiral must carry the spalled shell load safely. Apply a safety factor of **1.67** (used in IS code philosophy) on the spiral yield strength:

$$f_y \cdot a_s \ge \dfrac{f_{ck} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_g^2 - D_c^2\right)}{1.67 \cdot p}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute spiral ratio**

Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{4}{D_c p}$:

$$\dfrac{4 f_y a_s}{D_c p} \ge \dfrac{f_{ck} \cdot \pi\left(D_g^2 - D_c^2\right)}{1.67 \cdot D_c \cdot p^2} \cdot \dfrac{4}{1}$$

This gets cumbersome. Using a **simplified approach** widely adopted in textbooks (Pillai & Menon / Sinha & Roy):

**Assumption for simplification:** $A_g / A_c \approx D_g^2 / D_c^2$ (since both are circles)

Equating spiral capacity (after spalling) to spalled load:

$$\boxed{\rho_s = 0.36 \dfrac{A_g}{A_c} - 0.24}$$

This is the **final IS 456 expression**.

### Numerical Check (Worked Example)

**Given:** Circular column, $D_g = 500$ mm, $D_c = 450$ mm (clear cover 25 mm + spiral dia assumed 12 mm), $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Areas**

$$A_g = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(500)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^5\ \text{mm}^2$$

$$A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(450)^2 = 1.5904 \times 10^5\ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Spiral ratio required**

$$\rho_s = 0.36 \times \dfrac{1.9635}{1.5904} - 0.24 = 0.36 \times 1.2346 - 0.24 = 0.4445 - 0.24 = 0.2045$$

So $\rho_s \ge 0.2045$ (about **20.45%** volumetrically).

**Step 3 — Select pitch**

Let pitch $p = 50$ mm (within limits: $<75$ and $<D_c/6=75$ mm — OK).

**Step 4 — Solve for spiral area**

From $\rho_s = \dfrac{4 a_s}{D_c p}$:

$$a_s = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot D_c \cdot p}{4} = \dfrac{0.2045 \times 450 \times 50}{4} = 1150.3\ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Select spiral bar diameter**

For $a_s = 1150.3\ \text{mm}^2$, use **16 mm dia** bar ($a_s = 201\ \text{mm}^2$) — single spiral insufficient. Use **two spirals of 12 mm dia** ($2 \times 113 = 226\ \text{mm}^2$) or one spiral of **16 mm** ($201\ \text{mm}^2$).

Adjust pitch: with **12 mm bar** (single spiral), $a_s = 113\ \text{mm}^2$:

$$p = \dfrac{4 a_s}{\rho_s \cdot D_c} = \dfrac{4 \times 113}{0.2045 \times 450} = \dfrac{452}{92.025} \approx 4.91\ \text{mm}$$

That's too close. Choose **double spiral of 10 mm dia** ($2 \times 78.5 = 157\ \text{mm}^2$):

$$p = \dfrac{4 \times 157}{0.2045 \times 450} = \dfrac{628}{92.025} \approx 6.82\ \text{mm}$$

Still too close. **Increase column size** or use **higher yield steel**. Practical conclusion: **double spirals of 12 mm @ 50 mm pitch** is workable for larger columns.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 3.2 Confinement Pressure Derivation (Mander's Model — Simplified)

For a circular column with spiral of yield strength $f_y$, area $a_s$, pitch $p$, core diameter $D_c$:

**Step 1** — The spiral exerts **radial confining pressure** on the concrete core. From equilibrium of one spiral turn (free-body cut horizontally):

$$\text{Horizontal force from spiral} = F_h = \dfrac{f_y \cdot a_s \cdot \pi D_c}{p} \cdot \cos\alpha \approx \dfrac{f_y \cdot a_s \cdot \pi D_c}{p}$$

(For small helix angle, $\cos\alpha \approx 1$.)

**Step 2** — The confining pressure is this horizontal force per unit core circumferential area:

$$f_l = \dfrac{F_h}{\text{circumference} \times 1\ \text{mm}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{unit length}} = \dfrac{2 f_y a_s}{D_c \cdot p}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute spiral ratio $\rho_s = 4a_s/(D_c p)$:

$$f_l = \dfrac{f_y \cdot \rho_s}{2}$$

**Step 4** — Confined concrete strength:

$$f'_{cc} = f'_{co}\left(2.254\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{7.94 f_l}{f'_{co}}} - 2 \dfrac{f_l}{f'_{co}} - 1.254\right)$$

This is **Mander's full formula** (used in advanced seismic analysis).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Ties vs. Spirals

| Criterion | Lateral Ties | Spirals |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Discrete closed loops | Continuous helix |
| Confinement | Discontinuous (weak zones between ties) | Continuous uniform |
| Construction complexity | Simpler | More complex (winding) |
| Column shape | Any (rectangular/square/circular) | Mostly circular (or square with care) |
| Ductility enhancement | Moderate | High |
| Cost of steel | Less | More (continuous bar) |
| IS 456 spacing rule | $s_t$ formula above | $p$ formula above |
| Code application | Universal | Special columns / seismic |
| Failure mode after cover spall | Sudden load drop | Gradual ductile load transfer |

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Lateral Ties & Spirals in a Column

Below is a **Mermaid block-level functional flow** showing how lateral ties and spirals interact with the longitudinal bars and concrete core to deliver structural safety.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Axial Load P Applied on Column"] --> nodeB["Compression Acts on Concrete Core and Longitudinal Bars"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Concrete Tends to Expand Laterally (Poisson Effect)"}
    nodeC --> nodeD["Longitudinal Bars Tend to Buckle Outward"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Concrete Core Tends to Dilate"]

    nodeD --> nodeF["Lateral Ties Restrain Bars at Discrete Points"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Spirals Apply Continuous Radial Confining Pressure fl"]

    nodeF --> nodeH["Reduced Unsupported Length of Bars"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Buckling Resisted Continuously"]

    nodeH --> nodeI["Enhanced Concrete Core Strength fcc"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Increased Column Ductility"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Improved Shear Resistance Vs"]

    nodeI --> nodeL["Safer Load Transfer After Concrete Cover Spalls"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL

    nodeL --> nodeM["Ultimate Structural Safety Under Axial Plus Lateral Loads"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Tie Pattern in a Rectangular Column (ASCII Representation)

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐  ← Outer Hoop (closed tie)
        │  ●               ●               ●  │     (covers all corner bars)
        │                                     │
        │  ●        ●        ●        ●        │  ← Inner Crossties anchored
        │                                     │     to outer hoop with hooks
        │  ●               ●               ●  │
        └─────────────────────────────────────┘
                ↑                ↑
          Crosstie 1      Crosstie 2
        (90° + 135° hooks engaging outer hoop)
```

**Layout Rule (IS 13920):** Every corner bar **must be hooked by a tie corner at ≤135°**. Any longitudinal bar located **more than 75 mm** (face-to-face) from a laterally supported bar must itself be supported by a crosstie.

## 4.3 Schematic — Spiral in a Circular Column (ASCII Representation)

```
        Top View of Column Section:
        ┌───────────────────────┐
        │   ●   ●   ●   ●   ●   │  ← 8 longitudinal bars
        │  ●                 ●  │     arranged on circle
        │ ●  ╭─────────────╮  ● │
        │ ●  │   Spiral    │  ● │  ← Continuous helical
        │ ●  │   winding   │  ● │     spiral (drawn as
        │ ●  │   around    │  ● │     two visible loops)
        │ ●  │   core      │  ● │
        │ ●  ╰─────────────╯  ● │
        │  ●                 ●  │
        │   ●   ●   ●   ●   ●   │
        └───────────────────────┘

        Side View (unrolled spiral):
        ╭─╮   ╭─╮   ╭─╮   ╭─╮
        │ │   │ │   │ │   │ │     ← Each loop = 1 pitch p
        ╰─╯   ╰─╯   ╰─╯   ╰─╯
        ←─p─→
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the four functions of lateral ties in a reinforced concrete column.**

**Model Answer:**
The four functions of lateral ties are:
1. **Hold longitudinal reinforcement** in position during casting and compaction of concrete.
2. **Prevent buckling** of longitudinal bars under axial compression by providing lateral support at discrete intervals.
3. **Confine the concrete core**, thereby enhancing its compressive strength and ductility through triaxial stress state.
4. **Resist transverse shear forces**, contributing to the column's shear capacity along with main reinforcement.

*(Award full 3 marks for all four; partial marks for 2–3 correct.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between lateral ties and spirals in RC columns (any three points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Lateral Ties | Spirals |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discrete closed loops | Continuous helical winding |
| 2 | Suitable for any cross-section (rect./sq./circ.) | Mostly used for circular columns |
| 3 | Confinement is discontinuous (weak zones) | Confinement is continuous and uniform |
| 4 | Simpler to fabricate and place | Requires special bending equipment |
| 5 | Code: IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2 | Code: IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3 |

*(Any three points for 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**Q. A (a)** *Explain the IS 456:2000 code provisions for the spacing and diameter of lateral ties in a reinforced concrete column. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Minimum Diameter of Lateral Tie (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2 c):**

$$\phi_t \ge \dfrac{1}{4}\phi_{main,max}$$

i.e., the diameter of the tie bar shall be **at least one-fourth** of the largest diameter of the longitudinal bar used in the column.

**(ii) Maximum Spacing of Lateral Ties:**

The spacing $s_t$ shall be the **least** of:
- The **least lateral dimension** of the column ($B_{min}$)
- **Sixteen times** the diameter of the smallest longitudinal bar ($16\phi_{min}$)
- **Forty-eight times** the diameter of the tie bar ($48\phi_t$)
- **300 mm**

$$\boxed{s_t = \min(B_{min},\ 16\phi_{min},\ 48\phi_t,\ 300\ \text{mm})}$$

**(iii) Arrangement of Ties (IS 13920 additional):**

- Every corner and alternate longitudinal bar shall be tied by the **corner of a tie** at an included angle ≤ 135°.
- Where the bar-to-bar spacing **exceeds 75 mm** (face-to-face), additional **crossties** shall be provided.
- In **potential plastic hinge zones** of columns (under seismic loads), the spacing is reduced to:
$$s_t = \min\left(\dfrac{B_{min}}{4},\ 6\phi_{main},\ 100\ \text{mm}\right)$$

**(iv) Anchorage of Ties:**

The ends of the tie shall be properly anchored by **135° hooks** with straight extensions of at least $\mathbf{9\phi_t}$ or **75 mm** (whichever is greater) — this prevents the tie from opening under load.

**Conclusion:** Proper tie detailing is essential for column safety against buckling, confinement, and shear failure.

---

**Q. A (b)** *A 400 mm × 400 mm RC column is reinforced with 8 bars of 20 mm diameter. Using lateral ties, determine the spacing and diameter of ties as per IS 456. The column is **NOT in a seismic zone**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Column size: $B \times D = 400\ \text{mm} \times 400\ \text{mm}$
- Longitudinal bars: 8 nos. of 20 mm dia. → $\phi_{main} = 20\ \text{mm}$, $\phi_{min} = 20\ \text{mm}$
- Non-seismic zone → IS 456 provisions apply

**Step 1 — Diameter of Tie**

$$\phi_t \ge \dfrac{\phi_{main,max}}{4} = \dfrac{20}{4} = 5\ \text{mm}$$

Use **8 mm diameter** ties (standard available size, $\ge 5$ mm).

**Step 2 — Spacing Criteria**

Check all four limits:
- Least lateral dimension = **400 mm**
- $16\phi_{min} = 16 \times 20 = \mathbf{320\ mm}$
- $48\phi_t = 48 \times 8 = \mathbf{384\ \text{mm}}$
- **300 mm**

**Step 3 — Select Spacing**

$s_t = \min(400,\ 320,\ 384,\ 300) = \mathbf{300\ \text{mm}}$

Provide **8 mm dia. lateral ties @ 300 mm c/c spacing**.

**Step 4 — Crosstie Check**

Bar-to-bar spacing: $400/3 \approx 133\ \text{mm}$ (3 spaces for 4 bars on each face).

Wait — for 8 bars in 400 mm square: spacing face-to-face = $\dfrac{400 - 2 \times 40 - 20}{2} = 150\ \text{mm}$ (where 40 is cover + half tie dia. allowance).

Since 150 mm > 75 mm, **supplementary crossties** are required for the intermediate bars.

**Conclusion:**
- **Tie:** 8 mm dia. @ **300 mm c/c**
- **Crossties:** Required for intermediate bars (3 nos. per face approximately).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**Q. B (a)** *Explain the design philosophy behind providing spirals in circular RC columns. Derive the expression for the minimum volumetric ratio of spiral reinforcement as per IS 456. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Design Philosophy:**

Spirals are provided in circular columns primarily to ensure **safety against sudden collapse** when the outer concrete cover spalls off under overload (impact, earthquake, fire, or excessive axial load). Once the cover spalls:

- The cross-section is reduced to the **core only**.
- The spiral must now carry the **additional axial load** previously borne by the spalled cover, in addition to its own confinement duty.
- This **progressive failure mechanism** (cover spalls → spiral engages → ductile core failure) is **safer and more ductile** than the sudden brittle failure of a tied column.

**Derivation (Ref. Section 3.1 above):**

Setting up equilibrium between the spiral's vertical load-carrying capacity (after yielding) and the load transferred from the spalled concrete shell, with an IS-code safety factor of 1.67 applied to spiral yield strength:

$$\boxed{\rho_s = 0.36 \dfrac{A_g}{A_c} - 0.24}$$

This minimum ensures the spiral can develop enough axial capacity to redistribute loads safely after cover spalling.

**Code reference:** IS 456:2000 Clause 26.5.3.3.

---

**Q. B (b)** *Design the spiral reinforcement for a circular column of diameter 500 mm with 6 longitudinal bars of 20 mm diameter. Concrete grade M25, steel Fe415. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Column diameter $D_g = 500$ mm
- Cover = 50 mm; assume spiral diameter $\phi_t = 12$ mm
- Core diameter $D_c = D_g - 2(\text{cover} + \phi_t) = 500 - 2(50 + 12) = 500 - 124 = \mathbf{376\ \text{mm}}$
- $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa

**Step 1 — Areas**

$$A_g = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(500)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^5\ \text{mm}^2$$

$$A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(376)^2 = 1.1104 \times 10^5\ \text{mm}^2$$

$$\dfrac{A_g}{A_c} = \dfrac{1.9635}{1.1104} = 1.768$$

**Step 2 — Required spiral ratio**

$$\rho_s = 0.36 \times 1.768 - 0.24 = 0.6365 - 0.24 = \mathbf{0.3965}$$

**Step 3 — Pitch limits**

- Maximum pitch = 75 mm
- $D_c/6 = 376/6 = 62.7\ \text{mm}$
- $3s_t$ — assuming non-seismic: $s_t = \min(16\times 20,\ 48 \times 12,\ 500,\ 300) = \min(320,\ 576,\ 500,\ 300) = 300\ \text{mm}$
- $3s_t = 900\ \text{mm}$

So $p = \min(75,\ 62.7,\ 900) = \mathbf{62.7\ \text{mm}}$ → adopt **60 mm pitch**.

**Step 4 — Area of spiral bar required**

$$a_s = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot D_c \cdot p}{4} = \dfrac{0.3965 \times 376 \times 60}{4} = \dfrac{8943.84}{4} = 2235.96\ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Select spiral bar diameter**

Single 12 mm bar provides only **113 mm²** — insufficient. Need $a_s = 2236\ \text{mm}^2$.

Options:
- **One 12 mm spiral + extension**: impractical
- **Double spiral of 12 mm**: $2 \times 113 = 226\ \text{mm}^2$ — still insufficient
- **Use larger bar**: 16 mm gives $201\ \text{mm}^2$; 20 mm gives $314\ \text{mm}^2$ — both insufficient singly

**Practical design adjustment:**
- Increase pitch → reduce $\rho_s$ required (NOT possible, $\rho_s$ is min.)
- Increase column size or use higher strength concrete.

**Alternative (using single 12 mm spiral):**

Reverse calculation: with $a_s = 113\ \text{mm}^2$ (single 12 mm dia), the required pitch is:

$$p = \dfrac{4 a_s}{\rho_s D_c} = \dfrac{4 \times 113}{0.3965 \times 376} = \dfrac{452}{149.084} = 3.03\ \text{mm}$$

This is impractical (min. 60 mm in practice). Therefore, in practice either:
- Column size is **increased to 600 mm or more**, OR
- **Higher grade concrete (M30+)** is used, OR
- **Multiple spirals** (double or triple) are wound simultaneously.

**Practical Conclusion:**
For a 500 mm circular column, use **double spiral of 12 mm dia @ 60 mm pitch** with extra longitudinal bars to enhance core confinement. Re-check with **IS 13920** if seismic.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Ties & Spirals Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "LEAST of" rule** — Students write all four criteria but don't take the minimum. This costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Ignoring crosstie requirement** — When bar spacing > 75 mm, crossties must be shown in sketches. Drawings without crossties lose **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Hook angle confusion** — In seismic zones, ties must use **135° hooks with straight extension ≥ 10$\phi$** (IS 13920), NOT 90° hooks. Easy to mix up with non-seismic 90° hook provisions.
> 4. **Spiral pitch numerical errors** — Forgetting to convert $D_c$ from gross to **core diameter** (subtracting cover + spiral dia.). This gives wrong pitch.
> 5. **Not checking all three pitch limits** — 75 mm, $D_c/6$, and $3s_t$ — students often check only 75 mm.
> 6. **Volumetric ratio misinterpretation** — $\rho_s = 4a_s/(D_c p)$ is **not** $A_s/(b d)$ — students wrongly use longitudinal bar formula.
> 7. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always mention **mm** and **mm²** explicitly.
> 8. **Confining the term "tie" with "stirrups"** — Stirrups are in **beams**; **ties** are in **columns**. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Lateral Ties** = closed transverse loops at discrete spacing to **confine concrete** + **prevent bar buckling** + **resist shear**.
- **Spirals** = continuous helix for **superior uniform confinement**; mostly circular columns.
- **Tie dia** ≥ $\dfrac{\phi_{main,max}}{4}$ (commonly 8 mm or 10 mm used).
- **Tie spacing (IS 456)** = $\min(B_{min},\ 16\phi_{min},\ 48\phi_t,\ 300\ \text{mm})$.
- **Tie spacing (IS 13920, hinge zone)** = $\min(B_{min}/4,\ 6\phi_{main},\ 100\ \text{mm})$.
- **Spiral pitch** = $\min(75\ \text{mm},\ D_c/6,\ 3s_t)$.
- **Min. spiral ratio** $\rho_s \ge 0.36 \dfrac{A_g}{A_c} - 0.24$.
- **Spiral ratio expression** $\rho_s = \dfrac{4 a_s}{D_c p}$.
- **Hook requirement** ≥ $9\phi_t$ or 75 mm (whichever greater); 135° in seismic zones.
- **Crossties** mandatory if bar spacing > 75 mm (face-to-face).
- **Confinement pressure** $f_l = \dfrac{2 f_y a_s}{D_c p}$ → enhances $f_{ck}$.
- **After cover spalling**, spiral must carry the additional axial load safely → IS 456 derivation uses safety factor 1.67.
- **Tied columns** fail **suddenly** after cover spall; **spiral columns** fail **progressively and ductilely** → preferred for seismic safety.
- **References:** IS 456:2000 Cl. 26.5.3.2 (ties) & Cl. 26.5.3.3 (spirals); IS 13920:2016 (seismic detailing).

---

### 🔑 Master Mnemonic (For Last-Minute Revision)

> **"T-I-E-S"** for **T**ies: **T**ied, **I**ntervals, **E**very corner, **S**pacing = min(16$\phi$, 48$\phi_t$, B, 300).
>
> **"S-P-I-R-A-L-S"** for Spirals: **S**piral ratio ≥ 0.36$A_g/A_c$ − 0.24; **P**itch ≤ 75 mm, $D_c/6$, $3s_t$; **I**ncrease ductility; **R**einforce helically; **A**fter spalling ductile; **L**ateral pressure continuous; **S**afer in seismic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns** refers to the state of combined stress induced in a short, axially loaded structural member (column/strut) of rectangular cross-section when the applied compressive load is **eccentric** — i.e., it does not pass through the geometric centroid of the section but acts at a finite perpendicular distance (eccentricity, $e$) from it.

This eccentric load is statically equivalent to:
- An **axial compressive force** $P$ acting through the centroid, AND
- A **bending moment** $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis perpendicular to the direction of eccentricity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"When a column section is subjected to an eccentric compressive load, the stress at any point on the cross-section is the algebraic sum of the uniform direct (axial) compressive stress and the bending stress produced by the equivalent moment. The resulting stress distribution may be wholly compressive or partly tensile, depending on the magnitude of the eccentricity relative to the section's dimensions."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting on a bench (a rectangular wooden plank) placed at its **center** — the bench experiences a uniform push downward at all points. There is no tendency for it to tilt. But if you sit at the **edge** of the bench, two things happen simultaneously:
1. The **entire bench is being compressed** (everyone pushes down on the same material).
2. Because your weight is offset from the center, the bench wants to **tip or bend** — the side closer to you is squeezed harder, while the far edge is squeezed less (or even stretched/tensed in extreme cases).

This is exactly what happens to a column with an eccentric load:
- The **direct compression** tries to squash the entire section uniformly.
- The **bending effect** tries to make one fiber more compressed and the opposite fiber less compressed (or stretched).

The resultant stress at any fiber = Direct stress ± Bending stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in Safety Design?**
> Masonry columns (brick, stone, plain concrete) **cannot resist tension** — they crack on the tension face. The "middle-third rule" tells us exactly how far the load can be offset before tension develops. This is a **fundamental serviceability and safety criterion** in KTU board examinations and in real-world design of dam abutments, retaining walls, and brick piers.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notations

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------:|
| $P$ | Axial compressive load | **N** or **kN** |
| $e$ | Eccentricity of load from centroid | **mm** or **m** |
| $b$ | Width of rectangular section | **mm** or **m** |
| $d$ | Depth of rectangular section (in plane of bending) | **mm** or **m** |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area $= b \cdot d$ | **mm²** or **m²** |
| $I$ | Moment of inertia about centroidal axis $= \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | **mm⁴** or **m⁴** |
| $Z$ | Section modulus $= \dfrac{I}{(d/2)} = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ | **mm³** or **m³** |
| $k$ | Radius of gyration / half-depth $= d/2$ | **mm** or **m** |
| $y$ | Distance of extreme fiber from neutral axis | **mm** or **m** |
| $\sigma_{c,max}$ | Maximum compressive stress | **N/mm²** or **MPa** |
| $\sigma_{c,min}$ | Minimum (or tensile) stress at opposite fiber | **N/mm²** or **MPa** |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus of elasticity | **N/mm²** or **MPa** |

---

### 1.4 Stress Combination: The Fundamental Equation

The **resultant stress** at any fiber located at distance $y$ from the centroidal axis (where bending occurs about the axis perpendicular to eccentricity) is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M}{Z}$$

- The **first term** $\dfrac{P}{A}$ is the uniform **direct compressive stress**.
- The **second term** $\dfrac{M}{Z}$ is the **bending stress** (compressive on the side toward the load, tensile on the far side).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - Compression is taken as **positive (+)**.
> - Tension is taken as **negative (−)**.
> - Bending stress is **compressive (+)** on the side where the eccentric load lies, and **tensile (−)** on the opposite face.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Resolution of Eccentric Load

When a load $P$ is applied with eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis, the load is resolved into two equivalent effects at the centroid:

1. **Direct (Axial) Force:** $P$ acting along the centroidal axis → produces uniform stress $\sigma_{d} = \dfrac{P}{A}$.
2. **Bending Moment:** $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis → produces bending stress $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$.

### 2.2 Stress Distribution Across the Section

For a **rectangular column** of width $b$ and depth $d$, with eccentricity $e$ measured in the plane of depth:

- The **bending moment** $M = P \cdot e$ acts about the axis perpendicular to the direction of $e$.
- Maximum bending stress occurs at the **extreme fibers** ($y = \pm d/2$).

The stress at any point at distance $y$ from the centroidal axis:

$$\sigma_{y} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The stress distribution across the section is **linear** (because bending stress is linear and direct stress is uniform), forming a **trapezoidal stress block** when both stresses are of the same sign, or a **triangular-over-rectangular** block when the eccentricity is large enough to produce tension.

### 2.3 Maximum and Minimum Stresses (KTU Board Critical)

Substituting $y = \pm d/2$ (extreme fibers) in the combined stress equation:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{bd} - \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

These are the **two most tested formulas** in KTU board examinations on this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compression is positive, tension is negative.** So:
> - $\sigma_{max}$ (compressive, on the side of the load) is the larger positive value.
> - $\sigma_{min}$ is the value at the far fiber — it can be compressive (if $e$ is small) or tensile (if $e$ is large).

### 2.4 The Middle-Third Rule (Kern/Core Condition)

A **critical safety concept** for brittle materials (masonry, plain concrete, cast iron) is that the section should **not be subjected to any tensile stress**. This requires $\sigma_{min} \geq 0$:

$$\frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right) \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - \frac{6e}{d} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \leq \frac{d}{6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Middle-Third Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> *"For a rectangular column to have no tensile stress on any part of its cross-section under an eccentric compressive load, the load must lie within the middle-third of the section (i.e., the eccentricity from the centroid must not exceed one-sixth of the depth in the plane of bending)."*
>
> This defines the **Kern (Core) of the section**: a rhombus with vertices at $\pm d/6$ and $\pm b/6$ from the centroid.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Sign Convention |
|:-:|:--------|:------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{d} = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{P}{bd}$ | Direct (uniform) compressive stress | +ve (comp.) |
| 2 | $M = P \cdot e$ | Equivalent bending moment due to eccentricity | — |
| 3 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{6 P e}{b d^{2}}$ | Maximum bending stress at extreme fiber | ±ve |
| 4 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{bd}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{d}\right)$ | Max compressive stress (at fiber near load) | +ve |
| 5 | $\sigma_{min} = \dfrac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \dfrac{6e}{d}\right)$ | Min stress (at far fiber; tensile if $e > d/6$) | ±ve |
| 6 | $e \leq \dfrac{d}{6}$ | Middle-third rule (no tension) | Inequality |
| 7 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{FOS}$ | Allowable compressive stress design check | +ve |
| 8 | $f_{t} = \dfrac{\vert \sigma_{min} \vert}{FOS}$ | Allowable tensile stress design check (if applicable) | +ve |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of rectangular section | — |
| 10 | $Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ | Section modulus of rectangular section | — |
| 11 | $e_{max} = \dfrac{d}{6}$ | Maximum eccentricity for no tension | Limit value |
| 12 | $\sigma_{c,max} = \dfrac{2N}{3A_{comp}}$ | Stress in triangular stress block (no tension case) | +ve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The middle-third rule is **not just academic** — it is the design basis for:
> - Masonry dam cross-sections
> - Brick column footings
> - Gravity retaining wall stems
> - Chimneys and towers under wind load
> - Pre-cast concrete columns

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Stress Equation

#### Starting Point: Eccentric Load Resolution

A load $P$ acts at a point offset by eccentricity $e$ from the centroid $G$ of a rectangular column of width $b$ and depth $d$.

**Step 1:** Shift the load from its point of application to the centroid $G$ by introducing a **couple** (pure moment) equal and opposite to maintain equilibrium.

At the centroid, two effects are produced:
- A pure axial compressive force $P$ along the column axis.
- A bending moment $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis perpendicular to the direction of eccentricity.

**Step 2:** Compute the **direct (axial) stress** — uniform across the section:

$$\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{b \cdot d}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **bending stress** at a fiber distance $y$ from the neutral axis (centroidal axis):

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where $I = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ is the moment of inertia about the relevant centroidal axis.

**Step 4:** Apply the **principle of superposition** (valid for linearly elastic materials with small deformations):

$$\sigma_{total}(y) = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

Substituting $M = P e$:

$$\sigma_{total}(y) = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{P e \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 5:** At the **extreme fiber** ($y = +d/2$ on the side of the load):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{P e \cdot (d/2)}{b d^{3} / 12} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{12 P e \cdot d}{2 b d^{3}} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}}$$

Factoring out $\dfrac{P}{bd}$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

**Step 6:** At the **opposite extreme fiber** ($y = -d/2$ on the far side):

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{bd} - \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two equations are the **final, board-ready form** of the combined stress formulas. KTU examiners expect them in this factored form for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Middle-Third Rule

**Step 1:** For **no tension** anywhere on the cross-section, the minimum stress must be non-negative:

$$\sigma_{min} \geq 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expression:

$$\frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right) \geq 0$$

**Step 3:** Since $P$, $b$, $d$ are all positive, the inequality reduces to:

$$1 - \frac{6e}{d} \geq 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $e$:

$$\frac{6e}{d} \leq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \leq \frac{d}{6}$$

**Step 5:** This means the **load point** must lie within a distance $\dfrac{d}{6}$ on either side of the centroid. The total "no-tension zone" has a width of $\dfrac{d}{6} + \dfrac{d}{6} = \dfrac{d}{3}$, which is the **middle third** of the depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The "kern" (or core) of a rectangular section is a **rhombus** with vertices on the centroidal axes at distances $\pm b/6$ and $\pm d/6$. If the load point (foot of the load vector) lies **inside this rhombus**, the entire section is in compression.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A rectangular column of section **300 mm × 200 mm** carries an eccentric compressive load of **400 kN** at an eccentricity of **50 mm** along the major axis (depth direction). Calculate the maximum and minimum stresses. Check if the section develops any tension.

#### **Given:**
- $P = 400$ kN $= 400 \times 10^{3}$ N
- $b = 200$ mm (width)
- $d = 300$ mm (depth — direction of eccentricity)
- $e = 50$ mm

#### **Step 1: Compute Area**

$$A = b \cdot d = 200 \times 300 = 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

#### **Step 2: Compute Direct Stress**

$$\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{400 \times 10^{3}}{60{,}000} = 6.667 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive)}$$

#### **Step 3: Compute Section Modulus**

$$Z = \frac{b d^{2}}{6} = \frac{200 \times (300)^{2}}{6} = \frac{200 \times 90{,}000}{6} = 3 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

#### **Step 4: Compute Bending Moment**

$$M = P \cdot e = 400 \times 10^{3} \times 50 = 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

#### **Step 5: Compute Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{20 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{6}} = 6.667 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

#### **Step 6: Compute Maximum Compressive Stress**

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = 6.667 + 6.667 = 13.333 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive)}$$

#### **Step 7: Compute Minimum Stress**

$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} = 6.667 - 6.667 = 0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

#### **Step 8: Verify Middle-Third Rule**

$$\frac{d}{6} = \frac{300}{6} = 50 \text{ mm} = e \quad \text{✓ (boundary case)}$$

#### **Conclusion:**
The eccentricity is exactly at the **limit** $e = d/6$. The minimum stress is **zero** (no tension, but no reserve safety). The maximum compressive stress is **13.33 MPa**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to check the **middle-third condition** and conclude "no tension, design is safe." But here $e = d/6$ is a **boundary case** with zero stress on the far fiber. Any practical FOS would be violated. The design should be revised.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Eccentricity Range | $\sigma_{min}$ Sign | Stress Block Shape | Material Suitability |
|:------------------:|:--------------------:|:------------------:|:--------------------:|
| $e < d/6$ | Positive (compressive) | Trapezoid | Masonry, concrete, cast iron, brick — **SAFE** |
| $e = d/6$ | Zero | Triangle on rectangle | **Limit case** — not recommended |
| $e > d/6$ | Negative (tensile) | Triangle + partial tension zone | Requires ductile material (steel, RCC) |

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Resolution of Eccentric Load

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Eccentric Load P applied at distance e from centroid G"] --> B["Load Resolution at Centroid"]
    B --> C["Direct Axial Force P along centroidal axis"]
    B --> D["Bending Moment M = P x e about centroidal axis"]
    C --> E["Direct Stress sigma_d = P / A = P / b x d"]
    D --> F["Bending Stress sigma_b = M y / I = 6 P e / b d squared"]
    E --> G["Combined Stress sigma at any fiber"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["sigma_max = P over b d times one plus six e over d"]
    G --> I["sigma_min = P over b d times one minus six e over d"]
    H --> J["Maximum compressive stress at fiber near load"]
    I --> K["Minimum stress at far fiber"]
    K --> L{"Check e is less than or equal to d over 6?"}
    L -->|Yes| M["No tension: section is wholly in compression - SAFE for masonry"]
    L -->|No| N["Tension develops: requires ductile material like steel or RCC"]
```

```mermaid
graph LR

        A3 --> A4["ENTIRE SECTION IN COMPRESSION - No Cracking"]
    end
    subgraph S2["CASE 2: Boundary Eccentricity e equals d over 6"]
        B1["Triangular-over-Rectangle Stress Block"] --> B2["sigma_min equals zero at far edge"]
        B2 --> B3["LIMIT CASE - Design is on the verge of cracking"]
    end
    subgraph S3["CASE 3: Large Eccentricity e greater than d over 6"]
        C1["Triangular Stress Block with TENSION ZONE"] --> C2["sigma_min is NEGATIVE tensile"]
        C2 --> C3["TENSION at far fiber - Masonry will CRACK"]
        C3 --> C4["Use ductile material: Steel or RCC Column"]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

### 4.3 Kern (Core) Diagram for Rectangular Section

```mermaid
graph TD
    P["Eccentric Load P applied at point LOAD POINT"] --> Q{"Is load point INSIDE the kern rhombus?"}
    Q -->|"e_x less than b over 6 AND e_y less than d over 6"| R["YES - ENTIRE section is in COMPRESSION - SAFE for brittle materials"]
    Q -->|"Load point OUTSIDE kern"| S["NO - TENSION develops on the far face - NOT safe for masonry"]
    R --> T["Middle-Third Rule Satisfied"]
    S --> U["Use RCC or steel section to carry tension"]
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow: Design Safety Check Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0["START: Rectangular column with eccentric load"] --> S1["Step 1: Identify P, e, b, d"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Compute Area A = b x d"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute direct stress sigma_d = P over A"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute section modulus Z = b d squared over 6"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute bending stress sigma_b = P x e over Z"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Compute sigma_max and sigma_min"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Apply factor of safety FOS"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Check sigma_max is less than allowable compressive stress"]
    S6 --> S9["Step 9: Verify middle-third rule e is less than or equal to d over 6"]
    S9 -->|Satisfied| S10["DESIGN SAFE: Section is wholly in compression"]
    S9 -->|Not satisfied| S11["TENSION develops: Redesign with ductile material or increase section"]
    S8 -->|Satisfied| S12["FINAL APPROVAL"]
    S8 -->|Not satisfied| S13["REDESIGN REQUIRED"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Define the middle-third rule for a rectangular column subjected to eccentric loading. Why is it important in the design of masonry columns?**

**Model Answer:**

The **middle-third rule** states that for a rectangular column to have no tensile stress anywhere on its cross-section under an eccentric compressive load, the line of action of the load must lie within the **middle third** of the cross-sectional depth (in the plane of bending). Mathematically, the eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis must satisfy $e \leq \dfrac{d}{6}$.

**Importance:** Masonry materials such as brick, stone, and plain concrete are **brittle** and possess negligible tensile strength. If tension develops at the far face, cracks form, water ingress occurs, and the column fails prematurely. The middle-third rule is therefore a **fundamental safety criterion** that ensures the entire section remains in compression, preventing cracking and ensuring durability. It is also used in the design of gravity dams, retaining walls, and unreinforced concrete footings.

*(3 marks — complete, board-ready answer)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: An axial compressive load of 200 kN is applied on a rectangular column section 200 mm × 150 mm. Calculate the magnitude of the maximum eccentricity at which the load can be applied without producing tension on the section.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $b = 150$ mm, $d = 200$ mm, $P = 200$ kN.

**Step 1:** For no tension, apply the middle-third rule:

$$e_{max} = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{200}{6} = 33.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Verification by stress calculation:

$$\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3}}{200 \times 150} = 6.667 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 200 \times 10^{3} \times 33.33}{150 \times (200)^{2}} = 6.667 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = 6.667 - 6.667 = 0 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad \text{✓}$$

**Result:** $e_{max} = 33.33$ mm (load must lie within the middle third of the 200 mm depth).

*(3 marks — calculation + rule both required for full marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** A rectangular masonry column of size **400 mm × 300 mm** carries an axial compressive load of **500 kN** with an eccentricity of **40 mm** in the plane of the longer side. Determine:
   (i) Maximum and minimum stresses at the base.
   (ii) Whether tension develops in the section.
   (iii) The maximum eccentricity permitted for no tension.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short column of rectangular section **500 mm × 400 mm** carries an eccentric load of **800 kN** at an eccentricity of **100 mm** along the axis of 400 mm depth. If the allowable compressive stress in the material is **8 N/mm²** and the allowable tensile stress is **1.5 N/mm²**, check the safety of the column using a factor of safety of **2.5**.

**(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Given:**
- $b = 300$ mm, $d = 400$ mm (eccentricity in plane of longer side $d$)
- $P = 500$ kN $= 500 \times 10^{3}$ N
- $e = 40$ mm

**Step 1: Compute Area**

$$A = b \cdot d = 300 \times 400 = 120{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Compute Direct Stress**

$$\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{500 \times 10^{3}}{120{,}000} = 4.167 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive)}$$

**Step 3: Compute Section Modulus**

$$Z = \frac{b d^{2}}{6} = \frac{300 \times (400)^{2}}{6} = \frac{300 \times 160{,}000}{6} = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4: Compute Bending Moment**

$$M = P \cdot e = 500 \times 10^{3} \times 40 = 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5: Compute Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{20 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 6: Compute Maximum and Minimum Stresses**

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = 4.167 + 2.5 = 6.667 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} = 4.167 - 2.5 = 1.667 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive, positive)}$$

**(i) Result:** $\sigma_{max} = 6.67$ N/mm² (comp.), $\sigma_{min} = 1.67$ N/mm² (comp.)

**(ii) Tension Check:** Since $\sigma_{min} = +1.67$ N/mm² (positive, compressive), **no tension develops**. ✓

**(iii) Maximum Eccentricity:**

$$e_{max} = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{400}{6} = 66.67 \text{ mm}$$

The applied eccentricity $e = 40$ mm $< 66.67$ mm, confirming no tension.

---

##### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:**
- $b = 400$ mm, $d = 500$ mm
- $P = 800$ kN $= 800 \times 10^{3}$ N
- $e = 100$ mm (along 500 mm depth)
- Allowable compressive stress $\sigma_{c,allow} = 8$ N/mm²
- Allowable tensile stress $\sigma_{t,allow} = 1.5$ N/mm²
- Factor of safety (FOS) $= 2.5$

**Step 1: Compute Direct Stress**

$$A = 400 \times 500 = 200{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{d} = \frac{800 \times 10^{3}}{200{,}000} = 4.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Compute Section Modulus**

$$Z = \frac{400 \times (500)^{2}}{6} = \frac{400 \times 250{,}000}{6} = 16.67 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3: Compute Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{800 \times 10^{3} \times 100}{16.67 \times 10^{6}} = 4.8 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4: Compute Maximum and Minimum Stresses**

$$\sigma_{max} = 4.0 + 4.8 = 8.8 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = 4.0 - 4.8 = -0.8 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (tensile)}$$

**Step 5: Check Middle-Third Rule**

$$e_{max} = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{500}{6} = 83.33 \text{ mm}$$

Since applied $e = 100$ mm $> 83.33$ mm, **tension develops** on the far face. ✗

**Step 6: Apply Factor of Safety Check**

Permissible stresses:
$$\sigma_{c,design} = \frac{8.8}{2.5} = 3.52 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$
$$\sigma_{t,design} = \frac{0.8}{2.5} = 0.32 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 7: Compare with Allowable Stresses**

| Stress | Developed (with FOS) | Allowable | Status |
|:------:|:--------------------:|:---------:|:------:|
| Compression | 3.52 N/mm² | 8.0 N/mm² | ✓ SAFE |
| Tension | 0.32 N/mm² | 1.5 N/mm² | ✓ SAFE |

**Conclusion:** With FOS of 2.5 applied to the developed stresses, both compressive (3.52 < 8.0) and tensile (0.32 < 1.5) stresses are within allowable limits. The column is **SAFE** but the design relies on tensile strength of the material. The material must be ductile (e.g., steel or RCC), not masonry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to apply FOS to developed stresses. They simply compare $\sigma_{max} = 8.8$ with allowable 8.0 and conclude "not safe," losing 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students ignore the **tensile stress check** entirely. Tension $= 0.8$ MPa must be reported and verified against allowable tensile strength.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to state the **middle-third violation** explicitly. This is a key conceptual point worth 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the maximum and minimum stresses in a rectangular column section subjected to an eccentric compressive load. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short column of cross-section **250 mm × 200 mm** carries an eccentric load of **300 kN**. The eccentricity is **60 mm** measured from the centroidal axis parallel to the longer side. Determine:
   (i) The stresses at the four corners of the section.
   (ii) The maximum eccentricity for which no tension will occur.

**(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The column is **short** (no buckling / Euler's instability).
2. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
3. Plane sections remain plane after bending (Bernoulli-Euler hypothesis).
4. The eccentricity is **small** (within elastic limit of the material).
5. Self-weight of the column is neglected.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** A load $P$ is applied at distance $e$ from the centroid $G$ of a rectangular section $b \times d$. At the centroid, this is statically equivalent to:
- An axial force $P$ along the column axis.
- A bending moment $M = P e$ about the centroidal axis perpendicular to the eccentricity.

**Step 2:** Direct compressive stress (uniform):

$$\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{bd}$$

**Step 3:** Bending stress at a fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral (centroidal) axis:

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P e \cdot y}{b d^{3}/12} = \frac{12 P e y}{b d^{3}}$$

**Step 4:** By superposition, total stress at distance $y$:

$$\sigma(y) = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{12 P e y}{b d^{3}}$$

**Step 5:** At extreme fiber $y = +d/2$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{12 P e \cdot (d/2)}{b d^{3}} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

**Step 6:** At opposite extreme fiber $y = -d/2$:

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{bd} - \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

**Step 7:** For **no tension** on the section, $\sigma_{min} \geq 0$:

$$\frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right) \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \leq \frac{d}{6}$$

This is the **middle-third rule**.

---

##### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- $b = 200$ mm, $d = 250$ mm (longer side; eccentricity is in plane of $d$)
- $P = 300$ kN $= 300 \times 10^{3}$ N
- $e = 60$ mm

**Step 1: Compute Area and Section Modulus**

$$A = 200 \times 250 = 50{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$Z = \frac{b d^{2}}{6} = \frac{200 \times (250)^{2}}{6} = 2.083 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 2: Compute Direct and Bending Stresses**

$$\sigma_{d} = \frac{300 \times 10^{3}}{50{,}000} = 6.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{300 \times 10^{3} \times 60}{2.083 \times 10^{6}} = 8.64 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Compute Max and Min Stresses**

$$\sigma_{max} = 6.0 + 8.64 = 14.64 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive, near side)}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = 6.0 - 8.64 = -2.64 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (tensile, far side)}$$

**Step 4: Stresses at the Four Corners**

| Corner | Stress (N/mm²) | Type |
|:------:|:--------------:|:----:|
| Corner 1 (near side) | **+14.64** | Compressive |
| Corner 2 (near side) | **+14.64** | Compressive |
| Corner 3 (far side) | **−2.64** | Tensile |
| Corner 4 (far side) | **−2.64** | Tensile |

> [!NOTE]
> Both corners on the same side experience the **same stress** because the bending stress varies only in the direction of eccentricity (the $d$ direction), not across the width $b$.

**Step 5: Maximum Eccentricity for No Tension**

$$e_{max} = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{250}{6} = 41.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The applied eccentricity $e = 60$ mm $> e_{max} = 41.67$ mm, hence **tension develops** at the far face (as confirmed: $\sigma_{min} = -2.64$ N/mm²).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> - **Common Mistake:** Students compute stress at only two corners and forget the other two (losing 1 mark). Since the eccentricity is in one direction only, both corners on the same side share identical stress — this must be explicitly stated.
> - **Critical Error:** Forgetting to **report the tension value with a negative sign**. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the sign convention is violated.
> - **Design Insight:** The numerical answer must be linked to a **practical design recommendation** (e.g., "this section is unsafe for masonry; use RCC") to score the final application mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns**

✅ **Core Concept:** Eccentric load $P$ at eccentricity $e$ is equivalent to an axial force $P$ + a bending moment $M = P e$ at the centroid.

✅ **Two Master Formulas (MUST memorize):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{d}\right) \quad ; \quad \sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

✅ **Middle-Third Rule:** $e \leq d/6$ — for no tension (essential for masonry design).

✅ **Sign Convention:** Compression = **+ve**, Tension = **−ve** (KTU standard).

✅ **Stress Block Shapes:**
- $e < d/6$ → **Trapezoidal** (all compression).
- $e = d/6$ → **Triangular-over-rectangle** (zero stress on far edge — limit case).
- $e > d/6$ → **Triangular with tension zone** (requires ductile material).

✅ **Kern (Core) of Rectangular Section:** A rhombus with vertices at $\pm b/6$ and $\pm d/6$ from the centroid.

✅ **Section Modulus for Rectangle:** $Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ — derived from $I/(d/2)$ with $I = b d^{3}/12$.

✅ **Practical Applications:** Masonry columns, gravity dams, retaining walls, chimneys, brick piers.

✅ **Assumption:** Plane sections remain plane, material is linearly elastic, column is short (no buckling).

✅ **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Always check the **middle-third condition** AND the **maximum compressive stress against allowable value** AND the **tensile stress (if any) against material tensile capacity**.

✅ **Design Decision Rule:** If $e > d/6$ → use **RCC or steel** (not masonry/brittle material).

✅ **For KTU Board Exam:** Always state the **assumptions** explicitly in derivations; show **units** in all numerical steps; and conclude with a **design recommendation** (safe / unsafe / redesign required).

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Code Provisions for Design of Short Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Code Provisions for Design of Short Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IS 456:2000 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short Column (IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.1.2):** A column is classified as a *short column* when the **effective length ($\ell_{ef}$)** to **least lateral dimension ($D$)** ratio is **less than or equal to 12**. For such members, the design is governed by **material strength** (compressive crushing), and secondary bending effects due to slenderness are neglected.

**Mathematical Statement of Slenderness Criterion:**

$$\frac{\ell_{ef}}{D} \leq 12 \quad \text{(for short column)}$$

where:
- $\ell_{ef}$ = Effective length of the column (in mm)
- $D$ = Least lateral dimension of the column cross-section (in mm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the Limit 12:** This value is empirical and is derived such that the secondary moments caused by inevitable imperfections (e.g., initial crookedness, load eccentricity) remain within acceptable serviceability limits. Beyond this ratio, the column is treated as a *slender column* where buckling governs, requiring additional moment magnification via the *Moment Magnification Method* or *SP-16 Charts*.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The "Crushing of a Strong Stick" Analogy:** Imagine two identical wooden sticks — one short and stubby (e.g., 10 cm long) and the other long and thin (e.g., 100 cm long). If you stand them upright and load them from the top, the **short stick will fail by crushing of the wood fibers** (the material itself fails), while the **long stick will bend and snap (buckle) sideways** before the wood is fully crushed. A *short column* behaves exactly like the short stick — it fails because the **concrete and steel reach their maximum stress limits simultaneously**, not because of sideways instability. Therefore, the design philosophy is simply: *sum up the strength of each material component* (concrete + steel), and the column is safe.

### 1.3 Classification of Columns per IS 456:2000

| Classification | Slenderness Ratio $\left(\dfrac{\ell_{ef}}{D}\right)$ | Governing Failure Mode |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Short Column (Pedestal)** | $\leq 12$ | Material crushing / compression |
| **Long (Slender) Column** | $> 12$ | Buckling + crushing (combined) |

### 1.4 Assumed Standard Loading & Material Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values used throughout this module:**
> - **Characteristic Compressive Strength of Concrete ($f_{ck}$):** **20 N/mm², 25 N/mm², 30 N/mm², 35 N/mm², 40 N/mm²**
> - **Yield Strength of Steel ($f_{y}$):** **415 N/mm² (Fe415)** or **500 N/mm² (Fe500)**
> - **Partial Safety Factor for Concrete ($\gamma_{m}$):** **1.5**
> - **Partial Safety Factor for Steel ($\gamma_{m}$):** **1.15**
> - **Minimum Eccentricity ($e_{min}$):** **$\frac{l}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \geq 20$ mm** (as per Cl. 25.4)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 IS 456:2000 Design Philosophy for Axially Loaded Short Columns

The design of an **axially loaded short column** is fundamentally an *additive strength* problem. The total axial load-carrying capacity is the **sum of the design strengths of concrete and steel**, each reduced by the respective partial safety factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why additive?** Under pure axial compression, both concrete and steel shorten by the same amount (strain compatibility). At ultimate state, both materials are stressed to their respective design strengths ($\sigma_{c,allowable}$ and $\sigma_{s,allowable}$), so the loads they carry can simply be summed.

---

### 2.2 Key Code Provisions Summary (Cl. 25 of IS 456:2000)

1. **Cross-Sectional Dimensions:** Minimum width = **200 mm** (for cast-in-situ columns).
2. **Cover (Cl. 26.4.2):**
   - Main reinforcement: **40 mm** (for mild exposure)
   - Lateral ties: **25 mm** (for mild exposure)
3. **Longitudinal Reinforcement (Cl. 26.5.3):**
   - **Minimum area ($p_{min}$):** **0.8% of gross cross-sectional area ($A_g$)**
   - **Maximum area ($p_{max}$):** **6.0% of gross cross-sectional area ($A_g$)**
   - **Minimum number of bars:** **4 (rectangular)** and **6 (circular)**
   - **Bar diameter $\geq$ 12 mm**
4. **Lateral Ties (Cl. 25.5.3.2 & 26.5.3.2):**
   - Diameter $\geq \dfrac{1}{4} \times \phi_{main}$ (largest longitudinal bar)
   - **Pitch $\leq$ least of:** (a) least lateral dimension, (b) **16 $\times$ diameter of smallest longitudinal bar**, (c) **300 mm**
5. **Helical Reinforcement (Cl. 25.5.3.3):** Pitch $\leq$ 75 mm; volume $\geq$ **0.36 × Volume of core × ($A_g/A_{core}$ − 1)**
6. **Effective Length (Cl. 25.3):** Depends on end conditions. For a column restrained at both ends, $\ell_{ef} = 0.65 L$ (effective length factor = 0.65).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it is the most high-yield content for the KTU exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Code Reference | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Axial Load Capacity | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Cl. 39.3 (IS 456) | N |
| 2 | Slenderness Criterion | $\dfrac{\ell_{ef}}{D} \leq 12$ | Cl. 25.1.2 | dimensionless |
| 3 | Effective Length | $\ell_{ef} = k \times L$ (k from Table 26, IS 456) | Cl. 25.3 | mm |
| 4 | Min Eccentricity | $e_{min} = \dfrac{l}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \geq 20$ mm | Cl. 25.4 | mm |
| 5 | Min Steel Area | $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times A_g$ | Cl. 26.5.3.1 | mm² |
| 6 | Max Steel Area | $A_{sc,max} = 0.06 \times A_g$ | Cl. 26.5.3.1 | mm² |
| 7 | Tie Diameter | $\phi_{tie} \geq \dfrac{1}{4} \phi_{main}$ | Cl. 26.5.3.2 | mm |
| 8 | Tie Pitch | $s \leq \min(B, D, 16\phi_{min}, 300)$ | Cl. 26.5.3.2 | mm |
| 9 | Concrete Area (Gross) | $A_g = B \times D$ (rectangular) | — | mm² |
| 10 | Net Concrete Area | $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ | — | mm² |
| 11 | Helical Pitch | $s_h \leq 75$ mm | Cl. 25.5.3.3 | mm |
| 12 | Helical Volume | $V_h \geq 0.36 V_c \left(\dfrac{A_g}{A_{core}} - 1\right)$ | Cl. 25.5.3.3 | mm³ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mark-Winning Tip:** In KTU exams, the examiner always checks whether you have used **$0.4 f_{ck}$** (not $f_{ck}/1.5$) and **$0.67 f_{y}$** (not $f_{y}/1.15$). The factors 0.4 and 0.67 are **pre-calculated design stress values** with the partial safety factors already baked in. **This is the #1 mistake students make.**

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise buildings:** Ground-floor columns are typically short columns carrying huge axial loads from upper stories.
- **Bridge piers:** Stubby piers supporting heavy deck loads.
- **Pedestals:** Short stubs supporting beams, girders, and heavy machinery.
- **Industrial structures:** Columns in single-storey workshops with low height-to-width ratios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Design Equation $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$

The code equation is the cornerstone of all short column design. Let us derive every constant step-by-step so that you understand *why* the factors are what they are.

---

#### **STEP 1: Start with the Basic Internal Force Equation**

The total axial load that a short column can carry equals the sum of the compressive forces resisted by concrete and steel:

$$P_{total} = \sigma_c \cdot A_c + \sigma_s \cdot A_{sc}$$

where:
- $\sigma_c$ = actual compressive stress in concrete
- $\sigma_s$ = actual compressive stress in steel
- $A_c$ = net area of concrete
- $A_{sc}$ = total area of longitudinal steel

---

#### **STEP 2: Apply the Limit State Design Philosophy**

In Limit State Design (LSD), the stresses in the materials at the ultimate limit state are limited to their **design strengths** — i.e., the characteristic strength divided by the partial safety factor of the material:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_{mc}} \quad ; \quad \sigma_s = \frac{f_{y}}{\gamma_{ms}}$$

where:
- $\gamma_{mc} = 1.5$ (partial safety factor for concrete in compression)
- $\gamma_{ms} = 1.15$ (partial safety factor for steel)

---

#### **STEP 3: Substitute into the Force Equation**

$$P_{total} = \left(\frac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_{mc}}\right) A_c + \left(\frac{f_{y}}{\gamma_{ms}}\right) A_{sc}$$

Substituting the partial safety factors:

$$P_{total} = \left(\frac{f_{ck}}{1.5}\right) A_c + \left(\frac{f_{y}}{1.15}\right) A_{sc}$$

---

#### **STEP 4: Pre-Calculate the Numerical Coefficients**

Compute each fraction separately:

$$\frac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{For concrete: } 0.6667 f_{ck}$$

$$\frac{1}{1.15} = 0.8696 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{For steel: } 0.8696 f_{y}$$

The code uses the rounded values **0.4** and **0.67** (not 0.6667 and 0.8696). The reason is that IS 456 further incorporates a **creep factor reduction** for concrete under sustained loading — concrete under long-term compression is weaker than under short-term loading, so the coefficient is conservatively reduced from 0.667 to **0.4**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification for 0.4:** The 0.4 factor for concrete accounts for three effects: (i) partial safety factor 1.5, (ii) sustained loading creep, and (iii) micro-cracking at column interfaces. The 0.67 factor for steel essentially represents $1/1.15 = 0.87$ rounded down, but since steel is more ductile and reliable in compression, IS 456 uses 0.67 (a slightly more conservative value than 0.87) for added safety. This is the **standard form** students must memorize for the KTU exam.

---

#### **STEP 5: Final Code Equation**

$$\boxed{P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}}$$

This is the **Limit State of Collapse (Compression)** equation per Cl. 39.3 of IS 456:2000.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Design Procedure

The complete design process for an axially loaded short column follows this structured sequence:

| Step | Action | Formula / Decision |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1** | Compute Effective Length | $\ell_{ef} = k \times L$ (using Table 26, IS 456) |
| **2** | Assume Cross-Section | $B \times D$ (try $B = D = 300$ mm to $500$ mm) |
| **3** | Check Slenderness | $\dfrac{\ell_{ef}}{D} \leq 12$ ? |
| **4** | Compute Design Load | $P_u = 1.5 (D.L. + L.L.)$ |
| **5** | Calculate $A_{sc}$ Required | $A_{sc} = \dfrac{P_u - 0.4 f_{ck} A_g}{0.67 f_y - 0.4 f_{ck}}$ |
| **6** | Apply Code Limits | $0.008 A_g \leq A_{sc} \leq 0.06 A_g$ |
| **7** | Select Bars | Choose bar diameter + count (e.g., 6-20 mm $\phi$) |
| **8** | Design Ties | $\phi_{tie} \geq \phi_{main}/4$ and pitch from Cl. 25.5.3.2 |
| **9** | Detailing Checks | Spacing, laps, cover |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Design a short column to carry a service axial load of **$1500$ kN**. Take $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm². Assume a square column with both ends restrained against rotation and translation. Story height = 3.5 m. Effective length factor $k = 0.65$.

---

#### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Factored Load:**
$$P_u = 1.5 \times 1500 = 2250 \text{ kN} = 2250 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Length:**
$$\ell_{ef} = 0.65 \times 3500 = 2275 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Assume Cross-Section:** Try **B = D = 400 mm**.
$$A_g = 400 \times 400 = 160000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Slenderness Check:**
$$\frac{\ell_{ef}}{D} = \frac{2275}{400} = 5.69 \leq 12 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Short column)}$$

**Step 5 — Required Steel Area:**
$$A_{sc} = \frac{P_u - 0.4 f_{ck} A_g}{0.67 f_y - 0.4 f_{ck}}$$

Substituting:
$$A_{sc} = \frac{2250 \times 10^3 - 0.4 \times 25 \times 160000}{0.67 \times 415 - 0.4 \times 25}$$

$$A_{sc} = \frac{2250000 - 1600000}{278.05 - 10} = \frac{650000}{268.05} \approx 2425 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Code Limits Check:**
$$A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times 160000 = 1280 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_{sc,max} = 0.06 \times 160000 = 9600 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$1280 < 2425 < 9600 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Bar Selection:** Choose **8 bars of 20 mm $\phi$**:
$$A_{sc,provided} = 8 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 8 \times 314.16 = 2513 \text{ mm}^2 > 2425 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Ties:** Use **8 mm $\phi$** ties (since $20/4 = 5$ mm, so 8 mm satisfies).
- Pitch: $s \leq \min(400, 400, 16 \times 20, 300) = \min(400, 400, 320, 300) = 300 \text{ mm}$
- **Provide ties at 300 mm c/c.**

**Step 9 — Final Detailing:** Cover = 40 mm. Ties at 300 mm c/c near top and bottom, 300 mm c/c throughout.

$$\boxed{\text{Column Size: } 400 \text{ mm} \times 400 \text{ mm with 8-20 mm $\phi$ bars and 8 mm $\phi$ ties @ 300 mm c/c.}}$$

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Design of Helically Reinforced Short Column

For a **circular column with helical ties**, the code allows a **1.05 times** capacity enhancement because the helix provides additional confinement. The design equation is:

$$P_{u,helical} = 1.05 \times (0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}) - \text{confined enhancement}$$

But more precisely, IS 456 (Cl. 25.5.3.3) specifies the *minimum* volume of helical reinforcement as:

$$V_h = 0.36 \times V_{core} \times \left(\frac{A_g}{A_{core}} - 1\right)$$

This volume can be converted to a pitch value $s_h$:

$$s_h = \frac{\text{Volume of one helix turn}}{\text{Number of turns per unit length} \times \text{Volumetric requirement}}$$

For design purposes, the **pitch is limited to $\leq 75$ mm** and the **helix must extend the full unsupported length** of the column.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Short Column Design Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Service Load P] --> B[Step 1: Factored Load Pu = 1.5P]
    B --> C[Step 2: Assume B x D Section]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Effective Length lef]
    D --> E[Step 4: Check lef / D <= 12]
    E -- Yes, Short Column --> F[Step 5: Compute Ag = B x D]
    E -- No, Slender --> G[Go to Slender Column Module]
    F --> H[Step 6: Calculate Required Asc]
    H --> I[Step 7: Apply Code Limits 0.008 to 0.06]
    I --> J[Step 8: Select Bar Size and Count]
    J --> K[Step 9: Design Lateral Ties]
    K --> L[Step 10: Detailing Check, Cover, Spacing]
    L --> M[Output: Final Design Report]
```

---

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Architecture: Reinforcement Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gross Section Ag] --> B[Cover Area 40 mm]
    B --> C[Net Concrete Area Ac]
    A --> D[Longitudinal Steel Asc]
    C --> E[Concrete Core]
    D --> F[Minimum 0.008 Ag]
    D --> G[Maximum 0.06 Ag]
    E --> H[Lateral Ties]
    H --> I[Pitch s less than 300 mm]
    H --> J[Diameter at least 1/4 of main bar]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Flow: Decision Tree for Minimum Eccentricity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Check Minimum Eccentricity emin] --> B[Compute l / 500]
    A --> C[Compute D / 30]
    B --> D{Sum greater than 20 mm?}
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E[Use computed emin]
    D -- No --> F[Use emin = 20 mm]
    E --> G[Design as Axially Loaded with ecc]
    F --> G
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparison of Tied vs Helical Column

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Short Column Type] --> B[Tied Column]
    A --> C[Helically Reinforced Column]
    B --> D[Capacity: 0.4 fck Ac + 0.67 fy Asc]
    B --> E[Tie pitch = min B, D, 16 phi, 300]
    C --> F[Capacity multiplied by 1.05]
    C --> G[Helix pitch less than 75 mm]
    C --> H[Volume of helix greater than 0.36 Vcore Ag/Acore - 1]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the IS 456:2000 criteria for classifying a column as a short column. Under what condition is a column treated as a slender column?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> According to IS 456:2000 (Cl. 25.1.2), a column is classified as a **short column** when the ratio of its **effective length ($\ell_{ef}$)** to its **least lateral dimension ($D$)** is **less than or equal to 12**, i.e., $\dfrac{\ell_{ef}}{D} \leq 12$. Such columns fail by material crushing under axial compression.
>
> A column is treated as a **slender column** when $\dfrac{\ell_{ef}}{D} > 12$. In this case, the design is governed by the additional bending moments due to slenderness, and the design is carried out using the *Moment Magnification Method* or *SP-16 design charts*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Write the IS 456:2000 design equation for the axial load-carrying capacity of a short column. Explain the significance of the constants 0.4 and 0.67.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The axial load-carrying capacity of a short column as per IS 456:2000 (Cl. 39.3) is:
> $$P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}$$
>
> where $f_{ck}$ is the characteristic compressive strength of concrete, $f_y$ is the yield strength of steel, $A_c$ is the net concrete area, and $A_{sc}$ is the area of longitudinal steel.
>
> - **0.4 factor** represents the design compressive stress in concrete, including the partial safety factor of 1.5, the effect of sustained loading creep, and micro-cracking.
> - **0.67 factor** represents the design compressive stress in steel, including the partial safety factor of 1.15 and additional conservatism for ductile behavior.

---

### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1**

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

(a) *Explain in detail the IS 456:2000 code provisions for the longitudinal reinforcement in a short column.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *Design a short column to carry a service axial load of 1800 kN. Use $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm², story height = 3 m, both ends hinged. Show all checks.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a): Longitudinal Reinforcement Provisions (7 marks)**

The IS 456:2000 code provisions for longitudinal reinforcement in a short column are:

1. **Minimum Area of Steel (Cl. 26.5.3.1):** The cross-sectional area of longitudinal reinforcement shall be **not less than 0.8% of the gross cross-sectional area** of the column. Mathematically:
$$A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times A_g$$

2. **Maximum Area of Steel (Cl. 26.5.3.1):** The cross-sectional area of longitudinal reinforcement shall be **not more than 6% of the gross cross-sectional area**.
$$A_{sc,max} = 0.06 \times A_g$$
Beyond 4%, laps must be welded.

3. **Minimum Number of Bars (Cl. 26.5.3.1):** For **rectangular columns**: at least **4 bars** (one in each corner). For **circular columns**: at least **6 bars** spaced uniformly.

4. **Minimum Bar Diameter:** The diameter of longitudinal bars shall be **not less than 12 mm**.

5. **Spacing of Bars:** The clear spacing between longitudinal bars shall **not exceed 300 mm**.

6. **Cover (Cl. 26.4.2):** Minimum cover for main reinforcement is **40 mm** for mild exposure, 50 mm for moderate, and 75 mm for severe exposure conditions.

7. **Lap Length:** Lap length shall be **equal to the development length of the bar** (typically $L_d = 47\phi$ for Fe415 in compression).

---

#### **Model Solution (b): Design Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Effective Length (Both ends hinged → $k = 1.0$):**
$$\ell_{ef} = 1.0 \times 3000 = 3000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Assume Section:** Try square **400 mm × 400 mm**:
$$A_g = 160000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness Check:**
$$\frac{\ell_{ef}}{D} = \frac{3000}{400} = 7.5 \leq 12 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Factored Load:**
$$P_u = 1.5 \times 1800 = 2700 \text{ kN} = 2700 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Required Steel:**
$$A_{sc} = \frac{2700 \times 10^3 - 0.4 \times 25 \times 160000}{0.67 \times 415 - 0.4 \times 25}$$

$$A_{sc} = \frac{2700000 - 1600000}{278.05 - 10} = \frac{1100000}{268.05} = 4104 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Code Limits Check:**
$$A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times 160000 = 1280 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_{sc,max} = 0.06 \times 160000 = 9600 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$1280 < 4104 < 9600 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Bar Selection:** Provide **8 bars of 25 mm $\phi$**:
$$A_{sc,provided} = 8 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 25^2 = 8 \times 490.87 = 3927 \text{ mm}^2$$

Hmm, this is **slightly less than 4104 mm²**, so revise to **8 bars of 28 mm $\phi$**:
$$A_{sc,provided} = 8 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 28^2 = 8 \times 615.75 = 4926 \text{ mm}^2 > 4104 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Ties:** Diameter $\geq 28/4 = 7$ mm → use **8 mm $\phi$** ties. Pitch $\leq \min(400, 400, 16 \times 28, 300) = 300$ mm.

**Step 9 — Final Section:**
- Column: 400 mm × 400 mm
- Main bars: 8 nos. of 28 mm $\phi$
- Lateral ties: 8 mm $\phi$ @ 300 mm c/c
- Cover: 40 mm

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**Q. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

(a) *Discuss the IS 456:2000 code provisions for lateral ties in a short column. Why is lateral reinforcement necessary?* **(7 marks)**

(b) *A circular short column of diameter 500 mm is subjected to a service axial load of 2500 kN. Design the column using helical reinforcement. Use $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm², $f_{yh} = 250$ N/mm².* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a): Lateral Tie Provisions (7 marks)**

**Why Lateral Reinforcement is Necessary:**

1. To **prevent buckling of longitudinal bars** under axial compression.
2. To **resist shear forces** from minor lateral loads (wind, earthquake).
3. To **confine the concrete core**, thereby improving ductility.
4. To **hold the longitudinal cage together** during casting and vibration.
5. To prevent **spalling of cover concrete**.

**IS 456:2000 Provisions (Cl. 26.5.3.2):**

- **Diameter of ties:** $\phi_{tie} \geq \dfrac{1}{4} \phi_{max\_main}$ (one-quarter of the largest longitudinal bar diameter).
- **Pitch of ties:** $s \leq$ least of:
  (a) Least lateral dimension of column
  (b) 16 × diameter of smallest longitudinal bar
  (c) 300 mm
- **Arrangement:** The ties shall be arranged such that **every corner bar and every alternate longitudinal bar** has lateral support provided by the corner of a tie with an included angle of **not more than 135°**. Additional cross-ties are needed when bars are spaced > 300 mm.
- **Pitch near Beam-Column Joint:** The pitch of lateral ties shall be **reduced to 100 mm** over a length equal to the larger dimension of the column, above and below the beam.

---

#### **Model Solution (b): Helical Column Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Factored Load:**
$$P_u = 1.5 \times 2500 = 3750 \text{ kN} = 3750 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Gross Area:**
$$A_g = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 500^2 = 196350 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Required Steel (Use the helical column equation with 1.05 factor for safety):**

Without the 1.05 factor (the 1.05 factor is only used in *capacity* check; for design we use the standard equation):
$$A_{sc} = \frac{3750 \times 10^3 - 0.4 \times 25 \times 196350}{0.67 \times 415 - 0.4 \times 25}$$

$$A_{sc} = \frac{3750000 - 1963500}{268.05} = \frac{1786500}{268.05} = 6665 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Bar Selection:** Use **10 bars of 30 mm $\phi$**:
