# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between a riveted lap joint and a riveted butt joint. State one disadvantage of a single-cover butt joint compared to a double-cover butt joint.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lap Joint | Butt Joint |
|---|---|---|
| Plate arrangement | Two plates overlap each other | Two plates lie in the same plane, edge to edge |
| Shear on rivet | **Single shear** | **Single shear** (single cover) or **Double shear** (double cover) |
| Bending moment | Plates tend to **bend** under load (secondary bending stress) | Bending is much reduced due to symmetry (double cover) |
| Strength | Comparatively **lower** | **Higher** (especially double cover) |
| Material usage | No cover plate needed | Requires cover/strap plates |

**Disadvantage of single-cover butt joint:** The rivets are in **single shear only**, and the joint is **not symmetric** — this creates a bending moment that distorts the joint. Hence a single-cover butt joint is **weaker** than a double-cover butt joint for the same rivet diameter and plate thickness.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define the term "efficiency of a riveted joint." What is the typical range of efficiency for a properly designed structural riveted joint?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The efficiency of a riveted joint is the ratio of the **strength of the weakest section in the riveted joint** to the **strength of the solid (un-riveted) plate** of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\%$$

**Typical Range:** For a well-designed riveted joint in structural steelwork, the efficiency ranges between **60% and 75%** for staggered (zig-zag) riveting. Chain-riveted joints are typically less efficient (**30% to 60%**).

---

## Part B — Full Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3]`

A double-riveted **double-cover butt joint** connects two mild steel plates, each **12 mm thick**, using **20 mm diameter** rivets at a **pitch of 50 mm** in **zig-zag pattern**. The permissible stresses for the plate material and rivet material are:

- Tensile stress of plate: $\sigma_t = 420$ N/mm²
- Shear stress of rivet: $\tau = 350$ N/mm²
- Crushing stress: $\sigma_c = 700$ N/mm²

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The tearing, shearing, and crushing strengths of the joint per pitch length. Identify the governing failure mode. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The efficiency of the joint and the safe load per pitch length, assuming a factor of safety of **2.5**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $t = 12$ mm, $d = 20$ mm, $p = 50$ mm
- $n = 2$ rivets per pitch length (double riveted)
- $\sigma_t = 420$ N/mm², $\tau = 350$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 700$ N/mm²
- Double cover butt joint → **double shear** for rivets

**Step 1 — Tearing Strength $P_t$**

For a zig-zag (staggered) double-riveted joint, the critical tearing line passes through **one rivet hole only** (since the second rivet in the pair is offset in the adjacent row, not on the same transverse section). The net width = $(p - d)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \\
&= (50 - 20) \times 12 \times 420 \\
&= 30 \times 12 \times 420 \\
&= 1{,}51{,}200 \text{ N} = 151.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Shearing Strength $P_s$**

There are 2 rivets per pitch, each in **double shear**.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_s &= 2n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot \tau \\
&= 2 \times 2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^{2} \times 350 \\
&= 4 \times 0.7854 \times 400 \times 350 \\
&= 4{,}39{,}823 \text{ N} \approx 439.82 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Crushing Strength $P_c$**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c \\
&= 2 \times 20 \times 12 \times 700 \\
&= 3{,}36{,}000 \text{ N} = 336 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Governing Failure Mode**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{joint}} &= \min(151.2, 439.82, 336) = 151.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**The joint is governed by plate tearing failure at 151.2 kN.**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 5 — Strength of Solid Plate**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{solid}} &= p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 50 \times 12 \times 420 = 2{,}52{,}000 \text{ N} = 252 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{151.2}{252} \times 100 = 60\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Safe Load per Pitch Length**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{safe}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{\text{FoS}} = \dfrac{151.2}{2.5} = 60.48 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Governing mode is **plate tearing**; efficiency = **60%**; safe load = **60.48 kN** per pitch length.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, Alternative Choice]`

A single-riveted **lap joint** is used to connect two mild steel plates, each **8 mm thick**. The rivet diameter (as per **Unwin's formula**) is **17 mm** and the pitch is **45 mm**. The permissible stresses are:

- $\sigma_t = 400$ N/mm² (plate)
- $\tau = 300$ N/mm² (rivet)
- $\sigma_c = 600$ N/mm² (rivet/plate)

**Compute:**
**(a)** All three failure strengths per pitch length and identify the weakest mode. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The efficiency of the joint, and the safe tensile load the joint can carry per pitch length, taking a factor of safety of **3**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $t = 8$ mm, $d = 17$ mm, $p = 45$ mm
- $n = 1$ rivet per pitch (single riveted)
- Lap joint → **single shear** for the rivet
- $\sigma_t = 400$ N/mm², $\tau = 300$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 600$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Tearing Strength $P_t$**

The critical section passes through **one rivet hole** (single riveted).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \\
&= (45 - 17) \times 8 \times 400 \\
&= 28 \times 8 \times 400 \\
&= 89{,}600 \text{ N} = 89.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Shearing Strength $P_s$ (Single Shear)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_s &= n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot \tau \\
&= 1 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (17)^{2} \times 300 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 289 \times 300 \\
&= 68{,}094 \text{ N} \approx 68.09 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Crushing Strength $P_c$**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c \\
&= 1 \times 17 \times 8 \times 600 \\
&= 81{,}600 \text{ N} = 81.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Governing Failure Mode**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{joint}} &= \min(89.6, 68.09, 81.6) = 68.09 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**The joint is governed by rivet shear failure at 68.09 kN.**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 5 — Strength of Solid Plate**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{solid}} &= p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 45 \times 8 \times 400 = 1{,}44{,}000 \text{ N} = 144 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{68.09}{144} \times 100 = 47.28\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Safe Load per Pitch Length**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{safe}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{\text{FoS}} = \dfrac{68.09}{3} = 22.70 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Governing mode is **rivet shear**; efficiency = **47.28%**; safe load = **22.70 kN** per pitch length.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify single vs. double shear** — This is the #1 error. A lap joint and a single-cover butt joint are in *single* shear; a double-cover butt joint is in *double* shear. Wrong shear assumption = full marks lost on the shearing-strength sub-part.
> 2. **Counting the wrong number of rivet holes on the tearing line** — For a *chain-riveted* double-riveted joint, the line passes through **2 holes**; for a *zig-zag* double-riveted joint, the critical line passes through only **1 hole** (staggered). Mixing these up gives the wrong $P_t$.
> 3. **Choosing the maximum instead of the minimum** — The joint strength is the **minimum** of $P_t$, $P_s$, and $P_c$, not the maximum. The minimum is the failure mode that *actually occurs first*.
> 4. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety in the final answer** — KTU questions often ask for the *safe load*; students compute ultimate strength but forget to divide by FoS. Marks lost.
> 5. **Using Unwin's formula incorrectly** — Unwin's formula $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ is empirical; do not apply it to a *single* plate — use the **thinner plate thickness** if plates of different thicknesses are joined.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** — Always use N and mm consistently (N/mm² for stress), then convert final load to kN only at the end.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> - **Definition:** A fastener is a mechanical device to join structural members permanently (rivet) or temporarily (bolt).
> - **Joint Types:** Lap joint (single shear) and Butt joint (single or double cover).
> - **Rivet Head Types:** Snap, Pan, Conical, Countersunk, Steeple — know at least three.
> - **Bolt Types:** Black (unfinished), Turned (finished), HSFG (high-strength friction grip) — know the relative strength and precision.
> - **The Three Failure Modes:** Tearing of plate ($P_t$), Shearing of rivet ($P_s$), Crushing of plate/rivet ($P_c$).
> - **Master Formulas:** $P_t = (p - d) t \sigma_t$; $P_s = n \cdot (\pi/4) d^{2} \tau$ (multiply by 2 for double shear); $P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$.
> - **Unwin's Formula:** $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ mm (empirical; $t$ in mm).
> - **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = (P_{\text{joint}} / (p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t)) \times 100\%$. Typical range **40–75%**.
> - **Governing Strength:** Always take the **minimum** of $P_t, P_s, P_c$.
> - **Safe Load:** $P_{\text{safe}} = P_{\text{joint}} / \text{FoS}$. FoS = 1.5 (static) to 4.0 (impact/cyclic).
> - **Pattern Effect:** Zig-zag riveting is more efficient than chain riveting (lower net loss of plate section).
> - **Double Shear Advantage:** Double-cover butt joint doubles rivet shearing strength and reduces secondary bending.
> - **Crushing Rarely Governs** in steel-to-steel riveted joints because $\sigma_c$ is much higher than $\sigma_t$ and $\tau$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength of a Riveted Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strength of a Riveted Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RIVETED JOINT:** A *permanent, non-loosening fastening connection* formed by inserting a ductile metal pin (the **rivet**) through aligned holes in the plates to be joined, and then deforming the protruding tail (the *shop or field head*) by **hot or cold forging** so that the rivet shank fills the hole completely and develops a second head. The resulting assembly transfers load through **shear, bearing, and friction** at the rivet–plate interface.

The **Strength of a Riveted Joint** is the **maximum load** the joint can transmit before any one of the *four classical failure modes* is triggered. In KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education), this topic maps to:
- **CO-3:** *Apply design safety principles to evaluate the strength of mechanical joints and connections.*
- **Blooms Level:** L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze) → L5 (Evaluate).

The four classical failure modes that govern the design strength of a riveted joint are:

| # | Failure Mode | Location of Failure | Governing Stress |
|---|--------------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Tearing of plate between rivets** (parallel to load) | Plate net section | Tensile (σₜ) |
| 2 | **Tearing of plate across rivet hole** (perpendicular to load) | Plate between row & edge | Tensile (σₜ) |
| 3 | **Shearing of rivets** (single or double shear) | Rivet cross-section | Shear (τ) |
| 4 | **Bearing / Crushing of rivet on plate** | Rivet–hole contact surface | Bearing (σ_b) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Mandate (KTU Safety Philosophy):** The *actual safe load* of the joint equals the **lowest** of the four failure loads. A safe design *always* ensures that the joint is in equilibrium across all four failure modes — i.e., the designer identifies the **governing mode** and dimensions accordingly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Intuition Pump"

Imagine you are stitching two pieces of thick leather together using **thick nails and a hammer** instead of thread. The nails act exactly like rivets.

- When you pull the leather pieces apart (**tension**), the leather might **tear** between two adjacent nails. (This is the *plate-tearing failure*.)
- If the leather is too thick or the nails are too thin, the nails might **snap in half** at the joint. (This is *rivet shear*.)
- The leather might just **crush** the nail into a hole without the nail breaking — the wood gives way. (This is *bearing failure*.)
- Or the nails may **tear a bigger hole**, ripping the leather across the line of nails. (This is the *edge-tearing failure*.)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Stitched Wallet.** A stitched wallet uses *thread* (small area). A riveted steel bridge uses *rivets* (large area). The physics is identical: load must transfer through the **fastener** in shear, while the surrounding material resists **tearing** and **crushing**. This is the *safety evaluation* the KTU 2024 Scheme expects you to perform.

---

## 1.3 Geometric Terminology (Used Universally in KTU Board Exams)

Before any strength formula is applied, the student must internalize the following geometric parameters — *these are the dimensions examiners will quote in the question paper*:

| Symbol | Parameter | Definition |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| $d$ | **Nominal rivet diameter** | Diameter of the *un-driven* rivet shank (standard sizes: 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 24, 30 mm — Unwin's formula gives $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ mm where $t$ is plate thickness in mm) |
| $d'$ | **Gross / Drilled hole diameter** | $d' = d + 1.5$ mm for $d \leq 25$ mm; $d' = d + 2.0$ mm for $d > 25$ mm (clearance allowance) |
| $t$ | **Plate thickness** | Thickness of the thinner (main) plate in a butt joint, or the plate thickness in a lap joint |
| $p$ | **Pitch (longitudinal)** | Center-to-center distance between *consecutive* rivets measured *parallel to the direction of load* |
| $p_t$ | **Transverse pitch** | Center-to-center distance between *adjacent rows* of rivets measured *perpendicular* to load direction |
| $p_b$ | **Back pitch** | Pitch in the *back row* of a multi-rivet lap or butt joint |
| $p_d$ | **Diagonal pitch** | Center-to-center diagonal distance between the rivet in one row and the rivet in the next row, $p_d = \sqrt{p^2 + p_t^2}$ |
| $m$ | **Margin (edge distance)** | Distance from the *center* of the outermost rivet to the *nearest edge* of the plate, typically $m = 1.5d$ |
| $e$ | **Edge distance** | Distance from the *edge of the hole* to the plate edge, typically $e = 1.5d$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Conventions (Must Be Memorized):**
> - **Unwin's Formula:** $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ (in mm) — used to *select* the rivet diameter.
> - **Standard hole diameter:** $d' = d + \text{clearance}$ (clearance depends on $d$).
> - **Pitch limits:** $p_{\min} = 2.5d$ (so that the riveting tool can fit) and $p_{\max} \leq 32t$ or $300$ mm (whichever is less, to prevent buckling between rivets).
> - **Margin:** $m = 1.5d$ minimum for *punched* holes, $1.3d$ for *drilled* holes.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in *Safety in Design of Structures*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the course **OESFT722** is an *Open Elective* emphasizing the *safety* dimension of structural design. Riveted joints are pedagogically rich because:

1. **They illustrate the Factor-of-Safety (FoS) concept** — the lowest of the four failure loads divided by the working load defines the *actual* FoS.
2. **They demonstrate the Strength-Efficiency concept** — the ratio of joint strength to the strength of an *un-riveted* (solid) plate of the same dimensions.
3. **They are a classic exam-favourite** — almost every KTU Board paper (Dec 2022, June 2023, Dec 2023, June 2024) carries a 14-mark problem on this topic.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Safety Implication:** A single rivet *shear* failure in a bridge gusset plate can trigger progressive collapse (the "zipper effect"). The KTU safety philosophy demands that the designer identify the *weakest* mode and ensure *redundancy* — i.e., even if one rivet fails, the remaining rivets can still carry the load with adequate FoS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Governing Failure Theories (with Physical 'Why' and 'How')

### 2.1.1 Mode 1 — Tearing of the Plate (along a Parallel Section) — *Most Common Failure*

**Physical Why:** When the riveted plate is pulled in tension, the load is carried by the *net* cross-section of the plate between adjacent rivets. The "metal that has been removed to make the hole" cannot carry any load. Therefore, the *net* area is $(p - d') \times t$ per pitch length $p$.

**The load that tears this net section:**

$$P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

where $\sigma_t$ is the **allowable tensile stress of the plate material**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** If there are $n$ rivets per row, the tearing resistance is computed per pitch length and is *independent* of $n$ (because each pitch length has exactly one hole). The presence of multiple rivets per row in a *transverse* direction does NOT strengthen against this particular failure mode.

**Strengthening Strategy:** Increase the **pitch** $p$ (more material between holes) or use a *thicker* plate. The *upper bound* is $p \leq 32t$ (to prevent buckling) or $\leq 300$ mm.

---

### 2.1.2 Mode 2 — Tearing of the Plate Across the Edge Rivet

**Physical Why:** Between the centre of the *last* (edge) rivet and the *edge* of the plate, there is insufficient material. If this region is too narrow, the plate will crack from the *hole* to the *edge*.

**The load that causes edge-tearing:**

$$P_e = (m - d'/2) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \quad \text{per rivet}$$

**Strengthening Strategy:** Increase the **margin** $m$. The minimum acceptable value per KTU conventions is $m = 1.5d$.

---

### 2.1.3 Mode 3 — Shearing of the Rivets

**Physical Why:** When the upper plate tries to slide over the lower plate, the rivet shank acts as a *shear pin*. The shear plane is the interface between the two plates.

- **Single Shear:** Occurs in a *lap joint* (one shear plane per rivet).
- **Double Shear:** Occurs in a *butt joint with two cover plates* (two shear planes per rivet).

**Shear load capacity per rivet:**

$$P_{s,\text{single}} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

$$P_{s,\text{double}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau = \frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the **allowable shear stress of the rivet material**.

**Total joint shear capacity (for $n$ rivets):**

$$P_{\text{shear,total}} = n \cdot P_s$$

> [!TIP]
> **Heads-up for KTU 2024 valuation:** Always use the *diameter of the shank* ($d$, not $d'$) in shear area calculations. The *hole* diameter $d'$ is used for plate-tearing calculations. This is a 1-mark trap in KTU Board papers.

---

### 2.1.4 Mode 4 — Bearing / Crushing of the Rivet on the Plate

**Physical Why:** The rivet bears on the *projected* cylindrical surface of the hole. The contact area is approximated as the *rectangle* $d \times t$ (projected diameter × plate thickness). The hole *ellipse* (if the load is at an angle) is conservatively treated as a circle of diameter $d$.

**Bearing load per rivet:**

$$P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$$

where $\sigma_b$ is the **allowable bearing (crushing) stress of the rivet material** (typically $\approx 2\tau$ for mild steel).

**Total joint bearing capacity (for $n$ rivets):**

$$P_{\text{bearing,total}} = n \cdot P_b$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bearing vs. Shear — Which Governs?** For *thick* plates and *small* rivets, bearing tends to govern. For *thin* plates and *large* rivets, shear tends to govern. The designer must check *both*.

---

## 2.2 Strength of the Complete Joint (Per Pitch Length)

For a joint with $n$ rivets per row, the **total load** the joint can transmit is:

| Failure Mode | Strength Per Pitch Length |
|--------------|---------------------------|
| Tearing of plate | $P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ |
| Shearing of rivets | $P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ |
| Bearing of rivets | $P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ |
| Tearing across edge | $P_e = (m - d'/2) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ (per end rivet) |

> **The joint design strength is the MINIMUM of these four values.**

---

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. It contains EVERY formula you will need for the KTU 2024 Board Exam on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ | Unwin's formula — rivet diameter selection | mm |
| 2 | $d' = d + 1.5$ (or $+2.0$) mm | Drilled hole diameter | mm |
| 3 | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$ | Shear strength per rivet (single shear) | N |
| 4 | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau$ | Shear strength per rivet (double shear) | N |
| 5 | $P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | Bearing strength per rivet | N |
| 6 | $P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Tearing strength of plate (per pitch) | N |
| 7 | $P_e = (m - d'/2) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Tearing strength across edge (per end rivet) | N |
| 8 | $P_{\text{joint}} = \min\{P_t, n \cdot P_s, n \cdot P_b, P_e\}$ | **Design strength of joint** | N |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\%$ | **Efficiency of joint** | % |
| 10 | $p_{\min} = 2.5d$ | Minimum pitch (for tool access) | mm |
| 11 | $p_{\max} = \min\{32t, 300 \text{ mm}\}$ | Maximum pitch (anti-buckling) | mm |
| 12 | $m_{\min} = 1.5d$ (punched) or $1.3d$ (drilled) | Minimum margin | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Rule:** When computing shear or bearing area, use the **nominal shank diameter $d$** (the *un-driven* diameter, used in Unwin's formula). When computing plate-tearing net section, use the **hole diameter $d'$**. Mixing these up is the most common cause of marks lost in KTU valuations.

---

## 2.4 Efficiency of the Joint — The 'Strength-Quality Index'

The **efficiency** $\eta$ of a riveted joint is the *single most important* design metric in KTU 2024 questions:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Strength of riveted joint}}{\text{Strength of a solid (un-riveted) plate of the same size}} \times 100\%$$

For a **single-riveted lap joint**, a *direct* relation exists:

$$\eta = \frac{p - d'}{p} \times 100\%$$

For **multi-riveted** joints, the formula uses the *governing* failure load.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (Industry Standard):** A well-designed riveted joint should have an efficiency between **60% and 80%**. Below 60% the joint is *inefficient* (too much material removed); above 80% the rivets are *over-stressed* and may shear.

---

## 2.5 Types of Riveted Joints (KTU Taxonomy)

| Joint Type | Description | Typical Efficiency Range |
|------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Lap Joint — Single Riveted** | Two plates overlap; one row of rivets | 50% – 60% |
| **Lap Joint — Double Riveted** | Two plates overlap; two rows of rivets (chain or zigzag) | 60% – 70% |
| **Butt Joint — Single Strap** | Two main plates in line, one cover plate | 60% – 70% |
| **Butt Joint — Double Strap** | Two main plates in line, two cover plates (double shear) | 70% – 80% |

> **Chain vs. Zigzag (Staggered) riveting:** In chain riveting, the rivets in successive rows lie on the *same transverse line*. In zigzag, they are *offset* by $p_t/2$. Zigzag is preferred for better load distribution and reduced tearing tendency.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Civil:** Built-up steel girders, roof trusses, railway bridge gusset plates (mostly replaced by welding in modern practice, but *riveted heritage bridges* still exist worldwide).
- **Mechanical:** Pressure vessels, locomotive boilers, ship hulls (especially historical).
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft fuselage (earlier designs) and *failsafe* safety-critical connections.
- **KTU Safety Perspective:** Riveted joints illustrate the *weakest-link principle* in safety design — a system is only as strong as its lowest strength mode.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strength of Plate Against Tearing (Along a Pitch Length)

**Given:** A plate of thickness $t$ and width $w$ (which equals the pitch $p$ if we consider a single pitch length) is drilled with a hole of diameter $d'$ and subjected to tensile load $P$.

**Step 1 — Identify the cross-section that will tear first.**

The plate has two distinct cross-sections perpendicular to the load:
- The *gross* section (full width $p$, full thickness $t$): area $A_g = p \cdot t$.
- The *net* section (at the rivet hole, where material is missing): area $A_n = (p - d') \cdot t$.

> **Why the net section is critical:** The hole removes a strip of metal of width $d'$, so this strip carries *no* tensile stress. The remaining strip must carry the *entire* load.

**Step 2 — Express the tensile stress on the net section.**

$$\sigma_{\text{net}} = \frac{P}{A_n} = \frac{P}{(p - d') \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Set the net stress equal to the allowable tensile stress $\sigma_t$.**

For safe design, the net stress must not exceed the allowable:

$$\sigma_{\text{net}} \leq \sigma_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{P}{(p - d') \cdot t} \leq \sigma_t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum safe tensile load (tearing strength).**

$$P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \quad \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shear Strength of a Single Rivet (Single Shear)

**Given:** A rivet of diameter $d$ passes through a lap joint. The upper plate moves right while the lower plate remains stationary (or moves left). The rivet must resist this sliding motion.

**Step 1 — Identify the shear plane.**

The shear plane lies *exactly* at the interface between the upper and lower plates. There is *one* such plane — hence the name **single shear**.

**Step 2 — Compute the shear area.**

The shear plane is a *circle* of diameter $d$ (the rivet shank):

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

**Step 3 — Express the shear stress.**

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A_s} = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi}{4} d^2} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum safe shear load.**

$$\tau \leq \tau_{\text{allowable}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P \leq \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

$$\boxed{P_{s,\text{single}} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau} \quad \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shear Strength of a Single Rivet (Double Shear)

**Given:** A butt joint with **two** cover plates, one above and one below the main plates. When the main plates try to slide past each other, the rivet is sheared at *two* planes — one at the upper cover plate, one at the lower cover plate.

**Step 1 — Identify the shear planes.**

There are **two** shear planes per rivet — hence **double shear**.

**Step 2 — Compute the total shear area.**

$$A_s = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{2} d^2$$

**Step 3 — Set the shear stress equal to the allowable.**

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A_s} = \frac{2P}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum safe shear load.**

$$\boxed{P_{s,\text{double}} = \frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \cdot \tau} \quad \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Key Observation:** The double-shear strength is *exactly twice* the single-shear strength. This is why butt joints with double cover plates are *stronger* than lap joints of the same dimensions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bearing / Crushing Strength of a Single Rivet

**Given:** A rivet of diameter $d$ sits in a hole of diameter $d'$ in a plate of thickness $t$. A tensile load $P$ pushes the rivet against the *back* of the hole.

**Step 1 — Identify the contact surface.**

The actual contact between the rivet and the hole is a *cylindrical* surface of diameter $d$ and length $t$. For stress analysis, this is *projected* onto a plane perpendicular to the load, giving a *rectangle* of dimensions $d \times t$.

**Step 2 — Compute the projected bearing area.**

$$A_b = d \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Express the bearing stress.**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b} = \frac{P}{d \cdot t}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum safe bearing load.**

$$\sigma_b \leq \sigma_{b,\text{allowable}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P \leq d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$$

$$\boxed{P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b} \quad \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Joint Efficiency for a Single-Riveted Lap Joint

**Step 1 — Strength of the riveted joint.**

For a single-riveted lap joint, the *minimum* of $P_t$ and $P_s$ is the design strength. For a *conservative* design, we use the smaller of the two as $P_{\text{joint}}$:

$$P_{\text{joint}} = \min\{P_t, P_s\}$$

**Step 2 — Strength of a solid (un-riveted) plate of the same size.**

A solid plate of width $p$ and thickness $t$ carries:

$$P_{\text{solid}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

**Step 3 — Compute the efficiency ratio.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{(p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{p - d'}{p} \times 100\%} \quad \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The efficiency $\eta$ is simply the *fraction of plate material that is still carrying load* after the hole has been drilled. The hole is "wasted" metal; the smaller the hole relative to pitch, the higher the efficiency.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style — Full Step-by-Step)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A **single-riveted lap joint** connects two mild-steel plates, each **$t = 10$ mm thick** and **$w = 80$ mm wide**. The rivet diameter is $d = 20$ mm (nominal shank) and the drilled hole diameter is $d' = 21.5$ mm. The pitch is $p = 50$ mm. Allowable stresses are:
> - Plate tensile stress: $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa
> - Rivet shear stress: $\tau = 70$ MPa
> - Rivet bearing stress: $\sigma_b = 140$ MPa
>
> Calculate (a) the strength of the joint, (b) the efficiency of the joint.

### Part (a) — Strength of the Joint

**Step 1 — Tearing strength of the plate per pitch length.**

$$P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

$$P_t = (50 - 21.5) \cdot 10 \cdot 84 \quad \text{(all in mm, MPa → N)}$$

$$P_t = 28.5 \cdot 10 \cdot 84 = 28.5 \cdot 840$$

$$P_t = 23{,}940 \text{ N} \approx 23.94 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Shear strength of the single rivet (single shear — lap joint).**

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} (20)^2 \cdot 70 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 400 \cdot 70$$

$$P_s = 100\pi \cdot 70 = 7000\pi$$

$$P_s \approx 7000 \times 3.1416 = 21{,}991 \text{ N} \approx 21.99 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Bearing strength of the single rivet.**

$$P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$$

$$P_b = 20 \cdot 10 \cdot 140 = 200 \cdot 140 = 28{,}000 \text{ N} = 28.00 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the governing (minimum) strength.**

| Mode | Strength |
|------|----------|
| Tearing $P_t$ | 23.94 kN |
| Shear $P_s$ | **21.99 kN** ← minimum |
| Bearing $P_b$ | 28.00 kN |

$$\boxed{P_{\text{joint}} = 21.99 \text{ kN} \approx 22 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(governed by rivet shear)}}$$

### Part (b) — Efficiency of the Joint

**Step 1 — Strength of a solid plate of the same width (80 mm) and thickness (10 mm).**

$$P_{\text{solid}} = w \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 80 \cdot 10 \cdot 84 = 67{,}200 \text{ N} = 67.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the efficiency ratio.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{21.99}{67.2} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 32.72\% \quad (\text{very low — joint needs improvement!})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning (Common Mistake):** A common error is to *compute* $P_t$ using $d$ (the shank diameter) instead of $d'$ (the hole diameter). The KTU board examiner will deduct **1 mark** for this mistake. Always remember: $d$ → rivet shear/bearing; $d'$ → plate tearing.

### Improvement Suggestion (KTU 2024 Safety Context):

To raise the joint efficiency above 60%, the designer should:
1. **Increase the pitch** $p$ (e.g., $p = 70$ mm) to reduce tearing tendency.
2. **Use a butt joint with double cover plates** to engage double shear.
3. **Use two rivets per row** in a chain pattern.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Strength Table

| Joint Type | Rivets/Row ($n$) | Shear Mode | Strength (per pitch) | Typical Efficiency |
|------------|------------------|------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Lap, single-riveted | 1 | Single | $P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$ | 50 – 60% |
| Lap, double-riveted | 2 | Single | $P_s = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$ | 60 – 70% |
| Butt, single strap | 1 (per pitch) | Single | $P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$ | 55 – 65% |
| Butt, double strap | 1 (per pitch) | Double | $P_s = \frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau$ | 70 – 80% |

> **Design Rule:** A *double-strap butt joint* is the most efficient riveted joint possible (rivets in double shear) and is preferred for high-stress applications.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Failure-Mode Map of a Single-Riveted Lap Joint (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block diagram maps the *interactions* between the four failure modes, the geometry, and the stress state. This is a Mermaid `flowchart` — every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed (`fm` = failure mode) to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Tensile Load P Applied to Lap Joint] --> B{Joint Geometry Defined?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Rivet Diameter d, Hole d prime, Plate t, Pitch p, Margin m]
    C --> D[Compute Four Candidate Failure Loads]

    D --> fm1[FM1 Plate Tearing - Parallel Section]
    D --> fm2[FM2 Plate Tearing - Edge Section]
    D --> fm3[FM3 Rivet Shear]
    D --> fm4[FM4 Rivet Bearing / Crushing]

    fm1 --> E1[P_t equals open paren p minus d prime close paren times t times sigma_t]
    fm2 --> E2[P_e equals open paren m minus d prime over 2 close paren times t times sigma_t]
    fm3 --> E3[P_s equals pi over 4 times d squared times tau - single shear]
    fm3b[or pi over 2 times d squared times tau - double shear]
    fm4 --> E4[P_b equals d times t times sigma_b]

    E1 --> G[Identify MINIMUM Load P joint]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    fm3b --> G
    E4 --> G

    G --> H[Compute Efficiency eta equals P joint over P solid times 100 percent]
    H --> I{Acceptable Design?}
    I -->|eta 60 to 80 percent| J[DESIGN ACCEPTED - Report Factor of Safety]
    I -->|eta less than 60 percent| K[Redesign - Increase p, use butt joint, add rivets per row]
    I -->|eta greater than 80 percent| L[CAUTION - Rivets may be overstressed - check shear]

    style A fill:#ffe699,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style K fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - Start at the top with the **applied load**.
> - Identify the *four* candidate failure loads.
> - The **MINIMUM** of the four becomes the **joint design strength**.
> - The **efficiency** is then computed and checked against the **60–80% acceptance band**.

---

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic of a Double-Riveted Lap Joint (Zigzag Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLATE1[Top Plate - Main Plate 1]
        A1[Rivet R1] --- A2[Rivet R2] --- A3[Rivet R3]
    end

    subgraph PLATE2[Bottom Plate - Main Plate 2]
        B1[Rivet R4] --- B2[Rivet R5] --- B3[Rivet R6]
    end

    A1 -. Pitch p .-> A2
    A2 -. Pitch p .-> A3
    A1 -. Transverse Pitch p_t .-> B1
    A2 -. Transverse Pitch p_t .-> B2
    A3 -. Transverse Pitch p_t .-> B3

    R1[Rivet R1 at Edge] -. Margin m .-> EDGE1[Plate Edge Top]
    R6[Rivet R6 at Edge] -. Margin m .-> EDGE2[Plate Edge Bottom]

    style PLATE1 fill:#dae3f3,stroke:#333
    style PLATE2 fill:#dae3f3,stroke:#333
    style A1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style A2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style A3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style B1 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#333
    style B2 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#333
    style B3 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#333
```

> **Key Labels Visible in the Diagram:**
> - **$p$** = pitch (longitudinal, between consecutive rivets in the same row)
> - **$p_t$** = transverse pitch (perpendicular, between rows)
> - **$m$** = margin (distance from centre of last rivet to plate edge)
> - The *zigzag* offset of $p_t/2$ between rows is the standard pattern for *chain vs. zigzag* riveting.

---

## 4.3 Force Distribution Schematic (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOAD[Applied Tensile Load P]
        L1[External Force P]
    end

    subgraph PLATE[Plate Material - Net Section Area open paren p minus d prime close paren times t]
        P1[Resists via Tensile Stress sigma_t]
    end

    subgraph RIVET[Rivet Shank - Cross Section pi over 4 times d squared]
        R1[Resists Single Shear via stress tau]
        R2[Resists Double Shear via stress tau - 2 planes]
        R3[Resists Bearing on Hole Wall via stress sigma_b - projected area d times t]
    end

    L1 --> P1
    L1 --> R1
    L1 --> R2
    L1 --> R3

    P1 -.->|Compare Stresses| GOV{Which is Minimum?}
    R1 -.-> GOV
    R2 -.-> GOV
    R3 -.-> GOV

    GOV -->|Governing Mode| OUT[Defines Joint Design Strength]

    style LOAD fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#333
    style PLATE fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333
    style RIVET fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#333
    style GOV fill:#ffc000,stroke:#333
    style OUT fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> **Engineering Insight from the Diagram:**
> The applied load $P$ is *shared* among the plate (in tension) and the rivets (in shear and bearing). The *lowest-stress* (in terms of failure load) component dictates the joint strength. This is a classic *load-sharing* system in mechanical design.
