# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a *riveted joint*. List the **four** classical modes of failure of a riveted joint.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **riveted joint** is a permanent fastening formed by inserting a ductile metal pin (the rivet) through aligned holes in two or more plates and forming a second head by hot or cold forging.

The four classical failure modes are:
1. **Tearing of the plate** between adjacent rivets (parallel to load).
2. **Tearing of the plate** across the edge rivet (perpendicular to load).
3. **Shearing of the rivets** (single or double shear).
4. **Bearing / crushing of the rivet** on the plate.

> *(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing all four modes correctly.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** What is **efficiency of a riveted joint**? Write the formula for the efficiency of a *single-riveted lap joint*.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **efficiency** of a riveted joint is the *ratio* of the strength of the riveted joint to the strength of an *un-riveted* (solid) plate of the *same dimensions*, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Strength of riveted joint}}{\text{Strength of solid plate}} \times 100\%$$

For a **single-riveted lap joint**:

$$\eta = \frac{p - d'}{p} \times 100\%$$

where $p$ = pitch and $d'$ = hole diameter.

> *(Examiner's Note: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for formula.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** *(Comprehensive KTU ESE-Style Question)*
>
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> A **double-riveted lap joint** is used to connect two mild-steel plates, each of thickness $t = 12$ mm. The rivet diameter is $d = 22$ mm (nominal) and the hole diameter is $d' = 23.5$ mm. The pitch is $p = 65$ mm and the rivets are arranged in **chain pattern**. The allowable stresses are:
> - Plate tensile stress: $\sigma_t = 80$ MPa
> - Rivet shear stress: $\tau = 60$ MPa
> - Rivet bearing stress: $\sigma_b = 120$ MPa
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **strength of the joint per pitch length** and identify the *governing failure mode*.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compute the **efficiency of the joint** and comment on whether the design is safe. Suggest *one* design improvement.

### Model Solution — Question A, Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the joint parameters.**
- Plate thickness: $t = 12$ mm
- Rivet diameter: $d = 22$ mm
- Hole diameter: $d' = 23.5$ mm
- Pitch: $p = 65$ mm
- Number of rivets per row: $n = 2$ (double-riveted)
- Joint type: **Lap joint** → **Single shear** per rivet

**Step 2 — Compute tearing strength of the plate (per pitch length).**

$$P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

$$P_t = (65 - 23.5) \cdot 12 \cdot 80$$

$$P_t = 41.5 \cdot 12 \cdot 80 = 41.5 \cdot 960$$

$$P_t = 39{,}840 \text{ N} \approx 39.84 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute shear strength of the two rivets (single shear).**

$$P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

$$P_s = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (22)^2 \cdot 60$$

$$P_s = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 484 \cdot 60 = 2 \cdot 380.13 \cdot 60$$

$$P_s = 2 \cdot 22{,}808 = 45{,}616 \text{ N} \approx 45.62 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute bearing strength of the two rivets.**

$$P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$$

$$P_b = 2 \cdot 22 \cdot 12 \cdot 120 = 2 \cdot 22 \cdot 1{,}440$$

$$P_b = 2 \cdot 31{,}680 = 63{,}360 \text{ N} \approx 63.36 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the governing mode.**

| Mode | Strength (kN) |
|------|---------------|
| Tearing $P_t$ | **39.84** ← MINIMUM |
| Shear $P_s$ | 45.62 |
| Bearing $P_b$ | 63.36 |

**Governing failure mode: Plate tearing (tension failure of the net section).**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{joint}} = 39.84 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(governed by tearing of plate)}}$$

> *(Mark Allocation: 1 mark for parameters, 2 marks for $P_t$, 2 marks for $P_s$, 1 mark for $P_b$, 1 mark for identifying the governing mode.)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A, Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Strength of the solid plate (of width $p = 65$ mm and thickness $t = 12$ mm).**

$$P_{\text{solid}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 65 \cdot 12 \cdot 80$$

$$P_{\text{solid}} = 65 \cdot 960 = 62{,}400 \text{ N} = 62.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} \times 100\% = \frac{39.84}{62.4} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 63.85\%}$$

**Step 3 — Safety comment.**

The efficiency of **63.85%** lies *within* the *acceptable range of 60% to 80%* for a *well-designed* riveted joint. Hence, the design is **marginally safe** from a strength standpoint.

**Step 4 — Design improvement.**

To *increase* the joint efficiency, the designer can:
- **Increase the pitch** $p$ (e.g., to $p = 80$ mm) to reduce tearing tendency.
- **Convert to a butt joint with double cover plates** to engage rivets in *double shear* (which doubles the shear capacity).

> *(Mark Allocation: 2 marks for $P_{\text{solid}}$, 2 marks for $\eta$ calculation, 1 mark for safety comment, 2 marks for improvement suggestion.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE:**
>
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> A **single-riveted double-strap butt joint** is used to connect two mild-steel plates, each of thickness $t = 15$ mm. The rivet diameter is $d = 25$ mm (nominal) and the hole diameter is $d' = 26.5$ mm. The pitch is $p = 70$ mm. The allowable stresses are:
> - Plate tensile stress: $\sigma_t = 90$ MPa
> - Rivet shear stress: $\tau = 75$ MPa
> - Rivet bearing stress: $\sigma_b = 150$ MPa
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **strength of the joint per pitch length** and identify the governing failure mode.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compute the **efficiency of the joint** and verify if the design satisfies the **safety criterion** (efficiency between 60% and 80%).

### Model Solution — Question B, Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify joint type and parameters.**
- Joint type: **Butt joint with double strap → double shear** for each rivet.
- Plate thickness: $t = 15$ mm
- Rivet diameter: $d = 25$ mm; hole diameter: $d' = 26.5$ mm
- Pitch: $p = 70$ mm; Rivets per row: $n = 1$

**Step 2 — Tearing strength of the plate.**

$$P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

$$P_t = (70 - 26.5) \cdot 15 \cdot 90$$

$$P_t = 43.5 \cdot 15 \cdot 90 = 43.5 \cdot 1{,}350 = 58{,}725 \text{ N} \approx 58.73 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Shear strength of the rivet (DOUBLE shear).**

$$P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau$$

$$P_s = 1 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot (25)^2 \cdot 75$$

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 625 \cdot 75 = 312.5\pi \cdot 75$$

$$P_s = 23{,}437.5 \pi \approx 73{,}631 \text{ N} \approx 73.63 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Bearing strength of the rivet.**

$$P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$$

$$P_b = 1 \cdot 25 \cdot 15 \cdot 150 = 25 \cdot 2{,}250 = 56{,}250 \text{ N} = 56.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the governing mode.**

| Mode | Strength (kN) |
|------|---------------|
| Tearing $P_t$ | 58.73 |
| Shear $P_s$ (double) | 73.63 |
| Bearing $P_b$ | **56.25** ← MINIMUM |

**Governing failure mode: Bearing / crushing of the rivet on the plate.**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{joint}} = 56.25 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(governed by rivet bearing)}}$$

> *(Mark Allocation: 1 mark for parameters, 2 marks for $P_t$, 2 marks for $P_s$ (double shear), 1 mark for $P_b$, 1 mark for governing mode.)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B, Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Strength of the solid plate (width = $p = 70$ mm, thickness = 15 mm).**

$$P_{\text{solid}} = 70 \cdot 15 \cdot 90 = 70 \cdot 1{,}350 = 94{,}500 \text{ N} = 94.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} \times 100\% = \frac{56.25}{94.5} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 59.52\%}$$

**Step 3 — Safety verification.**

The efficiency of **59.52%** is *just below* the recommended 60% threshold. The design is therefore **marginally unsafe / sub-optimal**. The design **does not fully satisfy** the safety criterion (it falls 0.48% short of the 60% mark).

**Step 4 — Recommended modification.**

- **Use a *thicker* main plate** (e.g., $t = 18$ mm) to increase both the tearing and bearing capacity.
- **Increase the pitch** $p$ (e.g., to $p = 80$ mm) to raise the tearing resistance without weakening shear or bearing.

> *(Mark Allocation: 2 marks for $P_{\text{solid}}$, 2 marks for $\eta$, 2 marks for safety verification, 1 mark for recommendation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Before Writing the Exam!**
>
> **Mistake #1 (Very Common — Costs 1 Mark):** Using the *hole diameter* $d'$ in the shear or bearing area calculation. The correct diameter for shear/bearing is the **nominal shank diameter $d$**.
>
> **Mistake #2 (Costs 2 Marks):** Forgetting that a *double-strap butt joint* engages *double shear*, not single shear. The shear capacity is **doubled**.
>
> **Mistake #3 (Costs 1 Mark):** Confusing *chain* and *zigzag* riveting. The pitch $p$ is the *longitudinal* spacing; the transverse pitch $p_t$ is the *perpendicular* spacing. Both must be quoted correctly.
>
> **Mistake #4 (Costs 2 Marks):** Reporting the *efficiency* without comparing it to the **60%–80% acceptance band**. The KTU board examiner expects a *qualitative safety statement* alongside the numerical efficiency value.
>
> **Mistake #5 (Costs 1–2 Marks):** Mixing units (mm vs. cm, MPa vs. Pa). The KTU 2024 Scheme standardizes on **mm, MPa, kN**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. It is your *rapid-revision* sheet the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Four failure modes:** (1) Plate tearing — parallel, (2) Plate tearing — edge, (3) Rivet shear, (4) Rivet bearing.
- [ ] **Unwin's formula:** $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ (mm).
- [ ] **Hole diameter:** $d' = d + 1.5$ mm (for $d \leq 25$) or $d + 2.0$ mm (for $d > 25$).
- [ ] **Single shear strength per rivet:** $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$.
- [ ] **Double shear strength per rivet:** $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau$ (= 2× single shear).
- [ ] **Bearing strength per rivet:** $P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$.
- [ ] **Tearing strength per pitch length:** $P_t = (p - d') \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$.
- [ ] **Edge tearing strength per end rivet:** $P_e = (m - d'/2) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$.
- [ ] **Design strength of joint:** $P_{\text{joint}} = \min\{P_t, n \cdot P_s, n \cdot P_b, P_e\}$.
- [ ] **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Efficiency of single-riveted lap joint (direct):** $\eta = \dfrac{p - d'}{p} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Acceptable efficiency range:** 60% – 80% (industry standard).
- [ ] **Pitch limits:** $p_{\min} = 2.5d$ (tool access); $p_{\max} = \min(32t, 300 \text{ mm})$ (anti-buckling).
- [ ] **Margin:** $m_{\min} = 1.5d$ (punched) or $1.3d$ (drilled).
- [ ] **Use $d$ for rivet shear/bearing; use $d'$ for plate tearing.** (This is the most important "trap" to avoid.)
- [ ] **Lap joint = single shear; Double-strap butt joint = double shear.**
- [ ] **Chain riveting = rivets in successive rows on same transverse line; Zigzag = offset by $p_t/2$ (preferred for better load distribution).**
- [ ] **A butt joint with double straps is the *strongest* riveted joint (rivets in double shear).**
- [ ] **Efficiency below 60% → unsafe / waste of material; above 80% → rivets overstressed.**
- [ ] **In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the final answer must always include (i) governing mode identification, (ii) numerical strength, (iii) efficiency, and (iv) a one-line safety comment.**

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Board Examination!** 🌟
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Riveted Joints for Axially Loaded Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Riveted Joints for Axially Loaded Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Riveted Joint?

A **riveted joint** is a permanent, non-loosening fastening connection formed by inserting a ductile metal pin (called a *rivet*) through pre-formed or drilled holes in the members to be joined, and then upsetting (deforming) the protruding tail end of the rivet by hot or cold forging to form a *closing head*. The two ends — the *manufactured head* and the *closing head* — clamp the plates together through residual lateral pressure and friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *riveted joint* is a load-transmitting structural assembly in which the load is transferred between overlapping or abutting plates by means of rivets that resist shear, bearing (crushing), and tension, with the connecting plates resisting tearing, shearing, and bearing stresses, designed in accordance with the principle of *equal resistance* so that the joint fails simultaneously in all critical modes at the factored design load.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Stitched Leather" Intuition

Imagine two pieces of thick leather being joined together. You cannot simply glue them — the glue will fail under tension. Instead, you punch a row of holes and pass thick threaded nails (rivets) through them, then hammer the back end flat so it cannot pull out. The nails carry the *shear* (sideways cutting force) while the leather between the holes carries the *tension*. This is exactly what happens in a steel riveted connection — except the "leather" is structural steel plate and the "nails" are hot-worked carbon-steel rivets.

- A **lap joint** is like sewing two pieces of leather *overlapping each other* (offset).
- A **butt joint** is like sewing two pieces *edge-to-edge* with a *patch* (cover plate) on top and bottom, like a plaster cast on a broken bone.

## 1.3 Classification of Riveted Joints

| Type | Description | Load Transfer Mechanism |
|------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Lap Joint (Single Shear)** | Two plates overlap; rivets in single shear | Through one shear plane |
| **Butt Joint with Single Cover Plate** | Two plates butt against each other; one cover plate; rivets in single shear | Through one shear plane (the joint plane) |
| **Butt Joint with Double Cover Plate** | Two plates butt; two cover plates; rivets in double shear | Through two shear planes |

## 1.4 Key Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Vocabulary for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Pitch (p):** The centre-to-centre distance between consecutive rivets in a row measured along the direction of load.
> - **Gauge (g):** The centre-to-centre distance between adjacent rows of rivets (transverse to the load).
> - **Edge Distance (e):** The distance from the centre of a rivet hole to the nearest edge of the plate.
> - **Nominal Diameter (d):** The diameter of the *shank* of the rivet (or hole diameter, as per IS 800).
> - **Gross Diameter (d₀) / Hole Diameter:** The diameter of the drilled/punched hole, typically 1.5 mm to 2.0 mm larger than the rivet shank diameter to allow insertion.
> - **Efficiency (η):** The ratio of the strength of the *riveted joint* to the strength of the *un-riveted solid plate* of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage.

## 1.5 Why Study Riveted Joints in a "Safety in Design" Course?

Even though high-strength friction-grip (HSFG) bolts and welded connections have largely replaced rivets in *new* construction, the study of riveted joints is **fundamental to safety assessment of legacy industrial structures** (old bridges, factory sheds, transmission towers, railway structures built before the 1970s) which are still inspected for fitness under the Factories Act and Boiler Regulations. The *limit-state methodology* for riveted joints teaches the student the universal principle: **"A safe joint is one in which every possible mode of failure has been considered and designed against."**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Failure of a Riveted Joint (The "Big Four")

When an axially loaded riveted joint is pulled, it can fail in **four distinct modes**. A safe design must check *each* mode independently. The strength of the joint is the **minimum** of the four strengths (principle of *weakest link*).

### Mode 1 — Tearing of the Plate Between Rivets and Edge
The plate may tear along a line that starts from the *last rivet hole* and runs to the *edge* of the plate. This is resisted by the net area of the plate beyond the last rivet up to the edge.

### Mode 2 — Tearing of the Plate Across a Section Through the Rivet Holes (Tension Failure of Net Section)
The plate may tear *along a row* of rivet holes. The *gross* area is reduced to the *net* area because the holes subtract material. The relevant length is **(p − d)** per pitch length.

### Mode 3 — Shearing of the Rivets
The rivet shank is sheared across the joint plane. In a single-shear lap joint, there is **one** shear plane per rivet; in a double-cover butt joint, there are **two** shear planes per rivet.

### Mode 4 — Crushing (Bearing) Failure of Rivet or Plate
The rivet shank *crushes* against the hole wall due to the bearing pressure. The projected area of contact is taken as **d × t**.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Each Failure Mode

Let:
- **P** = external tensile load on the joint (N)
- **p** = pitch of rivets (mm)
- **d** = diameter of rivet hole (mm)
- **t** = thickness of main plate (mm) — use the *thinner* of the connected plates
- **n** = number of rivets *per pitch length* in the critical section
- **σ_t** = permissible (allowable) tensile stress in the plate (N/mm²)
- **τ_v** = permissible (allowable) shear stress in the rivet (N/mm²)
- **σ_c** = permissible (allowable) crushing (bearing) stress in the rivet/plate (N/mm²)
- **σ_at** = permissible (allowable) tensile stress in the plate used for the *gross* area check (used in efficiency calculation)

### Mode 1 — Tearing Resistance Across the Last Rivet Hole to Edge

$$P_{t,\text{edge}} = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \quad \text{(per pitch length)}$$

### Mode 2 — Tearing Resistance of Net Section Through the Rivet Holes

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \quad \text{(per pitch length)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Modes 1 and 2 share the same formula when the edge distance is *just* equal to the pitch; otherwise Mode 1 uses the *edge distance* e in place of pitch p in the formula: $P_{t,\text{edge}} = (e - d/2) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$. For the standard KTU 14-mark problem, we assume **edge distance = 1.5 × pitch**, which makes Mode 1 stronger than Mode 2 — so Mode 2 governs.

### Mode 3 — Shearing Resistance of Rivets (per pitch length)

For **n rivets** in the joint, with **n_s** number of shear planes per rivet:

$$P_s = n \cdot n_s \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau_v$$

For a single-shear lap joint: $n_s = 1$.
For a double-cover butt joint: $n_s = 2$.

### Mode 4 — Crushing (Bearing) Resistance (per pitch length)

$$P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$$

## 2.3 Strength of the Joint and Strength of the Solid Plate

The **strength of the joint per pitch length** is:

$$P_{\text{joint}} = \min\{P_t,\ P_s,\ P_c\}$$

The **strength of the un-riveted solid plate per pitch length** (without any holes) is:

$$P_{\text{plate}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{at}$$

## 2.4 Efficiency of the Riveted Joint

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{plate}}} \times 100\% = \frac{\min\{P_t,\ P_s,\ P_c\}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{at}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficiency is *always* less than 100%** because removing material (the hole) to insert the rivet inevitably weakens the plate. A high-quality design aims for **η ≥ 60% to 70%** for structural joints. If efficiency is very low, use a *closer pitch* or a *smaller-diameter rivet*.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Failure Mode Addressed |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | Tearing of plate (net section) | $P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$ | N | Tension in net area |
| 2 | Shearing of rivets | $P_s = n\,n_s\,\dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau_v$ | N | Shear in rivet shank |
| 3 | Crushing of rivet/plate | $P_c = n\,d\,t\,\sigma_c$ | N | Bearing stress |
| 4 | Strength of solid plate | $P_p = p\,t\,\sigma_{at}$ | N | Baseline (no holes) |
| 5 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_p} \times 100$ | % | Overall joint performance |
| 6 | Least pitch (from tearing = shearing) | $p = d + \dfrac{n\,n_s\,\pi\,d^2\,\tau_v}{4\,t\,\sigma_t}$ | mm | Equal-strength design |
| 7 | Least pitch (from tearing = crushing) | $p = d + \dfrac{n\,d\,\sigma_c}{\sigma_t}$ | mm | Equal-strength design |
| 8 | Standard edge distance | $e = 1.5\,p$ (typical) | mm | Edge-tearing prevention |
| 9 | Minimum edge distance (IS 800) | $e_{\min} = 1.5\,d_0$ (for sheared/hand-cut) ; $1.25\,d_0$ (for rolled/machine-cut) | mm | Code-prescribed minimum |
| 10 | Hole diameter (typical) | $d_0 = d + 1.5\text{ mm}$ (for $d \le 25$ mm) ; $d + 2.0$ mm (for $d > 25$ mm) | mm | Clearance for hot rivets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Warning for KTU Students:** The symbol **d** in the formula sheet refers to the **diameter of the *hole*** (not the rivet shank). The code specifies that *designs are made on the hole diameter* because the hole exists in the *as-built* structure. This is a **very common mistake** in board examinations.

## 2.6 Permissible Stresses (As Per IS 800:1984 — Legacy Code, used in older syllabi)

For the KTU 2024 Module 3, the *traditional allowable-stress* design values typically assumed in numerical problems are:

| Material | Tension σ_t | Shear τ_v | Crushing σ_c |
|----------|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Steel plates (Grade Fe 410)** | **140 N/mm²** | **100 N/mm²** | **300 N/mm²** |
| **Rivets (Grade Fe 410, hot-driven)** | — | **100 N/mm²** | **300 N/mm²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For *Keralite-built steel* (e.g., re-rolled sections from local mills) the stresses may be assumed at **140 / 100 / 280 N/mm²** in the absence of mill certificates. Always state the assumed values *at the top* of your KTU answer.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design equations of riveted joints are used by:

1. **Structural condition assessment engineers** evaluating century-old railway bridges, water-tanks, and industrial sheds.
2. **Forensic structural engineers** investigating the *Sampoong Department Store* and *Hyatt Regency walkway* type collapses where connection failure was the root cause.
3. **Boiler and pressure-vessel inspectors** under the Indian Boilers Regulation Act.
4. **Restoration architects** retrofitting heritage steel buildings (e.g., Kolkata's Howrah Bridge rivets).
5. **Aircraft and ship-building industries** (riveting is *still* the primary joining method in aluminium airframes because welding weakens the heat-affected zone of aluminium alloys).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shear Strength of a Rivet

Consider a single rivet of hole diameter **d** subjected to a shearing force. The shear failure surface is a *cylinder* of diameter **d** whose cross-section is a *circle* of area $\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$.

The shear stress at failure is **τ_v**. Therefore, the shearing resistance is:

$$P_s = (\text{Shear area}) \times (\text{Shear stress}) = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_v$$

**For n rivets, each with $n_s$ shear planes:**

$$P_s = n \cdot n_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_v$$

> **Reasoning behind the formula:** The rivet shank is treated as a *cylindrical pin*. When the connected plates try to slide past each other, the rivet must be *cut* across its cross-section (just like cutting a wire with scissors). The force required to cut the wire equals *shear area* × *shear strength* of the material.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Crushing (Bearing) Strength

The rivet bears against the *cylindrical* wall of the hole. We approximate the curved contact area by its **projected area** on a diametrical plane (this is a conservative, code-sanctioned simplification).

The projected area of one rivet against the plate of thickness **t** is:

$$A_{\text{projected}} = d \cdot t$$

The bearing (crushing) stress is **σ_c**. For **n** rivets, the crushing resistance is:

$$P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$$

> **Why the projected area?** The *true* contact between a cylinder (rivet) and a hole is a *half-cylindrical* surface. Its area is $\frac{\pi d t}{2}$. The code, however, allows the engineer to use the *simpler* flat projected area $d t$ to avoid confusion, and to maintain a *consistent safety margin*. This is a deliberate conservative assumption.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Tearing Strength of the Plate

The plate has a *row* of rivet holes. The load is transmitted *only* through the *net* metal between the holes. Consider one pitch length **p**:

- Original (gross) length: **p**
- Length of one hole: **d**
- Net length resisting tension: **p − d**
- Net cross-sectional area: $(p - d) \cdot t$

Tearing strength is the *net area* times the *tensile stress*:

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Efficiency Formula

By definition, **efficiency is the ratio of joint strength to plate strength.**

**Step 1:** Strength of *joint* (per pitch length), assuming tearing governs:

$$P_{\text{joint}} = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

**Step 2:** Strength of *un-riveted* plate (per pitch length):

$$P_{\text{plate}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{plate}}} = \frac{(p-d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} = \frac{p-d}{p}$$

**Step 4:** Express as percentage:

$$\eta = \frac{p - d}{p} \times 100\%$$

> **Key Insight:** When tearing governs (the most common design case), the efficiency is a *purely geometric ratio* that does not depend on the stresses! This makes it a very quick first-check for designers: if pitch is **4d**, then $\eta = \frac{4d - d}{4d} = 75\%$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Least Pitch (Equal-Strength Design)

A *well-designed* riveted joint should fail in *all modes simultaneously* at the ultimate load. This is the **principle of equal resistance** — the cornerstone of safe design.

### Case A: Equating Tearing and Shearing

We set $P_t = P_s$:

$$(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = n \cdot n_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_v$$

Solving for **p**:

$$p - d = \frac{n \cdot n_s \cdot \pi d^2 \cdot \tau_v}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$\boxed{p = d + \frac{n \cdot n_s \cdot \pi d^2 \cdot \tau_v}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}}$$

### Case B: Equating Tearing and Crushing

We set $P_t = P_c$:

$$(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$$

The **t** cancels:

$$p - d = \frac{n \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c}{\sigma_t}$$

$$\boxed{p = d + \frac{n \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c}{\sigma_t}}$$

### Case C: The Governing Pitch

The **governing (least) pitch** is the **larger** of the two values computed in Case A and Case B. This guarantees the joint is *equally strong* (or stronger) in both modes.

$$p_{\text{design}} = \max\{p_A,\ p_B\}$$

In practice, an additional **empirical rule of thumb** is also applied: $p_{\min} = 2.5\,d$ (so that the riveting tool can access the joint).

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Type Problem 1: Efficiency of a Lap Joint

**Problem Statement:**
A single-riveted lap joint connects two 10 mm thick plates with 20 mm diameter rivets at a pitch of 50 mm. The permissible stresses are: σ_t = 140 N/mm², τ_v = 100 N/mm², σ_c = 300 N/mm². Calculate the efficiency of the joint.

**Solution (exhaustive step-by-step):**

**Given:**
- $t = 10$ mm
- $d = 20$ mm
- $p = 50$ mm
- $n = 1$ (single riveted)
- $n_s = 1$ (lap joint — single shear)
- $\sigma_t = 140$ N/mm²
- $\tau_v = 100$ N/mm²
- $\sigma_c = 300$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Tearing strength of plate per pitch length:**

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (50 - 20) \times 10 \times 140$$

$$P_t = 30 \times 10 \times 140 = 42{,}000\ \text{N} = 42\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 2: Shearing strength of rivet:**

$$P_s = n \cdot n_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_v = 1 \times 1 \times \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 100$$

$$P_s = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} \times 100 = 100\pi \times 100 = 31{,}416\ \text{N} \approx 31.42\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 3: Crushing strength:**

$$P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 1 \times 20 \times 10 \times 300$$

$$P_c = 60{,}000\ \text{N} = 60\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 4: Strength of joint (weakest mode):**

$$P_{\text{joint}} = \min\{42,\ 31.42,\ 60\} = 31.42\ \text{kN}$$

**Shearing governs.**

**Step 5: Strength of un-riveted plate per pitch length:**

$$P_{\text{plate}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 50 \times 10 \times 140 = 70{,}000\ \text{N} = 70\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{plate}}} \times 100\% = \frac{31.42}{70} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 44.89\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** The joint is only 44.89% as strong as the solid plate. The rivet is *over-strong* in shear relative to the plate's tearing resistance, *or* equivalently, the *plate* is over-strong relative to the rivet. The designer should either *reduce the pitch* to weaken the rivet (but bring tearing and shearing into balance) or use a *smaller-diameter* rivet.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical — Type Problem 2: Design of Pitch for Equal Strength

**Problem Statement:**
Design a double-riveted double-covered butt joint for 16 mm thick plates. Rivet diameter = 22 mm. Permissible stresses: σ_t = 140 N/mm², τ_v = 100 N/mm², σ_c = 300 N/mm². Find the pitch for equal strength.

**Solution (exhaustive step-by-step):**

**Given:**
- $t = 16$ mm
- $d = 22$ mm
- $n = 2$ (double riveted)
- $n_s = 2$ (double cover → double shear)
- $\sigma_t = 140$ N/mm²
- $\tau_v = 100$ N/mm²
- $\sigma_c = 300$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Pitch from tearing = shearing (Case A):**

$$p_A = d + \frac{n \cdot n_s \cdot \pi d^2 \cdot \tau_v}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$p_A = 22 + \frac{2 \times 2 \times \pi \times 22^2 \times 100}{4 \times 16 \times 140}$$

$$p_A = 22 + \frac{4 \times \pi \times 484 \times 100}{8960}$$

$$p_A = 22 + \frac{608{,}212.0}{8960} = 22 + 67.88$$

$$\boxed{p_A = 89.88\ \text{mm} \approx 90\ \text{mm}}$$

**Step 2: Pitch from tearing = crushing (Case B):**

$$p_B = d + \frac{n \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c}{\sigma_t}$$

$$p_B = 22 + \frac{2 \times 22 \times 300}{140}$$

$$p_B = 22 + \frac{13{,}200}{140} = 22 + 94.29$$

$$\boxed{p_B = 116.29\ \text{mm} \approx 116\ \text{mm}}$$

**Step 3: Governing pitch:**

$$p_{\text{design}} = \max\{90,\ 116\} = 116.29\ \text{mm} \approx 116\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4: Sanity check (minimum pitch rule):**

$$p_{\min} = 2.5\,d = 2.5 \times 22 = 55\ \text{mm}\ \checkmark$$

$$p_{\max} = \min\{32\,t,\ 300\,\text{mm}\} = \min\{512,\ 300\} = 300\ \text{mm}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Efficiency of the designed joint:**

Using tearing formula (since the design forces tearing to be the *weakest* mode — we picked p_B which is the larger value, ensuring tearing governs):

$$\eta = \frac{p - d}{p} \times 100\% = \frac{116.29 - 22}{116.29} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 81.08\%}$$

> **Design Verdict:** The joint is 81% efficient — well above the 60% threshold. **Crushing governs** in this case. If we had used p_A = 90 mm, crushing would have failed first, defeating the purpose of the design.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Joint Type for Given Loading

| Loading Condition | Recommended Joint Type | Reason |
|-------------------|------------------------|--------|
| Light load, ease of fabrication | **Single-riveted lap** | Cheapest, simplest |
| Moderate load, no buckling concerns | **Double-riveted lap** | Higher efficiency |
| Heavy load, single plane of symmetry | **Double-covered butt, double riveted** | Rivets in double shear → smaller diameter |
| Tension member with no eccentricity | **Double-covered butt with stagged/chained pitch** | Symmetric load path |
| Corrosive environment | **Butt with cover plates flush** | Avoids moisture-trap lap |

---

## 3.9 Staggered vs Chain Pitch — Brief Note

- **Chain pitch:** All rivets in a row are aligned in the direction of load. Simpler to fabricate.
- **Staggered pitch:** Adjacent rows are offset by **p/2** (half-pitch). This *increases* the tearing length in a diagonal failure path from $(p - d)$ to $\sqrt{(p/2)^2 + p^2} - d = p\sqrt{1.25} - d \approx 1.118\,p - d$. This makes the joint *stronger* against diagonal tearing.

> [!NOTE]
> Staggered pitch is used in pressure-vessel and boiler work where the failure path is diagonal. It is *not* commonly examined in KTU at the introductory level but may be asked as a 3-mark conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — The Four Failure Modes of a Riveted Joint

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical architecture** of the failure-mode analysis, which is the decision-tree a design engineer follows when checking safety of a riveted joint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Tensile Load P Applied to Joint] --> B{Identify Joint Type}
    B --> B1[Lap Joint - Single Shear ns=1]
    B --> B2[Butt Joint - Single Cover ns=1]
    B --> B3[Butt Joint - Double Cover ns=2]
    B1 --> C[Compute Four Resistances]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Pt = p-d times t times sigma_t]
    C --> C2[Ps = n times ns times pi d squared over 4 times tau_v]
    C --> C3[Pc = n times d times t times sigma_c]
    C --> C4[Pp = p times t times sigma_at - Solid Plate]
    C1 --> D{Find Minimum Resistance}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> E[Compute Efficiency eta]
    D --> F{P_joint = Minimum}
    F --> G[Governing Failure Mode]
    G --> G1[If Pt is min - Tearing governs]
    G --> G2[If Ps is min - Shearing governs]
    G --> G3[If Pc is min - Crushing governs]
    G1 --> H[Apply Equal Strength Design]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Compute Pitch p_design = max pA pB]
    I --> J[Check Edge Distance e and Gauge g]
    J --> K[Final Safe Design]
    E --> K
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Lap Joint vs Butt Joint (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *functional cross-section* of the three joint types. It shows the relative positions of the main plates, cover plates, and the shear planes that develop in each rivet.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LapJoint[Lap Joint - Single Shear]
        L1[Plate A Top] --> L2[Rivet Shank Through Hole]
        L2 --> L3[Plate B Bottom]
        L1note[One shear plane at the lap interface] -.-> L2
    end
    subgraph ButtSingle[Butt Joint - Single Cover Plate]
        S1[Plate A] --> S2[Cover Plate on Top]
        S2 --> S3[Rivet in Single Shear]
        S3 --> S4[Plate B butted against Plate A]
        S4note[One shear plane at the butt interface] -.-> S3
    end
    subgraph ButtDouble[Butt Joint - Double Cover Plates]
        D1[Top Cover Plate] --> D2[Plate A]
        D2 --> D3[Plate B butted]
        D3 --> D4[Bottom Cover Plate]
        D2note[Rivet in Double Shear] -.-> D2
        D3note[Two shear planes between butt interface and covers] -.-> D3
    end
```

## 4.3 Failure-Mode Cross-Section Schematic (Functional Flow)

The following diagram shows *where* each failure mode occurs in the cross-section of the joint. This is a **functional block architecture** of the failure zones.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FailureZones[Failure Mode Zones in a Riveted Lap Joint]
        direction TB
        Z1[Zone 1: Plate Tearing Along Net Section] --> Z1d[Plane of action: between rivet holes]
        Z2[Zone 2: Rivet Shear Failure] --> Z2d[Plane of action: across rivet shank at lap interface]
        Z3[Zone 3: Rivet Crushing/Bearing Failure] --> Z3d[Plane of action: cylindrical contact at hole wall]
        Z4[Zone 4: Plate Tearing at Edge] --> Z4d[Plane of action: from last rivet to plate edge]
    end
    Z1d --> Output1[Failure of type Tension in net area]
    Z2d --> Output2[Failure of type Shear in rivet]
    Z3d --> Output3[Failure of type Bearing on hole]
    Z4d --> Output4[Failure of type Tension at edge]
```

## 4.4 Flowchart — Design Procedure for Safe Riveted Joint (KTU 14-mark)

The following Mermaid chart presents the **complete step-by-step design procedure** that a student should write as the model answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given Load P, Plate Thickness t, Material Properties]) --> S1[Step 1: Select Joint Type Lap or Butt]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Choose Rivet Diameter d - typically d = 6 times t to the power 0.5 for thin plates]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Determine Number of Rivets n from Ps and P]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Pitch pA from Tearing equals Shearing]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Pitch pB from Tearing equals Crushing]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Select p_design = max of pA and pB]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Check p_min = 2.5 times d and p_max = 32 times t or 300 mm]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute Edge Distance e = 1.5 times p_design]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute Efficiency eta and verify eta is 60 to 80 percent]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Final Sketch with all dimensions]
    S10 --> End([End: Safe Joint Designed])
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Failure-Mode Decision Tree (Alternative View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strength Check] --> B{Compute Pt, Ps, Pc}
    B --> C1[Pt is minimum?]
    B --> C2[Ps is minimum?]
    B --> C3[Pc is minimum?]
    C1 --> D1[REDESIGN: Increase Pitch p or Reduce t]
    C2 --> D2[REDESIGN: Reduce Pitch p or Add More Rivets n]
    C3 --> D3[REDESIGN: Increase Plate Thickness t or Reduce d]
    D1 --> E[Recheck All Four Modes]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{All Modes Satisfy eta is 60 to 80 percent?}
    F -->|Yes| G[ACCEPT Design]
    F -->|No| A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: List the four modes of failure of a riveted joint under axial tensile load.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four modes of failure of an axially loaded riveted joint are:
1. **Tearing of the plate** between adjacent rivet holes (net-section tension failure).
2. **Tearing of the plate** from the *last* rivet hole to the edge of the plate (edge-tearing failure).
3. **Shearing of the rivets** across the joint interface (single or double shear).
4. **Crushing (bearing) failure** of the rivet shank against the wall of the rivet hole.

---

### Question A2
**Q: Define "efficiency of a riveted joint" and state its typical range.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The *efficiency* of a riveted joint is the ratio of the *strength of the riveted joint* to the *strength of the un-riveted solid plate* of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage.

**Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{plate}}} \times 100\%$

**Typical Range:** **60% to 80%** for well-designed structural joints. Below 50% indicates a poor design. Above 85% is rarely achievable because the rivet holes must remove *some* material.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Design of a Double-Cover Butt Joint

**Q: A double-riveted double-covered butt joint is used to connect two plates of 18 mm thickness. The diameter of the rivet hole is 25 mm. Permissible stresses in the plate material are: tensile = 140 N/mm², shear in rivet = 100 N/mm², crushing = 280 N/mm². Determine:
(a) The pitch of the rivets for equal strength of the joint. (7 marks)
(b) The efficiency of the joint. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Plate thickness: $t = 18$ mm
- Rivet hole diameter: $d = 25$ mm
- Number of rivets per pitch: $n = 2$
- Number of shear planes per rivet: $n_s = 2$ (double cover)
- $\sigma_t = 140$ N/mm²
- $\tau_v = 100$ N/mm²
- $\sigma_c = 280$ N/mm²

---

**(a) Pitch of the rivets for equal strength (7 marks):**

**Case A — Equating tearing and shearing:**

$$p_A = d + \frac{n \cdot n_s \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau_v}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$p_A = 25 + \frac{2 \times 2 \times \pi \times 25^2 \times 100}{4 \times 18 \times 140}$$

$$p_A = 25 + \frac{4 \times \pi \times 625 \times 100}{10{,}080}$$

$$p_A = 25 + \frac{785{,}398}{10{,}080} = 25 + 77.92$$

$$\boxed{p_A = 102.92\ \text{mm}}$$

**Case B — Equating tearing and crushing:**

$$p_B = d + \frac{n \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c}{\sigma_t}$$

$$p_B = 25 + \frac{2 \times 25 \times 280}{140}$$

$$p_B = 25 + \frac{14{,}000}{140} = 25 + 100$$

$$\boxed{p_B = 125\ \text{mm}}$$

**Governing Pitch (the larger of the two):**

$$p_{\text{design}} = \max\{102.92,\ 125\} = \mathbf{125\ \text{mm}}$$

> **Note:** Crushing governs. We pick $p_B$ so that *both* tearing and crushing reach the design load simultaneously.

**Check the empirical limits:**

- $p_{\min} = 2.5\,d = 2.5 \times 25 = 62.5$ mm ✓
- $p_{\max} = \min(32\,t,\ 300\ \text{mm}) = \min(576,\ 300) = 300$ mm ✓

---

**(b) Efficiency of the joint (7 marks):**

Since the pitch $p = 125$ mm was selected from the *crushing* equation, the tearing and crushing modes reach the design load *simultaneously*; the weaker of these two governs the joint strength.

**Strength of joint per pitch length (tearing = crushing):**

$$P_{\text{joint}} = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (125 - 25) \times 18 \times 140$$

$$P_{\text{joint}} = 100 \times 18 \times 140 = 252{,}000\ \text{N} = 252\ \text{kN}$$

**Strength of un-riveted solid plate per pitch length:**

$$P_{\text{plate}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 125 \times 18 \times 140 = 315{,}000\ \text{N} = 315\ \text{kN}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{plate}}} \times 100\% = \frac{252}{315} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 80\%}$$

> **Design Verdict:** The joint is 80% efficient — a *very good* design. The crushing stress controls, and the joint is balanced in tearing and crushing.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Question

**Q: A single-riveted lap joint is to be designed for a tensile load of 90 kN. The plate thickness is 12 mm and the rivet diameter is 20 mm. The permissible stresses are: σ_t = 140 N/mm², τ_v = 100 N/mm², σ_c = 280 N/mm².
(a) Find the required number of rivets. (7 marks)
(b) Determine the pitch and efficiency of the joint. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Applied load: $P = 90$ kN = 90,000 N
- $t = 12$ mm, $d = 20$ mm
- Single riveted: $n = 1$
- Single shear: $n_s = 1$

**Permissible Stresses:** $\sigma_t = 140$ N/mm², $\tau_v = 100$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 280$ N/mm²

---

**(a) Number of rivets (7 marks):**

**Shearing strength of one rivet:**

$$P_{s,1} = n_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_v = 1 \times \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 100 = \pi \times 10{,}000 = 31{,}416\ \text{N}$$

**Crushing strength of one rivet:**

$$P_{c,1} = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 20 \times 12 \times 280 = 67{,}200\ \text{N}$$

**Governing rivet strength (the smaller):**

$$P_{r,\text{gov}} = \min\{31{,}416,\ 67{,}200\} = 31{,}416\ \text{N}\ \text{(shearing governs)}$$

**Number of rivets required:**

$$N = \frac{P}{P_{r,\text{gov}}} = \frac{90{,}000}{31{,}416} = 2.865$$

$$\boxed{N = 3\ \text{rivets (rounded up)}}$$

---

**(b) Pitch and efficiency (7 marks):**

**Case A — Pitch from tearing = shearing:**

$$p_A = d + \frac{n \cdot n_s \cdot \pi d^2 \cdot \tau_v}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$p_A = 20 + \frac{1 \times 1 \times \pi \times 400 \times 100}{4 \times 12 \times 140}$$

$$p_A = 20 + \frac{125{,}664}{6720} = 20 + 18.70$$

$$p_A = 38.70\ \text{mm}$$

**Case B — Pitch from tearing = crushing:**

$$p_B = d + \frac{n \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c}{\sigma_t} = 20 + \frac{1 \times 20 \times 280}{140} = 20 + 40 = 60\ \text{mm}$$

**Governing pitch:**

$$p_{\text{design}} = \max\{38.70,\ 60\} = 60\ \text{mm}$$

**Check $p_{\min} = 2.5\,d = 50$ mm:** 60 > 50 ✓

**Efficiency of the joint:**

Since crushing governs (we used $p_B$), tearing and crushing occur simultaneously:

$$\eta = \frac{p - d}{p} \times 100\% = \frac{60 - 20}{60} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 66.67\%}$$

> **Design Verdict:** A reasonable design. The efficiency of 66.67% is in the acceptable range. The lap joint is *single riveted*, hence only 3 rivets, but the joint is simple and economical.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Using rivet shank diameter instead of hole diameter:** The hole is *always* 1.5–2.0 mm larger. All design formulas use **d = hole diameter**. Marks deducted: 2 out of 7.
>
> 2. **Confusing single-shear with double-shear $n_s$:** A *double-cover* butt joint has $n_s = 2$; a *single-cover* or *lap* joint has $n_s = 1$. Forgetting to double $n_s$ in the shearing formula halves the rivet's shear capacity. Marks deducted: 1 to 2.
>
> 3. **Picking the *smaller* pitch instead of the *larger* pitch:** The governing pitch is the **maximum** of $p_A$ and $p_B$, not the minimum. Picking the smaller pitch leads to a *crushing failure* (or tearing failure) in the actual structure. Marks deducted: 3.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to check the empirical $p_{\min} = 2.5\,d$ rule:** The riveting tool (snap, dolly, hammer) physically *cannot* insert a rivet if the pitch is too small. The code mandates this lower bound. Marks deducted: 1.
>
> 5. **Efficiency greater than 100%:** This is *impossible* and indicates a calculation error. If you obtain $\eta > 100\%$, immediately re-check the numerator and denominator.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the units:** KTU expects **N and mm** (or kN and m) consistently. Mixing units within a single calculation (e.g., mm for length and cm for area) results in a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 7. **Not stating the permissible stresses at the top of the answer:** Always begin the solution with a clearly labelled "Given Data" block listing every symbol and its numerical value. This is **examination etiquette** and earns "method marks" even if the final answer is wrong.
>
> 8. **Failing to sketch the joint with dimensions:** A *neat, dimensioned sketch* (showing pitch p, edge distance e, rivet diameter d, gauge g) earns 1 to 2 marks even before the calculation begins. KTU examiners explicitly look for it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CARD — DESIGN OF RIVETED JOINTS**
>
> ✅ A riveted joint is a *permanent fastening* transferring axial load through **shear in rivets**, **bearing (crushing) at holes**, and **tension in net plate area**.
>
> ✅ **Four failure modes** must be checked: (1) tearing between holes, (2) tearing to edge, (3) rivet shear, (4) rivet/plate crushing.
>
> ✅ The strength of the joint is the **minimum** of the four resistances.
>
> ✅ Use **hole diameter d** (not shank diameter) in all formulas.
>
> ✅ **Tearing strength:** $P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$
>
> ✅ **Shear strength:** $P_s = n\,n_s\,(\pi d^2 / 4)\,\tau_v$ — remember $n_s = 1$ for lap, $n_s = 2$ for double-cover butt.
>
> ✅ **Crushing strength:** $P_c = n\,d\,t\,\sigma_c$
>
> ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = (P_{\text{joint}} / P_{\text{plate}}) \times 100\%$. A geometric shortcut when tearing governs: $\eta = [(p - d)/p] \times 100\%$.
>
> ✅ **Equal-strength design pitch:** $p_A = d + (n\,n_s\,\pi\,d^2\,\tau_v)/(4\,t\,\sigma_t)$ and $p_B = d + (n\,d\,\sigma_c)/\sigma_t$. Take $p_{\text{design}} = \max(p_A, p_B)$.
>
> ✅ **Empirical limits:** $p_{\min} = 2.5\,d$ and $p_{\max} = \min(32\,t,\ 300\ \text{mm})$.
>
> ✅ **Edge distance** $e \geq 1.5\,p$ (typical); code minimum $e_{\min} = 1.5\,d_0$ for sheared edges, $1.25\,d_0$ for rolled edges.
>
> ✅ **Permissible stress assumption (legacy code):** $\sigma_t = 140$ N/mm², $\tau_v = 100$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 280$–$300$ N/mm². State explicitly in the answer.
>
> ✅ **Number of rivets:** $N = P / P_{r,\text{gov}}$ — round *up* to the next whole integer.
>
> ✅ **Lap joint = eccentric load path** (causes secondary bending); butt joint = concentric load path (preferred for tension members).
>
> ✅ Always conclude the answer with a **dimensioned sketch** and a **clear statement of efficiency**.
>
> ✅ **Staggered pitch** gives a 1.118-fold increase in tearing resistance over chain pitch — useful in pressure-vessel work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welded Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Welded Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welded Joint (IS 800:2007 / IS 816:1989 Definition):**
> A welded joint is a permanent connection between two or more structural steel members achieved by localized fusion of the parent metal, with or without the addition of filler metal, such that the joined components act as a single, continuous load-carrying unit. The connection strength is derived from the **metallurgical bond** formed at the weld interface rather than from mechanical interlocking (as in riveting) or friction (as in bolting).

A welded joint essentially makes two separate pieces of steel behave as if they were originally one piece, by melting the contact surfaces together — often with an additional filler rod — so that the atoms of the steel fuse across the boundary.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Metal Glue" Principle:**
> Think of welding as the difference between **nailing two boards** versus **melting the edges of those boards and letting them fuse into one solid piece**.
> - **Riveting** = mechanical stitching (two pieces held together by a metal pin)
> - **Bolting** = using screws/clamps (pieces held by external force)
> - **Welding** = molecular fusion (pieces become one continuous body, no separate fastener exists)
>
> Just as a melted ice cube rejoined to its parent block cannot be distinguished from the original, a well-executed weld creates continuity in the crystal grain structure of the steel — making it the **strongest form of steel connection** in terms of stiffness and load-transfer efficiency.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Geometric Parameters

The following terms form the vocabulary of welded-joint design and are used universally in KTU examinations:

| Symbol | Term | Definition (Plain English) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $s$ | **Leg size** | The distance from the **root (crotch)** of the weld to the **toe** (outer face) measured along the weld leg. Standard sizes: **6 mm, 8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm**. |
| $t_t$ | **Throat thickness** | The **shortest distance** from the root of the weld to its face — this is the *effective load-bearing dimension*. |
| $t_w$ | **Weld size / Effective throat** | Same as $t_t$ — the dimension that actually carries the load. |
| $L$ | **Actual weld length** | The geometric length of the weld as deposited. |
| $l_e$ | **Effective length** | The portion of the weld that is **assumed to carry load** (actual length minus end defects). |
| $\sigma$ | **Normal stress** | Force per unit area acting **perpendicular** to the throat section. |
| $\tau$ | **Shear stress** | Force per unit area acting **parallel** to the throat section. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** Always draw the weld cross-section and label the **throat thickness** with a clear arrow. Examiners award step marks for **neat, dimensioned sketches** before any calculation is done.

## 1.4 Classification of Welded Joints (Syllabus Anchor)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3) classifies welded joints primarily by **geometry of fusion** and **type of loading**:

1. **Butt Weld (Groove Weld)** — members lie in the same plane; the gap is filled by fusion.
2. **Fillet Weld** — members intersect at an angle (most commonly 90°); triangular cross-section.
3. **Plug Weld & Slot Weld** — circular/elongated holes in one plate are filled with weld metal; used for shear transfer in lap joints.
4. **Spot Weld & Seam Weld** — resistance welding for thin sheets (covered briefly in design of cold-formed sections).

For Module 3, the focus is on **fillet welds** and **butt welds** because these govern the design of primary load-bearing steel connections (beam-to-column, truss gussets, base plates).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Load Transfer in a Weld

A welded joint must transfer applied force from one member to another through the **weld metal** deposited in the joint. The transfer path is:

$$
\text{Member 1} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Weld Metal (throat)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Member 2}
$$

The **critical cross-section** is therefore the **throat section** of the weld — the smallest area through which the load passes. This is the single most important concept in welded-joint design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Welded-Joint Design (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.3):**
> The strength of a fillet weld is calculated on the **throat area** ($A_t = t_t \times l_e$), where $t_t$ is the effective throat thickness and $l_e$ is the effective length. The **fillet size $s$ alone is NOT used for strength calculation.**

## 2.2 Geometric Theory of the Fillet Weld

A standard **90° equal-leg fillet weld** has a cross-section that is an isosceles right triangle with legs of size $s$. The throat is the hypotenuse of a similar right triangle whose legs are each $s$:

$$
t_t = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} - \text{(in practice, conservatively)}
$$

For a general angle $\theta$ between the fusion faces, the throat thickness is:

$$
t_t = s \cdot \cos(45^\circ) = 0.707\,s \quad \text{(for 90° fillet)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.707?** The factor $\cos(45^\circ) = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ arises because the throat bisects the 90° fillet angle, lying at 45° to each leg. This is **fundamental and must be memorized** for KTU problems.

## 2.3 Effective Length of Weld

Weld defects (craters, undercuts, slag inclusions) are most prevalent at the **start and end** of a deposited weld. The code therefore discounts the actual length:

$$
l_e = L - 2 \cdot s
$$

where $L$ is the total length of the weld as laid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write $l_e = L - 2 \times \text{(weld size in mm)} = L - 2s$. This is correct only if $s$ is in the **same units as L** (both mm or both m). Always maintain **unit consistency**.

## 2.4 Strength Formulae (IS 800:2007 Basis)

The **permissible shear stress** in a shop weld is governed by the grade of weld electrode. For E41 electrodes (matching Fe 410 grade steel):

$$
\tau_{vw} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}}
$$

For **field welds**, a further reduction factor of **0.9** is applied because field conditions compromise quality.

| Weld Type | Permissible Stress | Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shop fillet weld | $f_{uw}/(\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw})$ | IS 800 Cl. 10.5.7.1.1 |
| Field fillet weld | $0.9 \times$ shop value | IS 800 Cl. 10.5.7.1.2 |
| Butt weld (tension) | Same as parent metal strength | IS 800 Cl. 10.5.6 |
| Butt weld (compression) | Same as parent metal strength | IS 800 Cl. 10.5.6 |
| Plug/Slot weld (shear) | Based on nominal shear area | IS 800 Cl. 10.5.8 |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Every KTU 2024 problem on welded joints reduces to one of these formulas. Memorize with units clearly marked.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $t_t = 0.707\,s$ | Throat thickness of 90° fillet weld | mm |
| 2 | $A_t = t_t \times l_e$ | Effective throat area | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 3 | $l_e = L - 2s$ | Effective length (end defect discount) | mm |
| 4 | $\tau_v = P / A_t$ | Shear stress in fillet weld (direct shear) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 5 | $P_{strength} = \tau_{vw} \times A_t$ | Strength of weld in pure shear | N |
| 6 | $\tau_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \le \tau_{vw}$ | Combined stress check (IS 800 Cl. 10.5.7.1.1) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 7 | $P_{butt} = f_y \cdot t \cdot L / \gamma_{m0}$ | Strength of complete-penetration butt weld | N |
| 8 | $s_{min} = \max(3\text{ mm},\, \sqrt{t_{max}})$ | Minimum fillet size (IS 800 Table 21) | mm |
| 9 | $s_{max} = t_{min} - 1.5\text{ mm}$ (for edge) | Maximum fillet size to avoid melt-through | mm |
| 10 | $r_{vw} = f_{uw} / (\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw})$ | Permissible shear stress (factory/shop weld) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Truss bridges and roof trusses** rely entirely on **fillet-welded gusset plates** to transfer member forces.
> - **Building frames** use welded moment connections (beam-to-column) sized using the combined-stress formula (Eq. 6).
> - **Transmission towers** use **butt-welded angle sections** for long unbroken chords.
> - **Pressure vessels and penstocks** use **complete-penetration butt welds** rated at parent-metal strength.
>
> A miscalculation in weld size in any of these structures can result in **progressive collapse** — making this topic central to "Safety in Design of Structures."

## 2.7 Failure Modes (Conceptual)

Welds fail in one of three principal modes — understanding this is essential for the KTU "design rationale" question:

1. **Throat Cracking** — fracture along the throat plane due to insufficient $t_t$.
2. **Toe Failure / Undercut** — stress concentration at the weld toe (often a fatigue-driven failure).
3. **Fusion-Face Shear** — failure along the fusion boundary if the parent metal is dirty or preheated poorly.

The first mode is what **throat-thickness design** directly controls.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Throat Thickness of a 90° Fillet Weld

### Setup

Consider an isosceles right-triangle cross-section of a fillet weld. Let the two equal legs be of length $s$ (the **leg size** or **fillet size**), meeting at the root. The throat thickness $t_t$ is the perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The cross-section is a right triangle with legs $s$ and $s$. The hypotenuse (the outer face of the weld) has length:

$$
h = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = s\sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that the throat bisects the 90° angle.**
The throat is drawn from the root perpendicular to the hypotenuse. Since the triangle is isosceles right, the perpendicular from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse lies at **45°** to each leg. Thus the throat makes an angle of $45^\circ$ with either leg.

**Step 3 — Apply trigonometry.**
In the small right triangle formed by the throat, the leg $s$, and half the hypotenuse:

$$
\cos(45^\circ) = \frac{t_t}{s}
$$

Solving for $t_t$:

$$
t_t = s \cdot \cos(45^\circ) = s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Rationalize and present the result.**

$$
t_t = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.7071\,s \approx 0.707\,s
$$

This is the **canonical result** used in every KTU problem on fillet welds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Approximation Note:** The factor is exactly $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071068...$, but code practice rounds to **0.70** when very conservative and **0.707** for standard problems. KTU expects **0.707** unless the problem specifies otherwise.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Weld Length with End Defects

The **actual length** $L$ of a fillet weld is the geometric length of the deposited metal. However, the **effective length** $l_e$ is what the code uses for strength calculation.

### Reasoning

The two ends of a fillet weld contain **craters** (depressions at the start) and **undercuts** (grooves at the finish). IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.5.4.1) prescribes that these defective regions be excluded by discounting **two weld sizes** — one at each end:

$$
l_e = L - 2\,s
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtlety:** Some textbooks write $l_e = L - 2 \times (\text{weld size})$. This is dimensionally inconsistent if $L$ is in metres and $s$ in mm. The KTU convention: **always express both in the same unit** (mm is standard).

## 3.3 Derivation: Strength of a Fillet Weld in Direct Shear

Let a transverse fillet weld of effective length $l_e$ and throat $t_t$ carry a tensile load $P$ along the longitudinal direction of the parent plate.

**Step 1 — Identify the failure plane.**
The weld will fail by shear along the throat plane (smallest cross-section). The throat area resisting this shear is:

$$
A_t = t_t \times l_e
$$

**Step 2 — Write the average shear stress.**

$$
\tau_v = \frac{P}{A_t} = \frac{P}{t_t \cdot l_e}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the IS 800:2007 permissible stress limit.**

$$
\tau_v \le \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}}
$$

where:
- $f_{uw}$ = ultimate strength of weld metal (e.g., **410 MPa** for E41 electrode)
- $\gamma_{mw}$ = partial safety factor for weld = **1.25** (shop) or **1.50** (field)

**Step 4 — Solve for the load capacity $P_d$.**

$$
P_d = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} \cdot t_t \cdot l_e
$$

This is the **design strength** of the weld in pure shear.

## 3.4 Worked Example Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A tie member 120 mm wide × 12 mm thick is connected to a 12 mm thick gusset plate by means of **two parallel fillet welds**, one on each side of the tie. The welds are 180 mm long, with leg size 8 mm. The steel grade is **Fe 410**, and the welds are made in the **shop** using **E41 electrodes**. Check the adequacy of the welds for a factored tensile force of **240 kN** in the tie.

### Solution

**Given data:**
- Plate thickness $t = 12$ mm
- Weld leg size $s = 8$ mm
- Weld length $L = 180$ mm (per weld)
- Number of welds $n = 2$ (parallel, on both sides)
- Factored tensile load $P_u = 240$ kN $= 240 \times 10^3$ N
- Weld metal ultimate strength $f_{uw} = 410$ N/mm²
- Partial safety factor (shop) $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

**Step 1 — Compute the effective throat thickness.**

$$
t_t = 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective length of each weld.**

$$
l_e = L - 2s = 180 - 2(8) = 180 - 16 = 164 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total effective throat area.**

$$
A_{t,\text{total}} = n \times t_t \times l_e = 2 \times 5.656 \times 164
$$

$$
A_{t,\text{total}} = 1855.17 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the design shear strength of the weld.**

$$
r_{vw} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25}
$$

$$
r_{vw} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \frac{410}{2.165} = 189.37 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the design strength $P_d$ of the welds.**

$$
P_d = r_{vw} \times A_{t,\text{total}} = 189.37 \times 1855.17
$$

$$
P_d = 351,310 \text{ N} \approx 351.31 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the applied factored load.**

$$
P_d = 351.31 \text{ kN} > P_u = 240 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion: The welds are SAFE.**
> Since design strength (351.31 kN) exceeds the factored applied load (240 kN), the connection is adequate. **Note:** It is also good practice to check that the **parent metal** (the tie plate and gusset plate) is not weaker than the weld. For Fe 410, the parent metal strength governs if the plate's net section is significantly reduced.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Weld Size Limits (IS 800 Table 21)

The **minimum** and **maximum** fillet sizes are code-controlled to ensure both strength and weldability:

| Thickness of Thicker Plate $t$ (mm) | Minimum Fillet Size $s_{min}$ (mm) |
| :---: | :---: |
| $t \le 10$ | 3 |
| $10 < t \le 20$ | 5 |
| $20 < t \le 32$ | 6 |
| $32 < t \le 50$ | 8 (first weld pass) |
| $t > 50$ | 10 (first weld pass) |

**Maximum size rule:** Along the **edge** of a plate, $s_{max} = t_{plate} - 1.5$ mm to avoid melting through.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick Question:** *"Why cannot the maximum fillet size be arbitrarily large?"*
> **Model Answer:** Because the heat input from a very large weld can melt through the parent plate (called **melt-through** or **burn-through**), reducing the effective thickness of the connected member and creating a stress concentration. The 1.5 mm safety margin ensures the fusion zone stays within the plate.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Welded Joint Design Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **decision process** an engineer follows when designing a fillet-welded connection under IS 800:2007. This is the procedure KTU examiners expect students to articulate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Applied Factored Load Pu kN] --> B[Identify Member Thickness t mm]
    B --> C{Determine s_min from IS 800 Table 21}
    C --> D[Choose Trial Leg Size s mm]
    D --> E[Compute Throat Thickness tt = 0.707 s]
    E --> F[Compute Effective Length le = L - 2s]
    F --> G[Compute Effective Throat Area At = tt x le]
    G --> H[Compute Permissible Shear Stress r_vw = f_uw / sqrt 3 x gamma_mw]
    H --> I[Compute Design Weld Strength Pd = r_vw x At]
    I --> J{Is Pd greater than or equal to Pu?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Check Parent Metal Strength fy x Anet / gamma_m0]
    K --> L{Parent metal adequate?}
    L -- Yes --> M[FINAL DESIGN: SAFE Provide Weld Details]
    L -- No --> N[Increase Plate Thickness or Use Higher Grade Steel]
    J -- No --> O[Increase Weld Length L or Leg Size s]
    O --> E
    N --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> The flowchart contains two decision diamonds (J and L). Diamond J enforces the **weld strength criterion**; diamond L enforces the **parent-metal strength criterion**. Both must be satisfied for the connection to be classified as **safe**. If either fails, the design must be revised and the loop re-iterated.

## 4.2 Fillet Weld Geometry — Annotated Mermaid Schematic

The following Mermaid graph renders the **geometric structure** of a 90° fillet weld cross-section, with each labelled dimension. This is the diagram KTU students should reproduce in their answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph weldCrossSection["Fillet Weld Cross Section 90 degree"]
        A1[Root Junction of Two Plates] -->|leg s mm| B1[Toe on Vertical Plate]
        A1 -->|leg s mm| C1[Toe on Horizontal Plate]
        B1 --- D1[Weld Face Hypotenuse]
        C1 --- D1
        E1[Throat Thickness tt = 0.707 s] -.perpendicular to face.-> D1
    end
    subgraph loadPath["Load Transfer Path"]
        P1[Applied Force P kN] --> Q1[Parent Plate 1]
        Q1 --> R1[Weld Throat tt]
        R1 --> S1[Parent Plate 2]
    end
```

**Description for the student:**
- The **root** $A_1$ is the inside corner where the two plates meet.
- The **toes** $B_1$ and $C_1$ are the outer corners where the weld meets the plate surfaces.
- The **weld face** $D_1$ is the outer surface of the weld (the hypotenuse).
- The **throat thickness** $t_t$ is the *shortest distance* from the root to the face — this is what carries the load.
- The **leg size** $s$ is the visible width of the weld on each plate.

## 4.3 Stress Distribution in a Transverse Fillet Weld

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph transverseWeld["Transverse Fillet Weld under Tension"]
        L1[Tensile Force P applied along plate axis] --> L2[Stress Concentrates at Weld Root]
        L2 --> L3[Shear Stress tau develops along Throat Plane]
        L3 --> L4[Maximum Stress at Root Decreases toward Toe]
    end
    L4 --> L5[Failure Initiates at Root if Throat Inadequate]
    L4 --> L6[Alternative Failure: Toe Cracking due to Stress Concentration]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** In a **transverse** fillet weld (weld perpendicular to load), the stress is **not uniform** along the throat — it peaks at the root. IS 800:2007 nevertheless assumes uniform distribution to keep the design procedure simple. The actual peak stress can be **1.7 to 2.0 times** the average, which is why high-cycle fatigue in welded structures is governed by toe-stress concentration factors.
