# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q1(a)]**
>
> **Q1(a) (3 Marks):** Define a *fillet weld*. Sketch a 90° equal-leg fillet weld and label the **leg size**, **throat thickness**, **root**, and **toe**.

**Model Answer:**

A fillet weld is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces meeting at an angle between **0° and 135°** (most commonly 90°). It is the most common type of weld used for connecting plates, brackets, and gussets in structural steelwork.

**Sketch and labels:**

```
       |\
       | \  ← Weld face (hypotenuse)
   s   |  \
       |___\  ← Horizontal plate
        s
```

- **Root** — the inside corner where the two plates meet (apex of the triangle).
- **Toe** — the outer corner of the weld where it meets each plate face.
- **Leg size ($s$)** — the distance from the root to the toe along each plate face.
- **Throat thickness ($t_t$)** — the shortest distance from the root to the weld face, computed as $t_t = 0.707\,s$ for a 90° fillet.

The **effective area** for load transfer is the throat area $A_t = t_t \times l_e$, which is the basis of fillet-weld design as per IS 800:2007.

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Q1(b)]**
>
> **Q1(b) (3 Marks):** State the **minimum** and **maximum** fillet weld sizes for a 16 mm thick plate, as per IS 800:2007.

**Model Answer:**

According to **IS 800:2007, Table 21**, the **minimum** fillet weld size depends on the **thickness of the thicker plate** being joined.

For a plate thickness of **16 mm** (i.e., $10 < t \le 20$ mm):

- **Minimum leg size, $s_{min} = 5$ mm** — This is controlled by heat dissipation: a small weld on a thick plate would cool too fast, leading to brittleness and cracking.

- **Maximum leg size, $s_{max}$** — If the weld is along the **edge** of the plate, $s_{max} = t - 1.5$ mm $= 16 - 1.5 = 14.5$ mm. If the weld is not on the edge (e.g., in a lap joint interior), no upper limit is imposed by the code, but practical limits apply based on heat input and distortion control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A:**
> Students frequently quote $s_{min}$ from **Table 21** but forget the **edge-condition rule** for $s_{max}$. Always check whether the weld is along an *edge* or *not*. The 1.5 mm discount applies **only at edges** to prevent melt-through.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q2]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

A steel tie of cross-section 120 mm × 10 mm is welded to a 12 mm thick gusset plate using **two transverse fillet welds**, one at each end of the tie. Each weld is **100 mm long** with leg size **8 mm**. The steel is **Fe 410** grade, and welding is done in the **shop** with **E41 electrodes**. A factored tensile force of **180 kN** acts on the tie. Check the **adequacy of the welds** and the **adequacy of the parent tie section**.

#### Part (a) — Weld Strength Check (7 Marks)

**Given data:**
- $P_u = 180$ kN $= 180 \times 10^3$ N
- $s = 8$ mm, $L = 100$ mm (each weld)
- $f_{uw} = 410$ N/mm², $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop weld)

**Step 1 — Throat thickness.**

$$
t_t = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length of one weld.**

$$
l_e = L - 2s = 100 - 16 = 84 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Total effective throat area (2 welds).**

$$
A_{t,\text{total}} = 2 \times t_t \times l_e = 2 \times 5.656 \times 84 = 950.21 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Permissible shear stress in the weld.**

$$
r_{vw} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \times \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.37 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Design strength of the welds.**

$$
P_d = r_{vw} \times A_{t,\text{total}} = 189.37 \times 950.21 = 179{,}900 \text{ N} \approx 179.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with applied load.**

$$
P_d = 179.9 \text{ kN} < P_u = 180 \text{ kN}
$$

**Conclusion (Part a):** The welds are **slightly understrength** (deficit $\approx 0.1$ kN, about 0.06%). In a KTU exam, this is conventionally treated as **borderline inadequate** — the recommended remediation is to **increase the weld length to 105 mm** or **increase the leg size to 9 mm**.

#### Part (b) — Parent Metal Strength Check (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Gross area of the tie plate.**

$$
A_g = 120 \times 10 = 1200 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Net area.** (No holes in this tie — it is welded, not bolted.)

$$
A_n = A_g = 1200 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Design tensile strength of the plate (IS 800 Cl. 6.1).**

$$
T_{dg} = \frac{f_y \cdot A_g}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{250 \times 1200}{1.10} = 272{,}727 \text{ N} \approx 272.7 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times f_u \cdot A_n}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 1200}{1.25} = 354{,}240 \text{ N} \approx 354.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Governing strength (lower of the two).**

$$
T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}) = 272.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with applied load.**

$$
T_d = 272.7 \text{ kN} > P_u = 180 \text{ kN}
$$

**Conclusion (Part b):** The **parent metal is SAFE**. Strength utilization ratio $= 180/272.7 = 0.66$ (66%), which is acceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (Question A):**
> The most common mistake in this type of question is **forgetting to check the parent metal**. Students often stop at the weld strength check. The question **explicitly asks for the parent metal check** — examiners will deduct **up to 4 of the 7 marks** in part (b) if this step is omitted. **Always check both weld and parent metal** in KTU problems.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Q3]**

A bracket plate carrying a **factored load of 100 kN** is connected to the flange of an I-section column by **vertical fillet welds on both sides of the bracket**, as shown in a typical end-plate configuration. The welds are **150 mm long** with leg size **10 mm**. The bracket plate is **120 mm wide × 12 mm thick**. The weld is a **field weld** using **E41 electrodes**. Determine the **average shear stress** developed in the welds and check whether the welds are safe.

#### Part (a) — Shear Stress Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given data:**
- $P_u = 100$ kN $= 100 \times 10^3$ N
- $s = 10$ mm, $L = 150$ mm (each weld)
- $n = 2$ welds (both sides of bracket)
- Field weld: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$

**Step 1 — Throat thickness.**

$$
t_t = 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length of one weld.**

$$
l_e = L - 2s = 150 - 20 = 130 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Total effective throat area.**

$$
A_{t,\text{total}} = 2 \times t_t \times l_e = 2 \times 7.07 \times 130 = 1838.2 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Average shear stress developed.**

$$
\tau_v = \frac{P_u}{A_{t,\text{total}}} = \frac{100{,}000}{1838.2} = 54.40 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Permissible shear stress for a field weld.**

$$
r_{vw,\text{field}} = \frac{0.9 \times f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \times \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{0.9 \times 410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.50}
$$

$$
r_{vw,\text{field}} = \frac{369}{2.598} = 142.04 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

#### Part (b) — Safety Check and Conclusion (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compare developed stress with permissible stress.**

$$
\tau_v = 54.40 \text{ N/mm}^2 < r_{vw,\text{field}} = 142.04 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the strength utilization ratio.**

$$
\text{Utilization} = \frac{\tau_v}{r_{vw,\text{field}}} = \frac{54.40}{142.04} = 0.383 = 38.3\%
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (Question B):** The welds are **SAFE**. The developed shear stress (54.40 N/mm²) is well below the permissible value (142.04 N/mm²), and the connection uses only **38.3%** of its capacity. This is conservative and acceptable, though in practice the designer might reduce the weld size to **8 mm** to economize on weld metal and heat input, provided the parent-metal check still passes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (Question B):**
> A frequent error is using the **shop-weld permissible stress** ($r_{vw} = 189.37$ N/mm²) instead of the **field-weld value** ($r_{vw,\text{field}} = 142.04$ N/mm²). The **0.9 factor for field conditions** is mandatory when welding is done on site. Marks lost here are typically 2-3 per occurrence. Always state explicitly whether the weld is **shop or field** and apply the correct $\gamma_{mw}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Welded Joints**

- **Definition:** Welded joints are **permanent, monolithic** connections made by **fusion** of parent metal ± filler metal. No mechanical fastener remains in the final joint.

- **Two principal types** for Module 3: **Butt weld** (members in the same plane) and **Fillet weld** (members at an angle, usually 90°).

- **Fillet weld geometry — MUST MEMORIZE:**
  - $t_t = 0.707\,s$ (90° fillet)
  - $l_e = L - 2s$ (effective length with end defect discount)
  - $A_t = t_t \times l_e$ (effective throat area)

- **Permissible shear stress in weld:**
  - **Shop weld:** $r_{vw} = f_{uw} / (\sqrt{3} \times 1.25)$
  - **Field weld:** $r_{vw} = 0.9 \times f_{uw} / (\sqrt{3} \times 1.50)$

- **Code basis:** IS 800:2007 governs limit-state design of welded steel connections. Welding procedure is governed by **IS 816:1989** and **IS 9595:1996**.

- **Two criteria must be checked for every welded connection:**
  1. **Weld strength** (throat area × permissible shear stress $\ge$ applied factored load)
  2. **Parent metal strength** (gross-section yielding AND net-section rupture, governed by $f_y$ and $f_u$ respectively)

- **Weld size limits (IS 800 Table 21):**
  - $s_{min}$ depends on thicker plate thickness.
  - $s_{max} = t_{plate} - 1.5$ mm **only at edges** (to prevent burn-through).

- **Failure mode priority:** Throat cracking → Toe failure → Fusion-face failure. Design is controlled by preventing **throat cracking** via the $t_t$ formula.

- **For transverse vs parallel welds:** Transverse welds are stiffer and develop a more uniform stress; parallel (longitudinal) welds have a **strain compatibility** issue, and combined stress checks apply for welds under moment + shear.

- **Partial safety factors to remember:**
  - $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) / 1.50 (field)
  - $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (yielding)
  - $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (ultimate / rupture)

- **Always state** the **type of weld (shop or field)**, the **electrode grade**, and the **steel grade** in your answer — these determine $f_{uw}$, $f_y$, $f_u$, and the safety factors. Examiners often award step marks **for stating assumptions** before any calculation.

- **Practical tip:** In exam problems, **draw a neat cross-section sketch** showing the weld triangle, label the leg size $s$, the throat $t_t$, and the failure plane. This earns **easy step marks** and demonstrates understanding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Welded Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Welded Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **welded joint** is a permanent, non-bolted, non-riveted fusion connection formed by heating the base metals of two or more structural members to their plastic or molten state (typically using an electric arc, gas flame, friction, or resistance heating), with or without the addition of a filler metal, such that the parts coalesce at the molecular level and behave as a continuous, monolithic body upon solidification. The heat-affected zone (HAZ), the weld metal, and the parent metal together constitute the joint geometry.

In the context of **KTU 2024 OESFT722 – Safety in Design of Structures (Module 3)**, welded joints are examined as a primary load-transfer mechanism in steel structures, machine frames, pressure vessels, and bridges. The evaluation of welded joints from a **safety-in-design** perspective requires a balanced analysis of their structural advantages (strength, rigidity, leak-tightness) versus their latent disadvantages (residual stresses, brittleness, difficulty in inspection, and susceptibility to fatigue cracks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, welded joints are studied with a specific focus on **failure modes**, **inspection limitations**, and **safety implications during service**. Students must be able to justify *when* a welded joint is preferred over a bolted/riveted joint in a safety-critical design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you have **two pieces of chocolate bar** in front of you. If you were to simply tie them together with a string, they would still be two separate pieces held loosely — like a *bolted joint*. But if you were to **melt the ends of both pieces and let them fuse into one solid bar as they cool**, you would end up with what looks like a single, continuous chocolate slab — there is no string, no tie, no separator. That fused slab is exactly what a **welded joint** does for steel members.

The key insight is this:

- **Bolting / Riveting** = Mechanical Grip (parts are *held together* by external fasteners).
- **Welding** = Molecular Fusion (parts *become one piece* through metallurgical bonding).

Because welding turns two pieces into one continuous piece, it is generally **stronger and stiffer**, but it also means that:
- You cannot easily "undo" it (no disassembly).
- The heat used to fuse the metal leaves behind a "scar" — the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — which has different mechanical properties than the parent metal.
- Tiny invisible defects (porosity, slag inclusions, undercuts, cracks) can hide inside the weld, and these defects may not show up until the structure is under load.

This is the **safety paradox of welded joints**: they look clean and seamless on the outside, but their internal integrity is invisible to the naked eye.

---

## 1.3 Important Standards & Reference Codes

The following standard codes govern the design, fabrication, and inspection of welded joints in India and internationally:

| Code / Standard | Governing Body | Relevance to Safety |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 816: 1969** | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | Code of practice for use of metal arc welding for general construction in mild steel |
| **IS 2062** | BIS | Hot rolled medium and high tensile structural steel specification |
| **IS 9595** | BIS | Recommendations for metal arc welding of carbon and carbon-manganese steels |
| **IS 1182** | BIS | Recommended practice for radiographic examination of fusion welded butt joints in steel plates |
| **AWS D1.1** | American Welding Society | Structural Welding Code – Steel (international reference) |
| **EN 1090-2** | European Committee for Standardization | Execution of steel structures (welding requirements) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Memorize):** The minimum preheat temperature for **low-carbon structural steel (grade E250 / Fe 410W)** to avoid hydrogen-induced cracking is typically **≥ 100 °C** when the combined thickness exceeds **40 mm**, as per **IS 9595**.

---

## 1.4 KTU Board Examiner's Lens — What Defines a "Safe" Welded Joint?

From the perspective of **Safety in Design**, a welded joint is deemed safe when it satisfies the following three pillars:

1. **Adequate Strength:** The weld throat and leg sizes are sufficient to transfer the design loads without exceeding the permissible stresses in the parent metal or weld metal.
2. **Adequate Ductility:** The joint can undergo plastic deformation under overload conditions (i.e., it does not fail in a brittle manner).
3. **Inspectability & Reliability:** The joint geometry, material, and process permit **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** to detect flaws before the structure enters service.

These three criteria form the basis on which the advantages and disadvantages (covered in subsequent sections) must be evaluated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Matrix

## 2.1 Classification of Welded Joints (Foundation for Safety Analysis)

Before discussing the pros and cons, it is essential to classify welded joints, because **the safety implications differ vastly by joint type**.

### 2.1.1 By Type of Weld Geometry

- **Butt Weld:** Two members lie in the same plane and are joined edge-to-edge. *Highest strength, most efficient, preferred for primary load transfer.*
- **Fillet Weld:** Two members meet at an angle (typically 90°) and the weld is deposited in the triangular gap. *Most common in structural steelwork.*
- **Plug Weld / Slot Weld:** A circular or elongated hole is cut in one plate and filled with weld metal. *Used to transmit shear, or to prevent buckling of lapped plates.*
- **Edge Weld:** Made on the edge of one or both members. *Used for low-stress attachments.*

### 2.1.2 By Type of Loading

- **Parallel / Longitudinal Fillet Weld:** Load is parallel to the weld axis (shear). The throat is stressed in **shear**.
- **Transverse Fillet Weld:** Load is perpendicular to the weld axis. The throat is stressed in a **combination of shear and bending**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transverse fillet welds are approximately 30% stronger than longitudinal fillet welds of the same size** for static loading, due to a more favourable stress distribution and strain compatibility with the parent metal.

### 2.1.3 By Position of Welding

- **Flat (1G/1F):** Most efficient, easiest to inspect.
- **Horizontal (2G/2F):** Common in beams.
- **Vertical (3G/3F):** Requires skilled welders.
- **Overhead (4G/4F):** Most difficult, most defect-prone.

> The **welding position directly affects defect density** — overhead welds exhibit higher porosity, slag inclusion, and undercut rates. This is a critical safety factor.

---

## 2.2 Advantages of Welded Joints — Exhaustive Engineering Analysis

The following table is the **definitive KTU high-yield summary** of all advantages, classified by the engineering criterion they address.

| # | Advantage | Engineering Rationale | Safety-in-Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Permanent / Monolithic Connection** | The fusion bond creates a single, continuous body; no fasteners to loosen. | Eliminates risk of progressive loosening under vibration → critical for bridges, machine frames. |
| 2 | **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio** | The weld metal typically has strength ≥ parent metal (overmatching). | Reduces dead load, increases load-carrying efficiency. |
| 3 | **Greater Rigidity / Stiffness** | No relative slip between parts; behaves as one piece. | Smaller deflections under service load → improved serviceability. |
| 4 | **Leak-Tightness** | Continuous, gap-free joint — no gaskets or sealants required. | Mandatory for pressure vessels, storage tanks, pipelines. |
| 5 | **Versatility of Joint Geometry** | Can join any shape — tubes, plates, angles, I-sections, channels, T-sections. | Enables architecturally complex structures (e.g., space frames, tubular trusses). |
| 6 | **No Pre-Drilling Required** | Unlike bolted joints, the base metal is not weakened by holes. | Eliminates **net section failure** → full parent-metal strength is available. |
| 7 | **Smoother Surface / Better Aesthetics** | No protruding bolt heads, nuts, or rivets. | Reduces drag (aerodynamic / hydrodynamic) and improves fatigue behaviour at the surface. |
| 8 | **Easier to Coat / Paint** | Smooth surface allows uniform application of protective coatings. | Improves corrosion resistance → longer service life. |
| 9 | **Economical for Complex Shapes** | Less material wastage; reduced fabrication time for intricate assemblies. | Lower overall project cost. |
| 10 | **Suitable for Site & Workshop** | Portable equipment (arc welders) enable on-site fabrication. | Critical for repair work, shipbuilding, and field modifications. |
| 11 | **Easier Alignment of Members** | Minor mismatches can be absorbed by weld metal deposition. | Tolerates higher fabrication tolerances than bolting. |
| 12 | **Lower Weight of Structure** | No bolt/rivet mass. | Beneficial in aerospace, automotive, and tall-building applications. |
| 13 | **Better Fatigue Performance (for butt welds)** | Properly ground butt welds have a smooth profile, reducing stress concentrations. | Increases fatigue life under cyclic loading. |
| 14 | **Smaller Size of Joint** | Joint occupies minimal space. | Useful in compact machine designs. |
| 15 | **No Noise During Fabrication** | Welding is relatively quieter than riveting/pneumatic bolting. | Better occupational health & safety at the fabrication shop. |

---

## 2.3 Disadvantages of Welded Joints — Exhaustive Engineering Analysis

This is the **most critical section from a Safety-in-Design perspective** — every disadvantage represents a latent hazard.

| # | Disadvantage | Engineering Root Cause | Safety-in-Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High Initial Cost of Equipment** | Arc-welding machines, gas regulators, fume extractors, and NDT equipment are expensive. | Capital-intensive — limits small-scale fabricators. |
| 2 | **Requires Skilled Labour & Certified Welders** | Welding is a high-skill trade; quality is operator-dependent. | Unskilled welds → defects → catastrophic failures (e.g., 1980 Alexander Kielland platform, 1984 Bhopal intermediate storage failures). |
| 3 | **Difficult to Inspect Internally** | Weld flaws (porosity, slag, lack of fusion, cracks) may be hidden below the surface. | **CRITICAL:** Failure mode is often invisible until catastrophic collapse. |
| 4 | **Residual Stresses & Distortion** | Non-uniform heating and cooling induce locked-in stresses and shrinkage distortion. | Reduces fatigue life, may cause buckling of thin plates, misalignment of members. |
| 5 | **Brittleness in Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Rapid cooling produces coarse-grained microstructures (e.g., Widmanstätten ferrite, martensite). | HAZ is the **weakest zone** — prone to brittle fracture at low temperatures. |
| 6 | **Susceptibility to Fatigue Cracks** | Stress concentrations at weld toe, undercut, and profile irregularities initiate fatigue cracks. | Welded joints in bridges, cranes, and railway vehicles require careful fatigue design (IS 800:2007 / IRC codes). |
| 7 | **Difficulty in Rectification / Repair** | Repairing a defective weld often requires grinding out the defective zone and re-welding → introduces new residual stresses. | Costly, time-consuming, and may further weaken the joint. |
| 8 | **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) is Expensive & Time-Consuming** | Radiographic, ultrasonic, magnetic particle, and dye penetrant tests add 5–15% to project cost. | NDT is essential but increases total lifecycle cost. |
| 9 | **Fire & Health Hazards** | Arc radiation, molten metal spatter, toxic fumes (Mn, Cr, Ni, ozone, NOₓ), and electric shock. | Strict PPE and ventilation required. |
| 10 | **Limited to Similar Metals (in most processes)** | Fusion requires compatible metallurgy and thermal expansion coefficients. | Cannot easily weld steel to aluminium, or copper to steel. |
| 11 | **Permanent — Cannot be Disassembled** | Once welded, the joint cannot be undone without cutting. | Complicates maintenance, dismantling, and recycling. |
| 12 | **Heat Treatment Required for Thick Sections** | To relieve residual stresses and temper the HAZ, post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) is required for sections > ~32 mm. | Adds cost and shop-floor time. |
| 13 | **Lamellar Tearing Risk in T-joints** | Through-thickness shrinkage stresses in rolled steel sections can cause lamellar tearing. | Particularly problematic in thick T, K, and corner joints. |
| 14 | **Distortion During Welding** | Asymmetric thermal expansion/contraction causes warping, angular distortion, and bowing. | Compromises dimensional accuracy, requiring straightening. |
| 15 | **Galvanic / Corrosion Effects at Weld** | Weld metal and HAZ may have different electrochemical potential than the parent metal. | Weld becomes a preferential site for corrosion → accelerated degradation. |
| 16 | **Embrittlement at Low Temperatures** | Notch sensitivity increases at sub-zero temperatures. | Critical for cryogenic vessels, arctic structures, and LPG tanks. |
| 17 | **No Visual Indication of Quality** | A "good-looking" weld bead may contain lethal internal defects. | Visual inspection alone is grossly insufficient. |
| 18 | **Toxic Fume Generation** | Especially with stainless steel (Cr⁶⁺) and galvanised steel (Zn fumes → "metal fume fever"). | Health surveillance of welders is mandatory. |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Since this topic is primarily **qualitative-comparative**, the formulas below are the **essential quantitative anchors** that examiners will test.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols | Unit | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Throat thickness of fillet weld | $t_t = 0.707 \, s$ | $s$ = leg size | mm | Most frequently tested formula |
| 2 | Effective throat thickness | $t_e = 0.7 \, s$ | $s$ = leg size | mm | Per IS 800:2007 |
| 3 | Strength of fillet weld per unit length | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}$ | $f_{uw}$ = ultimate strength of weld, $\gamma_{mw}$ = partial safety factor | N/mm² | As per IS 800:2007 |
| 4 | Design shear strength of fillet weld | $F_{wd} = f_{wd} \times t_e$ | per unit length | N/mm | Used in Part B numericals |
| 5 | Minimum weld size (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.3.1) | $s_{min}$ depends on thicker plate thickness $t$ | Table 21 of IS 800 | mm | Common 2-mark question |
| 6 | Maximum effective throat thickness | $t_{e,max} = 0.7 \times s_{min}$ | — | mm | For shop/site welds |
| 7 | Effective length of fillet weld | $L_w = L - 2 s$ (or actual length − 2× leg size) | $L$ = total length | mm | End-crater deduction |
| 8 | Stress concentration factor (typical) | $K_t = 1.5$ to $3.0$ for transverse fillet welds | — | — | Fatigue design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** Most "advantages/disadvantages" questions appear as **Part A (3 marks)** short-answer questions. However, when the question asks to "compare welded and riveted joints" or "discuss welded joints with reference to safety," it becomes a **Part B (14 marks)** question that combines qualitative discussion + numerical design.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessels & Boilers:** Welded joints are mandatory due to leak-tightness requirements. The ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (Section VIII) and Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR) govern their design.
- **Bridges:** Modern steel bridges (e.g., Bandra-Worli Sea Link, Howrah Bridge replacements) are 100% welded with full penetration butt welds.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Welded tubular joints are the norm, but inspection and fatigue are critical concerns.
- **Automotive & Aerospace:** Welded joints minimise weight while maintaining rigidity.
- **Reinforced Concrete:** Welded wire mesh and welded couplers for rebar splicing rely on the same fusion principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Comparison Tables

## 3.1 Comparative Analysis: Welded vs. Riveted vs. Bolted Joints

This is the **most frequently asked analytical question** in KTU Module 3. The following derivation-style breakdown walks through every comparison axis.

### 3.1.1 Strength Considerations

| Criterion | Riveted Joint | Bolted Joint | Welded Joint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency (Strength of joint / Strength of solid plate) | 65 – 80 % | 70 – 85 % | **90 – 100 %** |
| Strength of weakest section | Plate at hole (net section) | Plate at hole (net section) | Weld metal / HAZ (not the plate) |
| Stress concentration | Very high (at hole edge) | High (at hole edge) | Moderate (at weld toe) |
| Reduction in section area | Yes (hole drilled) | Yes (hole drilled) | **No** (no hole) |
| Load transfer mechanism | Shear in rivet + Bearing on hole | Shear/tension in bolt + Bearing on hole | Direct stress transfer through fusion bond |

### 3.1.2 Rigidity & Stiffness

| Criterion | Riveted | Bolted (bearing-type) | Welded |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slip under load | Yes (initial slip) | Yes (slip possible) | **No slip** |
| Deflection under load | Larger | Larger | **Smallest** |
| Behaviour under vibration | Rivets may loosen | Bolts may loosen | Monolithic — no loosening |

### 3.1.3 Cost, Time & Labour

| Criterion | Riveted | Bolted (HSFG) | Welded |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial equipment cost | Low | Moderate | High |
| Skilled labour required | Moderate | Moderate | **High** |
| Fabrication time | Slow (drilling, fitting, riveting) | Moderate (drilling, fitting, tightening) | **Fast** (single-pass deposition) |
| Inspection cost | Low (visual + hammer test) | Moderate (torque + visual) | **High** (NDT mandatory) |
| Repair / rectification | Easy (re-rivet) | Easy (replace bolt) | Difficult (cut + re-weld) |

### 3.1.4 Safety & Reliability

| Criterion | Riveted | Bolted (HSFG) | Welded |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visible failure indicators | Yes (cracked rivet, loose head) | Yes (loosened bolt) | **No** (often sudden, brittle) |
| Suitability for dynamic / fatigue loads | Poor | Good | Moderate (depends on weld profile) |
| Temperature resistance | Good | Moderate (creep at high T) | **Excellent** (no fastener softening) |
| Leak-tightness | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: When to Choose Welded Joints (Safety-Driven Decision)

The decision logic below is the **KTU 2024 high-yield analytical framework** for safety-in-design questions.

**Step 1 — Identify the Failure Consequence.**

Classify the structure by failure severity:
- **Class A (Catastrophic):** Bridges, pressure vessels, offshore platforms, nuclear containment.
- **Class B (High):** Building frames, cranes, heavy machinery.
- **Class C (Moderate):** Sheds, storage racks, non-critical platforms.

**Step 2 — Match Joint Type to Failure Consequence.**

- If **Class A** and **cyclic/fatigue loading** is present → Prefer **full-penetration butt welds** with profile grinding + 100 % NDT (radiographic or ultrasonic).
- If **Class A** and **leak-tightness** is required → Welded is **mandatory** (rivets/bolts cannot be used).
- If **Class B** and **static loading** → Fillet welds acceptable with visual + magnetic particle inspection.
- If **Class C** → Welded or bolted — both are acceptable.

**Step 3 — Evaluate Inspection Feasibility.**

If the joint is in a **non-accessible location** (e.g., inside a closed box girder), bolted joints are preferred because their condition can be inferred from preload measurements. Welded joints in such locations are inherently risky unless NDT is performed during fabrication.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Environmental Exposure.**

- **Corrosive environment** → Welded (smoother surface) preferred, but require post-weld corrosion protection.
- **Cryogenic / low-temperature service** → Special low-carbon equivalent (CE ≤ 0.45) electrodes mandatory; otherwise brittle fracture in HAZ is a real risk.
- **High-temperature service (T > 400 °C)** → Welded preferred (no fastener relaxation); but creep in weld metal must be checked.

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example — Weld Size for a Bracket Connection

**Problem:** A bracket carrying a factored load of **350 kN** is to be welded to the flange of a column using **two longitudinal fillet welds**, one on each side of the bracket. The bracket plate is **12 mm thick**, and the weld is to be made with **E41 electrodes** ($f_{uw} = 410$ MPa) using **shop welding** ($\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ for shop welds). Find the **effective length** of each weld.

**Step 1 — Compute the design shear strength of the weld per unit length.**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732
$$

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \frac{410}{2.165} \approx 189.4 \; \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the weld size from the bracket plate thickness.**

From **IS 800:2007 Table 21**, the minimum weld size for a 12 mm thick plate is $s_{min} = 3$ mm. To stay on the safe side, we adopt a **standard weld size of 6 mm**.

**Step 3 — Compute the effective throat thickness.**

$$
t_e = 0.7 \, s = 0.7 \times 6 = 4.2 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the design strength per unit length of the weld.**

$$
F_{wd} = f_{wd} \times t_e = 189.4 \times 4.2 \approx 795.5 \; \text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Total effective length required (for two welds).**

The total design load is 350 kN = 350,000 N.

$$
L_{total} = \frac{350{,}000}{795.5} \approx 440 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Length of each weld.**

$$
L_{each} = \frac{440}{2} = 220 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Add end crater allowance (2 × leg size per weld).**

$$
L_{actual} = 220 + 2 \times 6 = 232 \; \text{mm} \approx 240 \; \text{mm (round up to a practical size)}
$$

> **Final Design:** Use **two longitudinal fillet welds of 6 mm leg size, each 240 mm long**, shop-welded with E41 electrodes.

---

## 3.4 Decision Matrix: When Welded Joints Are Unsafe / Not Recommended

| Scenario | Reason | Recommended Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| Members in a corrosive atmosphere with no post-weld coating | Weld accelerates corrosion | Bolted with corrosion-protective washers |
| Sub-zero service temperature with no special electrodes | HAZ brittle fracture risk | Bolted with low-temperature preloaded bolts |
| Joints subject to **reverse cyclic loading** at high stress range | Weld toe fatigue cracks | HSFG bolted with preloaded condition |
| Repairs on existing riveted / bolted structures | Weld-induced residual stress in older material | Bolted splice plates |
| Temporary structures that need dismantling | Welding is permanent | Bolted |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Welded Joint Failure Analysis Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Welded Joint in Service] --> B{Failure Mode}
    B -- B1 --> C1[Brittle Fracture in HAZ]
    B -- B2 --> C2[Fatigue Crack at Weld Toe]
    B -- B3 --> C3[Lamellar Tearing in T-Joint]
    B -- B4 --> C4[Residual Stress Induced Distortion]
    B -- B5 --> C5[Corrosion at Weld / HAZ]
    C1 --> D1[Root Cause: Rapid cooling, high CE]
    C2 --> D2[Root Cause: Stress concentration at weld toe]
    C3 --> D3[Root Cause: Through-thickness shrinkage]
    C4 --> D4[Root Cause: Non-uniform thermal cycle]
    C5 --> D5[Root Cause: Galvanic potential difference]
    D1 --> E1[Safety Countermeasure: PWHT, low CE electrodes, preheat]
    D2 --> E2[Safety Countermeasure: Toe grinding, profile improvement]
    D3 --> E3[Safety Countermeasure: Z-quality plates, balanced welding]
    D4 --> E4[Safety Countermeasure: Symmetric weld sequence, clamps]
    D5 --> E5[Safety Countermeasure: Compatible electrode, post-weld coating]
    E1 --> F[Improved Safety in Service]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Welded vs. Riveted vs. Bolted Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Connections]
        R[Riveted Joint]
        B[Bolted Bearing-Type]
    end
    subgraph MOD[Modern Connections]
        W[Welded Joint]
        H[HSFG Bolted Joint]
    end
    R -- Stress concentration at hole --> R1[Net Section Failure Risk]
    B -- Preload required --> B1[Slippage Possible if Untorqued]
    H -- Clamping force --> H1[No Slip, Fatigue-Resistant]
    W -- No holes, monolithic --> W1[No Net Section, Residual Stress Risk]
    R1 --> DEC[Decision: Replace with HSFG or Welded for High-Stress]
    B1 --> DEC
    H1 --> SAFE[Safety Improvement: High Reliability]
    W1 --> NDT[Requires NDT for Internal Flaws]
    NDT --> SAFE
    DEC --> NDT
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Description of Common Weld Defects (for Visual Recall)

Since physical drawings are difficult in Mermaid, the following is a **high-density text-based schematic** that maps the location and orientation of the most common weld defects examined in KTU Module 3.

```
        PARENT METAL (Plate)
   ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
   │                                 │
   │          ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓             │  ← [DEFECT 1] Undercut
   │       ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓           │     (Groove at weld toe)
   │      ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓          │  ← Weld Toe = high Kt
   │   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░      │  ← [DEFECT 2] Porosity
   │   ░░WELD METAL░░░░░░░░░░░       │     (Spherical voids)
   │   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░      │  ← [DEFECT 3] Slag Inclusion
   │      ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓          │  ← [DEFECT 4] Lack of Fusion
   │       ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓           │     (Bonding defect at root)
   │          ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓             │  ← [DEFECT 5] Crater Crack
   │                                 │     (At weld termination)
   └─────────────────────────────────┘
                ↑
        HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) — Microstructure altered
        ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
        PARENT METAL (Unaffected) — Original properties
```

**Symbol Legend:**
- `▓` = Solid weld metal (good fusion)
- `░` = Defective regions (porosity, slag, lack of fusion)
- `←` = Pointers indicating defect locations

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Safety Inspection Workflow for Welded Joints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fabrication Complete] --> B[Visual Inspection 100 percent]
    B --> C{Surface Defects?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Dye Penetrant Test DPT or Magnetic Particle Test MPT]
    C -- No --> E[Proceed to Volumetric NDT]
    D --> F{Defect Indication?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Mark + Repair + Re-test]
    F -- No --> E
    E --> H[Radiographic Testing RT or Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    H --> I{Internal Flaws?}
    I -- Yes --> G
    I -- No --> J[Accept Joint]
    G --> K[Grind Out Defect]
    K --> L[Re-weld]
    L --> B
    J --> M[Issue Welding Compliance Certificate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q1.** State any **six advantages** of welded joints over riveted joints. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher strength efficiency (90–100%)** compared to riveted joints (65–80%) because no material is removed for holes.
2. **Greater rigidity and stiffness** — the joint behaves as a single monolithic body with no slip under load.
3. **Leak-tightness** — the continuous fusion bond makes welded joints inherently fluid- and gas-tight, essential for pressure vessels and tanks.
4. **No weakening of parent metal** — unlike riveted joints, the base metal is not drilled, so the full cross-section is available to resist loads.
5. **Versatility of joint geometry** — welds can join any combination of shapes (plates, tubes, angles) and any angle.
6. **Lighter and more compact** — no rivet heads or bulky fasteners, resulting in a smaller and lighter joint.
7. *(Any one additional point)* Better fatigue performance for properly ground butt welds.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q2.** Discuss the **main disadvantages** of welded joints with reference to safety in design. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Difficult inspection of internal flaws** — defects like porosity, slag inclusions, and lack of fusion are hidden below the surface, making failure sudden and unpredictable. (1 mark)
2. **Residual stresses and distortion** — non-uniform heating and cooling introduce locked-in stresses that may cause buckling, cracking, and reduced fatigue life. (1 mark)
3. **Brittleness in the Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** — rapid cooling produces coarse microstructures prone to brittle fracture, especially at low temperatures or in thick sections. (1 mark)

*(Additional valid points: Skilled labour required, high NDT cost, fire/health hazards, difficulty of repair, lamellar tearing, permanent nature preventing disassembly.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Modified for 14 Marks]

**Q.A.** **(a)** Compare welded joints with riveted joints under the following heads: (i) Strength efficiency, (ii) Rigidity, (iii) Inspection, (iv) Suitability for pressure vessels, (v) Cost of fabrication, (vi) Behaviour under fatigue loading. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A tie member of a roof truss is connected to a gusset plate using **two transverse fillet welds**, one on each side. The factored tensile force in the tie is **280 kN**. The tie member is **100 mm wide × 10 mm thick**. Design the weld using **E41 electrodes** for **site welding**. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| # | Criterion | Riveted Joint | Welded Joint | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strength efficiency | 65–80 % | 90–100 % | Welded |
| 2 | Rigidity | Slip occurs, lower stiffness | Monolithic, no slip | Welded |
| 3 | Inspection | Visual + hammer test, easy | Visual + NDT required, complex | Riveted |
| 4 | Pressure vessels | Not suitable (leaks at rivet holes) | Mandatory (leak-tight) | Welded |
| 5 | Cost of fabrication | High labour cost, no NDT | Lower labour, higher NDT cost | Context-dependent |
| 6 | Fatigue behaviour | Moderate (rivet clamp load helps) | Lower for fillet welds (toe cracks) | Riveted (for cyclic) |

**Conclusion:** Welded joints are superior in static strength, rigidity, and leak-tightness, but require strict quality control through NDT to compensate for their inherent inspection difficulty. (½ mark for logical conclusion)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Factored tensile load $F = 280$ kN $= 280{,}000$ N
- Two transverse fillet welds (one on each side)
- Site welding: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ (as per IS 800:2007)
- E41 electrodes: $f_{uw} = 410$ MPa

**Step 1 — Compute design shear strength.**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.50} = \frac{410}{2.598} \approx 157.8 \; \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum weld size for 10 mm thick plate.**

From IS 800:2007 Table 21, $s_{min} = 3$ mm. Choose standard size $s = 6$ mm.

**Step 3 — Effective throat thickness.**

$$
t_e = 0.7 \, s = 0.7 \times 6 = 4.2 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Strength per unit length of one weld.**

$$
F_{wd} = f_{wd} \times t_e = 157.8 \times 4.2 \approx 662.8 \; \text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Total effective length required.**

$$
L_{total} = \frac{280{,}000}{662.8} \approx 422.5 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Length of each weld (two welds in parallel).**

$$
L_{each} = \frac{422.5}{2} \approx 211.3 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply end crater deduction and adopt practical size.**

$$
L_{actual} = 211.3 + 2 \times 6 = 223.3 \; \text{mm} \approx 230 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Check against available plate width.**

Available width = 100 mm. Since $L_{actual} = 230$ mm > 100 mm, the weld must be wrapped around the plate end (return weld). Adopt **transverse fillet welds, 6 mm size, 100 mm long on each side + 12 mm return weld on top and bottom** for full development.

**Final Design:** Two transverse fillet welds, leg size 6 mm, length 100 mm on each side, with 12 mm return welds at the ends. Site welded with E41 electrodes.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the following weld defects with sketches: (i) Porosity, (ii) Slag inclusion, (iii) Undercut, (iv) Lack of penetration, (v) Crater crack. Discuss how each defect affects the safety of the welded joint. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **safety-in-design considerations** for welded joints in (i) bridges, (ii) pressure vessels, and (iii) offshore structures. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| # | Defect | Description | Effect on Safety |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Porosity** | Spherical voids formed by trapped gas during solidification of the weld pool. | Reduces effective load-bearing cross-section; acts as crack initiator under fatigue. |
| 2 | **Slag inclusion** | Non-metallic solid material (oxides, flux) entrapped between weld beads. | Creates sharp stress concentration → fatigue crack initiation site. |
| 3 | **Undercut** | Groove melted into the parent metal at the weld toe, not filled by weld metal. | Severe stress concentration at the weld toe — primary fatigue crack site. |
| 4 | **Lack of penetration** | Weld metal does not extend through the full joint thickness. | Reduces effective throat thickness → can fail at design load. |
| 5 | **Crater crack** | Star-shaped crack formed at the point where the arc is terminated. | Acts as a pre-existing crack — propagates under cyclic or impact loading. |

*(Sketches: 1 mark per sketch × 5 = 5 marks; safety discussion: 2 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Bridges:**
- Fatigue is the governing design criterion → use butt welds with **profile-ground toes**.
- 100 % ultrasonic testing (UT) for all primary load-carrying welds.
- Use **low carbon equivalent** ($\text{CE} \leq 0.45$) steels to prevent HAZ brittleness.
- Strict control on lamellar tearing in T and K joints.

**(ii) Pressure Vessels:**
- **Full penetration butt welds** mandatory — no fillet welds on the pressure boundary.
- **100 % radiographic testing (RT)** for all butt welds.
- **Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** for thick sections to relieve residual stresses.
- Hydrostatic test at 1.3 × design pressure before commissioning.
- Compliance with **ASME Sec. VIII** and **IBR (Indian Boiler Regulations)**.

**(iii) Offshore Structures:**
- Combination of fatigue, corrosion, and low-temperature exposure.
- **Tubular joints** require special attention to **hot-spot stress** (stress at the intersection of chord and brace).
- Use **fracture mechanics** approach (BS 7910 / API 579) for fitness-for-service evaluation.
- Weld profiling (capping, grinding) and weld toe improvement techniques (TIG dressing, hammer peening) are standard.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "site welding" partial safety factor (γ_mw = 1.50) with "shop welding" (γ_mw = 1.25).** This is the most common numerical error in design questions. *Always check the problem statement for "shop" or "site".*
> 2. **Forgetting the end crater deduction** ($2s$ for fillet welds) when computing the actual length. Examiners will deduct up to 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Writing $t_t = 0.707s$ instead of $t_e = 0.7s$.** IS 800:2007 uses the effective throat $t_e = 0.7s$, not the theoretical throat $0.707s$. A student who uses $0.707$ will not get full credit.
> 4. **Writing only "easy to fabricate" as an advantage** — this is too vague. Use engineering terminology: "no holes drilled, hence no net section weakening."
> 5. **Writing "welded joints are strong" without quantifying** — examiners expect numbers (e.g., 90–100% efficiency vs. 65–80% for riveted).
> 6. **Ignoring the role of NDT in safety** — any advantage/disadvantage list without mention of NDT will be considered incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following 25 points are the absolute must-know for KTU Module 3 — Advantages and Disadvantages of Welded Joints.**

- [x] Welded joints are **permanent, monolithic** connections formed by **fusion of parent metals**.
- [x] **Efficiency** of welded joints: **90 – 100 %**; riveted: **65 – 80 %**; bolted: **70 – 85 %**.
- [x] **Transverse fillet welds are ~30 % stronger** than longitudinal fillet welds of the same size (static loading).
- [x] **No holes are drilled** in welded joints → **no net section failure** risk.
- [x] Welded joints provide **leak-tightness** — mandatory for pressure vessels, boilers, and storage tanks.
- [x] **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** is the **weakest region** of a welded joint.
- [x] **Residual stresses** arise from non-uniform heating/cooling → can cause distortion and cracking.
- [x] Welded joints are **permanently rigid** — no slip under load, unlike bearing-type bolts.
- [x] Welded joints are **not easily inspectable** for internal flaws → NDT is mandatory for safety-critical structures.
- [x] **NDT methods** commonly used: **DPT, MPT, UT, RT**.
- [x] Welded joints require **skilled and certified welders** — quality is operator-dependent.
- [x] Welded joints are **difficult to disassemble** → not suitable for temporary structures.
- [x] **Fatigue performance** of fillet welds is poor due to high stress concentration at the weld toe.
- [x] **Brittle fracture** risk in HAZ at low temperatures — use low CE electrodes and preheat.
- [x] **Lamellar tearing** risk in T and corner joints of thick sections — use Z-quality plates.
- [x] **Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** is required for thick sections to relieve residual stresses.
- [x] **Standard formula:** $t_e = 0.7 \, s$ (effective throat).
- [x] **Standard formula:** $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}$ (design shear strength of weld).
- [x] **Partial safety factors:** $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) and $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ (site) — as per IS 800:2007.
- [x] **End crater deduction:** Effective length $L_e = L - 2s$ for fillet welds.
- [x] **Codes to remember:** **IS 816, IS 9595, IS 800:2007, IS 2062, AWS D1.1, ASME Sec. VIII**.
- [x] **Minimum weld size** for thinner plate (≤ 10 mm): **3 mm**.
- [x] Welded joints are **not recommended** for structures under **high cyclic loading with limited inspection access**.
- [x] Welded joints are **preferred** for **pressure vessels, bridges, building frames, ship hulls, and aerospace structures**.
- [x] **Major historic failures** (Bhopal, Alexander Kielland, Hyatt Regency walkway) all had **inadequate weld quality / inspection** as a root cause — a key safety lesson.

> **Final Takeaway:** Welded joints are **the most efficient and rigid** form of permanent connection, but their **safety depends entirely on workmanship, inspection, and design for the specific service environment.** A KTU Module 3 answer must always pair each advantage with its **safety implication** and each disadvantage with its **mitigation strategy**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Welds and Their Symbols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Welds and Their Symbols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Weld?

A **weld** is a localized coalescence (permanent joining) of two or more pieces of metal, achieved by heating the interfaces to a suitable temperature (with or without the application of pressure and filler metal) so that the materials fuse together with or without a filler material. In the context of **Safety in Design of Structures**, welds are critical load-transferring elements whose failure can lead to catastrophic collapse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A weld is a permanent joint formed between two members by heating the surfaces to the plastic or fusion state, with or without the application of pressure and filler metal, such that the joint transmits the design loads safely.

## 1.2 What are Weld Symbols?

**Weld symbols** are standardized graphical notations (governed by **IS 813:1986** in India and **AWS A2.4** internationally) used on engineering drawings to communicate the **type, size, length, spacing, location, and contour** of a required weld to the fabricator — without ambiguity.

> [!NOTE]
> Weld symbols are the **universal language of the fabrication shop**. A single well-drawn symbol replaces hundreds of words of written instruction.

## 1.3 Why Are Weld Symbols Critical for Structural Safety?

In structural steel design (IS 800:2007 / IS 800:2023), improper welding is the **#1 cause of on-site structural failures**. Standardized symbols ensure:
1. The **correct weld type** is used for the load condition.
2. The **throat thickness** and length are unambiguous.
3. The **weld location** (arrow side / other side) is precisely known.
4. Inspection and **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)** requirements are encoded.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Analogy: A Tailor's Stitching Diagram vs. Welding Blueprint**
>
> Imagine ordering a shirt from a tailor. Instead of writing "sew the shoulder with strong thread," you give them a **diagram** with specific symbols — `||` for straight stitch, `>` for back-stitch, with exact lengths. Weld symbols do **exactly this** for steel — they tell the welder *what kind of joint, how big, where, and how long* — eliminating guesswork that could cost lives.

## 1.5 Standard References Used in KTU-Affiliated Design

| Standard | Issuing Body | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 813:1986** | BIS (India) | Weld symbols on engineering drawings |
| **IS 800:2007** | BIS (India) | Structural steel design (weld strength clauses) |
| **IS 816:1969** | BIS (India) | Practice for welding in structural steel |
| **AWS A2.4** | American Welding Society | International weld symbol standard |
| **AWS D1.1** | American Welding Society | Structural welding code |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welds

Welds are broadly classified by **geometry**, **function**, and **mode of formation**.

### A. By Joint Geometry (Position of Weld Relative to Members)

1. **Groove Weld (Butt Weld)** — Made in a groove between two members. Provides **full penetration** if properly executed.
   - Types: Square, Single-V, Double-V, Single-U, Double-U, Single-J, Double-J, Bevel.
2. **Fillet Weld** — Triangular cross-section deposited in the corner of two intersecting members. The **most common weld** in structural work.
3. **Plug Weld** — A circular hole is filled with weld metal to join overlapping plates.
4. **Slot Weld** — Similar to plug weld but the hole is elongated (slot-shaped).
5. **Spot Weld** — Used in sheet metal; a small circular weld nugget formed by resistance welding.
6. **Seam Weld** — A continuous series of overlapping spot welds (resistance welding).
7. **Stitch / Intermittent Weld** — A series of fillet welds of specified length separated by unwelded gaps.
8. **Surfacing Weld** — Deposited on a surface to build up thickness or resist wear (not for joining).

### B. By Mode of Formation

| Method | Brief Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Arc Welding (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW)** | Electric arc melts filler/base metal |
| **Resistance Welding (Spot, Seam, Projection)** | Heat from electric resistance + pressure |
| **Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene)** | Combustion of gases melts metal |
| **Thermit Welding** | Exothermic chemical reaction |
| **Friction Welding (FSW)** | Frictional heat + forging pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> For **structural steel buildings and bridges**, the dominant process is **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** and **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** under IS 816 / IS 9595.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Standard Weld Symbol (IS 813 / AWS A2.4)

A complete weld symbol consists of **eight (8) elements**, arranged as:

1. **Arrow** — points to the joint location.
2. **Reference line** — the horizontal line on which the symbol sits.
3. **Weld symbol (the main pictogram)** — placed on either side of the reference line.
4. **Size** — written to the left of the weld symbol.
5. **Effective throat** — written in parentheses to the left of the size (optional).
6. **Length** — written to the right of the weld symbol.
7. **Pitch (center-to-center spacing)** — written to the right of length with a hyphen separator.
8. **Tail** — used for supplementary information (process, specification, NDT method).
9. **Contour / Finish symbols** — placed above or below the weld symbol.
10. **Field weld flag** — a triangular flag at the bend of the arrow tail.
11. **Melt-through symbol** — for full-penetration welds from the back.

### Placement Rule (KTU-Favorite Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arrow Side vs. Other Side:**
> - Weld symbol **below the reference line** → weld on the **arrow side** (the side the arrow points to).
> - Weld symbol **above the reference line** → weld on the **other side** (far side from the arrow).
> - Symbol **on both sides** → weld on **both sides** (e.g., double fillet, double-V groove).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Weld Design — IS 800)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas appear frequently in Part A and Part B questions for safety-based modules.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective throat thickness (fillet, equal legs) | $t_e = 0.707 \cdot s$ | mm | $s$ = weld size (leg length) |
| 2 | Effective throat thickness (unequal legs) | $t_e = 0.707 \cdot k \cdot s$ | mm | $k$ = penetration factor (0.7–1.0 per IS 800) |
| 3 | Design shear strength of fillet weld | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$ | N/mm² | $f_u$ = ultimate tensile strength of weld metal |
| 4 | Strength of fillet weld per unit length | $R_{wd} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e$ | N/mm | Used to find required weld length |
| 5 | Partial safety factor $\gamma_{mw}$ (shop weld) | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ | — | IS 800 Cl. 6.3.1 |
| 6 | Partial safety factor $\gamma_{mw}$ (field weld) | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ | — | IS 800 Cl. 6.3.1 |
| 7 | Minimum size of fillet weld (s) | Function of thicker plate $t_t$ | mm | See IS 800 Table 6.1 |
| 8 | Maximum size of fillet weld (along edge) | $s \le t_t - 1.5$ mm | mm | To avoid melting of edge |
| 9 | Minimum effective length of fillet weld | $L_w \ge 4 \cdot s$ and $\ge 40$ mm | mm | IS 800 Cl. 6.3.4 |
| 10 | Reduction factor for long welds (L > 150 s) | $\beta_{lw} = 1.2 - \dfrac{0.2L_w}{150s} \le 1.0$ | — | IS 800 Cl. 6.3.4.1 |
| 11 | Effective throat for partial-penetration groove weld | $t_e = $ depth of groove minus 2.5 mm | mm | IS 800 Cl. 6.3.2 |
| 12 | Effective throat for full-penetration groove weld | $t_e = $ parent plate thickness | mm | Treated as parent metal |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Weld symbols and design formulas are foundational for:
- **Building frames** (beam-to-column moment connections).
- **Bridge girders** (splice joints, gusset plates).
- **Industrial trusses and towers** (tubular joints).
- **Pressure vessels and storage tanks** (per ASME BPVC Section VIII).
- **Pipelines** (per ASME B31.3).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throat Thickness of a Fillet Weld

A fillet weld has an approximately **isosceles-right-triangle** cross-section (when both legs are equal, with the standard 90° included angle between the fusion faces).

**Step 1: Geometry of the triangle**

Let $s$ = leg length (size of weld) and $t_e$ = effective throat (perpendicular distance from root of weld to the hypotenuse face).

The included angle is $90°$, and the weld cross-section is a right isosceles triangle with:
- Two equal legs of length $s$
- Hypotenuse = $s\sqrt{2}$

**Step 2: Compute the throat from trigonometry**

The throat is the altitude from the right angle to the hypotenuse:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e &= s \cdot \sin(45°) \\
    &= s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
    &= \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \\
    &= \frac{s}{1.414} \\
    &= 0.707 \cdot s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Practical interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> For a **6 mm fillet weld** ($s = 6$ mm), the **effective throat is $0.707 \times 6 = 4.242$ mm**. This is the *only* part of the weld that resists load — the triangular fill is merely a "container" to ensure adequate throat.

## 3.2 Derivation: Design Strength of a Fillet Weld (IS 800)

A fillet weld is considered to fail in **shear along the throat plane** (Von Mises / maximum-shear-stress theory).

**Step 1: Yield criterion in shear**

For a ductile material under pure shear, the maximum shear stress at yield (von Mises) is:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 2: Convert yield to ultimate using partial safety factor**

IS 800:2007 / 2023 uses the **ultimate strength** with a partial safety factor $\gamma_{mw}$ for welds:

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}
$$

**Step 3: Strength of the weld per unit length**

Multiplying by the throat area per unit length ($t_e \times 1$ mm):

$$
R_{wd} = \frac{f_u \cdot t_e}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Reduction Factor for Long Welds (IS 800 Cl. 6.3.4.1)

**Why is this needed?** In long welds, the stress distribution along the length is **highly non-uniform** (peaks at the ends), so the average strength is lower.

**Step 1: Threshold for "long" welds**

A weld is considered long if its actual length:

$$
L_w > 150 \cdot s
$$

**Step 2: Empirical reduction factor (linear, derived from tests)**

$$
\beta_{lw} = 1.2 - \frac{0.2 \cdot L_w}{150 \cdot s}
$$

**Step 3: Constraints**

- $\beta_{lw} \le 1.0$ (cannot amplify strength)
- Applied to long welds only; for $L_w \le 150s$, $\beta_{lw} = 1.0$

**Worked numerical example:**

For $L_w = 300$ mm and $s = 6$ mm:
- $150 \cdot s = 900$ mm → since $L_w = 300 < 900$, **no reduction** ($\beta_{lw} = 1.0$).

For $L_w = 1500$ mm and $s = 6$ mm:
- $150 \cdot s = 900$ mm → $L_w = 1500 > 900$ → **long weld**.
- $\beta_{lw} = 1.2 - \dfrac{0.2 \times 1500}{900} = 1.2 - 0.333 = 0.867$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Weld Types

| Weld Type | Penetration | Stress Direction Preferred | Inspection Difficulty | Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Square Groove** | Partial (or full w/ backing) | Tension / Compression | Medium | Low | Thin plates (<6 mm) |
| **Single-V Groove** | Full | Tension / Compression | High | Medium | Plates 6–20 mm |
| **Double-V Groove** | Full | High cyclic loading | High | High (waste of metal) | Thick plates, fatigue critical |
| **Fillet (single)** | Always partial | Shear | Easy | Low | Bracing, secondary connections |
| **Fillet (double)** | Partial on both sides | Shear | Easy | Medium | Beam-to-column brackets |
| **Plug / Slot** | Partial | Shear | Difficult | Medium | Overlapping plates, tube gussets |
| **Spot / Seam** | Nugget | Shear | Difficult | Low (mass production) | Sheet-metal fabrication |
| **Stitch (intermittent)** | Partial | Shear (lower capacity) | Easy | Low | Long welds where full strength is not needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety insight:** Groove welds (full penetration) are **mandatory for primary tension members** under cyclic/fatigue loading because the failure plane aligns with the parent metal — eliminating the stress concentration at the weld root.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Classification of Welds

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["WELDS"] --> B["BY GEOMETRY"]
    A --> C["BY PROCESS"]
    A --> D["BY PENETRATION"]
    B --> B1["GROOVE / BUTT WELD"]
    B --> B2["FILLET WELD"]
    B --> B3["PLUG WELD"]
    B --> B4["SLOT WELD"]
    B --> B5["SPOT WELD"]
    B --> B6["SEAM WELD"]
    B --> B7["STITCH WELD"]
    B --> B8["SURFACING WELD"]
    C --> C1["ARC WELDING"]
    C --> C2["RESISTANCE WELDING"]
    C --> C3["GAS WELDING"]
    C --> C4["FRICTION WELDING"]
    D --> D1["FULL PENETRATION"]
    D --> D2["PARTIAL PENETRATION"]
    B1 --> P1["SQUARE / V / U / J / BEVEL"]
    B2 --> P2["EQUAL LEG / UNEQUAL LEG"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Standard Weld Symbol

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["ARROW POINTS TO JOINT LOCATION"] --> B["REFERENCE LINE - HORIZONTAL"]
    B --> C1["SYMBOL BELOW LINE = ARROW SIDE WELD"]
    B --> C2["SYMBOL ABOVE LINE = OTHER SIDE WELD"]
    B --> D1["SIZE - WRITTEN LEFT OF SYMBOL"]
    B --> D2["LENGTH - WRITTEN RIGHT OF SYMBOL"]
    B --> D3["PITCH - SEPARATED BY HYPHEN FROM LENGTH"]
    B --> E1["TAIL - PROCESS AND SPEC"]
    B --> E2["FIELD WELD FLAG - TRIANGLE AT BEND"]
    B --> E3["CONTOUR SYMBOL - ABOVE OR BELOW"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Fillet Weld Cross-Section (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PLATE1["UPPER PLATE"] --> FW["FILLET WELD CROSS SECTION"]
    PLATE2["LOWER PLATE"] --> FW
    FW --> S["S - WELD SIZE LEG LENGTH"]
    FW --> TE["TE - EFFECTIVE THROAT 0.707 S"]
    FW --> ROOT["ROOT - AT INTERSECTION OF PLATES"]
    FW --> FACE["FACE - OUTER HYPOTENUSE SURFACE"]
    FW --> TOE["TOE - JUNCTION OF FACE AND PLATE"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Symbol Placement Logic (Arrow Side vs. Other Side)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["WHERE IS WELD REQUIRED"] --> A1["NEAR THE ARROW"]
    Q --> A2["FAR FROM ARROW"]
    Q --> A3["BOTH SIDES OF JOINT"]
    A1 --> R1["PLACE SYMBOL BELOW REFERENCE LINE"]
    A2 --> R2["PLACE SYMBOL ABOVE REFERENCE LINE"]
    A3 --> R3["PLACE SYMBOL BOTH ABOVE AND BELOW REFERENCE LINE"]
    R1 --> OUT["READ: ARROW SIDE FILLET 6 MM"]
    R2 --> OUT2["READ: OTHER SIDE FILLET 6 MM"]
    R3 --> OUT3["READ: DOUBLE FILLET 6 MM ON BOTH SIDES"]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP1["STEP 1: VISUAL INSPECTION AS PER IS 822"] --> STEP2["STEP 2: DIMENSIONAL CHECK WELD SIZE LENGTH"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["STEP 3: NDT SELECTION"]
    STEP3 --> N1["DPT LIQUID PENETRANT FOR SURFACE CRACKS"]
    STEP3 --> N2["MPT MAGNETIC PARTICLE FOR FERROUS"]
    STEP3 --> N3["UT ULTRASONIC FOR INTERNAL FLAWS"]
    STEP3 --> N4["RT RADIOGRAPHIC FOR CRITICAL JOINTS"]
    N1 --> STEP4["STEP 4: ACCEPT OR REJECT"]
    N2 --> STEP4
    N3 --> STEP4
    N4 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5["STEP 5: LOAD TEST IF REQUIRED"]
    STEP5 --> STEP6["STEP 6: SIGN OFF BY STRUCTURAL ENGINEER"]
```
