# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a fillet weld. State the equation for its effective throat thickness.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces at right angles. (1 mark)

The **effective throat thickness** $t_e$ is the perpendicular distance from the weld root to the hypotenuse face: (1 mark)

$$
t_e = 0.707 \times s
$$

where $s$ is the weld size (leg length). (1 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Distinguish between the "arrow side" and "other side" of a weld symbol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In a weld symbol (as per IS 813), the **reference line** separates two halves: (1 mark)
- The **arrow side** is the half *below* the reference line — any weld symbol placed here means the weld is on the side the arrow points to. (1 mark)
- The **other side** is the half *above* the reference line — the weld is on the far side of the joint from the arrow. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Classify the various types of welds commonly used in structural steel fabrication. Explain the salient features of groove welds and fillet welds with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12 mm thick plate is to be fillet-welded to a 16 mm thick plate using 6 mm size fillet welds on both sides. Calculate the effective throat and the design strength of the weld per mm length. Take $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Classification & Features (7 marks)

Welds are classified by **geometry** and **process** (2 marks).

**Groove (Butt) Weld:** Made in the gap between two abutting members. Weld metal fills the groove. (1 mark)
- *Salient features:* Full penetration possible; suitable for tension and compression members; high strength; requires edge preparation (V, U, J, bevel); sensitive to fit-up and quality. (1 mark)

**Fillet Weld:** Triangular cross-section in the corner of two intersecting members. (1 mark)
- *Salient features:* Most common; no edge prep needed; does not require full penetration; works best in **shear**; minimum size governed by thicker plate (per IS 800). (1 mark)

*(Sketch credit: 1 mark for any one labeled diagram.)*

#### Part (b) — Strength Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $s = 6$ mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

**Step 1: Effective throat**

$$
t_e = 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 6 = 4.242 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2: Design shear strength of weld metal**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \frac{410}{2.165} = 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 3: Strength per mm length (single weld)**

$$
R_{wd} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e = 189.4 \times 4.242 = 803.5 \text{ N/mm} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 4: For double-sided fillet weld**

$$
R_{wd,total} = 2 \times 803.5 = 1607 \text{ N/mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 5: Result**

$$
\boxed{R_{wd,total} \approx 1607 \text{ N/mm per mm of weld length, total for both sides}}
$$

(1 mark for final answer with units and statement.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the standard elements of a weld symbol as per IS 813:1986. Explain the rules for placement of weld symbols with respect to the reference line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 mm thick plate is connected to a 20 mm thick gusset plate by fillet welds only. The joint is subjected to a factored tensile force of 300 kN. Design the weld using 6 mm size fillet welds on three sides of the plate. Take $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Weld Symbol Elements (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):** A labeled diagram showing:
- Arrow line
- Reference line (horizontal)
- Weld symbol below and above reference line
- Size, length, pitch
- Tail with process note
- Field weld flag

**Description (2 marks):** A standard weld symbol has **8 elements**: arrow, reference line, basic weld symbol, size, length, pitch, tail, and contour/finish symbol.

**Placement rules (2 marks):**
1. Symbol **below** the reference line → weld on **arrow side**.
2. Symbol **above** the reference line → weld on **other side**.
3. Symbols on **both sides** → weld on **both sides** of the joint.

#### Part (b) — Weld Design (7 marks)

**Given:** Plate $t_1 = 10$ mm, gusset $t_2 = 20$ mm, $P_u = 300$ kN, $s = 6$ mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

**Step 1: Effective throat**

$$
t_e = 0.707 \times 6 = 4.242 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2: Design strength of weld per mm length**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
R_{wd} = 189.4 \times 4.242 = 803.5 \text{ N/mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 3: Total weld length required**

$$
L_w = \frac{P_u}{R_{wd}} = \frac{300 \times 1000}{803.5} = 373.4 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 4: Distribute on three sides of a rectangular plate**

Assuming the plate is, say, 100 mm × 150 mm (breadth × length), the perimeter available is:
- Two vertical sides: $2 \times 100 = 200$ mm
- One bottom side: $150$ mm
- Total available: $350$ mm
- Add 12 mm (one leg length) at the top end to round off return → $350 + 12 = 362$ mm

Since $362 < 373.4$, **either increase the plate size or use a 8 mm weld**. (2 marks)

**Step 5: Re-check with $s = 8$ mm**

$$
t_e = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
R_{wd} = 189.4 \times 5.656 = 1071 \text{ N/mm}
$$

$$
L_w = \frac{300000}{1071} = 280 \text{ mm}
$$

A plate of 90 mm × 100 mm gives $2(90) + 100 + 12 = 292$ mm ≥ 280 mm ✓ (1 mark)

**Step 6: Final answer**

**Use 8 mm fillet welds on three sides of a 90 mm × 100 mm plate**, with a return at the top. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$ in $f_{wd}$** — many students use $f_{wd} = f_u / \gamma_{mw}$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using yield stress $f_y$ instead of ultimate $f_u$** — IS 800:2007 / 2023 mandates $f_u$ for welds.
> 3. **Wrong $\gamma_{mw}$** — must be **1.25 for shop welds** and **1.50 for field welds**.
> 4. **Confusing arrow side vs. other side** — this is a free 2-mark loss in symbol-sketch questions.
> 5. **Not converting kN to N** — units mistake causes 50% of the calculation to be wrong.
> 6. **Omitting effective throat calculation** in strength problems — without $t_e$, you cannot compute weld capacity.
> 7. **Using full plate thickness for groove weld throat** without confirming "full penetration" — partial-penetration grooves use (groove depth − 2.5 mm).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Weld symbol standard:** **IS 813:1986** (India) / **AWS A2.4** (international).
- [ ] **Reference line placement:** Symbol **below** = arrow side; **above** = other side; **both** = double weld.
- [ ] **Effective throat formula (fillet, equal legs):** $t_e = 0.707 \, s$.
- [ ] **Design strength of weld (per unit area):** $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}$.
- [ ] **Partial safety factor:** $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop), $1.50$ (field).
- [ ] **Strength of fillet weld per mm length:** $R_{wd} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e$.
- [ ] **Minimum weld size** depends on thicker plate thickness (IS 800 Table 6.1).
- [ ] **Maximum weld size along an edge:** $s \le t_t - 1.5$ mm.
- [ ] **Minimum effective weld length:** $L_w \ge 4s$ **and** $L_w \ge 40$ mm.
- [ ] **Long-weld reduction factor** applied when $L_w > 150s$.
- [ ] **Full-penetration groove weld** treated as parent metal in strength calc.
- [ ] **Partial-penetration groove weld** throat = (groove depth − 2.5 mm).
- [ ] **Weld types to remember:** Groove, Fillet, Plug, Slot, Spot, Seam, Stitch, Surfacing.
- [ ] **Preferred process for structural steel:** SMAW (manual) and SAW (heavy sections).
- [ ] **Weld strength is governed by the throat area**, not the visible face — the diagonal face is decorative.
- [ ] **Weld symbol language is universal** — mastering the 8 elements eliminates ambiguity in fabrication drawings.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Symbol Placement — "LOW for NEAR":**
> - **L**ower / be**LOW** the line → weld on the side the arrow **NEAR**s / points to.
> - **U**pper / ab**O**ve the line → weld on the **O**pposite / Other side.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Fillet Weld
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Fillet Weld

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (As per IS 800:2007)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fillet Weld (Clause 9.7.1, IS 800:2007):**
> A fillet weld is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces at an angle. Fillet welds are used to join two surfaces meeting at an angle ranging from **60° to 120°**, with the most common configuration being two plates at **90°**.

**Key Geometric Parameters of a Fillet Weld:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Size of weld | $s$ | Minimum leg length of the fillet weld triangle |
| Effective throat thickness | $t_e$ | Minimum distance from weld root to weld face (the design-critical dimension) |
| Actual throat thickness | $t_t$ | Actual perpendicular distance from root to hypotenuse face |
| Length of weld | $l_w$ | Total effective length over which weld is effective |
| Angle between fusion faces | $\theta$ | Angle between the two plates being joined |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a standard 90° fillet weld with equal legs:**
> The actual throat thickness $t_t = s \sin(45°) = 0.707s \approx 0.7s$.
> This **0.7 factor** is the single most important constant in fillet weld design — commit it to memory.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **gluing two wooden blocks** at a right angle using a triangular bead of adhesive running along the joint edge.

- The **adhesive triangle's two legs** (sides touching the wood) = **weld legs (size $s$)**.
- The **shortest distance from the triangle's inner corner (root) to its slanted outer face (hypotenuse)** = **throat thickness ($t_t$)**.
- This throat is where the **shear stress concentrates** when you try to pull the blocks apart — just like the glue line taking the load.

In a steel fillet weld, failure does **NOT** occur by tearing along the legs of the weld. Instead, the weld shears along its **throat plane**. This is why the strength of a fillet weld is governed by the **throat area** = ($t_e \times l_w$), not by the leg size directly.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"The weld fails at its throat — never at its legs."* This is the governing principle behind every fillet weld design formula in IS 800.

---

### 1.3 Governing IS 800:2007 Clauses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Reference:** The design of fillet welds in India is governed by:
> - **Clause 9.7** — Design of Fillet Welds (general provisions)
> - **Clause 10.5.3** — Design Strength of Fillet Welds (strength calculations)
> - **Clause 10.5.7** — Reduction in Design Strength for Long Welds
> - **Table 1** — Partial Safety Factors for Welds ($\gamma_{mw}$)

The course outcome alignment assumes the student can apply Clause 10.5.3 to compute the **safe load-carrying capacity** of any fillet weld configuration encountered in steel construction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometry of Fillet Weld — Step-by-Step Logic

A fillet weld forms an approximately triangular cross-section. Consider two plates meeting at angle $\theta$:

**Step 1 — Identify the two legs:**
- Leg 1 = $s_1$ (along Plate 1 surface)
- Leg 2 = $s_2$ (along Plate 2 surface)
- Size $s$ = $\min(s_1, s_2)$ — the *smaller* leg governs (per IS 800:2007, Clause 9.7.2.1)

**Step 2 — Compute the actual throat thickness $t_t$:**
By trigonometry on the triangle, the perpendicular distance from root to hypotenuse is:

$$t_t = \frac{s_1 \cdot s_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2 - 2 s_1 s_2 \cos\theta}}$$

**Step 3 — For the standard case of equal legs ($s_1 = s_2 = s$) and $\theta = 90°$:**

$$t_t = \frac{s \cdot s}{\sqrt{s^2 + s^2 - 0}} = \frac{s^2}{s\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071s$$

Hence the **constant 0.7** that appears everywhere in fillet weld design.

**Step 4 — Effective throat thickness $t_e$:**
IS 800 allows the actual throat to be increased slightly when the fusion angle $\theta$ deviates from 90°. The **correlation factor $K$** is applied:

$$t_e = K \cdot s$$

Where $K$ values are tabulated in Table 21, IS 800:2007 (also see Section 2.3 below).

**Step 5 — Why the throat governs failure:**
When a fillet weld is loaded in shear, the stress trajectory concentrates along the shortest plane — the throat. Failure occurs by **shear yielding along the throat plane**, NOT by tearing along the legs. This justifies the use of throat area in strength formulas.

---

### 2.2 Design Strength Formula (Clause 10.5.3.1, IS 800:2007)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Formula for Fillet Weld Design:**
>
> $$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$
>
> Where:
> - $f_u$ = ultimate tensile strength of the weld electrode / parent metal (whichever is lower)
> - $\gamma_{mw}$ = **1.25** for *shop welding*, **1.50** for *site welding*
> - The $\sqrt{3}$ arises from von Mises yield criterion conversion ($\sigma_y / \sqrt{3} \approx 0.577\sigma_y$)

The **factored design strength per unit length** of the weld is:

$$R_{wd} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e = \frac{f_u \cdot t_e}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

The **factored load capacity** of a weld of length $l_w$ is:

$$V_{dsg} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e \cdot l_w$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Reference |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Actual throat (90°, equal legs) | $t_t = 0.7s$ | mm | Geometry |
| 2 | Actual throat (general $\theta$) | $t_t = \dfrac{s_1 s_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2 - 2s_1 s_2 \cos\theta}}$ | mm | Geometry |
| 3 | Effective throat | $t_e = K \cdot s$ | mm | Cl. 9.7.2.3 |
| 4 | Correlation factor ($K$) at $\theta = 60°$ | $0.70$ | — | Table 21 |
| 5 | Correlation factor ($K$) at $\theta = 70°\text{-}90°$ | $0.70$ | — | Table 21 |
| 6 | Correlation factor ($K$) at $\theta = 100°$ | $0.65$ | — | Table 21 |
| 7 | Correlation factor ($K$) at $\theta = 110°\text{-}120°$ | $0.58$ | — | Table 21 |
| 8 | Design stress in weld | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | Cl. 10.5.3.1 |
| 9 | $\gamma_{mw}$ (shop weld) | **1.25** | — | Table 1 |
| 10 | $\gamma_{mw}$ (site weld) | **1.50** | — | Table 1 |
| 11 | Strength per unit length | $R_{wd} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e$ | N/mm | Derived |
| 12 | Total weld capacity | $V_{dsg} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e \cdot l_w$ | N (kN) | Derived |
| 13 | Min. weld size (IS 800 Table 22) | depends on thicker plate | mm | Table 22 |
| 14 | Max. weld size (along edge) | $s \leq t - 1.5$ mm (for $t \leq 10$); $\leq t$ for $t>10$ | mm | Cl. 9.7.2.2 |
| 15 | Min. effective length | $l_e \geq 4 \cdot s$ AND $\geq 40$ mm | mm | Cl. 9.7.3 |
| 16 | Long weld reduction factor $\beta_{Lw}$ | $1.2 - \dfrac{0.2L}{150 t_t} \geq 0.6$ (when $L > 150 t_t$) | — | Cl. 10.5.7.1 |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using $\gamma_{mw} = 1.10$ (which is $\gamma_{m0}$ for steel yielding). The weld partial safety factor is **different** — it is $\gamma_{mw}$, NOT $\gamma_{m0}$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fillet welds are the **workhorse of structural steel fabrication**. They appear in:
- **Beam-to-column connections** (moment end-plate, fin plate)
- **Bracket connections** (shear tabs, gusseted joints)
- **Stiffener welding** to webs and flanges of plate girders
- **Built-up sections** (welded I-sections, box girders)
- **Truss member connections** at gusset plates

> [!TIP]
> Approximately **70-80% of all welds** placed in structural steelwork are fillet welds. Mastery of this topic is non-negotiable for any structural designer.

---

### 2.5 Strength Reduction for Long Welds (Clause 10.5.7.1)

For a long fillet weld where the length $L$ exceeds **150 times the throat thickness** ($150 t_t$), the **shear stress distribution becomes non-uniform** (lower at the ends due to shear lag). IS 800 mandates a reduction:

$$\beta_{Lw} = 1.2 - \frac{0.2 L}{150 t_t}$$

Subject to the limits: $\mathbf{0.6 \leq \beta_{Lw} \leq 1.0}$

The **reduced design strength** becomes:

$$V_{dsg,reduced} = \beta_{Lw} \cdot f_{wd} \cdot t_e \cdot l_w$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Throat Thickness for a 90° Fillet Weld with Equal Legs

**Given:** A fillet weld of equal leg length $s$, joining two plates meeting at $\theta = 90°$.

**Step 1:** Set up the geometry. The weld forms an isoceles right triangle with legs $s$ and hypotenuse $h$.

$$h = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = s\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 2:** The actual throat thickness is the perpendicular from the right-angle vertex (root) to the hypotenuse.

**Step 3:** Use the area equivalence: Area of triangle = (1/2)(leg)(leg) = (1/2)(hypotenuse)(throat).

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot s \cdot s = \frac{1}{2} \cdot h \cdot t_t$$

$$\frac{s^2}{2} = \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot t_t$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t_t$:

$$t_t = \frac{s^2}{s\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Rationalize the denominator:

$$t_t = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.7071\,s$$

$$\boxed{t_t \approx 0.7\,s}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Design Stress $f_{wd}$ from First Principles

**Given:** Fillet weld in pure shear loading. Parent metal/weld metal ultimate strength = $f_u$.

**Step 1:** Identify the failure mode. Shear yielding of the throat plane.

**Step 2:** Apply von Mises yield criterion. For pure shear, yielding occurs when:

$$\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3:** For ultimate limit state design, replace $\sigma_y$ with $f_u$ and introduce partial safety factor $\gamma_{mw}$:

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 4:** Rename as design shear strength of weld $f_{wd}$:

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical check. For E410 electrodes ($f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$):

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{wd} &= \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} \\
&= \frac{410}{2.165} \\
&= 189.4 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{f_{wd} \approx 189 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ for E410 shop welds}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacity of a Fillet Weld Group Subjected to Combined Loading

**Scenario:** A bracket connection has fillet welds subjected to direct shear $F$ AND a moment $M$. The weld group rotates about its centroid.

**Step 1 — Compute direct shear stress:**

$$\tau_1 = \frac{F}{\sum t_e \cdot l_w}$$

**Step 2 — Compute shear stress due to moment:** Treating each weld segment as a line, the stress due to moment is:

$$\tau_2 = \frac{M \cdot r}{I_p}$$

Where $r$ = distance from centroid to the weld element, and $I_p = \sum l_w \cdot r^2$ (polar moment of weld group about centroid).

**Step 3 — Combine (vector addition since both are shears):**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_1^2 + \tau_2^2 + 2 \tau_1 \tau_2 \cos\phi}$$

For the worst case (when vectors are aligned): $\tau_{max} = \tau_1 + \tau_2$ (conservative).

**Step 4 — Design check:**

$$\tau_{max} \leq f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A 100 mm × 100 mm bracket plate is connected to a column flange using two vertical fillet welds of size 8 mm and two horizontal fillet welds of size 8 mm (a C-shaped weld layout). The bracket carries a factored load of 200 kN at an eccentricity of 150 mm. Electrode: E410 ($f_u = 410$ MPa). Shop welding. Verify adequacy.

**Solution Framework:**

**Step 1 — Identify geometry:**
- Vertical welds: 2 × 100 mm (top and bottom of C)
- Horizontal welds: 2 × 100 mm (left and right vertical legs)

Wait — for a typical bracket welded to a column flange, layout is two vertical welds along the sides of the bracket. Let me redo:

- Two vertical fillet welds, each length 100 mm
- Eccentricity $e = 150$ mm

**Step 2 — Weld group properties:**

$$\sum l_w = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

For two parallel vertical welds separated by distance $b = 100$ mm, centroid lies at mid-thickness.

$$I_{xx} = 2 \cdot \frac{t_e \cdot l^3}{12} = 2 \cdot \frac{1 \cdot 100^3}{12} = 166{,}667 \text{ mm}^3$$

Wait — this is for welds treated as lines. Actually for $I_p$:

$$I_p = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$$

With $I_{xx}$ about horizontal axis through centroid = $2 \cdot t_e \cdot 100^3/12$ and $I_{yy}$ about vertical axis = $2 \cdot t_e \cdot (50^2)(100) = 500{,}000 t_e$.

Hmm — let me present a cleaner worked example.

**Cleaner Worked Example:** *Single vertical weld pair carrying eccentric load*

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Plate size | 200 mm × 150 mm |
| Weld size $s$ | 8 mm |
| Effective throat $t_e$ | $0.7 \times 8 = 5.6$ mm |
| Weld length (each vertical) | 150 mm |
| Number of welds | 2 |
| Factored load $F$ | 180 kN |
| Eccentricity $e$ | 100 mm |
| Electrode | E410, shop weld |

**Direct shear stress:**

$$\tau_1 = \frac{F}{\sum t_e l_w} = \frac{180 \times 10^3}{2 \times 5.6 \times 150} = \frac{180{,}000}{1{,}680} = 107.14 \text{ MPa}$$

**Moment $M = F \times e = 180 \times 0.1 = 18$ kN·m = $18 \times 10^6$ N·mm**

**Polar moment of inertia of weld group:**

For two parallel welds of length $L$ separated by $b$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= 2 \cdot t_e \cdot L^3/12 \\
I_{yy} &= 2 \cdot t_e \cdot L \cdot (b/2)^2 \\
I_p &= I_{xx} + I_{yy}
\end{aligned}$$

With $L = 150$ mm, $b = 200$ mm, $t_e = 5.6$ mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= 2 \cdot 5.6 \cdot 150^3/12 = 31{,}500{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 \\
I_{yy} &= 2 \cdot 5.6 \cdot 150 \cdot 100^2 = 168{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 \\
I_p &= 199{,}500{,}000 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum $r$:** $r_{max} = \sqrt{(L/2)^2 + (b/2)^2} = \sqrt{75^2 + 100^2} = 125$ mm

**Shear stress due to moment at farthest point:**

$$\tau_2 = \frac{M \cdot r_{max}}{I_p} = \frac{18 \times 10^6 \times 125}{199.5 \times 10^6} = 11.28 \text{ MPa}$$

**Combined stress (worst case, aligned):**

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_1 + \tau_2 = 107.14 + 11.28 = 118.42 \text{ MPa}$$

**Allowable:**

$$f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Check:** $118.42 < 189.4 \implies$ **SAFE** ✓

---

### 3.5 Long Weld Reduction Example

A fillet weld of throat $t_t = 5$ mm and length $L = 1200$ mm. Is reduction needed?

$L = 1200$ mm; $150 t_t = 150 \times 5 = 750$ mm.

Since $L > 150 t_t$, reduction applies:

$$\beta_{Lw} = 1.2 - \frac{0.2 \times 1200}{750} = 1.2 - 0.32 = 0.88$$

So the design capacity is reduced to **88%** of the calculated value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fillet Weld Geometry — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Plates Meeting at Angle θ] --> B{Fusion Face Geometry}
    B --> C[Leg 1: s₁ along Plate 1]
    B --> D[Leg 2: s₂ along Plate 2]
    C --> E[Weld Root: Inner Corner]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Throat Plane: Critical Failure Section]
    F --> G[Actual Throat t_t = 0.7s for 90°]
    G --> H[Effective Throat t_e = K·s]
    H --> I[Design Strength f_wd · t_e · L_w]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366
    style F fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style I fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600
```

### 4.2 Design Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Identify Loading Type] --> S2{Direct Shear or Eccentric Load?}
    S2 -->|Direct Shear| S3[Compute Force per unit length]
    S2 -->|Eccentric| S4[Resolve into Direct Shear + Moment]
    
    S3 --> S5[Compute f_wd = f_u / √3·γ_mw]
    S4 --> S5
    
    S5 --> S6[Assume weld size s, length L_w]
    S6 --> S7[Compute t_e = 0.7·s for 90°]
    S7 --> S8{Length > 150·t_t?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Apply β_Lw Reduction]
    S8 -->|No| S10[β_Lw = 1.0]
    S9 --> S11[Compute Capacity R = β_Lw · f_wd · t_e · L_w]
    S10 --> S11
    
    S11 --> S12{Capacity ≥ Applied Load?}
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Design SAFE - Adopt s]
    S12 -->|No| S14[Increase s or L_w]
    S14 --> S6
    
    style S8 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style S13 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600
    style S14 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
```

### 4.3 Force Flow in a Bracket Connection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Factored Load P on Bracket] --> P1[Resolved into Direct Shear V]
    P --> P2[and Moment M = P × e]
    
    P1 --> PS[Direct Shear Stress: τ₁ = V/Σt_eL_w]
    P2 --> PM[Moment Shear: τ₂ = M·r/I_p]
    
    PS --> CB[Combined: τ_max = √τ₁² + τ₂² + 2τ₁τ₂cosφ]
    PM --> CB
    
    CB --> CK{τ_max ≤ f_wd?}
    CK -->|Yes| OK[SECTION SAFE]
    CK -->|No| NG[Increase s or L_w]
    
    style P fill:#e1f5ff
    style CB fill:#fff4e1
    style OK fill:#e1ffe1
    style NG fill:#ffe1e1
```

### 4.4 Weld Size Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Determine Thicker Plate t] --> D2[Consult IS 800 Table 22: Min s]
    D2 --> D3[Compute Max s ≤ t_edge limit]
    D3 --> D4[Assume Trial s within range]
    D4 --> D5[Verify Min Length L_w ≥ 4s and 40mm]
    D5 --> D6[Compute t_e = 0.7s]
    D6 --> D7[Compute R_wd = f_wd · t_e · L_w]
    D7 --> D8{R_wd ≥ Factored Load?}
    D8 -->|Yes| FIN[Adopt s]
    D8 -->|No| D9[Increase s by 1 or 2 mm]
    D9 --> D6
    
    style D1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style D8 fill:#fff4e1
    style FIN fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a computational or decision step. Follow the arrows sequentially when solving a design problem. The amber-colored blocks are decision points where code-based checks govern (consult IS 800 Table 22 for minimum sizes).

---

### 4.5 IS 800:2007 Minimum Fillet Weld Sizes (Table 22 — For Reference)

| Thickness of Thicker Plate (mm) | Min. Size of Fillet Weld (mm) | Max. Size for Edge Welding (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| ≤ 10 | 3 | $t$ |
| 10 – 20 | 5 | $t - 1.5$ |
| 20 – 32 | 6 | $t - 1.5$ |
| 32 – 50 | 8 (first run), 10 (min fill) | $t - 1.5$ |
| > 50 | 10 | $t - 1.5$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the terms: (i) size of fillet weld, (ii) throat thickness, and (iii) effective throat thickness. How are they related?

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Size of weld ($s$):** The minimum leg length of the fillet weld triangle. When legs are unequal, $s$ = smaller leg length.

(ii) **Throat thickness ($t_t$):** The perpendicular distance from the weld root to the hypotenuse face of the weld triangle. For a 90° equal-leg fillet weld, $t_t = 0.7s$.

(iii) **Effective throat thickness ($t_e$):** The throat thickness modified by a correlation factor $K$ that accounts for the angle $\theta$ between fusion faces. Per IS 800:2007, $t_e = K \cdot s$.

**Relationship:** $t_e \leq t_t \leq s$, with $t_t = 0.7s$ for $\theta = 90°$ and $t_e = K \cdot s$ where $K \leq 1.0$ for all valid angles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** Explain why the partial safety factor $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ is specified for site welding as against $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ for shop welding.

**Model Answer:**

The higher safety factor for site welding reflects the **reduced quality control** achievable outside the controlled environment of a fabrication workshop:

1. **Environmental factors:** Wind, humidity, temperature fluctuations affect arc stability and weld metal properties at site.
2. **Welding position:** Site welds are often performed in overhead or vertical positions where fusion is more difficult.
3. **Operator skill variability:** Less specialized welders may handle site work.
4. **Inspection limitations:** Non-destructive testing (NDT) is harder to perform on site.

The result is lower reliability, justifying the **20% increase** in $\gamma_{mw}$ from 1.25 to 1.50, which reduces allowable design stress by a similar factor.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

A bracket plate 200 mm wide and 250 mm deep is welded to the flange of a column using two vertical fillet welds, each 220 mm long. The bracket transmits a factored load of 280 kN at an eccentricity of 120 mm from the face of the column. Using electrode E410 ($f_u = 410$ MPa), shop welding:

**(a)** Determine the size of the fillet weld required.
**(b)** Check whether a long-weld reduction factor is applicable.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Determination of weld size [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Design shear strength of weld:

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** For two vertical welds, treat them as line elements.

Direct shear stress:

$$\tau_1 = \frac{F}{\sum t_e \cdot l_w} = \frac{280 \times 10^3}{2 \cdot t_e \cdot 220}$$

Moment $M = F \times e = 280 \times 0.12 = 33.6$ kN·m

Polar moment of inertia of weld group (two parallel welds):

$$I_{xx} = \frac{2 \cdot t_e \cdot 220^3}{12}$$

Since welds are vertical and parallel, $I_{yy}$ term is for parallel welds: $I_{yy} = 2 \cdot t_e \cdot 220 \cdot (100)^2$ assuming welds are 200 mm apart? Let me re-specify — for two parallel vertical welds separated by 200 mm:

$$I_{yy} = 2 \cdot t_e \cdot 220 \cdot 100^2 = 4{,}400{,}000 \cdot t_e$$

For the conservative approach, we use $r_{max} = \sqrt{(110)^2 + (100)^2} = \sqrt{12100 + 10000} = \sqrt{22100} = 148.66$ mm.

Moment-induced shear at $r_{max}$:

$$\tau_2 = \frac{M \cdot r_{max}}{I_{xx} + I_{yy}}$$

$$\tau_2 = \frac{33.6 \times 10^6 \times 148.66}{t_e \left(\frac{2 \times 220^3}{12} + 4.4 \times 10^6\right)}$$

$$\tau_2 = \frac{33.6 \times 10^6 \times 148.66}{t_e \left(1.7747 \times 10^6 + 4.4 \times 10^6\right)} = \frac{4.995 \times 10^9}{t_e \cdot 6.1747 \times 10^6} = \frac{808.95}{t_e}$$

Combined (worst case) stress:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_1 + \tau_2 = \frac{280{,}000}{440 t_e} + \frac{808.95}{t_e} = \frac{636.36 + 808.95}{t_e} = \frac{1445.31}{t_e}$$

Setting $\tau_{max} = f_{wd}$:

$$\frac{1445.31}{t_e} = 189.4 \implies t_e = 7.63 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to weld size:

$$s = \frac{t_e}{0.7} = \frac{7.63}{0.7} = 10.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $s = 12$ mm** (next higher standard size).

**Verification with $s = 12$ mm:**

$$t_e = 0.7 \times 12 = 8.4 \text{ mm}$$

$$\tau_1 = \frac{280{,}000}{2 \times 8.4 \times 220} = 75.76 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_2 = \frac{808.95}{8.4} = 96.30 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 75.76 + 96.30 = 172.06 \text{ MPa} < 189.4 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**(b) Long weld reduction check [7 marks]**

With $s = 12$ mm: $t_t = 0.7 \times 12 = 8.4$ mm
Length $L = 2 \times 220 = 440$ mm

$$150 \cdot t_t = 150 \times 8.4 = 1260 \text{ mm}$$

Since $L = 440$ mm $< 1260$ mm, **long weld reduction is NOT applicable**.

$$\beta_{Lw} = 1.0$$

**Conclusion:** Use 12 mm fillet welds, 220 mm long, on both sides. No reduction required.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Two plates 12 mm and 10 mm thick are to be joined by a parallel fillet weld of length 200 mm on each side of a 100 mm wide lap joint. The joint is to transmit a factored tensile load of 150 kN. Electrode E410, site welding conditions. Check the adequacy of the joint.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Design stress in weld:

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.50} = 157.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Determine maximum allowable weld size:
Thicker plate = 12 mm. For edge welding with $t > 10$ mm: $s_{max} = t = 12$ mm. Actually, per IS 800 Cl. 9.7.2.2: $s_{max} = t - 1.5 = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5$ mm.

**Step 3:** Trial weld size $s = 10$ mm.

Effective throat: $t_e = 0.7 \times 10 = 7$ mm

Minimum size from IS 800 Table 22 (for 12 mm plate): 5 mm ✓

Minimum effective length per IS 800 Cl. 9.7.3: $L \geq 4s = 40$ mm AND $L \geq 40$ mm. Provided $L = 200$ mm ✓

**Step 4:** Capacity of two parallel fillet welds:

$$V_{dsg} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e \cdot l_w = 157.8 \times 7 \times (2 \times 200)$$

$$V_{dsg} = 157.8 \times 7 \times 400 = 441{,}840 \text{ N} = 441.84 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Check:

$$V_{dsg} = 441.84 \text{ kN} > F = 150 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6:** Long weld reduction check:

$$150 \cdot t_t = 150 \times 7 = 1050 \text{ mm} > L = 400 \text{ mm}$$

$\beta_{Lw} = 1.0$, no reduction.

**Step 7:** Shear in parent metal check (Clause 10.5.4):

$$V_{dpm} = \frac{A_{vn} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}}$$

For thinner plate (10 mm): $A_{vn} = (0.9 \times 100) \times 10 = 900$ mm² (gross section reduced for shear lag)

$$V_{dpm} = \frac{900 \times 250}{1.732 \times 1.10} = \frac{225{,}000}{1.905} = 118{,}108 \text{ N} = 118.1 \text{ kN}$$

> Since $V_{dpm} < V_{dsg}$, the **parent metal in shear governs**. The connection capacity is **118.1 kN < 150 kN**, hence the joint is **NOT adequate** with 10 mm welds.

**Redesign:** Either increase plate thickness or use higher-grade steel. Alternatively, increase overlap length.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting parent metal shear check** — Students often check only weld capacity and miss Clause 10.5.4 (shear in parent plate at fusion face). This accounts for ~3-4 marks lost in typical valuation.
> 2. **Using $\gamma_{mw} = 1.10$ instead of 1.50** for site welds — This error inflates allowable stress by 20% and is a common mark-loser.
> 3. **Confusing $t_t$ with $t_e$** — Both appear in formulas. Effective throat $t_e = K \cdot s$ is used for design; actual throat $t_t = 0.7s$ is used for long-weld reduction check.
> 4. **Not rounding up weld size to next available size** — Weld sizes come in 1 mm or 2 mm increments; round UP to be conservative.

---

### 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Fillet weld size $s$** = minimum leg length (smaller of the two legs if unequal)
- [ ] **Actual throat $t_t = 0.7s$** for 90° equal-leg weld (the famous constant!)
- [ ] **Effective throat $t_e = K \cdot s$**, where $K$ depends on fusion angle $\theta$
- [ ] **Design stress** $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$
- [ ] $\gamma_{mw} = \mathbf{1.25}$ (shop) or $\mathbf{1.50}$ (site) — memorize both!
- [ ] **For E410 shop welds:** $f_{wd} \approx 189$ MPa
- [ ] **For E410 site welds:** $f_{wd} \approx 158$ MPa
- [ ] **Long weld reduction:** Apply if $L > 150 t_t$; $\beta_{Lw} = 1.2 - 0.2L/(150 t_t)$, with $0.6 \leq \beta_{Lw} \leq 1.0$
- [ ] **Min weld length:** $l_w \geq 4s$ AND $\geq 40$ mm
- [ ] **Max weld size at edge:** $s \leq t - 1.5$ mm (for $t > 10$ mm)
- [ ] **Min weld size:** Per IS 800 Table 22 (depends on thicker plate)
- [ ] **Always check parent metal shear** (Clause 10.5.4) — weld strength alone is insufficient
- [ ] **Failure plane is the throat, not the legs** — this is the fundamental premise
- [ ] **Combined loading:** Direct shear + moment-induced shear combined vectorially

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mantra for the Exam Hall:**
> *"Fillet weld fails at its throat; strength = $\frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\gamma_{mw}} \times 0.7s \times L_w \times \beta_{Lw}$."*
> This single sentence encodes the entire design philosophy of fillet welds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DESIGN OF BUTT WELD

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.6):**
> A **butt weld** is a weld deposited in a gap between two members lying in approximately the same plane, in which the deposited metal fuses with the parent metal to form a joint. It is the most efficient type of weld because the stress is transmitted through the weld in a manner similar to the parent metal, with no eccentricity of force.

### 1.1 Classification of Butt Welds

| Type | Description | Weld Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Square Butt Weld** | Used for thin plates (< 6 mm). Edges are square-cut and butted together. | `⎯⎯⎯` |
| **Single-V Butt Weld** | Used for plates 6–20 mm. One side bevelled to a V-shape. | `V` |
| **Double-V Butt Weld** | Used for plates > 20 mm. Both sides bevelled to reduce distortion. | `V-V` |
| **Single-U Butt Weld** | Used for thicker plates; less weld metal required. | `U` |
| **Double-U Butt Weld** | For very thick plates; economical in weld metal. | `U-U` |
| **Single-J / Double-J Butt Weld** | For curved sections where U preparation is impractical. | `J` |

### 1.2 Two Broad Design Categories

> [!NOTE]
> **Complete Penetration Butt Weld (CPBW)** — Weld metal penetrates the **full thickness** of the connected plates. The effective throat thickness equals the **full thickness of the thinner plate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Incomplete / Partial Penetration Butt Weld (IPBW)** — Weld does not penetrate through the full plate depth (used only when full strength is not required). The effective throat equals the **depth of penetration actually achieved**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Glued Book Pages" Model

Imagine two sheets of paper held together by glue:

- **Fillet Weld** = glue applied along the *edges* of the paper — the bond is only at the surface, so when you pull the sheets apart, the glue is stressed in **shear along its sides**.
- **Butt Weld** = glue applied *between* the two sheets, filling the entire gap — the force now travels **straight through the joint**, just like in an uncut piece of paper.

This is why butt welds are stronger and preferred wherever practical — the load path is **direct, concentric, and eccentricity-free**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Principle:** The strength of a butt weld is governed by the **weaker** of (a) the weld metal, and (b) the parent (base) metal. Hence, matching electrodes are essential.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Philosophy of Butt Welds (IS 800:2007 — Limit State Method)

Under the **Limit State Method**, butt welds are designed such that:

$$
\text{Design Action} \leq \text{Design Strength}
$$

For a butt weld, the **design strength** $f_{wd}$ is the **smaller** of:

1. **Strength of weld metal**
2. **Strength of parent metal in the heat-affected zone (HAZ)**

### 2.2 Effective Throat Thickness ($t_t$)

$$
t_t = \text{full thickness of the thinner plate joined}
$$

(For complete penetration butt welds.)

> [!NOTE]
> The effective throat thickness is **not** measured — it is *defined* as the plate thickness. This is a critical conceptual point students often miss.

### 2.3 Effective Length ($l_w$)

$$
l_w = \text{actual length of the weld (provided it is not less than } 4 \times t_t\text{)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $l_w < 4 \, t_t$, the weld is classified as **ineffective for transmitting force** because the stress concentration at the ends is too severe.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Reference (IS 800:2007) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective throat thickness | $t_t = t_{plate}$ (thinner plate) | mm | Cl. 9.6.1.2 |
| Effective length | $l_w \geq 4 \, t_t$ | mm | Cl. 9.6.1.3 |
| Design strength of weld metal | $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw})$ | N/mm² | Cl. 9.6.1.4 |
| Design strength of parent metal | $f_{td} = f_u / \gamma_{mb}$ | N/mm² | Cl. 9.6.1.4 |
| Partial safety factor (weld) | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop), $1.50$ (field) | — | Table 5, Cl. 5.4.1 |
| Partial safety factor (parent) | $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ | — | Table 5 |
| Reduction factor for long joints | $\beta_{lw} = 1.2 - 0.2\,L_j \leq 1.0$ | — | Cl. 10.3.3.1 |
| Average shear stress | $\tau_{vm} = F / (l_w \, t_t)$ | N/mm² | — |
| Average bending stress | $f_{m,bm} = M / (l_w \, t_t^2)$ | N/mm² | — |
| Combined stress check | $\sqrt{f_m^2 + 3\,\tau_m^2} \leq f_{wd}$ | N/mm² | Cl. 9.6.1.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$f_u$ here refers to the ultimate tensile strength of the *weld metal* (or the smaller of weld/base metal $f_u$).**

### 2.5 Design Strength Equations

**Weld Metal (using yield + ultimate criteria):**

$$
f_{yw} = f_{yw,\text{nom}} / \gamma_{mw}
$$

$$
f_{uw} = f_{uw,\text{nom}} / \gamma_{mw}
$$

**Where:**
- $f_{yw,\text{nom}} = 0.8 \, f_u$ of weld electrode (typical)
- $f_{uw,\text{nom}} = f_u$ of weld electrode

> The design uses the **smaller** of these two values as $f_{wd}$.

### 2.6 Design for Axial Force

$$
F \leq f_{wd} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t
$$

Solving for required length:

$$
l_w \geq \frac{F}{f_{wd} \cdot t_t}
$$

### 2.7 Reduction Factor for Long Welds ($\beta_{lw}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cl. 10.3.3.1, IS 800:2007:** For long welds (especially fillet), the average shear strength reduces due to non-uniform stress distribution. Though primarily for fillet welds, the same concept applies when combined stresses occur.

$$
\beta_{lw} = 1.2 - 0.2\,L_j \leq 1.0
$$

Where $L_j$ is the length of the joint in the direction of force transfer, in metres, divided by 100.

### 2.8 Combined Axial + Bending + Shear

When a butt weld is subject to direct shear and bending simultaneously (e.g., in beam-column connections):

$$
\sqrt{f_b^2 + 3\,\tau_v^2} \leq \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{mb} \cdot \sqrt{3}}
$$

Or equivalently using the weld strength:

$$
\sqrt{f_b^2 + 3\,\tau_v^2} \leq f_{wd}
$$

Where:
- $f_b = \dfrac{M}{Z_w}$ = bending stress in weld
- $\tau_v = \dfrac{V}{A_w}$ = shear stress in weld
- $Z_w = \dfrac{t_t \cdot l_w^2}{6}$ = section modulus of weld throat
- $A_w = t_t \cdot l_w$ = area of weld throat

### 2.9 Real-World Utility

Butt welds are used in:
- **Column splices** (transmitting full axial compression)
- **Tension member splices** (lattice girders, truss chords)
- **Beam-to-column moment connections** (in Moment-Resisting Frames, MRFs)
- **Built-up plate girders** (flange-to-web connections)
- **Pressure vessels and penstocks** (where leak-proofing is critical)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Design Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Design Workflow for a Butt Weld

The KTU-recommended procedure for designing a butt weld is summarised below as a **sequential algorithm**:

### Step 1 — Identify the Type of Joint

Determine whether the joint transmits:
- **Pure axial tension/compression**
- **Pure bending**
- **Pure shear**
- **Combined loading** (most common in practice)

### Step 2 — Calculate the Effective Throat Thickness

$$
t_t = \min(t_1,\, t_2)
$$

where $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the thicknesses of the two plates being joined.

> [!NOTE]
> **For unequal thickness plates:** The thicker plate may be *tapered* (slope ≤ 1 in 4, IS 800 Cl. 9.6.1.2) so that $t_t$ equals the thinner plate.

### Step 3 — Determine Material Properties

Select matching electrode such that:
$$
f_u(\text{weld metal}) \geq f_u(\text{base metal})
$$

**Standard Electrode Selection (per IS 814 / AWS):**

| Base Metal | Electrode Class | $f_y$ (N/mm²) | $f_u$ (N/mm²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fe 410 (IS 2062)** | E43xx (e.g., E4310) | **330** | **410** |
| **Fe 540 (IS 2062)** | E51xx (e.g., E5111) | **400** | **540** |

### Step 4 — Compute Design Strength

$$
f_{wd} = \min\left( \frac{f_{u,\text{weld}}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}},\, \frac{f_{u,\text{base}}}{\gamma_{mb}} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Required Effective Length

For axial loading:
$$
l_{w,\text{req}} = \frac{F}{f_{wd} \cdot t_t}
$$

Check:
$$
l_{w,\text{req}} \geq 4 \, t_t \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 6 — Check Combined Stresses (if applicable)

$$
\sqrt{f_b^2 + 3\,\tau_v^2} \leq f_{wd}
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Reduction Factor (for long joints)

$$
l_{w,\text{design}} = \frac{l_{w,\text{req}}}{\beta_{lw}} \quad \text{(if } L_j > 100\,t_t\text{)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Axial Tension Butt Weld

**Problem:**
Two plates of thickness 12 mm and 16 mm are joined by a complete penetration single-V butt weld. The joint is subjected to an axial tensile force of **250 kN**. The plates are of grade Fe 410. The weld is made in the **shop**. Verify the weld.

### Given Data
$$
F = 250 \text{ kN} = 250{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

$$
t_1 = 12 \text{ mm},\quad t_2 = 16 \text{ mm}
$$

Material: Fe 410 → $f_u = 410$ N/mm², $f_y = 250$ N/mm²

Weld metal: matching electrode with $f_{u,w} = 410$ N/mm²

### Solution

**Step 1 — Effective throat thickness:**

$$
t_t = \min(12,\, 16) = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length required (assume full length is effective):**

$$
l_{w,\text{req}} = \frac{F}{f_{wd} \cdot t_t}
$$

Compute $f_{wd}$:

$$
f_{wd,\text{weld}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = \frac{410}{2.165} \approx 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
f_{wd,\text{base}} = \frac{410}{1.25} = 328 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\therefore f_{wd} = \min(189.4,\, 328) = \mathbf{189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

(weld metal governs)

**Step 3 — Required length:**

$$
l_{w,\text{req}} = \frac{250{,}000}{189.4 \times 12} = \frac{250{,}000}{2272.8} \approx \mathbf{110 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Check minimum length criterion:**

$$
4 \, t_t = 4 \times 12 = 48 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $110 > 48$, the criterion is satisfied. ✓

**Step 5 — Provide actual weld length:**

Provide $l_w = 110$ mm (or round up to 120 mm with 5 mm setback at each end).

> [!TIP]
> **End setback:** Actual weld length is taken as the *gross* length minus 2 × throat thickness at each end to account for crater cracks.

**Step 6 — Check combined stress:** Only axial tension exists, so combined stress check is not required. ✓

### Final Result
$$
\boxed{l_w = 110 \text{ mm, throat thickness } t_t = 12 \text{ mm}}
$$

The weld is **SAFE**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Combined Bending and Shear

**Problem:**
A plate girder flange is connected to the web by a complete penetration butt weld. The local bending moment at the section is $M = 80$ kN·m and shear is $V = 120$ kN. Flange thickness = 20 mm, web thickness = 12 mm. Both materials Fe 410. Verify.

### Given
$$
M = 80 \text{ kN·m} = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
V = 120 \text{ kN} = 120{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

$$
t_{\text{web}} = 12 \text{ mm},\quad t_{\text{flange}} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

### Solution

**Step 1 — Effective throat thickness** (butt weld between flange and web):

$$
t_t = \min(t_f,\, t_w) = \min(20,\, 12) = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length:** Provided full length $l_w = 1000$ mm.

Check $4 t_t = 48$ mm. Since $1000 \gg 48$, OK. ✓

**Step 3 — Design strength:** (same as before)

$$
f_{wd} = 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Bending stress in weld:**

$$
Z_w = \frac{t_t \cdot l_w^2}{6} = \frac{12 \times 1000^2}{6} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
f_b = \frac{M}{Z_w} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{40 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Shear stress in weld:**

$$
A_w = t_t \cdot l_w = 12 \times 1000 = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V}{A_w} = \frac{120{,}000}{12{,}000} = \mathbf{10 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Combined stress check (Cl. 9.6.1.5):**

$$
\sqrt{f_b^2 + 3\,\tau_v^2} \leq f_{wd}
$$

$$
\sqrt{40^2 + 3 \times 10^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 300} = \sqrt{1900} = \mathbf{43.6 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

Since $43.6 < 189.4$, the weld is **SAFE**. ✓

> [!TIP]
> Even though stresses look modest, the combined-stress formula is the **governing check** in almost every design involving both $M$ and $V$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Design of Butt Weld Length for Given Load

**Problem:** Design the length of a complete penetration butt weld to connect two plates of size 200 mm × 16 mm carrying a factored tensile load of **450 kN**. The plates are of Fe 410 and the weld is shop-welded.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Throat thickness:**

$$
t_t = 16 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{same thickness both sides})
$$

**Step 2 — Design strength:**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} \approx 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Required effective length:**

$$
l_{w,\text{req}} = \frac{F}{f_{wd} \cdot t_t} = \frac{450{,}000}{189.4 \times 16} = \frac{450{,}000}{3030.4} \approx \mathbf{148.5 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Add end setbacks:**

For $t_t = 16$ mm, end setback $e = 2 t_t = 32$ mm.

$$
l_{w,\text{gross}} = 148.5 + 32 = 180.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Provide $l_w = \mathbf{185 \text{ mm}}$ (rounded up).

**Step 5 — Verify minimum length:**

$$
4 \, t_t = 64 \text{ mm} < 148.5 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

### Final Design

$$
\boxed{l_w = 185 \text{ mm, butt weld CPBW, V-preparation, E43 electrode}}
$$

---

# 4. Comparative Matrix: Butt Welds vs. Fillet Welds

This is a high-yield KTU exam comparison often asked as a 3-mark question.

| Criterion | Butt Weld | Fillet Weld |
|---|---|---|
| **Stress transmission** | Direct, concentric | Eccentric, shear on throat |
| **Strength efficiency** | **100%** (equal to base metal) | **60–80%** of base metal |
| **Weld metal required** | More (full depth) | Less (along edges) |
| **Edge preparation** | Required (beveling) | Not required |
| **Distortion** | More (due to heat) | Less |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Inspection** | Radiographic / ultrasonic | Visual / dye penetrant |
| **Typical application** | Splice joints, pressure vessels | Bracket connections, lap joints |
| **Design strength formula** | $f_u/(\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw})$ | $f_u/(\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}) \times 0.7$ (fillet factor) |
| **Critical parameter** | Throat thickness = plate thickness | Throat thickness = $0.7 \times$ leg size |

---

## 5. Special Design Considerations

### 5.1 Tapered Transitions for Unequal Thickness Plates

When plates of different thicknesses are joined by a butt weld:

$$
\text{Slope} \leq \frac{1}{4} \quad \text{(i.e., 1 vertical to 4 horizontal)}
$$

This avoids stress concentration at the abrupt change in section.

### 5.2 Stress Concentration at Weld Ends

> [!WARNING]
> Crater cracks, undercuts, and slag inclusions at weld ends reduce effective length. Always **subtract 2 × throat thickness** from the gross length to get the effective length unless proper run-on/run-off tabs are used.

### 5.3 Welding Process Selection (for KTU Awareness)

| Process | Application | Position |
|---|---|---|
| **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc)** | Site welding | All |
| **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc)** | Shop welding | All |
| **SAW (Submerged Arc)** | Heavy plates, long welds | Flat, Horizontal |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | Stainless steel, root pass | All |

### 5.4 Defects to Watch (Safety Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **From a Safety in Design perspective**, the following defects render a butt weld **UNSAFE** and must be repaired:
> - Lack of penetration
> - Lack of fusion
> - Porosity > 3% area
> - Undercut > 0.5 mm deep
> - Crater cracks
> - Overlap (insufficient fusion)
> - Slag inclusions in critical sections

### 5.5 Inspection Methods

| Method | Defect Detected | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual (VT)** | Surface cracks, undercut | IS 822 / AWS D1.1 |
| **Dye Penetrant (PT)** | Surface cracks | IS 3658 |
| **Magnetic Particle (MT)** | Surface/near-surface cracks | IS 3703 |
| **Ultrasonic (UT)** | Internal defects, lack of fusion | IS 4260 |
| **Radiographic (RT)** | Internal defects, porosity | IS 1182 |

```mermaid
    I --> J
    J --> K["Add End Setbacks e = 2 times tt"]
    K --> L["Compute Gross Length and Finalize"]
    L --> M["End: Weld is SAFE or REVISE"]
```

```mermaid
    bw -->|"Stress Flow Path"| web["Beam Web"]
    web -->|"Fillet Weld"| bm

    check -->|"Otherwise"| NG["Revise Section"]
```

# 7. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Q1. Differentiate between a butt weld and a fillet weld. State the design strength formula for both as per IS 800:2007.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **butt weld** is a weld made in a gap between two members lying in the same plane, where the deposited metal fuses with the parent metal and transmits the load **directly through the throat**.

A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces at right angles, transmitting load through **shear on its throat**.

| Aspect | Butt Weld | Fillet Weld |
|---|---|---|
| Load path | Direct, concentric | Eccentric, shear |
| Throat thickness | Full plate thickness | $0.7 \times$ leg size |
| Design strength | $f_u/(\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw})$ | $f_u/(\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw})$ |

> The butt weld is more efficient as **no 0.7 factor** is applied.

---

#### **Q2. What is the effective throat thickness for a complete penetration butt weld? Why is the minimum effective length specified as $4 t_t$?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

For a **complete penetration butt weld**, the effective throat thickness $t_t$ is taken as the **full thickness of the thinner plate** being joined. It is not measured but defined as per IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.6.1.2.

The **minimum effective length** is specified as $4 \, t_t$ because:
- Weld ends suffer from **stress concentration** (crater cracks, undercuts).
- A short weld has disproportionately high end stresses relative to its length.
- Beyond $4 t_t$, the central portion of the weld reaches uniform stress distribution.

Hence, if $l_w < 4 t_t$, the weld is considered **ineffective** for transmitting the design force.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Two steel plates of 200 mm width and 12 mm thickness are to be joined using a complete penetration butt weld to transmit a factored tensile force of 280 kN. The plates are of grade Fe 410 and the welding is done in the workshop. Design the butt weld and verify its safety.**

### Part (a) — Determination of Throat Thickness and Design Strength (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- Plate dimensions: 200 mm × 12 mm
- Factored tensile force: $F = 280$ kN = 280,000 N
- Material: Fe 410, $f_u = 410$ N/mm², $f_y = 250$ N/mm²
- Welding location: **Shop** → $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

**Step 2 — Effective throat thickness:**

Since both plates have the same thickness:

$$
t_t = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Design strength of weld:**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25}
$$

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{410}{2.165} \approx 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with parent metal strength:**

$$
f_{td} = \frac{f_u}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{410}{1.25} = 328 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\therefore f_{wd} = \min(189.4,\, 328) = \mathbf{189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

(weld metal governs)

### Part (b) — Length Calculation and Safety Verification (7 Marks)

**Step 5 — Required effective length:**

$$
l_{w,\text{req}} = \frac{F}{f_{wd} \cdot t_t} = \frac{280{,}000}{189.4 \times 12}
$$

$$
l_{w,\text{req}} = \frac{280{,}000}{2272.8} \approx \mathbf{123.2 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 6 — Check minimum length criterion:**

$$
4 \, t_t = 4 \times 12 = 48 \text{ mm} < 123.2 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Add end setbacks:**

End setback per side $= 2 t_t = 24$ mm (in absence of run-on tabs).

$$
l_{w,\text{gross}} = 123.2 + 2 \times 24 = 171.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Provide $\boxed{l_w = 175 \text{ mm}}$.

**Step 8 — Check plate width adequacy:**

The plate width is 200 mm. Since the weld length is well within the plate width, **no edge tear-out** occurs. ✓

**Step 9 — Reduction factor for long joints:**

$$
L_j = 175 \text{ mm} = 0.175 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\beta_{lw} = 1.2 - 0.2 \times 0.175 \approx 1.165, \text{ but } \beta_{lw} \leq 1.0
$$

So $\beta_{lw} = 1.0$. No reduction needed.

**Step 10 — Final Verification:**

$$
\text{Strength provided} = f_{wd} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t = 189.4 \times 175 \times 12 = 397{,}740 \text{ N}
$$

$$
= 397.7 \text{ kN} > 280 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \quad \textbf{SAFE}
$$

### **Final Design Summary**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Weld type | Complete Penetration Butt Weld (V-preparation) |
| Electrode | E43 (matching Fe 410) |
| Throat thickness $t_t$ | 12 mm |
| Effective length $l_w$ | 175 mm |
| Safety factor used | 1.25 (shop) |
| Status | **DESIGN IS SAFE** ✓ |

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A plate girder has a flange plate of size 300 mm × 25 mm and a web plate of 12 mm thickness. They are joined by a complete penetration butt weld on each side of the web. At a particular section, the factored bending moment is 120 kN·m and the factored shear force is 90 kN. Verify the safety of the butt weld. Take $f_u$ = 410 N/mm², shop welding.**

### Part (a) — Bending Stress in Butt Weld (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Effective throat thickness:**

The butt weld transmits force from the flange (25 mm) to the web (12 mm).

$$
t_t = \min(25,\, 12) = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length:** Provided full length of weld:

$$
l_w = 300 \text{ mm}
$$

Check: $4 \, t_t = 48$ mm $< 300$ mm. ✓

**Step 3 — Design strength:**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} \approx 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Section modulus of weld throat:**

The weld resists bending about the centroidal axis of the flange-web connection. Since the weld is on both sides of the web (in a T-joint), each weld contributes. But here, treat it as a single throat zone:

$$
Z_w = \frac{t_t \cdot l_w^2}{6} = \frac{12 \times 300^2}{6} = \frac{12 \times 90{,}000}{6} = 180{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Bending stress:**

$$
f_b = \frac{M}{Z_w} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{180{,}000} = \mathbf{666.7 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL:** Students often compute $Z_w$ using the *web depth* instead of the *weld length*. Here, the butt weld connects the **flange thickness direction** to the web, so the section modulus is based on $l_w$ being the dimension perpendicular to bending.

Since $f_b > f_{wd}$, **the weld is NOT safe under bending alone.**

**Step 6 — Reconsider geometry:**

In a plate girder, the butt weld between flange and web is essentially in **shear** (transmitting the horizontal shear flow), not in pure bending. The bending is carried by the flange-web combination. The butt weld only transfers the **horizontal shear flow**:

$$
q = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I}
$$

where $Q$ is the first moment of the flange area about the neutral axis. However, since the problem states $M$ and $V$ directly, we apply the combined stress formula.

### Part (b) — Combined Stress Verification (7 Marks)

**Step 7 — Shear stress in weld:**

$$
A_w = t_t \cdot l_w = 12 \times 300 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V}{A_w} = \frac{90{,}000}{3600} = \mathbf{25 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

**Step 8 — Combined stress (per IS 800 Cl. 9.6.1.5):**

$$
\sqrt{f_b^2 + 3\,\tau_v^2} = \sqrt{666.7^2 + 3 \times 25^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{444{,}489 + 1875} = \sqrt{446{,}364} \approx \mathbf{668.1 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

Since $668.1 \gg 189.4$ N/mm², **the weld is NOT SAFE.**

**Step 9 — Redesign considerations:**

Since bending stress is dominant, we must recognise that **a butt weld is unsuitable** for transferring bending in a flange-web connection. Instead, use:
- **Fillet welds on both sides** (double fillet) which transfer horizontal shear only, or
- **Increase $l_w$** to reduce $f_b$, but in a 300 mm plate girder, this is geometrically limited.

**Step 10 — Practical redesign using fillet welds:**

For a horizontal shear flow $q = VQ/I$, design fillet welds on **both sides** of the web using:

$$
\text{Strength of fillet per mm} = f_{wd,\text{fillet}} \times 0.7 \times s
$$

where $s$ = leg size.

Assume $s = 8$ mm fillet, weld on both sides:

$$
f_{wd,\text{fillet}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\text{Strength per mm} = 189.4 \times 0.7 \times 8 = 1060.6 \text{ N/mm}
$$

For 1 mm of weld length on each side, total = $2 \times 1060.6 = 2121.2$ N/mm.

> This is a more appropriate design for the flange-web connection.

### **Conclusion:**

A **butt weld in flange-web of a plate girder** is generally **overstressed in bending**. The recommended solution is **double fillet welds** (one each side of the web).

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALL — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4·$t_t$ minimum length check** — Costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "effective throat" with "leg size"** — Butt weld throat = plate thickness (no 0.7 factor). Students mistakenly apply the 0.7 fillet factor to butt welds.
> 3. **Ignoring end setbacks** — Always add $2 \times t_t$ to the gross length.
> 4. **Wrong partial safety factor** — Use $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ for *shop*, $1.50$ for *field/site* welding.
> 5. **Applying fillet-weld reduction factor $\beta_{lw}$ to short butt welds** — Not required for butt welds of moderate length.

---

## 8. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DESIGN OF BUTT WELDS**

- ✅ **Throat thickness ($t_t$) = full thickness of the thinner plate** (for complete penetration). NO 0.7 factor.
- ✅ **Minimum effective length = $4 \cdot t_t$**.
- ✅ **End setback = $2 \cdot t_t$ per side** (unless run-on/off tabs are used).
- ✅ **Design strength**: $f_{wd} = f_u/(\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw})$ governs (weld metal is usually weaker).
- ✅ **Partial safety factor**: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop), $1.50$ (site/field).
- ✅ **Combined stress check**: $\sqrt{f_b^2 + 3\tau_v^2} \leq f_{wd}$ — **always apply for M + V loading**.
- ✅ **Butt weld is 100% efficient** in transmitting axial load, unlike fillet weld (60–80%).
- ✅ **Match electrode strength to base metal**: Fe 410 → E43; Fe 540 → E51.
- ✅ **Inspection**: Butt welds in critical joints use **RT or UT** (per IS 1182 / IS 4260).
- ✅ **Long-joint reduction** $\beta_{lw}$ applies when $L_j$ is large; not for short connections.
- ✅ **Tapered transition** required when joining plates of unequal thickness (slope ≤ 1:4).
- ✅ **Safety lens**: Always inspect for **lack of penetration, lack of fusion, and crater cracks** — these are catastrophic defects.
