| **IS 2062** | Hot Rolled Medium and High Tensile Structural Steel |
| **IS 822** | Code of Practice for Inspection of Welds |
| **IS 1182** | Recommended Practice for Radiographic Examination |
| **IS 4260** | Recommended Practice for Ultrasonic Testing |

> **End of Module 3 — Design of Butt Weld**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Plug and Slot Weld
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Plug and Slot Weld

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 800:2007 Terminology)

A **plug weld** is a type of fillet weld made by filling a circular hole in one member with filler metal, thereby joining it to the surface of an overlapping second member, where the surfaces are in contact. The transfer of load occurs through **shear on the faying surface** developed by the weld metal that fills the hole.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Faying Surface (Definition):** The contact surface between the two parts being joined by the weld. In a plug or slot weld, this is the plane through which shear is transmitted.

A **slot weld** is essentially a plug weld in which the hole is elongated (rectangular with rounded ends). It is used when the design demands a longer shear-transfer path or when a circular hole is geometrically infeasible at the joint.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two flat steel plates that need to be lapped (overlapped) and joined. If you cannot use a long fillet weld along the edge — for example, due to space constraints or aesthetic reasons — you can:
- **Drill a hole** through the top plate and pour molten weld metal into it (like filling a cup with cement). The hardened weld metal locks the two plates together → **Plug Weld**.
- **Cut a slot** (rectangular groove with rounded ends) and fill it the same way → **Slot Weld**.

Think of it as a **"mechanical rivet made of weld metal"** rather than a solid rivet. The hole itself is the mould; the weld metal is the material that, once solidified, behaves as the fastener.

## 1.3 Why Use Plug/Slot Welds?

| Scenario | Reason |
|---|---|
| Lap joints in sheet metal assemblies | Avoid long visible fillet welds |
| Hollow sections (CHS) end connections | Provide shear continuity through slots |
| Repair work | Reinforce or reconnect existing members |
| Aesthetic/architectural demands | Concealed connection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (per IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4.7):**
> - Minimum hole diameter: $(t + 8\text{ mm})$ **or** $2.25\,t$, whichever is greater
> - Maximum hole diameter: $\le 2.5\,t$
> - Minimum spacing between holes: $4 \times d$ (plug) or $4 \times w$ (slot)
> - Minimum edge distance: $1.5 \times d$ (plug) or $1.5 \times w$ (slot)
> - Partial safety factor for weld: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$
>
> where $t$ = thickness of the part containing the hole, $d$ = hole diameter, $w$ = slot width.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Plug and Slot Welds

The load transfer mechanism proceeds in three structured steps:

1. **Step 1 — Geometric Compatibility Check:** Verify the hole/slot geometry satisfies the **minimum and maximum** dimensional limits. A hole that is too small cannot develop sufficient shear area; one that is too large becomes a stress concentrator and weakens the base plate.

2. **Step 2 — Shear Plane Identification:** Unlike a fillet weld (where shear acts on the throat), the plug/slot weld develops shear **on the faying surface itself** — i.e., on the cylindrical surface of the weld metal filling the hole. The weld metal acts like a small cylindrical (or prismatic) shear key.

3. **Step 3 — Strength Verification:** The design strength is governed by the **smaller** of:
   - Shear rupture of the weld metal in the hole/slot
   - Shear/tearing of the base metal surrounding the hole
   - Bearing failure of the base metal under the weld plug

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Min. hole/slot width | $d_{\min} = \max\!\big[(t+8),\ 2.25\,t\big]$ | mm | IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7.1 |
| 2 | Max. hole diameter | $d_{\max} = 2.5\,t$ | mm | IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7.1 |
| 3 | Max. slot length | $L_{\max} = 10\,t$ | mm | IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7.1 |
| 4 | Min. centre-to-centre spacing | $p = 4d$ (plug) or $4w$ (slot) | mm | IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7.2 |
| 5 | Min. edge distance | $e = 1.5d$ (plug) or $1.5w$ (slot) | mm | IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7.3 |
| 6 | Effective shear area of plug | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | mm² | – |
| 7 | Effective shear area of slot | $A_s = w \cdot L_{\text{eff}}$ | mm² | – |
| 8 | Shear strength of weld metal | $\tau_{vd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} = \dfrac{0.7\,f_u}{\sqrt{3}}$ *(for grade E410 electrodes, $f_u = 410$ MPa)* | MPa | IS 800 Cl. 9.5.4 |
| 9 | Design strength of plug | $P_{d,\text{plug}} = A_p \cdot \tau_{vd}$ | N | – |
| 10 | Design strength of slot | $P_{d,\text{slot}} = A_s \cdot \tau_{vd}$ | N | – |
| 11 | Strength reduction factor (long slot) | $0.75 \cdot P_{d,\text{slot}}$ if $L > 1.5\,w$ | – | IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7.4 |
| 12 | Partial safety factor | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ | – | IS 800 Table 5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Plug and slot welds are routinely used in **tubular truss connections**, **ship hull plating**, **storage tank shells**, and **light-gauge steel assemblies** where lap joints must be made without long fillet runs. The design ensures that the welded shear key is as strong as the base metal it connects.

## 2.3 Why the Reduction Factor for Long Slots?

When slot length $L > 1.5\,w$, the **shear stress distribution along the length of the slot becomes non-uniform** (it tends to concentrate near the ends). To account for this, IS 800:2007 prescribes a **0.75 multiplier** on the design strength of long slots to prevent premature shear failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Think of a long, thin shear key. The load enters from both ends and concentrates there. The middle portion is under-loaded. Multiplying by 0.75 is the designer's way of "averaging out" this non-uniformity safely.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Design Strength of a Plug Weld

We begin with the fundamental shear-stress relationship on a cylindrical surface.

**Step 1 — Identify the shear plane.**

For a plug weld of diameter $d$ in a plate of thickness $t$, the weld metal that fills the hole is in shear along the cylindrical interface between the weld plug and the surrounding base metal. The effective **shear area** is taken as the **nominal cross-sectional area of the hole** (an idealisation adopted by IS 800:2007):

$$
A_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the von Mises / Tresca shear yield criterion.**

For ductile weld metal, the shear yield strength $\tau_y$ is related to the ultimate tensile strength $f_u$ of the weld electrode by:

$$
\tau_y \;=\; \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

This is derived from the **von Mises yield criterion** for pure shear: $f_y = \sqrt{3}\,\tau_y \;\Rightarrow\; \tau_y = f_y / \sqrt{3}$. For weld metal, the corresponding ultimate shear strength is:

$$
\tau_u \;=\; \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the partial safety factor.**

Following Limit State Design, the **design shear strength** of the weld metal is:

$$
\tau_{vd} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_u}{\gamma_{mw}} \;=\; \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\;\gamma_{mw}}
$$

For the standard electrode grade **E410** ($f_u = 410$ MPa) and $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$:

$$
\tau_{vd} \;=\; \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3}\times 1.25} \;=\; \dfrac{410}{2.1651} \;\approx\; 189.36\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the design strength (factored load capacity).**

$$
P_d \;=\; A_p \cdot \tau_{vd} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\;\gamma_{mw}}\right)
$$

Or equivalently, in the compact KTU-board form:

$$
\boxed{\,P_d \;=\; \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{0.7\,f_u}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{mw}}\,}
$$

(using $0.7 f_u$ as the nominal ultimate strength per IS 800 weld classification).

## 3.2 Derivation: Design Strength of a Slot Weld

The slot weld follows identical logic, except the effective area uses the slot's rectangular geometry.

**Step 1 — Effective shear area of slot.**

$$
A_s \;=\; w \cdot L_{\text{eff}}
$$

where $w$ = width of slot, and $L_{\text{eff}}$ = effective length (the slot length minus the two end radii; for a fully rounded slot, $L_{\text{eff}} = L - w$ if length includes the rounded ends — verify per drawing).

**Step 2 — Design strength.**

$$
P_d \;=\; A_s \cdot \tau_{vd} \;=\; w \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\;\gamma_{mw}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 0.75 reduction for long slots.**

If $L > 1.5\,w$:

$$
P_{d,\text{reduced}} \;=\; 0.75 \cdot w \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\;\gamma_{mw}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Plug Weld Design (Model Solution Pattern)

**Given:**
- Plate thickness $t = 12$ mm
- Hole diameter $d = 25$ mm
- Electrode grade E410 ($f_u = 410$ MPa)
- Factored applied load $P_u = 120$ kN

**Step 1 — Check geometric limits.**

$$
d_{\min} = \max\!\big[(12+8),\ 2.25 \times 12\big] = \max(20,\ 27) = 27\ \text{mm}
$$

**❗ Note:** The provided $d = 25$ mm is **less than** the minimum required (27 mm). The design would need to be revised to $d \ge 27$ mm.

Let us re-evaluate with $d = 30$ mm.

$$
d_{\max} = 2.5 \times 12 = 30\ \text{mm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad d = 30\ \text{mm is at the limit (acceptable)}.
$$

**Step 2 — Effective shear area.**

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi (30)^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 900}{4} = 706.86\ \text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Design shear strength of weld metal.**

$$
\tau_{vd} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.36\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Design strength of plug weld.**

$$
P_d = 706.86 \times 189.36 = 133{,}847\ \text{N} \approx 133.85\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety check.**

$$
P_d = 133.85\ \text{kN} \;>\; P_u = 120\ \text{kN} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{Safe}\ \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trick:** The "0.85 in. = 22 mm" type question often tests whether the student *reads the limits first*. Always verify **$d_{\min}$ and $d_{\max}$** before any strength calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Plug Weld Geometric Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Input Parameters: Plate Thickness t, Plate Width B, Electrode Grade"] --> B{"Geometric Limit Check"}
    B -- "Compute d_min = max of t+8 and 2.25t" --> C{"d_min Satisfied?"}
    B -- "Compute d_max = 2.5t" --> D{"d_max Satisfied?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Compute Effective Shear Area A_p = pi d squared divided by 4"]
    C -- "No" --> F["REDESIGN: Increase d to d_min"]
    D -- "Yes" --> G["Proceed to Strength Check"]
    D -- "No" --> H["REDESIGN: Reduce d to d_max"]
    E --> G
    G --> H2["Compute Shear Strength of Weld tau_vd = f_u over sqrt of 3 times gamma_mw"]
    H2 --> I["Compute Design Strength P_d = A_p times tau_vd"]
    I --> J{"P_d Greater Than or Equal to P_u ?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["DESIGN SAFE: Connection Adequate"]
    J -- "No" --> L["REDESIGN: Add More Plugs or Increase Diameter"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Slot Weld Design Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Input: Plate Thickness t, Slot Width w, Slot Length L"] --> S2{"Width Check: w between w_min and w_max"}
    S2 -- "Pass" --> S3{"Length Check: L less than or equal to 10t"}
    S2 -- "Fail" --> S2R["Adjust w to w_min or w_max"]
    S3 -- "Pass" --> S4{"Long Slot: L greater than 1.5 w ?"}
    S3 -- "Fail" --> S3R["Reduce L to 10t"]
    S4 -- "Yes" --> S5["Apply Reduction Factor 0.75 to Design Strength"]
    S4 -- "No" --> S6["Full Design Strength Applicable"]
    S5 --> S7["Compute A_s = w times L_eff"]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8["Compute P_d = 0.75 times A_s times tau_vd  OR  A_s times tau_vd"]
    S8 --> S9{"P_d Sufficient for P_u ?"}
    S9 -- "Yes" --> SOK["DESIGN SAFE"]
    S9 -- "No" --> SFR["REDESIGN: Increase Slot Length or Add Slots"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Plug vs Slot Weld Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Two Plates to be Lap-Joined"] --> P2{"Load Direction: In-Plane Shear"}
    P2 -- "Yes" --> P3{"Joint Length Available?"}
    P3 -- "Long Joint, Edge Weld Possible" --> P4["Use Standard Fillet Weld"]
    P3 -- "Restricted / Concealed" --> P5{"Geometry of Shear Path?"}
    P5 -- "Point Load / Concentrated" --> P6["PLUG WELD: Circular Hole"]
    P5 -- "Line Load / Distributed" --> P7["SLOT WELD: Elongated Hole"]
    P6 --> P8["Verify d_min, d_max"]
    P7 --> P9["Verify w_min, w_max, L_max"]
    P8 --> P10["Compute P_d Plug"]
    P9 --> P11["Compute P_d Slot with 0.75 factor if L greater than 1.5 w"]
    P10 --> P12["Compare with Factored Load"]
    P11 --> P12
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a plug weld and a slot weld. State any two limitations of plug welds as per IS 800:2007.**

**Model Answer:**

A **plug weld** is formed by filling a **circular hole** in one member with weld metal to fuse it with the surface of an overlapping member. A **slot weld** uses an **elongated (rectangular with rounded ends) opening** for the same purpose.

**Limitations of plug welds (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4.7):**
1. They **shall not be used to resist direct tension** — only shear on the faying surface is permitted.
2. The **diameter is restricted** to a maximum of $2.5\,t$ (where $t$ is the thickness of the part containing the hole) — limiting the shear area and hence the capacity per plug.
3. Plug welds are **less effective than slot welds** for transferring distributed shear along a line, as the shear area is concentrated at a point.

*(Any two valid points fetch full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State the minimum and maximum dimensional limits for the diameter of a plug weld hole as per IS 800:2007.**

**Model Answer:**

For a plug weld hole in a plate of thickness $t$:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{\min} \;=\; \max\!\big[(t + 8)\text{ mm},\ 2.25\,t\big]\,} \quad\quad \boxed{\,d_{\max} \;=\; 2.5\,t\,}
$$

Example: For $t = 10$ mm, $d_{\min} = \max(18, 22.5) = 22.5$ mm, $d_{\max} = 25$ mm.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the design philosophy of plug welds with a neat sketch. State the conditions where plug welds are preferred over fillet welds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plate of thickness **10 mm** is to be connected to another plate using a plug weld. The hole diameter provided is **28 mm**. Using **E410 grade electrodes**, check whether the design is safe for a factored load of **90 kN**. Use IS 800:2007. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Design Philosophy of Plug Welds (7 marks)**

The plug weld design philosophy is based on the following key principles:

1. **Load Transfer Mechanism:** The plug weld transfers load through **shear on the faying surface** of the weld metal filling the hole. The cylindrical surface of the weld plug acts as the shear plane.

2. **Shear Area:** The effective shear area is the **nominal cross-sectional area of the hole** (not the perimeter), per IS 800:2007 simplification:

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}
$$

3. **Geometric Constraints:** IS 800:2007 prescribes:
   - $d \ge \max(t+8,\ 2.25\,t)$
   - $d \le 2.5\,t$
   - Centre-to-centre spacing $\ge 4d$
   - Edge distance $\ge 1.5d$

4. **Preferred over Fillet Weld When:**
   - Joint length is restricted and a continuous fillet weld is infeasible
   - Aesthetic / concealed connection is required
   - Lap joint is in thin sheet metal with limited edge access
   - Tubular sections where access is only from one side

5. **Design Strength:**

$$
P_d = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\;\gamma_{mw}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Check (7 marks)**

**Given:** $t = 10$ mm, $d = 28$ mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa (E410), $P_u = 90$ kN, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

**Step 1 — Geometric checks.**

$$
d_{\min} = \max\!\big[(10+8),\ 2.25 \times 10\big] = \max(18,\ 22.5) = 22.5\ \text{mm}
$$

Since $d = 28 \ge 22.5$ mm ✓

$$
d_{\max} = 2.5 \times 10 = 25\ \text{mm}
$$

Since $d = 28 > 25$ mm ❌ **Geometric violation — redesign required.**

**Step 2 — Redesign with $d = 25$ mm (maximum permitted).**

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi (25)^{2}}{4} = 490.87\ \text{mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\tau_{vd} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.36\ \text{MPa}
$$

$$
P_d = 490.87 \times 189.36 = 92{,}944\ \text{N} \approx 92.94\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Safety check.**

$$
P_d = 92.94\ \text{kN} \;>\; P_u = 90\ \text{kN} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{Safe (with } d = 25\text{ mm)}\ \checkmark
$$

**Final Conclusion:** The original diameter of 28 mm violates the $d_{\max}$ limit. Redesign to $d = 25$ mm gives $P_d \approx 92.94$ kN, which is just adequate for the applied factored load of 90 kN.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a sketch, explain the geometry and design of a slot weld. What is the purpose of the 0.75 reduction factor in long-slot weld design? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **slot weld** of width **22 mm** and length **120 mm** is used in a **12 mm thick plate** with **E410 electrodes**. Determine the design strength of the slot weld as per IS 800:2007. Is the design adequate for a factored load of **250 kN**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Slot Weld Geometry and Design (7 marks)**

A **slot weld** is formed by filling an elongated hole (slot) cut in one member with weld metal to fuse it to the surface of the overlapping member. The slot has **rounded ends** (semicircular) to avoid stress concentration.

**Geometry limits (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.4.7):**
- Slot width $w$ follows the same limits as plug diameter: $w_{\min} = \max(t+8,\ 2.25\,t)$ and $w_{\max} = 2.5\,t$
- Slot length $L \le 10\,t$
- Spacing between slots $\ge 4\,w$
- Edge distance $\ge 1.5\,w$

**Design strength:**

$$
P_d = w \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\;\gamma_{mw}}
$$

**Purpose of 0.75 reduction factor (for $L > 1.5\,w$):**
The 0.75 factor accounts for the **non-uniform shear stress distribution** along the length of a long slot. The shear tends to concentrate near the ends, leaving the central portion under-stressed. The factor conservatively reduces the effective capacity to avoid premature shear rupture.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $w = 22$ mm, $L = 120$ mm, $t = 12$ mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa, $P_u = 250$ kN, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

**Step 1 — Geometric checks.**

$$
w_{\min} = \max\!\big[(12+8),\ 2.25 \times 12\big] = \max(20,\ 27) = 27\ \text{mm}
$$

Since $w = 22 < 27$ mm ❌ **Geometric violation — slot width too small.**

**Step 2 — Redesign with $w = 27$ mm.**

$$
L_{\max} = 10 \times 12 = 120\ \text{mm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad L = 120\ \text{mm is acceptable at the upper limit.}
$$

Check long-slot condition: $L = 120$ mm vs. $1.5\,w = 1.5 \times 27 = 40.5$ mm.

Since $L = 120 > 1.5\,w = 40.5$ mm → **Apply 0.75 reduction factor.**

**Step 3 — Effective shear area.**

Assuming $L_{\text{eff}} = L - w$ (length excluding rounded end diameters):

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = 120 - 27 = 93\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
A_s = 27 \times 93 = 2511\ \text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Shear strength of weld metal.**

$$
\tau_{vd} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.36\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Design strength (with 0.75 factor for long slot).**

$$
P_d = 0.75 \times 2511 \times 189.36 = 356{,}576\ \text{N} \approx 356.58\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Safety check.**

$$
P_d = 356.58\ \text{kN} \;>\; P_u = 250\ \text{kN} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{Safe}\ \checkmark
$$

**Final Conclusion:** The original slot with $w = 22$ mm violates the $w_{\min}$ requirement. Redesign to $w = 27$ mm with the 0.75 reduction (since $L > 1.5\,w$) gives $P_d \approx 356.58$ kN, which is **adequate** for the applied factored load.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the geometric limits check first** — many students compute $P_d$ without verifying $d_{\min}$ and $d_{\max}$. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks purely for this step.
> 2. **Missing the 0.75 factor for long slots** — if the slot length is more than $1.5 \times$ width, marks are deducted for overestimating strength.
> 3. **Wrong shear area for slot** — using the full rectangle $w \times L$ instead of $w \times (L - w)$ when the length is measured inclusive of the rounded ends.
> 4. **Unit mistakes** — confusing MPa with N/mm². They are numerically equal; just write units consistently.
> 5. **Mixing up $f_u$ and $f_y$** — for weld design, $f_u$ of the **electrode** is used, not the base plate yield strength.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Plug weld = circular hole**; **Slot weld = elongated hole with rounded ends**.
- ✅ Plug/slot welds transfer load via **shear on the faying surface** of the weld plug.
- ✅ **Minimum hole/slot width:** $\max(t+8,\ 2.25\,t)$ — whichever is greater.
- ✅ **Maximum hole/slot width:** $\le 2.5\,t$.
- ✅ **Maximum slot length:** $\le 10\,t$.
- ✅ **Minimum centre-to-centre spacing:** $4d$ (plug) / $4w$ (slot).
- ✅ **Minimum edge distance:** $1.5d$ (plug) / $1.5w$ (slot).
- ✅ **Design strength formula:** $P_d = A \cdot \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\;\gamma_{mw}}$.
- ✅ **Long-slot reduction factor:** Multiply by **0.75** if $L > 1.5\,w$.
- ✅ **Partial safety factor:** $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$.
- ✅ **Standard electrode:** E410 → $f_u = 410$ MPa → $\tau_{vd} \approx 189.36$ MPa.
- ✅ **Plug/slot welds are NOT permitted to resist direct tension.**
- ✅ Always check **geometric limits FIRST** before any strength computation.
- ✅ Use **nominal area of the hole/slot** as the shear area (IS 800 simplification).
- ✅ For **E250 grade base plate** with E410 electrode, the strength is governed by the weld electrode strength (lower of the two governs).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Bolted Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Bolted Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Bolted Joint** is a detachable, statically or dynamically loaded mechanical fastening system comprising a bolt, nut, and (optionally) washers, in which the bolt is subjected to combined pre-load (induced by tightening), external working load, and shear forces depending on the joint configuration. The **Design of a Bolted Joint** under the KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT722) refers to the systematic analytical procedure used to determine the nominal diameter, grade, and number of bolts required so that the induced stresses (tensile, shear, and equivalent von-Mises stress) remain within permissible limits prescribed by relevant **ISO 898-1** property classes and factor-of-safety criteria dictated by the safety philosophy of the course.

### 1.2 Joint Classifications (Used Throughout This Module)

| Joint Type | Loading Nature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Tension / Pre-loaded Joint** | Bolt loaded in pure tension + preload | Cylinder head, pressure vessel cover |
| **Shear Joint (Non-preloaded)** | Bolt shank resists transverse shear | Lug joints, bracket connections |
| **Combined Shear and Tension Joint** | Both tension and shear act simultaneously | Eccentrically loaded brackets |
| **Eccentrically Loaded Joint** | Bolt experiences direct shear + tensile torsion | Crane base, motor mounting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The current NEP-2020 aligned syllabus emphasizes the *load-deformation behaviour*, *preload scatter*, and *safety factor decomposition* (separating installation, material, and operational uncertainties) — this is a recurring two-mark question.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are tightening the cap of a pressure cooker. The cap is held by a clamp that presses the rubber gasket down. That downward pressing force is called the **preload** — the bolt was already stretched even before steam pressure pushed the cap up. When the cooker is pressurized, the steam tries to lift the cap, *reducing* the stretch of the bolt slightly. The bolt is therefore not "seeing" the full external load — it only sees the *change*. This is the essence of a **preloaded joint**: the bolt carries very little variation in stress even under huge fluctuating external loads, which is why properly tightened bolts last forever even in vibrating engines.

Conversely, a **non-preloaded (shear) joint** is like a door hinge pin — there is no axial stretching. The pin simply resists sideways push. The bolt shank has to take the full shear load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Safety Principle:** A preloaded bolt is safer under fatigue because the mean stress (set by preload) dominates the variable stress amplitude. KTU expects students to compute the **dynamic stress amplitude** explicitly.

### 1.4 Standard Metric Bolt Designation Used in KTU Problems

For all design calculations in this module we adopt the **ISO metric coarse thread series (ISO 724)** with fundamental deviation *'H'* for external threads. Standard property classes used in board problems: **8.8, 9.8, 10.9, 12.9**. The numbers denote: *Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) ÷ 100* and *Yield-to-UTS ratio × 10*.

Example: **Class 10.9** means $R_m = 1000 \text{ MPa}$ and $R_{p0.2} = 0.9 \times 1000 = 900 \text{ MPa}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bolt Geometry and Stress Areas (IS 1367 / ISO 898)

Every bolt problem in KTU starts with the relationship between **nominal (major) diameter $d$**, **pitch $p$**, and the **tensile stress area $A_s$**. Three key derived diameters are used:

1. **Pitch diameter** $d_2 \approx d - 0.6495 \, p$
2. **Minor (core) diameter** $d_3 \approx d - 1.2269 \, p$  (Shigley notation; textbook form $d_1 = d - 1.0825\sqrt{p}$)
3. **Tensile stress area** $A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( \dfrac{d_2 + d_3}{2} \right)^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $A_s$ and not $\pi d^2/4$?** Because the helix of the thread reduces the load-carrying cross-section. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if students use the *nominal* diameter instead of $A_s$ for tensile strength.

### 2.2 Strength of a Bolt in Pure Tension (Static Case)

The governing equation used by every standard design code (VDI 2230, Shigley, IS 800, PSG Design Data Book) for sizing a bolt in pure tension is:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F_{\text{axial}}}{A_s} \le \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

where:
- $F_{\text{axial}}$ = total tensile service load on the bolt (N)
- $A_s$ = tensile stress area (mm²)
- $S_{yt}$ = yield strength of bolt material (MPa)
- $n$ = factor of safety against yielding

Rearranged to solve for the required stress area:

$$A_s \ge \frac{n \cdot F_{\text{axial}}}{S_{yt}}$$

Once $A_s$ is known, the standard metric bolt is selected from PSG tables (e.g., M10 → $A_s = 58.0$ mm², M12 → $A_s = 84.3$ mm²).

### 2.3 Strength of a Bolt in Shear

For a single-shear lap joint with a bolt of nominal diameter $d$:

$$\tau = \frac{F_s}{\frac{\pi}{4} d^2} \le \frac{S_{sy}}{n_s}$$

For a **double-shear** (or two-cover-plate) joint the shear area doubles:

$$\tau = \frac{F_s}{2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} d^2} \le \frac{S_{sy}}{n_s}$$

where $S_{sy} \approx 0.577 \, S_{yt}$ (von-Mises criterion) is the allowable shear yield strength.

### 2.4 Preloaded Joint — Force Distribution Analysis (The Core of Module 3)

When an external tensile load $P$ is applied to a preloaded joint, the actual increment of bolt load $\Delta F_b$ and the decrement of clamping force $-\Delta F_c$ are governed by the **joint stiffness ratio** $\Phi$:

$$\Phi = \frac{C_b}{C_b + C_m}$$

$$\Delta F_b = \Phi \cdot P \qquad \text{and} \qquad \Delta F_c = (1 - \Phi) \cdot P$$

where:
- $C_b$ = stiffness of the bolt (N/mm)
- $C_m$ = stiffness of the members being clamped (N/mm)
- $P$ = external working load (N)
- $\Phi$ is called the **bolt-load factor** or **joint stiffness constant** (typically 0.15 – 0.35 for most engineering joints).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The preloaded bolt design condition** (failure criterion used in KTU board problems): the bolt must not yield *and* the joint must not separate. Therefore the **maximum bolt load** is:
> $$F_{b,\text{max}} = F_i + \Phi \cdot P$$
> where $F_i$ is the **installation preload** (residual tensile force after tightening). For a properly designed joint, $F_i \approx 0.75 \, S_p \, A_s$ for permanent joints, where $S_p$ is the proof strength of the bolt grade.

### 2.5 Combined Shear and Tension (Eccentrically Loaded Joint)

For a bolt located at radius $r$ from the centroid of a bolt group subjected to an in-plane eccentric load $W$ at eccentricity $e$:

1. **Direct shear** on the farthest bolt:
$$F_s = \frac{W}{n}$$

2. **Torsional shear** (causing additional tangential shear):
$$F_t = \frac{W \cdot e \cdot r_{\text{max}}}{\sum r_i^2}$$

3. **Resultant shear** on the critical bolt:
$$F_R = \sqrt{F_s^2 + F_t^2}$$

4. **Maximum principal / von-Mises stress:**
$$\tau_{\text{eq}} = \frac{16 F_R}{\pi d^2} \le \frac{S_{sy}}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For bolts under combined axial tension AND shear** (e.g., bracket joints), the governing equivalent stress is the **modified-Mohr criterion** widely accepted for KTU board problems:
> $$\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} + \frac{4 F}{\pi d^2} \cdot K \le S_{sy}$$
> (this variant appears in PSG Data Book, Section 15.14 — students are expected to memorize the form).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All units in **MPa (N/mm²)** and **mm** unless stated. Use $\vert$ symbol as `\\vert` inside any markdown cell to avoid breaking the table.

| # | Design Quantity | Governing Formula | Typical Factor of Safety $n$ | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tensile stress in bolt | $\sigma_t = F \big/ A_s$ | $n = 2 - 4$ (static) | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Shear stress in bolt (single) | $\tau = F_s \big/ (\pi d^2 / 4)$ | $n_s = 3 - 5$ | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Shear stress in bolt (double) | $\tau = F_s \big/ (2 \cdot \pi d^2 / 4)$ | $n_s = 3 - 5$ | ★★★★ |
| 4 | Joint stiffness ratio | $\Phi = C_b \big/ (C_b + C_m)$ | — | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Bolt load increment | $\Delta F_b = \Phi \cdot P$ | — | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | Clamp load decrement | $\Delta F_c = (1 - \Phi) \cdot P$ | — | ★★★★ |
| 7 | Max bolt load (preload case) | $F_{b,\max} = F_i + \Phi P$ | $F_i \le 0.75 S_p A_s$ | ★★★★★ |
| 8 | Resultant shear (eccentric) | $F_R = \sqrt{F_s^2 + F_t^2}$ | — | ★★★★★ |
| 9 | Tensile stress area (ISO) | $A_s = (\pi / 4) [(d_2 + d_3)/2]^2$ | — | ★★★★ |
| 10 | Bolt grade 8.8 / 10.9 | $R_m = 100 \cdot \text{first};\ R_{p0.2} = \text{ratio} \cdot R_m$ | — | ★★★★★ |
| 11 | Tightening torque (approx) | $T_t = K \cdot d \cdot F_i$  (K = 0.2) | — | ★★★ |
| 12 | Proof strength $S_p$ for grade $xy$ | $S_p = (xy/100)_{\text{first digit}} \cdot \sigma_{B,\min}$ | — | ★★★★ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The preloaded bolt is the *single most common mechanical connector on Earth* — virtually every internal-combustion engine, gas turbine, hydraulic cylinder, rail wheel, and pressure vessel relies on it. The design safety philosophy taught in this module underpins the **PVP (Pressure Vessel Piping) codes**, **ASME BPVC Section VIII**, and the **European EN 1993-1-8 (Eurocode 3)** bolt design rules. Mastering the **joint stiffness constant $\Phi$** is what distinguishes a *bookish designer* from a competent machine-design engineer.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Strength of a Bolt in Pure Tension from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define the load scenario.**
Consider a bolt of nominal diameter $d$ with a metric coarse thread, pitch $p$, subjected to an axial tensile service load $F$. The load is transferred through the threaded shank, where the cross-section is reduced from $\pi d^2 / 4$ to the *tensile stress area* $A_s$.

**Step 2 — Express the geometric relation between diameters.**
From ISO 724 thread geometry, the root (minor) diameter is:

$$d_1 = d - 1.0825 \sqrt{p}$$

and the pitch diameter is:

$$d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, p$$

**Step 3 — Compute the tensile stress area.**
The tensile stress area is conventionally defined as the area of a circle whose diameter equals the *arithmetic mean* of $d_2$ and $d_1$:

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{d_2 + d_1}{2} \right)^2$$

Substituting the expressions for $d_1$ and $d_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d_2 + d_1}{2} &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[(d - 0.6495 p) + (d - 1.0825 \sqrt{p})\bigr] \\
&= d - 0.3248 p - 0.5412 \sqrt{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $p \ll d$ (typical for coarse metric threads), the dominant reduction is via $\sqrt{p}$, and this is why the **PSG Data Book tabulates $A_s$ directly** for standard sizes — students are *not* expected to re-derive it numerically, only to know the formula.

**Step 4 — Apply the strength condition.**
For the bolt to remain in the elastic range with factor of safety $n$:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_s} \le \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$

Solving for the *required* stress area:

$$\boxed{\,A_{s,\text{req}} = \frac{n \, F}{S_{yt}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Select the standard bolt size.**
Compare $A_{s,\text{req}}$ with the PSG tabulated $A_s$ values and select the next larger size (e.g., if $A_{s,\text{req}} = 70$ mm² then M12 with $A_s = 84.3$ mm² is selected).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Bolt Load in a Preloaded Joint under External Tension

**Step 1 — Identify the components of load on the bolt.**
The total axial force in a tightened bolt at any instant is:

$$F_b = F_i + \Delta F_b$$

where $F_i$ is the *preload* (residual tension after tightening, before external load is applied) and $\Delta F_b$ is the additional tension due to the external load $P$.

**Step 2 — Compatibility condition.**
When the external load $P$ is applied, the joint (clamped members) shortens by $\delta_c$ and the bolt extends by an *equal* amount (because they are physically connected in series along the load axis):

$$\delta_c = \delta_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta F_c}{C_m} = \frac{\Delta F_b}{C_b}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium condition.**
The total external load is shared between the bolt (which gains $\Delta F_b$) and the joint (which loses $\Delta F_c$):

$$P = \Delta F_b + \Delta F_c$$

**Step 4 — Solve the two equations.**
From the compatibility condition: $\Delta F_c = (C_m / C_b) \Delta F_b$. Substituting into equilibrium:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \Delta F_b + \frac{C_m}{C_b} \Delta F_b \\
P &= \Delta F_b \left( 1 + \frac{C_m}{C_b} \right) \\
\Delta F_b &= \frac{P}{1 + (C_m / C_b)} = \frac{C_b}{C_b + C_m} \cdot P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the joint stiffness constant.**

$$\boxed{\,\Phi = \frac{C_b}{C_b + C_m}\,} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta F_b = \Phi \, P$$

This $\Phi$ is the **load ratio**: the fraction of the external load that *adds* to the bolt tension. Typical values 0.15 – 0.35.

**Step 6 — Maximum bolt load and yield check.**

$$F_{b,\max} = F_i + \Phi P \le S_p \cdot A_s$$

For proof-strength criterion (Kerala University preferred form):

$$\sigma_{\text{equiv}} = \frac{F_{b,\max}}{A_s} \le \frac{S_p}{n}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Resultant Shear Stress in an Eccentrically Loaded Bolt Group

**Step 1 — Geometry of the bolt group.**
Consider $n$ bolts arranged in a circle (or rectangle) of pitch-circle radius $r$. An in-plane load $W$ acts at eccentricity $e$ from the centroid of the bolt group.

**Step 2 — Decompose the load into direct and torsional components.**
The direct shear on *any* bolt, identical for all bolts if they are in a single pitch circle:

$$F_s = \frac{W}{n}$$

The torsional moment is $M = W \cdot e$. The tangential shear on bolt $i$ at radius $r_i$:

$$F_{t,i} = \frac{M \cdot r_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} r_k^2}$$

The maximum occurs at the bolt with the largest $r_i$ (typically $r_{\max}$). For a circular pitch circle, all $r_i = r$, so $\sum r_i^2 = n r^2$ and:

$$F_{t,\max} = \frac{W \cdot e \cdot r}{n \cdot r^2} = \frac{W e}{n r}$$

**Step 3 — Combine vectorially.**
The direct shear and torsional shear are perpendicular, so the resultant on the critical bolt is:

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_s^2 + F_t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert force to shear stress.**
Using a circular cross-section, the average shear stress is:

$$\tau = \frac{F_R}{A_{\text{bolt}}} = \frac{4 F_R}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the safety condition.**

$$\tau = \frac{4 F_R}{\pi d^2} \le \frac{S_{sy}}{n_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d \ge \sqrt{\frac{4 n_s F_R}{\pi S_{sy}}}$$

This is the **standard KTU board form** for sizing bolts in eccentrically loaded joints.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes and Their Governing Stress Equations

| Failure Mode | Location of Critical Stress | Governing Equation | Common Factor of Safety |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile yielding of bolt** | Thread root | $\sigma_t = F / A_s \le S_{yt} / n$ | $n = 2 - 4$ |
| **Shear yielding of bolt shank** | Bolt cross-section | $\tau = 4 F_s / (\pi d^2) \le S_{sy} / n_s$ | $n_s = 3 - 5$ |
| **Joint separation** | Clamped interface | $F_i \ge P \cdot (1 - \Phi)$ | — |
| **Bearing on plate / bolt** | Plate hole surface | $\sigma_b = F / (d \cdot t) \le S_y / n$ | $n = 2 - 3$ |
| **Tearing of plate (net section)** | Net section through hole | $\sigma = F / \{(b - d) t\} \le S_{yt} / n$ | $n = 2 - 3$ |
| **Combined shear + tension** | Bolt cross-section | $\tau_{\text{eq}}^2 + 3\sigma_t^2 \le S_y^2$ | $n = 2 - 4$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Bolted Joint Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Define Working Load P] --> A2{Is Joint Preloaded?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Compute Stiffness Ratio Phi]
    A2 -- No --> A4[Direct Shear / Tension Analysis]
    A3 --> A5[Compute Delta Fb = Phi times P]
    A5 --> A6[Set Preload Fi = 0.75 Sp As]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Fb max = Fi + Delta Fb]
    A7 --> A8[Select Bolt Grade 8.8 10.9 12.9]
    A8 --> A9[Lookup As from PSG Table]
    A9 --> A10[Check Fb max / As le Sp / n]
    A10 -- Pass --> A11[Check Joint Separation Fi ge P 1 minus Phi]
    A10 -- Fail --> A12[Increase Bolt Size]
    A11 -- Pass --> A13[Final Selection Done]
    A11 -- Fail --> A12
    A4 --> A14[Compute Direct Shear Fs = W / n]
    A14 --> A15[Eccentric E]
    A15 -- Yes --> A16[Compute Torsional Ft = W e r / sum r2]
    A15 -- No --> A17[Skip Torsional]
    A16 --> A18[Resultant FR = sqrt Fs2 + Ft2]
    A17 --> A18
    A18 --> A19[Required d from tau eq]
    A19 --> A20[Select Standard Bolt]
    A12 --> A20
    A20 --> A13
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B2 --> B4{Member Stiffness Cm}

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C2 --> C7[Bolt Necking]

    C4 --> C11[Von Mises Combined Stress]

```

### 4.4 Schematic Explanation (Text-Block Equivalent)

Since a 2-D cross-section of a bolted joint with thread profile, washer, and clamp members cannot be rendered in Mermaid without violating the label-formatting rules, the following **block-level functional architecture** is provided:

- **Layer 1 — Clamped Member (top plate):** experiences compression $F_c$ from preload, relieved by $(1-\Phi) P$ under external load.
- **Layer 2 — Gasket / Washer:** transmits the clamping pressure; not designed here (assumed rigid).
- **Layer 3 — Bolt Head:** bears the counter-face pressure; depth ≈ $0.7 d$.
- **Layer 4 — Bolt Shank (unthreaded region):** unstressed axially; only a pilot for the threaded portion.
- **Layer 5 — Threaded Region (stress area $A_s$):** the **critical cross-section** where tensile / shear / combined stress is evaluated.
- **Layer 6 — Nut:** distributes the clamping force over the bottom face.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** When asked to draw a bolted joint, students often forget to show the *grip length* $l_g$ (unthreaded + threaded shank held by nut). Examiner usually awards 1 mark for the correct identification of grip length and 1 mark for marking the tensile stress area $A_s$ at the thread root.
