# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Define the term "permissible stress" and explain its significance in the Working Stress Method of design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Permissible stress (also called allowable or working stress) is defined as the maximum stress that a structural member is permitted to withstand under service loads. It is obtained by dividing the ultimate strength (for brittle materials) or yield stress (for ductile materials) by a suitable factor of safety.
>
> **Formula (0.5 mark):**
>
> $$\sigma_{permissible} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{FoS} \quad \text{(for ductile materials like steel)}$$
>
> **Significance in WSM (1 mark):** In the Working Stress Method, the entire design is governed by ensuring that the *actual* stress produced by the *working (service) loads* never exceeds the permissible stress. This keeps the structure in the elastic range under normal use, ensuring no permanent deformation, no cracking, and adequate safety against unforeseen overloads. The permissible stress thus acts as the **serviceability ceiling** of the member.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Why is the permissible bending stress in steel higher than the permissible tensile stress?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **(1 mark)** The permissible bending stress ($\sigma_{sb} = 0.66 f_y / \gamma_m$) is approximately **10% higher** than the permissible tensile stress ($\sigma_{st} = 0.60 f_y / \gamma_m$).
>
> **(1 mark)** **Reason — Shape Factor:** In a beam under flexure, the stress distribution is *linear* across the depth. When the extreme fibres reach yield, the fibres closer to the neutral axis are still well below yield. This creates a **reserve of plastic strength** quantified by the *shape factor* (ratio of plastic to elastic moment capacity), which is about **1.10 to 1.18 for standard I-sections**.
>
> **(1 mark)** Because of this additional reserve, a **lower factor of safety** is justified for bending than for pure tension, leading to a numerically higher permissible bending stress. IS 800:2007 codifies this through different coefficients (0.66 vs 0.60).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of factor of safety. Discuss in detail how the magnitude of the factor of safety is decided for different materials and loading conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tie member in a roof truss carries an axial tensile load of **250 kN** under working (service) conditions. The member is fabricated from **mild steel (Fe 410)** with the following properties: yield stress $f_y = 250$ MPa, ultimate tensile stress $f_u = 410$ MPa. Using the Working Stress Method with a factor of safety of **1.78**, determine:
  (i) the permissible tensile stress, and  
  (ii) the required cross-sectional area of the tie. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Concept of Factor of Safety (3 marks):**

The Factor of Safety (FoS) is a numerical multiplier that converts the theoretical (laboratory-measured) strength of a material into a *safe working value*. It is defined as:

$$FoS = \frac{\text{Ultimate (or Yield) Stress}}{\text{Permissible Stress}}$$

The FoS accounts for **all uncertainties** in design, material, fabrication, and service conditions. It is a *bundle of sub-factors*, each addressing a specific source of uncertainty:

$$FoS_{overall} = FoS_1 \times FoS_2 \times FoS_3 \times \dots$$

**How FoS is decided (4 marks):**

The magnitude of FoS depends on:

1. **Type of material:**
   - **Ductile materials (mild steel, aluminium):** Lower FoS (~1.5 to 2.0) because they give visible warning before failure (large deformation).
   - **Brittle materials (cast iron, concrete):** Higher FoS (~3.0 to 4.0) because failure is sudden with no warning.

2. **Type of loading:**
   - **Dead load:** Low variability → Lower contribution to FoS.
   - **Live load:** Moderate variability → Moderate contribution.
   - **Wind, seismic, impact:** High variability → Largest contribution.

3. **Mode of failure:**
   - **Static failure:** Lower FoS.
   - **Fatigue failure:** Higher FoS (because cyclic loading causes progressive damage).

4. **Consequences of failure:**
   - **Critical structures (bridches, nuclear plants):** Higher FoS.
   - **Non-critical (partitions, temporary works):** Lower FoS.

5. **Reliability of analysis:**
   - If the structure is statically determinate and analysis is exact → Lower FoS.
   - If indeterminate with approximate analysis → Higher FoS.

6. **Code-specified values:** IS 800:2007 specifies $FoS = 1.67$ for steel yield and $1.78$ for ultimate in WSM.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given Data:**
- Axial tensile load: $P = 250$ kN $= 250 \times 10^3$ N
- Yield stress: $f_y = 250$ MPa $= 250$ N/mm²
- Ultimate stress: $f_u = 410$ MPa
- Factor of safety: $FoS = 1.78$

**(i) Permissible Tensile Stress (3 marks):**

Since mild steel is **ductile**, yielding governs (not rupture):

$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FoS} = \frac{250}{1.78}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{st} = 140.45 \text{ MPa}}$$

*Alternative cross-check:* Using IS 800 WSM formula directly:

$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{0.60 \times f_y}{\gamma_m} = \frac{0.60 \times 250}{1.10} = \frac{150}{1.10} \approx 136.36 \text{ MPa}$$

Both values are in the same ballpark — minor difference due to code convention.

**(ii) Required Cross-Sectional Area (4 marks):**

For equilibrium under axial tension:

$$\sigma_{actual} = \frac{P}{A_{req}} \leq \sigma_{st}$$

$$A_{req} \geq \frac{P}{\sigma_{st}}$$

Substituting values (keeping units consistent: $P$ in N, $\sigma$ in N/mm² → $A$ in mm²):

$$A_{req} = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{140.45}$$

$$A_{req} = 1779.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

Converting to cm²:

$$\boxed{A_{req} = 17.80 \text{ cm}^2}$$

**Conclusion:** A standard **ISA 80×50×8 mm** angle (gross area $\approx 9.9$ cm²) is insufficient; one would need **2 ISA 80×50×8 mm angles** placed back-to-back (total area $\approx 19.8$ cm²) to safely carry the 250 kN tension.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the various permissible stresses used in the design of steel structures as per IS 800:2007. Provide the expressions and physical significance of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported steel beam of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **30 kN/m** (working load, inclusive of self-weight). The beam is an **ISMB 450** section with section modulus $Z_{xx} = 1550.8$ cm³. Yield stress of steel is **250 MPa**. Using the Working Stress Method, check the adequacy of the beam in bending. Take permissible bending stress $\sigma_{sb} = 165$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**The four major permissible stresses in IS 800:2007 are:**

**(1) Permissible Tensile Stress — $\sigma_{st}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{0.60 \, f_y}{\gamma_m}$$

For $f_y = 250$ MPa, $\gamma_m = 1.10$:

$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{0.60 \times 250}{1.10} = 136.36 \text{ MPa} \approx 150 \text{ MPa (with FoS = 1.67)}$$

**Physical significance:** Used in axial tension members (ties, hangers, bottom chords of trusses) where the failure mode is yielding across the entire cross-section.

**(2) Permissible Compressive Stress — $\sigma_{sc}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_{sc} = \frac{0.60 \, f_y}{\gamma_m}$$

Numerically identical to $\sigma_{st}$ for short columns (slenderness ratio $< 12$). For **longer columns**, the Euler buckling stress becomes governing and $\sigma_{sc}$ is further reduced via the buckling class curves (a, b, c, d in IS 800).

**Physical significance:** Used for compression members (columns, struts, top chords of trusses) where the failure mode is either yielding (short) or buckling (long).

**(3) Permissible Bending Stress — $\sigma_{sb}$ (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{sb} = \frac{0.66 \, f_y}{\gamma_m} = \frac{0.66 \times 250}{1.10} = 150 \text{ MPa (approx. 165 MPa with FoS 1.51)}$$

**Physical significance:** Used in beams under flexure. The coefficient **0.66 > 0.60** reflects the **shape factor benefit** — reserve strength from interior fibres being below yield when extreme fibres reach yield.

**(4) Permissible Shear Stress — $\tau_{sv}$ (2 marks):**

$$\tau_{sv} = \frac{0.45 \, f_y}{\gamma_m} = \frac{0.45 \times 250}{1.10} = 102.27 \text{ MPa (approx. 110 MPa)}$$

**Physical significance:** Used for checking web shear in beams. The lower coefficient (0.45) is because shear failure is **brittle** — no warning, sudden yielding of the web.

**Summary Table (additional):**

| Stress Type | Coefficient on $f_y$ | Permissible Value (Fe250) |
|---|---|---|
| Tensile | 0.60 | 150 MPa |
| Compressive | 0.60 | 150 MPa |
| Bending | 0.66 | 165 MPa |
| Shear | 0.45 | 110 MPa |
| Bearing | 0.75 | 187.5 MPa |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- Span: $L = 6$ m
- UDL: $w = 30$ kN/m
- Section: ISMB 450, $Z_{xx} = 1550.8$ cm³
- Yield stress: $f_y = 250$ MPa
- Permissible bending stress: $\sigma_{sb} = 165$ MPa

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment for simply supported beam with UDL:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{30 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{30 \times 36}{8} = \frac{1080}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = 135 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to consistent units:**
$M_{max} = 135 \times 10^6$ N·mm
$Z_{xx} = 1550.8$ cm³ $= 1550.8 \times 10^3$ mm³

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{135 \times 10^6}{1550.8 \times 10^3}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 87.06 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 87.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with permissible stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = 87.06 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs} \quad \sigma_{sb} = 165 \text{ MPa}$$

Since **87.06 MPa < 165 MPa**, the beam is **SAFE in bending**.

**Step 5 — Compute the utilisation ratio:**

$$\text{Utilisation} = \frac{87.06}{165} \times 100\% = 52.76\%$$

The beam is operating at only **~53% of its capacity**, suggesting that a smaller section (e.g., ISMB 350 or ISMB 400) could potentially be more economical.

**Conclusion:** The ISMB 450 section is **adequate** (safe) for the given loading, with a comfortable margin of safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Forgetting to convert kN·m to N·mm and cm³ to mm³ gives answers off by a factor of **1000** — the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks in Q.B(b).
> 2. **Choosing the wrong permissible stress:** Using $\sigma_{st}$ instead of $\sigma_{sb}$ for bending → under-design → unsafe structure → **zero marks** on the safety check.
> 3. **Brittle vs Ductile confusion:** Using ultimate stress instead of yield stress for steel (or vice versa for cast iron) → wrong FoS → wrong permissible stress.
> 4. **Forgetting the partial safety factor:** Using $0.60 f_y$ directly as permissible stress (skipping $\gamma_m = 1.10$) → overestimates strength → unsafe design.
> 5. **Confusing WSM with LSM:** Mixing factored loads with WSM permissible stresses → incorrect and dangerous.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Permissible Stresses):**

- ✅ **Core Definition:** Permissible stress = Material strength ÷ Factor of Safety
- ✅ **Ductile materials** → Use **yield stress** $\sigma_y$ as the basis
- ✅ **Brittle materials** → Use **ultimate stress** $\sigma_u$ as the basis
- ✅ **Five key permissible stresses for steel (Fe250, IS 800):**
  - Tensile: **150 MPa** (0.60 f_y)
  - Compressive: **150 MPa** (0.60 f_y)
  - Bending: **165 MPa** (0.66 f_y)
  - Shear: **110 MPa** (0.45 f_y)
  - Bearing: **187.5 MPa** (0.75 f_y)
- ✅ **Permissible stress for concrete:** $\sigma_c = 0.67 f_{ck} / \gamma_c$
- ✅ **Shape factor** explains why $\sigma_{sb} > \sigma_{st}$
- ✅ **Single governing inequality:** $\sigma_{actual} \leq \sigma_{permissible}$
- ✅ **Combined loading:** Use **interaction formula** (e.g., Perry-Robertson for beam-columns)
- ✅ **In LSM**, permissible stress ≈ $f_y / \gamma_m$ — the numerical values are nearly identical to WSM
- ✅ **IS Code anchors:** IS 800:2007 (steel), IS 456:2000 (concrete), ASME BPVC (pressure vessels)
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** Always check **units** (N vs kN, mm² vs cm², MPa vs N/mm²), always check **failure mode**, always state **which IS clause** you are referring to.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Axially Loaded Tension Member
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Axially Loaded Tension Member

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Axially Loaded Tension Member:** A structural member subjected to a tensile force acting along its longitudinal centroidal axis, with the line of action of the load passing through the centroid of the cross-section, thereby producing **uniform tensile stress distribution** across the gross cross-sectional area.

In the context of **Limit State Design of Steel Structures (as per IS 800:2007)**, an axially loaded tension member is a straight structural element whose primary function is to resist pulling (tensile) axial forces without undergoing buckling. Typical examples include:

- **Tension chords** of roof trusses
- **Tie members** in bracing systems (cross bracing, longitudinal bracing)
- **Tension flanges** of plate girders (at the support region)
- **Cables** and **suspenders** in cable-stayed and suspension bridges
- **Tie rods** in industrial sheds and pre-engineered buildings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Focus:** The primary failure modes of a tension member that must be checked by a designer are:
> 1. **Yielding of Gross Section** (excessive elongation)
> 2. **Rupture of Net Section** (stress concentration at holes)
> 3. **Block Shear Failure** (combination of tension and shear at end connections)
> 4. **Slenderness Control** (to prevent excessive vibration/sag)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tug-of-War Rope" Model:**
> Imagine a group of people playing **tug-of-war** with a thick jute rope tied at both ends. Each fibre of the rope is pulling together along the same axis. As long as the rope's cross-section is intact, the entire cross-section shares the load equally (this is **gross section yielding**).
>
> Now, suppose we tie a knot in the middle of the rope for attaching a load. The knot creates a "weak spot" where some of the fibres are bent and displaced, reducing the effective fibres resisting tension at that cross-section (this is the **net section**).
>
> If we cut a small hole in the rope to pass a hook, the fibres at that hole are completely removed, again reducing the load-carrying area (**net section rupture**).
>
> If the rope is too long and thin, even when being pulled, it tends to swing like a pendulum or vibrate excessively, making it inefficient (**slenderness control**).
>
> Finally, if we cut a notch at the end of the rope to attach a clip, the notch may tear out a rectangular block of material under combined pull and shear (**block shear failure**).

The design of tension members is essentially a careful balancing act — we choose a **cross-section, a connection detail, and a length** such that the member safely resists the applied tensile load without failing in any of the above modes.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partial Safety Factor for Material | $\gamma_{m0}$ | **1.10** | For yielding (cl. 5.4.1, IS 800:2007) |
| Partial Safety Factor for Material | $\gamma_{m1}$ | **1.25** | For ultimate stress (rupture) |
| Partial Safety Factor for Material | $\gamma_{mb}$ | **1.25** | For bearing-type bolts |
| Modulus of Elasticity | $E$ | **200,000 N/mm²** or **200 GPa** | For structural steel |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.30** | For structural steel |
| Maximum Slenderness Ratio (Tension, Normal) | $\lambda_{max}$ | **350** | IS 800:2007, Table 3 |
| Maximum Slenderness Ratio (Tension, Subject to Reversal) | $\lambda_{max}$ | **250** | Prevents sag under self-weight reversal |
| Yield Stress (Fe 410) | $f_y$ | **250 N/mm²** | Mild steel designation |
| Yield Stress (Fe 250) | $f_y$ | **240 N/mm²** | Older designation |
| Ultimate Tensile Stress (Fe 410) | $f_u$ | **410 N/mm²** | Min. ultimate |

---

## 1.4 Failure Modes — Visual Schematic

> [!NOTE]
> A well-designed tension member must satisfy **ALL FOUR** failure modes simultaneously. The **smallest** of the four design strengths governs the member capacity.

$$
T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}, T_{db}, T_{d,\text{slenderness}})
$$

where each $T_{d}$ represents the design tensile strength against a particular failure mode.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophy of Design (IS 800:2007 Limit State Method)

The **Limit State Method** ensures the structure remains **fit for use** under all expected loads during its design life. For a tension member, two fundamental limit states apply:

- **Limit State of Serviceability (SLS):** Controls excessive deflection, vibration, and sag.
- **Limit State of Ultimate Strength (ULS):** Controls yielding, rupture, and block shear.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Failure Mode Analysis

### Failure Mode 1 — Yielding of Gross Section (Clause 6.1, IS 800:2007)

**Mechanism:** When the average tensile stress over the gross cross-section reaches the yield stress $f_y$, the member undergoes excessive inelastic elongation, rendering it unserviceable even though it has not fractured.

**Why this matters:** Once yielding occurs, the member cannot recover its original length upon unloading. In a statically determinate truss, this causes permanent sag. In an indeterminate structure, it causes force redistribution that may overload adjacent members.

**Design Criterion:**
$$
T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $A_g$ is the **gross cross-sectional area**, NOT reduced for holes. This is because yielding is a ductile phenomenon that allows load redistribution around stress concentrations.

---

### Failure Mode 2 — Rupture of Net Section (Clause 6.3, IS 800:2007)

**Mechanism:** At bolt holes, material is removed, and the stress concentration at the hole edge causes local fracture before the average stress on the net area reaches $f_u$. To account for this, IS 800 uses an empirical efficiency factor (the **shear lag factor**).

**Why this matters:** Failure at the net section is **brittle** (sudden, without warning). Designers must ensure the net section is sufficiently strong to carry the entire factored load.

**Design Criterion:**
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_n}{\gamma_{m1}}
$$

where:
- $0.9$ is the **shear lag factor** ($U$) for standard bolted connections in plates and rolled sections.
- $A_n$ is the **net effective area** (gross area minus area of bolt holes).
- $f_u$ is the **ultimate tensile strength** of the material.

**Net Area Calculation (Cl. 6.3.1):**
$$
A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_h \cdot t + \sum \frac{s^2 \cdot t}{4g}
$$

where:
- $n$ = number of bolts in the critical section
- $d_h$ = diameter of bolt hole = bolt diameter + **2 mm** (clearance, Table 11, IS 800)
- $t$ = thickness of plate
- $s$ = pitch of staggered bolts (longitudinal spacing)
- $g$ = gauge (transverse spacing between staggered rows)

---

### Failure Mode 3 — Block Shear Failure (Clause 6.4, IS 800:2007)

**Mechanism:** At the end of a tension member with bolted connections, a "block" of material can tear out along the shear planes parallel to the load and tension planes perpendicular to the load. This is a combined shear + tension failure.

**Why this matters:** Block shear is a frequent failure mode in **short connections** (e.g., end connections of gusset plates in trusses). It often governs over yielding in practical connections.

**Design Criterion:**
$$
T_{db} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y / \sqrt{3} + A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}
$$

**Alternate Criterion** (when net shear rupture controls):
$$
T_{db} = \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_{vn} + A_{tg} \cdot f_y / \sqrt{3}}{\gamma_{mb}}
$$

The **smaller** of these two governs.

**Block Shear Areas:**
- $A_{vg}$ = gross shear area along shear planes
- $A_{vn}$ = net shear area along shear planes (subtracting bolt holes)
- $A_{tg}$ = gross tension area perpendicular to load
- $A_{tn}$ = net tension area (subtracting bolt holes)

---

### Failure Mode 4 — Slenderness Control (Cl. 3.8, Table 3, IS 800:2007)

**Mechanism:** Even though tension members don't buckle, excessively slender members:
- Sag excessively under self-weight
- Vibrate in wind or dynamic loads
- Look aesthetically undesirable

**Design Criterion:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{L}{r} \leq \lambda_{max}
$$

| Member Type | $\lambda_{max}$ |
|---|---|
| Members normally in tension (no stress reversal) | **350** |
| Members subject to reversal of stress | **250** |
| Members pre-tensioned to avoid sag | **Lateral stiffness sufficient** |

where:
- $L$ = unsupported length
- $r$ = governing radius of gyration = $\sqrt{I/A}$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Design Parameter | Formula | Clause (IS 800) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Strength (Yielding) | $T_{dg} = \dfrac{A_g f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | 6.1 | N |
| 2 | Design Strength (Rupture) | $T_{dn} = \dfrac{0.9 f_u A_n}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | 6.3 | N |
| 3 | Design Strength (Block Shear I) | $T_{db1} = \dfrac{A_{vg} f_y / \sqrt{3} + A_{tn} f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | 6.4 | N |
| 4 | Design Strength (Block Shear II) | $T_{db2} = \dfrac{0.9 f_u A_{vn} + A_{tg} f_y / \sqrt{3}}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | 6.4 | N |
| 5 | Net Area (Staggered Holes) | $A_n = A_g - n d_h t + \sum \dfrac{s^2 t}{4g}$ | 6.3.1 | mm² |
| 6 | Bolt Hole Diameter | $d_h = d_b + 2$ mm | Table 11 | mm |
| 7 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L}{r}$ | 3.8 | — |
| 8 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | — | mm |
| 9 | Required Area | $A_{req} = \dfrac{T_u \gamma_{m0}}{f_y}$ | — | mm² |
| 10 | Bolt Hole Area | $A_{hole} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_h^2$ | — | mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL UNITS CONVERSION:** $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$. Always work in **N and mm** to avoid conversion errors. $1 \text{ kN} = 1000 \text{ N}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design of axially loaded tension members is the **backbone** of:
1. **Roof Trusses** in industrial buildings (factories, warehouses, aircraft hangars)
2. **Transmission Line Towers** (electricity grid infrastructure)
3. **Bridge Trusses** (railway and highway bridges)
4. **Offshore Platforms** (jacket structures for oil rigs)
5. **Crane Booms and Jibs** (lifting equipment)
6. **Cable-Supported Roofs** (stadiums, airports)

Failure of these members can lead to **catastrophic progressive collapse**, making their proper design essential for public safety.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Section Rupture Strength (Theoretical Basis)

### Starting Point: Stress Concentration at a Bolt Hole

Consider a flat plate of width $B$ and thickness $t$ with a single bolt hole of diameter $d_h$. The plate is subjected to uniform tensile load $T$.

**Step 1:** At a section passing through the hole, the resisting area is reduced.
$$
A_n = (B - d_h) \cdot t
$$

**Step 2:** The **average** stress on the net section is:
$$
\sigma_{avg} = \frac{T}{A_n}
$$

**Step 3:** However, due to **stress concentration**, the peak stress at the hole edge can be 2 to 3 times the average stress. The theoretical elastic stress concentration factor $K_t$ depends on the ratio $d_h/B$.

**Step 4:** In ductile steel, local yielding redistributes the stress, but the **effective** ultimate strength is reduced. IS 800 accounts for this with a shear lag factor $U = 0.9$ for typical plates.

**Step 5:** The ultimate tensile load on the net section is:
$$
T_{u,\text{net}} = 0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_n
$$

**Step 6:** Applying the partial safety factor $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$:
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_n}{\gamma_{m1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.9** is empirical. It represents the combined effect of stress concentration, shear lag (load not being uniformly distributed across the section), and strain hardening effects.

---

### Worked Example 1 — Single Row of Bolts

A flat plate of width $B = 100$ mm and thickness $t = 12$ mm has **3 bolts in a single row** at the end connection. Bolt diameter = 16 mm. Find the design tensile strength against rupture.

**Given:**
- $B = 100$ mm, $t = 12$ mm
- $n = 3$ bolts (in critical section)
- $d_b = 16$ mm

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Hole Diameter} \quad d_h &= d_b + 2 = 16 + 2 = 18 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Step 2: Net Area} \quad A_n &= (B - n \cdot d_h) \cdot t = (100 - 3 \cdot 18) \cdot 12 \\
A_n &= (100 - 54) \cdot 12 = 46 \cdot 12 = 552 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Step 3: For Fe 410 Steel} \quad f_u &= 410 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\text{Step 4: Design Strength} \quad T_{dn} &= \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_n}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.9 \cdot 410 \cdot 552}{1.25} \\
T_{dn} &= \frac{203,688}{1.25} = 162,950 \text{ N} \approx 162.95 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Staggered Holes (Critical Path Analysis)

### The Need for Path Analysis

When bolts are arranged in a **staggered pattern**, the failure path is not always a straight line across the critical section. The path may "zigzag" between bolts in different rows, picking up additional material.

### The Empirical Stagger Formula

For each staggered segment, the **net area is increased** by an amount:
$$
\Delta A = \frac{s^2 \cdot t}{4g}
$$

This is derived from the principle that the failure path follows an inclined plane, and the inclined length provides additional resistance compared to a straight transverse cut.

**Total Net Area (for the critical zigzag path):**
$$
A_n = A_g - \sum(n_i \cdot d_h \cdot t) + \sum \frac{s^2 \cdot t}{4g}
$$

---

### Worked Example 2 — Staggered Bolts Critical Path

A plate 200 mm × 10 mm has bolts of 18 mm diameter arranged in two rows. Row 1 has 2 bolts, Row 2 has 2 bolts. Pitch $p = 50$ mm, gauge $g = 40$ mm. Find the critical net area.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Gross Area} \quad A_g &= 200 \cdot 10 = 2000 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Step 2: Hole Diameter} \quad d_h &= 18 + 2 = 20 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Step 3: Direct Path (Row 1: 2 bolts)} \quad A_{n1} &= 2000 - (2)(20)(10) = 1600 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Step 4: Direct Path (Row 2: 2 bolts)} \quad A_{n2} &= 2000 - (2)(20)(10) = 1600 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Step 5: Zigzag Path} \quad A_{n3} &= 2000 - 2(20)(10) - 2(20)(10) + 2 \cdot \frac{50^2 \cdot 10}{4 \cdot 40} \\
A_{n3} &= 2000 - 400 - 400 + 2 \cdot \frac{2500 \cdot 10}{160} \\
A_{n3} &= 2000 - 800 + 312.5 = 1512.5 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Step 6: Critical (Minimum) Net Area} \quad A_n &= \min(1600, 1600, 1512.5) = 1512.5 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Block Shear Strength

### Mechanism

For an end connection with bolts, the "block" of material between the bolt line and the edge of the gusset plate may tear out. The block has:

- **Two shear planes** along the bolt lines (parallel to load) — area $A_v$
- **One tension plane** perpendicular to load — area $A_t$

### Design Formulas (IS 800 Cl. 6.4)

$$
T_{db1} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}} + A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}
$$

or

$$
T_{db2} = \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_{vn} + A_{tg} \cdot \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}}}{\gamma_{mb}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **factor $1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$** converts yield stress in tension to yield stress in shear (using von Mises criterion). The partial safety factor $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ applies to bolts and connections.

---

### Worked Example 3 — Block Shear Capacity

A tension member plate of width 120 mm, thickness 10 mm, is connected to a gusset by **2 bolts of 16 mm diameter** arranged in line (one column). Edge distance = 35 mm, bolt spacing = 60 mm. Check for block shear.

**Given:**
- Plate width $B = 120$ mm, thickness $t = 10$ mm
- $n = 2$ bolts (in shear plane)
- $d_h = 16 + 2 = 18$ mm
- Edge distance $e = 35$ mm
- Bolt pitch (length of tear block) $L_v = 60$ mm

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shear Plane Length (each side)} \quad L_v &= e + \text{pitch} - \frac{d_h}{2} \\
&\text{(Effective shear length = edge distance + half pitch)} \\
&\text{Two shear planes per block} \\
\\
\text{Gross Shear Area} \quad A_{vg} &= 2 \cdot L_v \cdot t = 2 \cdot (35 + 60) \cdot 10 \\
&\text{(Note: simplifying assumption)} \\
&\text{Take } L_v = 35 + 60 = 95 \text{ mm} \\
A_{vg} &= 2 \cdot 95 \cdot 10 = 1900 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\\
\text{Net Shear Area} \quad A_{vn} &= A_{vg} - n \cdot d_h \cdot t = 1900 - 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 10 \\
A_{vn} &= 1900 - 360 = 1540 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\\
\text{Gross Tension Area} \quad A_{tg} &= (\text{width across tension plane} - \text{no holes}) \cdot t \\
\text{Assume width} = 120 - 2(35) &= 50 \text{ mm (typical assumption)} \\
A_{tg} &= 50 \cdot 10 = 500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\\
\text{Net Tension Area} \quad A_{tn} &= A_{tg} - 0 = 500 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (no holes on tension plane)} \\
\\
\text{Trial 1: Gross Shear + Net Tension} \quad T_{db1} &= \frac{1900 \cdot \frac{250}{1.732} + 500 \cdot 410}{1.25} \\
T_{db1} &= \frac{1900 \cdot 144.34 + 205,000}{1.25} \\
T_{db1} &= \frac{274,246 + 205,000}{1.25} = \frac{479,246}{1.25} \\
T_{db1} &= 383,397 \text{ N} \approx 383.4 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Slenderness-Based Stiffness Check

For a tension member, slenderness doesn't cause collapse but governs **serviceability**.

$$
\lambda = \frac{L}{r}, \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}
$$

For a single angle section:
- $r_{min} = r_{vv}$ (about the weak axis, parallel to one leg)
- Use $L = L_{member}$ for the actual unsupported length

---

### Worked Example 4 — Complete Design of a Tension Member

**Problem:** Design a tension member of length 3.0 m to carry a factored tensile load of 250 kN. The member is connected to a 10 mm thick gusset plate using 18 mm diameter bolts (grade 4.6). Use Fe 410 steel.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Required Gross Area} \\
&A_{req} = \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y} = \frac{250 \cdot 10^3 \cdot 1.10}{250} = 1100 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Try a Single Angle ISA } 65 \times 65 \times 6 \text{ mm} \\
&A_g = 761 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (insufficient)} \\
&\text{Try } 75 \times 75 \times 8 \text{ mm:} \quad A_g = 1140 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \\
&\text{Select: ISA } 75 \times 75 \times 8 \text{ mm} \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 3: Check Yielding} \\
&T_{dg} = \frac{1140 \cdot 250}{1.10} = 259,091 \text{ N} = 259.1 \text{ kN} > 250 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 4: Design the Connection} \\
&\text{Shear capacity of 18 mm bolt (grade 4.6, double shear)} \\
&V_{dsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot (n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb}) / \gamma_{mb} \\
&\text{For 18 mm bolt: } A_{nb} = \frac{\pi}{4}(16)^2 = 201 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{201}{1.25} = 74,266 \text{ N} \approx 74.27 \text{ kN} \\
&\text{Number of bolts} = \lceil 250 / 74.27 \rceil = 4 \text{ bolts} \\
&\text{Provide 4 bolts in 2 rows × 2 columns} \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 5: Check Rupture at Net Section} \\
&d_h = 18 + 2 = 20 \text{ mm} \\
&\text{Critical section: 2 bolts at distance } 25 \text{ mm from edge} \\
&A_n = (75 - 0.5 \cdot 20) \cdot 8 - (20 \cdot 8) = 560 - 160 = 400 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&\text{(For angle: deduct one hole at gauge line)} \\
&T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot 410 \cdot 400}{1.25} = 118,080 \text{ N} = 118 \text{ kN} < 250 \text{ kN} \quad \times \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 6: Increase Member Size} \\
&\text{Try Double Angle 2L } 65 \times 65 \times 8 \text{ mm: } A_g = 2 \times 990 = 1980 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&\text{Now net section capacity:} \\
&A_n = 2 \times [990 - 0.5 \times 20 \times 8 - 20 \times 8] \approx 2 \times 400 = 800 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot 410 \cdot 800}{1.25} = 236,160 \text{ N} = 236 \text{ kN} < 250 \text{ kN} \\
&\text{Still insufficient — increase further} \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 7: Final Selection} \\
&\text{Try } 75 \times 75 \times 10 \text{ mm angle, single: } A_g = 1430 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&\text{For 4 bolts, net area calculation (proper):} \\
&A_n = (75 - 0.5 \cdot 20 - 20) \cdot 10 \text{ (approx)} = 450 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot 410 \cdot 450}{1.25} = 132,840 \text{ N} = 132.8 \text{ kN} \\
&\text{Increase to 6 bolts or use double angles} \\
\\
&\textbf{Final: Use Double Angle 2L } 75 \times 75 \times 8 \text{ mm, 6 bolts} \\
&A_g = 2 \cdot 1140 = 2280 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&r_{min} = 14.6 \text{ mm} \\
&\lambda = \frac{3000}{14.6} = 205 < 350 \quad \checkmark \\
&T_{dg} = \frac{2280 \cdot 250}{1.10} = 518 \text{ kN} > 250 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \\
&\textbf{Member is safe.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Decision Flow for Tension Member Design

| Step | Check | Formula | Action if Fails |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yielding of Gross Section | $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Increase cross-section |
| 2 | Rupture of Net Section | $T_{dn} = 0.9 f_u A_n / \gamma_{m1}$ | Reduce bolts per row, increase thickness |
| 3 | Block Shear | $T_{db}$ formulas | Increase edge distance or connection length |
| 4 | Slenderness | $\lambda = L/r \leq 350$ | Use thicker/bigger section for higher $r$ |
| 5 | Bolt Shear/Bearing | $V_{dsb}, V_{dpb}$ | Add more bolts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **design tensile strength** $T_d$ is the **MINIMUM** of all failure mode strengths. A member passes design only if $T_d \geq T_u$ (factored applied load).
