# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "effective length" of a column. How is it related to the actual length of the column?**

**Model Answer:**

Effective length of a column is the length of an equivalent pin-ended column that has the same buckling load as the given column with its actual end conditions. It is the distance between two successive points of zero bending moment (inflection points) on the buckled shape.

It is related to the actual (unsupported) length $L$ by:

$$L_e = K \cdot L$$

where $K$ is the **effective length factor**, which depends on the rotational and translational end restraints of the column. For an ideal pin-ended column, $K = 1.0$ and $L_e = L$. For a fixed-free column, $K = 2.0$ and $L_e = 2L$. The effective length is always greater than or equal to the actual length in most practical cases.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State the theoretical values of the effective length factor $K$ for the following end conditions: (i) Both ends pinned, (ii) Both ends fixed, (iii) One end fixed and the other end free.**

**Model Answer:**

The theoretical values of the effective length factor $K$ are:

| S.No. | End Conditions | Theoretical $K$ |
|:-----:|----------------|:--------------:|
| (i)   | Both ends pinned (hinged) | **1.0** |
| (ii)  | Both ends fixed | **0.5** |
| (iii) | One end fixed, other end free (cantilever) | **2.0** |

The values are derived from Euler's buckling theory, where the smallest root of the characteristic transcendental equation (depending on boundary conditions) gives the critical load. Since $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^{\,2}}$ and $L_e = K \cdot L$, the smallest root of the boundary-value problem yields $K = 1.0$, $0.5$, and $2.0$ for the three cases above respectively.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part B question offers a choice between two alternatives. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B, but not both.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for Euler's critical buckling load for a **column fixed at one end and free at the other**. Hence determine the **effective length factor $K$** for this case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **mild steel column** of length **5 m** is **effectively held in position at both ends** but **not restrained against rotation**. The cross-section is **ISMB 300** (I-section). Find the **safe axial load** the column can carry. Use the following data: $A = 4761 \text{ mm}^2$, $I_{xx} = 7.327 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$, $I_{yy} = 3.103 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$, $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\sigma_{ac} = 130 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Setup:** Column of length $L$ fixed at bottom ($x = 0$) and free at top ($x = L$). Axial compressive load $P$ applied at the free end.

**Governing Equation:** (Full derivation provided in Section 3.1)

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P y = P \delta$$

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. The general solution is:

$$y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx) + \delta$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $y(0) = 0 \Rightarrow B = -\delta$

At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $y'(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0$

Therefore: $y(x) = \delta [1 - \cos(kx)]$

At $x = L$ (free end): $M(L) = 0 \Rightarrow y(L) = \delta$

Substituting: $\delta [1 - \cos(kL)] = \delta \Rightarrow \cos(kL) = 0$

Smallest root: $kL = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$

**Effective Length Factor:** Comparing with $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^{\,2}}$:

$$L_e^{\,2} = 4L^2 \Rightarrow L_e = 2L \Rightarrow \boxed{K = 2.0}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify end conditions:** "Effectively held in position at both ends but not restrained against rotation" implies **both ends pinned** (rotational restraint absent, lateral translation prevented).

Per IS 800 Table 11, recommended design $K = 1.0$.

**Step 2 — Effective length:**

$$L_e = K \cdot L = 1.0 \times 5.0 = 5.0 \text{ m} = 5000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Buckling about weak axis (yy-axis):** I-section is more slender about the yy-axis (lower $I_{yy}$), so buckling occurs about this axis.

$$r_{yy} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{yy}}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.103 \times 10^6}{4761}} = \sqrt{651.75} = 25.53 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r_{yy}} = \dfrac{5000}{25.53} = 195.85$$

Since $\lambda$ is very high, Euler's formula applies.

**Step 5 — Euler's critical load:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{yy}}{L_e^{\,2}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (3.103 \times 10^6)}{(5000)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 6.206 \times 10^{11}}{2.5 \times 10^7} = 244.97 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Safe load (Limit State Method):**

$$P_d = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{244.97}{1.10} = 222.7 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{d} \approx 222.7 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **effective length** for columns in a **framed structure**. Differentiate between **braced (non-sway)** and **unbraced (sway)** frames with respect to effective length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The effective length of a **timber column** in a building is **3.5 m**. The column is **circular in cross-section** with **diameter 150 mm**. Both ends are **fixed**. The timber has $E = 1.2 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2$ and the **allowable compressive stress parallel to grain** is $\sigma_{ac} = 10 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Determine: (i) Euler's critical buckling load, (ii) the safe load on the column. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Concept of Effective Length in Frames:**

In a real building frame, columns are not isolated members — they are interconnected with beams at the floor levels. The rotational restraint offered by the connecting beams at the column ends modifies the **rotational stiffness** of the column, which in turn alters its effective length factor $K$. As a result, the effective length of a column in a frame is not simply a function of its own end conditions, but also depends on the **relative stiffness of the beams framing into the column** at both ends.

**Stiffness Ratio (G):** The alignment chart uses the dimensionless ratio:

$$G = \dfrac{\sum (E_c I_c / L_c)}{\sum (E_b I_b / L_b)}$$

where the summation runs over all columns and beams meeting at a joint.

**Braced vs. Unbraced Frames:**

| Aspect | Braced (Non-Sway) Frame | Unbraced (Sway) Frame |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------------|
| Lateral translation at column tops | **Prevented** by braces, shear walls, or floor diaphragm | **Permitted** — column tops translate |
| Effective length factor $K$ | **Smaller** ($K \leq 1.0$) | **Larger** ($K > 1.0$, often $\geq 1.2$) |
| Buckling load | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| Design | More economical | Conservative design required |
| Alignment chart | Side-sway **inhibited** branch | Side-sway **permitted** branch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** In an unbraced frame, the column buckles in a **sidesway mode** where both ends translate laterally. This mode is much more flexible than in-brace buckling, leading to much larger effective lengths (often $K > 1.5$).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Cross-section properties (circular column, $D = 150$ mm):**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (150)^2}{4} = 17671.46 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi (150)^4}{64} = 2.485 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.485 \times 10^7}{17671.46}} = \sqrt{1406.25} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Effective length (both ends fixed, IS 800 recommended $K = 0.65$):**

$$L_e = K \cdot L = 0.65 \times 3.5 = 2.275 \text{ m} = 2275 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r} = \dfrac{2275}{37.5} = 60.67$$

**Step 4 — Euler's critical load:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times (1.2 \times 10^4) \times (2.485 \times 10^7)}{(2275)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^4 \times 2.485 \times 10^7}{5.176 \times 10^6}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.943 \times 10^{12}}{5.176 \times 10^6} = 5.685 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 568.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Safe load:**

Material capacity:

$$P_{mat} = \sigma_{ac} \cdot A = 10 \times 17671.46 = 1.767 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 176.7 \text{ kN}$$

Since $P_{mat} = 176.7 \text{ kN} \ll P_{cr} = 568.5 \text{ kN}$, the column is **NOT** a long column — the safe load is governed by **material crushing**.

$$\boxed{P_{safe} = 176.7 \text{ kN} \text{ (governed by material strength)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Insight:** Always compare Euler's buckling load with the material yield capacity. The **smaller** of the two governs the design. This comparison is the hallmark of a well-answered KTU design question.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Using the theoretical $K$ instead of the IS-recommended design $K$.** The IS code (Table 11 of IS 800) provides **slightly higher design values** to account for imperfections. Using $K = 0.5$ for a fixed-fixed column (instead of $K = 0.65$) is a frequent error leading to a non-conservative design — and a **penalty of up to 2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing actual length $L$ with effective length $L_e$.** Always report both values explicitly in your answer.
>
> 3. **Buckling about the wrong axis.** For an I-section, the **yy-axis (minor axis)** almost always governs because $I_{yy} \ll I_{xx}$. Use $I_{yy}$ and $r_{yy}$, not $I_{xx}$ and $r_{xx}$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to apply the partial safety factor $\gamma_{m0}$.** Euler's critical load $P_{cr}$ must be divided by $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ to obtain the **design load $P_d$**.
>
> 5. **Not checking if Euler's formula is applicable.** Euler's formula is valid only for **long columns** ($\lambda \geq \lambda_{critical}$). For short columns, the safe load is governed by **material crushing**, not buckling.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong units.** A common pitfall is mixing $L$ in meters with $I$ in mm⁴ and $E$ in N/mm². Convert all lengths to **mm** before substituting into the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 4: Effective Length**

- ✅ **Effective length $L_e$** is the length of an equivalent pin-ended column with the same buckling load.
- ✅ **Fundamental relation:** $L_e = K \cdot L$, where $K$ is the **effective length factor** (dimensionless).
- ✅ **Euler's critical load (generalized):** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$.
- ✅ **Theoretical $K$ values:** Pin-Pin = 1.0, Fixed-Fixed = 0.5, Fixed-Free = 2.0, Fixed-Pin = 0.7, Fixed-Guide = 1.0.
- ✅ **IS 800 Recommended Design $K$ values:** Pin-Pin = 1.0, Fixed-Fixed = **0.65**, Fixed-Free = **2.0**, Fixed-Pin = **0.80**, Fixed-Guide = **1.20**.
- ✅ **Slenderness ratio:** $\lambda = L_e / r = (K \cdot L) / r$.
- ✅ **Radius of gyration:** $r = \sqrt{I/A}$.
- ✅ **Frame classification:** Braced (non-sway) → smaller $K$; Unbraced (sway) → larger $K$.
- ✅ **Alignment chart inputs:** Stiffness ratio $G = \dfrac{\sum (E_c I_c / L_c)}{\sum (E_b I_b / L_b)}$.
- ✅ **Buckling about weak axis** (lower $I$) **governs** for unsymmetric sections like I-beams.
- ✅ **Design load:** $P_d = P_{cr} / \gamma_{m0}$ where $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ per IS 800.
- ✅ **Long vs. short columns:** If Euler's load < material yield load → long column (buckling governs); else → short column (yielding governs).
- ✅ **Quadratic effect:** Since $P_{cr} \propto 1/L_e^{\,2}$, a 10% error in $L_e$ produces ~19% error in buckling load.
- ✅ **Practical design rule:** Always use the **higher** of theoretical $K$ and the **recommended design $K$** from IS 800 Table 11 for safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Slenderness Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maximum Slenderness Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Slenderness Ratio (λ_max)** is the *highest permissible value* of the ratio of the **effective length** of a compression member to its **appropriate radius of gyration**, as stipulated by the relevant design code, beyond which the member becomes disproportionately vulnerable to **buckling failure** under axial compressive loading.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{KL}{r_{min}}$$

Where:
- $K$ = Effective length factor (depends on end conditions)
- $L$ = Unsupported (actual) length of the member
- $r_{min}$ = Minimum radius of gyration of the cross-section

> [!NOTE]
> According to **IS 800:2007** (Clause 7.1.2.1 and 3.8), the **maximum slenderness ratio** for a **compression member in steel** is limited to **180**. For a tension member, it is **400**, and for a member subjected to reversal of stress during erection, it is **350**.

According to **IS 456:2000** (Clause 25.3.2 and 25.3.3), the slenderness limits for **reinforced concrete columns** are governed by the ratio of the unsupported length to the least lateral dimension.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a **thin, long, dried bamboo stick** vertically and pressing down on its top with your palm. You will feel it **wobble and snap sideways** with relatively little effort. Now, take a **short, thick piece of hardwood** of the same length and press down. It barely deflects, even under significant force.

The bamboo stick failed because it was *too slender* — its length was disproportionately large compared to its thickness. In engineering terms, its **slenderness ratio was too high**.

Similarly, a compression member (column) in a building carries the load of the floors above. If the column is too long and too thin, it tends to **buckle sideways** rather than crush. Codes therefore impose a **ceiling (maximum value)** on the slenderness ratio to prevent such catastrophic instability.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"The taller and thinner the column, the more it wants to bend — so the code says: 'not too tall, not too thin!'"*

## 1.3 Key Physical & Geometric Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unsupported Length | $L$ | mm | Actual clear distance between supports |
| Effective Length | $KL$ | mm | Length that *behaves* like an equivalent pin-ended column |
| Radius of Gyration | $r$ | mm | $\sqrt{I/A}$; measure of cross-section's resistance to buckling |
| Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda$ | Dimensionless | $KL/r$; index of buckling vulnerability |
| Maximum Permissible | $\lambda_{max}$ | Dimensionless | Code-defined upper safety threshold |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background: Why a Limit Exists

The phenomenon of **buckling** is a stability issue distinct from crushing. As per **Euler's theory (1744)**, a perfectly straight, pin-ended, axially loaded column fails by lateral elastic buckling at a critical load:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$

Critical buckling stress becomes:

$$f_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Key Insight:** As $\lambda$ *increases*, $f_{cr}$ *decreases* following an **inverse-square law**. Beyond a code-defined threshold, the member cannot be guaranteed to resist even a fraction of the design load safely — hence a **mandatory upper limit** on $\lambda$ is enforced.

## 2.2 Classification of Columns Based on Slenderness

| Column Type | Slenderness Range | Failure Mode | Applicable Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stocky (Short)** | $\lambda < 12$ (RC) / very low | Pure crushing | No buckling considered |
| **Short** | $12 \le \lambda \le 60$ (RC) | Combined crushing + secondary moment | Empirical curves (IS 456) |
| **Medium** | $60 < \lambda \le 180$ | Inelastic / Elastic buckling | IS 800 multiple curves |
| **Long / Slender** | $\lambda$ near **180** | Pure elastic Euler buckling | Euler's theory |

## 2.3 Effective Length Factor (K) — End Condition Chart

| End Condition | Theoretical K | Practical K (IS 800) |
|---|---|---|
| Both ends pinned | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| One end fixed, other free (cantilever) | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| Both ends fixed | 0.5 | 0.65 |
| One end fixed, other pinned | 0.7 | 0.80 |
| One end fixed, other guided (translation prevented) | 1.0 | 1.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 800:2007 Table 11** provides recommended K-values accounting for partial restraints in real framed structures. The **effective length** in such frames is given by $L_{eff} = K L$, where $L$ is the unsupported length measured center-to-center of restraints.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for KTU Board Examination on Maximum Slenderness Ratio.**

| # | Formula | Description | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$ | Definition of slenderness ratio | Fundamental |
| 2 | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of gyration | Fundamental |
| 3 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_{eff}^{2}}$ | Euler's critical buckling load | Euler (1759) |
| 4 | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Euler critical stress | Derived |
| 5 | $\lambda_{max} = 180$ (Steel compression) | IS 800 max limit | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2.1 |
| 6 | $\lambda_{max} = 400$ (Steel tension) | IS 800 tension limit | IS 800 Cl. 3.8 |
| 7 | $\dfrac{L_{eff}}{D} \le 12$ (RC short column, braced) | RC column minimum dimension check | IS 456 Cl. 25.1.2 |
| 8 | $L \le 60 b$ (RC braced, no lateral movement) | Unsupported length to least lateral dim. | IS 456 Cl. 25.3.1 |
| 9 | $L \le 30 b$ (RC unbraced, lateral sway possible) | Unsupported length to least lateral dim. | IS 456 Cl. 25.3.1 |
| 10 | $L_{eff} = 0.65 L$ to $2.0 L$ | Effective length for framed columns | IS 456 / IS 800 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The maximum slenderness ratio concept governs the design of:
- **Building columns** in multi-storey RCC and steel frames
- **Truss compression chords** (e.g., top chord of industrial truss roof)
- **Bridge piers** and **tower legs**
- **Tall sign posts**, **flag poles**, **transmission towers**
- **Struts in scaffolding** and **formwork systems**

If a designer specifies a member with $\lambda > \lambda_{max}$, the structure is *deemed unsafe* under code regulations — leading to **rejection of design submission** by the Structural Reviewer or Building Authority.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Critical Slenderness for Transition between Crushing and Buckling (IS 800)

In **IS 800:2007**, the limiting slenderness ratio $\lambda_{lim}$ separates the **crushing-governed regime** from the **buckling-governed regime**. It is the slenderness at which the Euler critical stress equals the material yield stress $f_y$.

**Step 1: Equate Euler stress to yield stress**

$$f_{cr} = f_{y}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Euler stress expression**

$$\frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda_{lim}^{2}} = f_{y}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\lambda_{lim}$**

$$\lambda_{lim}^{2} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{f_{y}}$$

**Step 4: Take square root**

$$\lambda_{lim} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{f_{y}}}$$

**Step 5: For E = 200,000 N/mm² (steel) and f_y = 250 N/mm² (Fe 410 grade)**

$$\lambda_{lim} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{200{,}000}{250}} = \pi \sqrt{800}$$

$$\lambda_{lim} = \pi \times 28.2843 = 88.86 \approx 88.86$$

**Step 6: Rounded value per IS 800**

$$\lambda_{lim} \approx 89.9 \;\; (\text{practically taken as } 90)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For $\lambda < 90$ (Fe 410 steel):** governed by yielding/crushing. For $\lambda > 90$:** governed by elastic/inelastic buckling (use buckling curve 'a/b/c/d' of IS 800). The maximum permissible is always capped at **180**.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Maximum Permissible Unsupported Length for an RC Column (IS 456)

**Step 1: Recall the IS 456 slenderness limit for a braced column without lateral sway**

$$\frac{L}{b} \le 60$$

Where $L$ is the unsupported length and $b$ is the least lateral dimension.

**Step 2: For an unbraced column (lateral sway permitted), the limit is stricter**

$$\frac{L}{b} \le 30$$

**Step 3: If effective length $L_{eff} = L$ (pinned ends) and radius of gyration $r = b/\sqrt{12}$**

$$\lambda = \frac{L}{r} = \frac{L \sqrt{12}}{b}$$

**Step 4: Substituting $L/b = 60$ (braced case)**

$$\lambda = 60 \times \sqrt{12} = 60 \times 3.4641 = 207.85$$

This is why **IS 456** does *not* use the term "slenderness ratio = 180" — instead, it controls slenderness *indirectly* via the **L/b ratio**, which translates to an effective $\lambda$ of about 208 for the worst braced case.

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A steel column of length **3.5 m** has both ends **pinned**. The cross-section is a **ISMB 300** with $r_{min} = 26.0$ mm. The column is subjected to axial compression. **Check whether the slenderness ratio satisfies IS 800:2007 limits.**

**Step 1: Identify the effective length factor K**

For both ends pinned (theoretical), $K = 1.0$ (per IS 800 Table 11).

**Step 2: Compute effective length**

$$L_{eff} = K \times L = 1.0 \times 3500 \;\; \text{mm} = 3500 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3: Apply the slenderness ratio definition**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} = \frac{3500}{26.0}$$

$$\lambda = 134.62$$

**Step 4: Compare with IS 800 limit**

$$\lambda = 134.62 \;\; \lt \;\; \lambda_{max} = 180 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Classify the column**

Since $\lambda = 134.62 > 90$ (the limiting slenderness for Fe 410), the column is **slender** and **buckling-governed**. The design must use the **buckling curve** (column curve) approach of **IS 800 Cl. 7.1.4.2** with design compressive stress $f_{cd}$ obtained from Table 9.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification trick:** If the column passed both $\lambda < 180$ *and* crushing strength checks, it is safe for axial compression under IS 800.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: IS 800 vs IS 456 Slenderness

| Aspect | IS 800:2007 (Steel) | IS 456:2000 (Concrete) |
|---|---|---|
| Governing parameter | $\lambda = KL/r$ | $L/b$ (L to least lateral dim.) |
| Maximum permitted | 180 (compression) | 60 (braced) / 30 (unbraced) |
| Equivalent $\lambda$ | Direct | $\approx 208$ (braced) / $\approx 104$ (unbraced) |
| Failure theory | Euler + Perry-Robertson | Empirical + minimum eccentricity |
| Code clause | 7.1.2.1 | 25.3.1, 25.3.2 |
| Application range | Trusses, building frames, towers | RCC columns in buildings, bridges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram: Slenderness Ratio Decision Tree (IS 800)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Compression Member] --> B[Determine Unsupported Length L]
    B --> C[Identify End Conditions]
    C --> D[Select Effective Length Factor K]
    D --> E[Compute Effective Length KL]
    E --> F[Determine r_min from Section Properties]
    F --> G[Calculate Slenderness Ratio lambda]
    G --> H{Is lambda less than 180?}
    H -- Yes --> I{Lambda less than 90?}
    H -- No --> J[STOP: Redesign Section or Reduce Length]
    I -- Yes --> K[Crushing Governed - Check Yielding]
    I -- No --> L[Buckling Governed - Use Column Curve]
    K --> M[Check Cross-Section Strength]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Design Satisfies IS 800]
    J --> O[Increase Section Size or Add Bracing]
    O --> D
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Effective Length Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input: Column End Conditions] --> B1[Pin-Pin: K=1.0]
    A1 --> C1[Fixed-Fixed: K=0.65]
    A1 --> D1[Fixed-Pin: K=0.80]
    A1 --> E1[Fixed-Free: K=2.0]
    B1 --> F1[Effective Length KL]
    C1 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Radius of Gyration r_min]
    G1 --> H1[Slenderness Ratio lambda]
    H1 --> I1{Compare to Code Limit}
    I1 --> J1[Acceptable Design]
    I1 --> K1[Modification Required]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Column Behaviour Under Increasing Slenderness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stocky Column lambda less than 12] --> S1A[Crushes Under Load]
    S2[Short Column lambda 12 to 60] --> S2A[Crushes with Secondary Moment]
    S3[Medium Column lambda 60 to 90] --> S3A[Inelastic Buckling Failure]
    S4[Long Column lambda 90 to 180] --> S4A[Elastic Euler Buckling]
    S5[Beyond 180] --> S5A[Code Violation - Unsafe]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above flowcharts represent the **decision logic** the examiner expects a student to articulate when solving a "check for slenderness" problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Maximum Slenderness Ratio" as applied to a steel compression member. State the maximum permissible value as per IS 800:2007.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Maximum Slenderness Ratio** of a compression member is the *highest permissible* value of the ratio of its **effective length (KL)** to its **minimum radius of gyration (r_min)**, beyond which the member is considered too slender to be used safely in a structure.

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{KL}{r_{min}}$$

As per **Clause 7.1.2.1 of IS 800:2007**, the **maximum slenderness ratio** for a **steel compression member** is restricted to **180**, and for a **tension member** it is **400**.

*(1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for code clause & value.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between "effective length" and "actual length" of a column. How is the effective length factor K determined?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Actual Length (L) | Effective Length (KL) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Physical clear distance between supports | Length that *behaves* as an equivalent pin-ended column |
| Determinant | Geometric, measured on site | Theoretical, depends on end restraints |
| Numerical value | Equals L always | $K \times L$ where K is between 0.5 and 2.0 |

The **effective length factor K** is determined from the **end boundary conditions** of the column as per **IS 800 Table 11** (or **IS 456 Table E-1** for RC columns). It accounts for rotational and translational restraints provided by the adjoining members/connections.

*(1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for K value range, 1 mark for code reference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** A steel column 4.0 m long is **effectively held in position at both ends but not restrained against rotation**. It is built-up of two ISMC 250 channels placed back-to-back with a 200 mm clear gap, connected by lacing. Compute the **maximum slenderness ratio** of the column. Take: $r_{min} = 27.4$ mm (about the axis parallel to webs). Is the column safe as per IS 800:2007? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **physical significance of maximum slenderness ratio** in the design of compression members. Why is the limit stricter for **braced** columns compared to **unbraced** columns in IS 456:2000? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Identify end conditions and K factor**

For "held in position at both ends but not restrained against rotation" — this corresponds to **one end fixed, other pinned** condition. From IS 800 Table 11:

$$K = 0.80$$

**Step 2: Compute effective length**

$$L_{eff} = K \times L = 0.80 \times 4000 \;\; \text{mm} = 3200 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute slenderness ratio**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} = \frac{3200}{27.4} = 116.79$$

**Step 4: Compare with IS 800 limit**

$$\lambda = 116.79 \;\; < \;\; \lambda_{max} = 180$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**

✅ The column **satisfies** the slenderness limit. However, since $\lambda > 90$, the column is **buckling-governed**, and the design compressive stress must be obtained from the **column buckling curve** of IS 800 Table 9.

*(1 mark for K factor, 1 mark for L_eff, 1 mark for lambda computation, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for classification, 2 marks for conclusion and additional design note.)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Physical Significance of Maximum Slenderness Ratio (4 marks):**

1. **Buckling Prevention:** A compression member subjected to axial load tends to fail by lateral buckling. The slenderness ratio $\lambda$ is an *index* of this vulnerability. As $\lambda$ increases, the critical buckling load decreases following Euler's inverse-square law.

2. **Safety Threshold:** The code-imposed maximum $\lambda_{max} = 180$ (IS 800) ensures the member has *adequate* lateral stiffness to resist *unintended* lateral deflections, accidental eccentricities, and material imperfections.

3. **Serviceability & Stability:** It prevents excessive lateral vibration, drift, and second-order (P-Delta) effects, especially in multi-storey frames.

4. **Economic Design:** Capping $\lambda$ forces the designer to choose *deeper or stiffer* sections or add **lateral bracing**, preventing use of impractically slender members.

**Why IS 456 Limit is Stricter for Unbraced Columns (3 marks):**

In **unbraced (sway) frames**, the entire column is susceptible to **sidesway buckling** under lateral loads (wind/seismic). The unsupported length effectively amplifies because lateral translation is added to column bending. Hence IS 456 specifies:
- **Braced column:** $L/b \le 60$ (translation prevented at both ends)
- **Unbraced column:** $L/b \le 30$ (translation may occur; second-order effects are more severe)

The stricter limit for unbraced columns **ensures stability against lateral sway buckling** and prevents P-Delta collapse.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** A **reinforced concrete column** has unsupported length of 6.0 m and cross-section 300 mm × 450 mm. Both ends are restrained against translation but **rotation is not fully prevented**. Using IS 456:2000 guidelines, **check the slenderness** of the column assuming it is **(i) braced** and **(ii) unbraced** against sideway. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **neat sketch or flow diagram**, describe the **step-by-step procedure** for checking the slenderness of a steel column as per IS 800:2007. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Unsupported length, $L = 6.0$ m = 6000 mm
- Cross-section: $b = 300$ mm (least lateral), $D = 450$ mm

**Case (i): Braced Column (No Sideway)**

Per **IS 456 Cl. 25.3.1**, for a braced column:

$$\frac{L}{b} \le 60$$

$$\frac{6000}{300} = 20 \le 60 \;\; \checkmark$$

✅ The column is **safe and can be designed as a short column** (no slenderness reduction required).

**Case (ii): Unbraced Column (Sideway Permitted)**

Per **IS 456 Cl. 25.3.1**, for an unbraced column:

$$\frac{L}{b} \le 30$$

$$\frac{6000}{300} = 20 \le 30 \;\; \checkmark$$

✅ The column also satisfies the unbraced limit, **but additional moments due to slenderness (minimum eccentricity)** must be considered in the design per **IS 456 Cl. 25.4**.

**Conclusion:**
In both cases the column passes, but in Case (ii) the designer must:
- Apply the **minimum eccentricity** of $e_{min} = \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \ge 20$ mm
- Design the section for **axial load + additional bending moment**

*(1 mark for given data, 1 mark for braced check, 1 mark for unbraced check, 2 marks for conclusion, 2 marks for additional design consideration.)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Step-by-Step Procedure for Checking Slenderness of a Steel Column (IS 800:2007):**

| Step | Action | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the compression member and its **unsupported length L** | From structural drawing |
| 2 | Determine the **end conditions** at both ends (pinned/fixed/free) | Site / design assumption |
| 3 | Select the **effective length factor K** from IS 800 Table 11 | Table 11 |
| 4 | Compute **effective length**: $L_{eff} = K \times L$ | Cl. 7.1.2.1 |
| 5 | Calculate the **minimum radius of gyration** $r_{min}$ from section tables | IS 808 / SP 6 |
| 6 | Compute **slenderness ratio**: $\lambda = L_{eff}/r_{min}$ | Cl. 7.1.2.1 |
| 7 | **Compare** with $\lambda_{max} = 180$. If exceeded → **redesign** | Cl. 7.1.2.1 |
| 8 | **Classify** the column: $\lambda < 90$ (crushing) or $\lambda > 90$ (buckling) | Cl. 7.1.4.1 |
| 9 | Obtain the **design compressive stress $f_{cd}$** from buckling curves | Table 9(a)/(b)/(c)/(d) |
| 10 | Apply **safety check**: $P_d \le A_g \times f_{cd}$ / $\gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 7.1.4.2 |

*(Each step ≈ 0.7 marks; neat sketch adds 0.5 marks; logical flow adds 0.5 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing tension vs compression limits:** Writing 400 instead of 180 for a *compression* member. (Costs 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the K factor:** Using $L$ directly instead of $KL$ in the slenderness formula. (Costs 1-2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing IS 800 and IS 456 clauses:** Citing wrong code reference. (Costs 0.5-1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring minimum eccentricity for RC:** Failing to mention $e_{min}$ for slender RC columns. (Costs 1-2 marks)
> 5. **Skipping the $\lambda_{lim} = 90$ classification:** This is a frequent KTU question and missing it loses easy marks.
> 6. **Not specifying units:** Slenderness ratio is dimensionless, but length/radius must be in **same units** (preferably mm).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r_{min}}$ — the index of buckling vulnerability
- ⭐ **IS 800:2007 — Maximum Slenderness Ratio Limits:**
   - Compression members: **180**
   - Tension members: **400**
   - Members under stress reversal during erection: **350**
- ⭐ **IS 456:2000 — L/b limits:**
   - Braced column: **60**
   - Unbraced column: **30**
- ⭐ **K factors (memorize the table):** Pin-Pin = 1.0, Fixed-Fixed = 0.65, Fixed-Pin = 0.80, Fixed-Free = 2.0
- ⭐ **Limiting slenderness for Fe 410 steel:** $\lambda_{lim} \approx 90$ (separates crushing vs buckling regimes)
- ⭐ **Euler's formula:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^{2}}$ — inversely proportional to square of slenderness
- ⭐ **Radius of gyration:** $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ — always use the **least** value for compression members
- ⭐ **Practical design tip:** If $\lambda$ exceeds 180, **either** increase section size **or** add lateral bracing — do not "accept" the design
- ⭐ **RC column slenderness:** Always check both braced and unbraced conditions; apply minimum eccentricity $e_{min}$ if slender
- ⭐ **Common KTU Board question pattern:** "Check slenderness" or "Classify the column" — these are 7-14 mark standard questions
- ⭐ **Units consistency:** Express $L$ and $r_{min}$ in the **same units** (mm recommended) to avoid dimensional errors

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beams: Design Procedure for Laterally Supported and Unsupported Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beams: Design Procedure for Laterally Supported and Unsupported Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Beam Classification

In steel structural design (governed by **IS 800:2007** and aligned with **IS 800:2024** standards), beams are broadly classified based on their **lateral restraint conditions** along the span. This classification is fundamental because it directly dictates the **mode of failure**, the **design check equations**, and the **safety philosophy** applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The lateral support condition of a beam is the single most critical parameter that determines whether a beam will fail by **yielding (plastic/compact section)** or by **lateral-torsional buckling (LTB)**.

### Formal Definitions

**1. Laterally Supported Beam (Lateral Restraint Provided)**

A beam is termed *laterally supported* when its **compression flange** is prevented from moving laterally (sideways) at sufficiently close intervals along the span. The lateral restraint is typically provided by:
- Floor slabs (RCC/steel decking) connected to the top flange
- Purlins connected through sheeting
- Secondary beams framing into the primary beam
- Bracing systems (plan bracing, sag rods)

$$ \text{Spacing of lateral restraint} \leq 0.4 L \quad \text{(for simply supported conditions)} $$

**2. Laterally Unsupported Beam (Lateral Restraint Not Provided)**

A beam is termed *laterally unsupported* when its compression flange is **free to move laterally** over the entire span (or a major portion of it). Such beams are susceptible to **lateral-torsional buckling (LTB)** — a failure mode combining lateral deflection and twisting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compactness Concept:** Before lateral support conditions are considered, a beam cross-section must first be classified as **Plastic, Compact, Semi-Compact, or Slender** based on flange and web slenderness ratios ($\lambda_f$, $\lambda_w$). This classification is independent of the lateral support condition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ruler on a Table"

Imagine a thin steel ruler held horizontally on the tip of your finger:

- **Laterally Supported Scenario:** Now press the top edge of the ruler down at multiple points along its length (simulating purlins holding the top flange). The ruler bends in pure **flexure** — failure is by yielding of the material. This is the **laterally supported case**.

- **Laterally Unsupported Scenario:** Now press down on the ruler with no lateral support along its length. The ruler does not just bend downward — it **bows sideways AND twists** out of the plane. This 3D failure is **lateral-torsional buckling (LTB)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** A typical roof purlin connected to corrugated metal sheeting acts as a **laterally supported** beam. However, a gantry girder spanning between crane columns (where no effective lateral support exists between the columns) acts as a **laterally unsupported** beam requiring LTB checks.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (IS 800:2007)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Partial Safety Factor for Material ($\gamma_{m0}$) | **1.10** |
| Partial Safety Factor for Steel ($\gamma_{m1}$) | **1.25** |
| Modulus of Elasticity ($E$) | **200,000 N/mm² = 2 × 10⁵ MPa** |
| Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) | **0.30** |
| Yield Stress ($f_y$) for Fe 410 | **250 N/mm²** |
| Yield Stress ($f_y$) for Fe 540 | **410 N/mm²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — Why Two Different Procedures?

The fundamental difference in design philosophy stems from the **limit state** being checked:

| Case | Governing Limit State | Design Strength Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Laterally Supported | **Yielding / Plastic Moment** | $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ |
| Laterally Unsupported | **Lateral-Torsional Buckling** | $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_{bd} / \gamma_{m0}$ |

Where **$f_{bd}$ is the design bending compressive stress** which is **less than** $f_y$ and depends on the **non-dimensional slenderness ratio** $\lambda_{LT}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Laterally Supported Beam Design

**Step 1 — Section Classification**
Classify section as Plastic / Compact / Semi-Compact using:
- $\lambda_f = b_f / (2 t_f)$ (outstand of flange)
- $\lambda_w = d / t_w$ (web)

**Step 2 — Shear Capacity Check**
$$ V_{dn} = V_{dp} \quad \text{(for plastic/compact section)} $$
$$ V_d = A_v f_{yw} / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0}) $$

**Step 3 — Moment Capacity Calculation**

For **Plastic Section:**
$$ M_{dp} = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0} $$

For **Compact Section:**
$$ M_{dc} = \beta_b Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0} $$

For **Semi-Compact Section:**
$$ M_{dy} = \beta_b Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0} \quad \text{(elastic moment capacity)} $$

Where:
- $\beta_b$ = **Bending Moment Factor** (ratio of actual moment to max moment under uniform moment)
- $Z_p$ = Plastic Section Modulus
- $Z_e$ = Elastic Section Modulus

**Step 4 — High Shear Check (Shear Buckling)**
If $V > 0.6 V_d$, the moment capacity must be **reduced** due to shear yielding effects on web.

**Step 5 — Deflection Check**
$$ \delta_{actual} \leq \delta_{permissible} = L/300 \text{ (general)} $$

**Step 6 — Check for Web Bearing, Web Crippling**

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: Laterally Unsupported Beam Design

**Step 1 — Section Classification** (Same as above)

**Step 2 — Shear Capacity Check** (Same as above)

**Step 3 — Compute Effective Length**
For simply supported beams, $L_{LT} = L$ (span). For continuous beams:
$$ L_{LT} = k_1 L_1 + k_2 L_2 + \ldots $$

**Step 4 — Calculate Section Properties**
$$ r_{yy} = \sqrt{\frac{I_y}{A}} \quad \text{(radius of gyration about minor axis)} $$
$$ h_f = \text{distance between shear centers of flanges} $$

**Step 5 — Compute Elastic Critical Moment ($M_{cr}$)**
$$ M_{cr} = \beta_b \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{(L_{LT}/r_{yy})^2} \cdot \frac{I_w}{I_y} + \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{(L_{LT})^2} \cdot h_f^2 \cdot \frac{\pi^2 E}{4(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \frac{t_f^3}{...}} $$

Simplified formula used in KTU problems:
$$ M_{cr} = \frac{\pi}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{E I_y G I_t} \cdot \left[ 1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 G I_t} \right]^{0.5} $$

Where:
- $I_y$ = Second moment of area about minor axis
- $I_t$ = Torsion constant
- $I_w$ = Warping constant
- $G$ = Shear modulus = $E/[2(1+\nu)]$ = **76,923 N/mm²**

**Step 6 — Calculate Non-Dimensional Slenderness**
$$ \lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}} $$

**Step 7 — Calculate Design Bending Compressive Stress ($f_{bd}$)**

Three approaches per IS 800:2007 — **Low, Medium, and High slenderness** ranges:

$$ f_{bd} = \chi_{LT} \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} $$

Using the Perry-Robertson formula:
$$ f_{bd} = \frac{f_y \cdot \chi_{LT}}{\gamma_{m0}} $$
$$ \chi_{LT} = \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \leq 1.0 $$

$$ \phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2] $$

**Step 8 — Compute Design Moment Capacity**
$$ M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_{bd} \quad \text{(plastic/compact sections)} $$

**Step 9 — Serviceability Check** (Deflection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU 2024 Scheme questions, the design procedure for laterally unsupported beams is often asked with a **given $M_{cr}$** (already computed) so students focus on $\lambda_{LT}$, $f_{bd}$, and $M_d$ calculation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicable Case |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Plastic Moment | $M_p = Z_p f_y$ | N·mm | Both |
| 2 | Design Plastic Moment | $M_{dp} = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N·mm | Lat. Supported |
| 3 | Design Shear Capacity | $V_d = A_v f_{yw} / (\sqrt{3}\gamma_{m0})$ | N | Both |
| 4 | Elastic Critical Moment | $M_{cr} = \frac{\pi}{L_{LT}}\sqrt{EI_y GI_t}\left(1 + \frac{\pi^2 EI_w}{L_{LT}^2 GI_t}\right)^{0.5}$ | N·mm | Lat. Unsupported |
| 5 | Non-dim. Slenderness (LTB) | $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_{cr}}$ | — | Lat. Unsupported |
| 6 | Auxiliary parameter | $\phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2]$ | — | Lat. Unsupported |
| 7 | Reduction factor | $\chi_{LT} = 1/[\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}]$ | — | Lat. Unsupported |
| 8 | Design bending stress | $f_{bd} = \chi_{LT} f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N/mm² | Lat. Unsupported |
| 9 | Design Moment (LTB) | $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_{bd}$ | N·mm | Lat. Unsupported |
| 10 | Slenderness Limit | $\lambda_{LT} \leq \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_d}$ | — | Check |

> [!NOTE]
> **$\alpha_{LT}$ (imperfection factor)** per IS 800:2007 Table 14:
> - Rolled I-section: **0.21**
> - Welded I-section: **0.49**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Industrial Roof Purlins** — Laterally supported (top flange restrained by sheeting).
2. **Gantry Girders (Crane Beams)** — Laterally unsupported between column supports.
3. **Bridge Cross-Girders with Open Deck** — Often laterally unsupported.
4. **Cantilevered Beams in Sign Gantries** — Severely laterally unsupported.
5. **Walkway Beams with Grating** — Partially supported (effective support).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_{cr}}$

**Starting Point:** The Euler-type buckling analogy for beams in flexure.

Consider a beam in pure bending (uniform moment $M$). The compression flange behaves as a **column** that can buckle laterally between restraint points. However, unlike a pure column:
- The compression flange is restrained vertically by the tension flange (torsional coupling).
- Warping stiffness contributes to resistance.
- The "effective length" is $L_{LT}$ (between lateral restraints).

**Setting up the critical condition:**

The elastic critical moment is the moment at which the beam becomes neutrally stable (bifurcation point). For a doubly symmetric I-section, equating the strain energy of bending to the work done by lateral deflection:

$$ M_{cr} \propto \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L_{LT}^2} \quad \text{(lateral bending contribution)} $$

$$ M_{cr} \propto \frac{G I_t}{L_{LT}} \quad \text{(St. Venant torsion contribution)} $$

**Combining both effects:**

$$ M_{cr} = \frac{\pi}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{E I_y \cdot G I_t \left(1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 G I_t}\right)} $$

**Non-dimensionalization:**

We want a non-dimensional measure of how close the applied moment is to the critical moment. Define:

$$ \lambda_{LT}^2 = \frac{M_p \text{ (capacity if fully plastic)}}{M_{cr} \text{ (elastic critical)}} $$

$$ \lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}} $$

**Why the square root?**

- It makes $\lambda_{LT}$ dimensionless (N·mm / N·mm = ratio).
- It represents the ratio of "capacity in the absence of buckling" to "capacity with buckling."
- When $\lambda_{LT} \ll 1$: beam is "stocky" — fails by yielding (no LTB).
- When $\lambda_{LT} \gg 1$: beam is "slender" — fails by elastic LTB.

**Final reasoning chain:**

$$ \begin{aligned} \text{Critical moment available} &= M_{cr} \\ \text{Moment the section could carry plastically} &= M_p = Z_p f_y \\ \text{Non-dimensional slenderness} &= \lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\frac{M_p}{M_{cr}}} \end{aligned} $$

## 3.2 Derivation: Perry-Robertson Formula for $f_{bd}$

Starting from the assumption that the beam has a small initial bow (imperfection) and a slenderness ratio $\lambda_{LT}$, the Perry-Robertson approach gives the **average stress** at failure as:

**Step 1 — Set up governing equation**

For an axially loaded column with initial bow $e_0$, the failure occurs when:
$$ \sigma_{avg} = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_{max} = f_y \text{ at the extreme fibre} $$

**Step 2 — Apply to beam with lateral imperfection $\delta_0$**

$$ f_{max} = f_{avg} \left(1 + \frac{\delta_0 e}{r^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - P/P_{cr}} \right) = f_y $$

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalize using $\lambda_{LT}$**

$$ f_{avg} = \frac{f_y}{1 + \frac{\alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2)}{\chi_{LT}}} $$

Rearranging gives:
$$ \phi_{LT} = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2\right] $$

And:
$$ \chi_{LT} = \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \leq 1.0 $$

**Step 4 — Final design stress**

$$ f_{bd} = \chi_{LT} \cdot \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} $$

**Step 5 — Practical design moment**

$$ M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_{bd} $$

**Verification with limits:**
- When $\lambda_{LT} \to 0$ (stocky): $\phi_{LT} \to 0.5$, $\chi_{LT} \to 1.0$, $f_{bd} \to f_y/\gamma_{m0}$ ✓ (full plastic moment)
- When $\lambda_{LT} \to \infty$ (very slender): $\phi_{LT} \to \lambda_{LT}^2/2$, $\chi_{LT} \to 1/\lambda_{LT}^2$, $f_{bd} \to f_y/(\gamma_{m0} \lambda_{LT}^2)$ ✓ (Euler-type)

## 3.3 Comparative Algorithm Matrix

| Step | Laterally Supported Beam | Laterally Unsupported Beam |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Section classification (Plastic/Compact/...) | Section classification (Plastic/Compact/...) |
| 2 | Shear capacity check | Shear capacity check |
| 3 | Compute $\beta_b$ from moment diagram | Compute $\beta_b$ from moment diagram |
| 4 | Compute $M_{dp}$ (or $M_{dc}$, $M_{dy}$) | Compute $L_{LT}$, $M_{cr}$ |
| 5 | Check: $M_u \leq M_d$ | Compute $\lambda_{LT}$ |
| 6 | High shear check if needed | Compute $\phi_{LT}$, $\chi_{LT}$ |
| 7 | Deflection check | Compute $f_{bd}$ |
| 8 | Web bearing/crippling | Check: $M_u \leq M_d$ |
| 9 | — | Deflection check |

## 3.4 KTU 2024 Style Worked Example Outline

**Problem Type (14 marks):** A simply supported I-beam with given span, section properties, loading. Design for: (a) Laterally supported case (b) Laterally unsupported case.

**Solution Flow:**

**(a) Laterally Supported Case:**
1. Compute $M_u$, $V_u$ from loads.
2. Find $\beta_b$ from table (e.g., for simply supported with UDL = 1.14).
3. Assume $f_y = 250$ N/mm², $Z_p$ given/assumed.
4. $M_{dp} = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$
5. Check $M_u \leq M_{dp}$, $V_u \leq V_d$.

**(b) Laterally Unsupported Case:**
1. Given $I_y$, $I_t$, $I_w$ of the section.
2. $L_{LT} = L$ (for simply supported with unrestrained compression flange).
3. Compute $M_{cr}$ from formula.
4. $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_{cr}}$.
5. $\phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2]$.
6. $\chi_{LT} = 1/[\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}]$.
7. $f_{bd} = \chi_{LT} f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.
8. $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_{bd}$.
9. Compare $M_d$ with the supported case — the unsupported case gives a **lower $M_d$**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beam Cross Section I-Section] --> B{Loading and Restraint Condition}
    
    B -- Supported Top Flange --> C[Laterally Supported Beam]
    C --> C1[Failure Mode: Yielding or Plastic Collapse]

    B -- Unsupported Top Flange --> D[Laterally Unsupported Beam]
```
