# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "width-to-thickness ratio" of a plate element and explain its significance in steel section classification as per IS 800:2007.**

**Model Answer:**

The **width-to-thickness ratio (b/t)** of a plate element is defined as the ratio of the flat clear width of the plate (measured between adjacent stiffeners, webs, or free edges) to its thickness. It is a **non-dimensional slenderness parameter** that quantifies the susceptibility of the plate to **local buckling** under compressive stresses.

**Significance in IS 800:2007:**
- The b/t ratio determines the **section classification** (Class 1, 2, 3, or 4).
- Plates with low b/t ratios are **stocky** and can develop full plastic moment (Class 1/2).
- Plates with high b/t ratios are **slender** and buckle locally before yielding, requiring the **effective width method** (Class 4).
- The b/t ratio is compared against **limiting values** in Table 2 of IS 800:2007, which are functions of the **imperfection factor $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$**.

**(3 Marks — Definition 1M, Significance 2M)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is the effective width concept for slender plate elements? Why is it necessary in safety-based design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **effective width concept** is a design methodology used for **Class 4 (slender)** plate elements that are prone to local buckling. After buckling initiates, the actual stress distribution becomes **non-uniform**, with higher stresses concentrated near the supported edges and lower stresses at the centre. The concept, originally proposed by **Von Karman (1932)** and later refined by **Winter (1946)**, replaces this non-uniform distribution with an equivalent **uniform stress** acting over a **reduced effective width** $b_{eff}$.

**Necessity in safety-based design:**
1. **Prevents unsafe overestimation** of load-carrying capacity by accounting for the actual post-buckling behaviour.
2. Provides a **realistic reserve of strength** by accounting for initial imperfections and residual stresses.
3. Enables **code-compliant design** of cold-formed steel sections, box columns, and other slender elements where full yielding cannot be achieved.
4. Maintains the **ductile failure mode** by ensuring strength prediction matches actual structural behaviour.

**(3 Marks — Concept 1M, Necessity 2M)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`

**(a)** A steel I-section has the following properties:
- Flange width $b_f = 200$ mm, Flange thickness $t_f = 12$ mm
- Web height $h_w = 400$ mm, Web thickness $t_w = 8$ mm
- Yield stress $f_y = 250$ MPa

**Classify the cross-section** as per IS 800:2007. Check both flange and web, and determine the governing class of the section. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the same section, if the flange thickness is reduced to **8 mm** (other dimensions unchanged), **reclassify the section** and **compute the effective width and effective area** of the outstanding flange element using the **effective width method**. Take $\nu = 0.3$, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A (a) — Section Classification**

**Step 1: Compute the Imperfection Factor**
$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{f_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{250}{250}} = 1.0$$

**Step 2: Classify the Outstanding Flange (Rolled Section)**

For an **outstanding compression element of a rolled I/H section** (IS 800:2007 Table 2):
- Class 1 limit: $9.4\varepsilon = 9.4$
- Class 2 limit: $10.5\varepsilon = 10.5$
- Class 3 limit: $15.7\varepsilon = 15.7$

Actual b/t ratio (taking $b = b_f/2 = 100$ mm for outstanding flange):
$$\frac{b}{t_f} = \frac{100}{12} = 8.33$$

**Comparison:** $8.33 < 9.4$ → **Flange is Class 1 (Plastic)**

**Step 3: Classify the Web in Flexural Compression**

For the **web of a rolled section subjected to flexural compression**:
- Class 1 limit: $84\varepsilon = 84$
- Class 2 limit: $105\varepsilon = 105$
- Class 3 limit: $126\varepsilon = 126$

Actual b/t ratio (taking $b = h_w = 400$ mm):
$$\frac{h_w}{t_w} = \frac{400}{8} = 50$$

**Comparison:** $50 < 84$ → **Web is Class 1 (Plastic)**

**Step 4: Overall Section Classification**

Since both flange and web are **Class 1**, the **overall section is Class 1 (Plastic)**. Plastic design methodology is applicable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistake #1:**
> Many students use the **full flange width $b_f = 200$ mm** instead of the half-width (outstanding portion) for the b/t ratio of an I-flange. This is incorrect. Per IS 800:2007, the width of an outstanding element is measured from the **free edge to the face of the web**, i.e., $b = b_f/2$ for symmetric I-sections. Marks are routinely lost here.

---

### **Solution to Question A (b) — Effective Width Computation**

**Step 1: Reclassify with Reduced Flange Thickness**

New $t_f = 8$ mm:
$$\frac{b}{t_f} = \frac{100}{8} = 12.5$$

**Comparison:** $10.5 < 12.5 < 15.7$ → **Flange is Class 3 (Semi-Compact)**

(Other elements unchanged: Web remains Class 1.)

**Step 2: Section is Not Slender — Verify Whether Effective Width Applies**

Since the actual b/t = 12.5 is **less than 15.7** (the Class 3 limit), the flange is **semi-compact** and does NOT require the effective width method. The design uses **elastic stress distribution with reduced moment capacity**.

**However**, the question explicitly asks to apply the effective width method as a hypothetical design exercise. Proceed as follows:

**Step 3: Compute Critical Buckling Stress of Outstanding Flange**

For an outstanding compression element, $k = 0.425$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{cr} &= \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 \\
            &= \frac{0.425 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{10.92} \cdot \left(\frac{8}{100}\right)^2 \\
            &= \frac{8.39 \times 10^5}{10.92} \times 0.0064 \\
            &= 76831.5 \times 0.0064 \\
            &= 491.72 \;\; \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute Plate Slenderness Parameter**
$$\lambda_p = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\frac{250}{491.72}} = \sqrt{0.5084} = 0.7130$$

**Step 5: Apply Winter's Formula**

Since $\lambda_p = 0.7130 > 0.673$, the element is treated as slender:
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{\lambda_p - 0.22}{\lambda_p^2} = \frac{0.7130 - 0.22}{0.7130^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.4930}{0.5084} = 0.9697
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Effective Width of One Outstanding Flange**
$$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b = 0.9697 \times 100 = 96.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Effective Area of Total Flange (Both Sides)**
$$A_{eff,flange} = 2 \times b_{eff} \times t_f = 2 \times 96.97 \times 8 = 1551.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

(Original full flange area = $2 \times 100 \times 8 = 1600$ mm² → **3.0% reduction**)

**Step 8: Final Effective Section Properties**
- Effective flange area: $1551.5$ mm²
- Web area (unchanged, Class 1): $400 \times 8 = 3200$ mm²
- **Total effective area**: $A_{eff} = 1551.5 + 3200 = 4751.5$ mm²

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistake #2:**
> Students frequently forget the **boundary condition constant 0.22 in Winter's formula** and use the older Von Karman version $\rho = 1/\lambda_p$. While this gives a slightly higher value, it is **non-conservative** and violates IS 800:2007 Clause 9.3.2.2. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for using the wrong formula.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**(a)** Explain the **derivation of the critical buckling stress** of a simply supported thin plate under uniform in-plane compression. State clearly the role of the **buckling coefficient $k$** and how it varies for different boundary conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **plate girder** of span 20 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 50 kN/m (including self-weight). Design the **web thickness** as per IS 800:2007, assuming:
- Web depth $d = 1500$ mm
- Yield stress $f_{yw} = 250$ MPa
- Use **unstiffened web** condition. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B (a) — Plate Buckling Derivation**

**Step 1: Setup the Plate Geometry**

A thin rectangular plate of width $b$, thickness $t$, length $a$, is subjected to uniform compressive force $N_x$ per unit length along the $x$-axis. The plate is simply supported on all four edges.

**Step 2: Plate Buckling Differential Equation**

The classical plate equilibrium equation in the post-buckled state is:
$$D\left(\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial y^4}\right) + N_x \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Flexural Rigidity per Unit Width**
$$D = \frac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5 \cdot t^3}{12(1-0.30^2)} = 1.83 \times 10^4 \cdot t^3 \;\; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Assume Buckled Shape (Navier Solution)**

For simply supported edges, the deflection admits the form:
$$w = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} a_{mn} \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

**Step 5: Apply Rayleigh-Ritz Energy Method**

The total potential energy $\Pi = U - W$ is minimised by equating its variation to zero, leading to:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

**Step 6: Buckling Coefficient $k$ for Different Conditions**

| Boundary Condition | $k$ Value |
|---|---|
| Simply supported on all four edges, uniform compression | **4.0** |
| Clamped on all four edges, uniform compression | **6.97** |
| One edge clamped, three edges simply supported (outstanding element) | **0.425** |
| Simply supported, pure shear | **5.35** |
| Simply supported, pure bending | **23.9** |

**Step 7: Numerical Insight for Steel Plate ($b/t = 50$)**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{10.92 \times 2500} = \frac{7.896 \times 10^6}{27300} = 289.2 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

For $f_y = 250$ MPa, this is adequate. For $b/t = 100$:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times \pi^2 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{10.92 \times 10000} = 72.3 \;\; \text{MPa} < f_y$$

→ Plate buckles before yielding. **Slender classification triggered.**

---

### **Solution to Question B (b) — Web Thickness Design**

**Step 1: Compute Design Shear Force**

For a simply supported plate girder under UDL, maximum shear at support:
$$V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{50 \times 20}{2} = 500 \text{ kN}$$

Design shear force (factored):
$$V_d = 1.5 \times 500 = 750 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Imperfection Factor**
$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{250}} = 1.0$$

**Step 3: Web Thickness for Unstiffened Web (IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.6.1)**

For an **unstiffened web** (no intermediate transverse stiffeners), the web thickness must satisfy:
$$t_w \geq \frac{d}{200} = \frac{1500}{200} = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Web Thickness for Stiffened Web (For Comparison)**

If the web is stiffened to permit tension field action:
$$t_w \geq \frac{d}{67\varepsilon}\sqrt{\frac{f_{yw}}{250}} = \frac{1500}{67 \times 1.0} \times 1.0 = 22.39 \text{ mm}$$

This is the **minimum thickness for the web to develop full shear capacity** with stiffeners. For the unstiffened case, **7.5 mm is acceptable but design shear capacity is reduced**.

**Step 5: Provide Practical Thickness**

Adopt the next higher standard thickness:
$$\boxed{t_w = 8 \text{ mm (adopt)}}$$

**Step 6: Verify Shear Capacity Without Tension Field**

The shear buckling strength of the web (elastic) is:
$$\tau_{cr} = \frac{k_v \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t_w}{d}\right)^2$$

For an unstiffened web, $k_v = 5.35$ (approximately, depending on aspect ratio). With $t_w = 8$ mm:
$$\tau_{cr} = \frac{5.35 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{10.92} \times \left(\frac{8}{1500}\right)^2 = 96.74 \times 2.844 \times 10^{-5} = 0.00275 \;\; \text{kN/mm}^2$$

Wait, recompute:
$$\tau_{cr} = \frac{5.35 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{10.92} \times \left(\frac{8}{1500}\right)^2 = 9.674 \times 10^5 \times 2.844 \times 10^{-5} = 27.52 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

Since $\tau_{cr} = 27.52$ MPa is **much less than $f_{yw}/\sqrt{3} = 144.3$ MPa**, the web will buckle in shear. The design shear capacity is governed by the **post-buckling reserve** in this case (only 27.5 MPa elastic buckling strength is available before stiffening is required).

**Step 7: Provide Final Design**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Web depth $d$ | 1500 mm |
| **Web thickness adopted $t_w$** | **8 mm (unstiffened, conservative)** |
| Recommended stiffener spacing | 750 mm c/c (if upgraded to stiffened) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistake #3:**
> Many students **directly adopt $t_w = d/200$** without checking whether the resulting web has adequate shear capacity. The 200-factor is a **serviceability limit** to control web waviness during handling, NOT a strength criterion. Always verify **post-buckling shear capacity** for unstiffened webs. Marks are deducted if shear verification is missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — PLATE THICKNESS**

- [ ] **b/t ratio is the master parameter** for section classification in IS 800:2007.
- [ ] **Imperfection factor** $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$ scales all b/t limits with material grade.
- [ ] **Class 1 (Plastic) sections** allow full plastic hinge rotation — preferred for seismic/moment-resisting frames.
- [ ] **Class 4 (Slender) sections** require the **effective width method** (Winter's formula).
- [ ] **Winter's reduction factor:** $\rho = (\lambda_p - 0.22)/\lambda_p^2$ for $\lambda_p > 0.673$, else $\rho = 1.0$.
- [ ] **Buckling coefficient $k$** for outstanding flange = **0.425**; for internal element = **4.0**; for shear web = **5.35**.
- [ ] **Outstanding flange** is measured from **web face to free edge** (half flange width for symmetric I-section).
- [ ] **Overall section class = worst (highest number) class** among flange and web.
- [ ] **Web thickness limits** (IS 800:2007): $d/200$ for unstiffened, $d/67\varepsilon$ for stiffened with tension field.
- [ ] **Effective width method accounts for** initial imperfections, residual stresses, and load eccentricities.
- [ ] **Critical buckling stress** decreases with $(b/t)^2$ — doubling the b/t ratio **quadruples** the buckling risk.
- [ ] **Cold-formed steel sections** are always Class 4 → effective width method is mandatory.
- [ ] **Plate girder webs** must be checked for both **shear buckling** and **bearing/bending buckling** separately.
- [ ] **Partial safety factors** for IS 800:2007: $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$.
- [ ] **Steel constants to memorise:** $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $12(1-\nu^2) = 10.92$.
- [ ] **Post-buckling reserve** in plate girders (tension field action) is permitted ONLY for **stiffened webs** with adequate transverse stiffeners.
- [ ] **For Class 4 sections, design capacity reduces by factor** $A_{eff}/A_{gross}$ — never use gross area for slender sections.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Beam End Connectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT722 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simple Beam End Connectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Beam End Connectors** are structural fastening elements used in steel construction to join a beam to a supporting member (column, girder, or wall) such that the connection transmits **predominantly vertical shear force** while offering **negligible rotational restraint** (i.e., it behaves like a pinned/articulated joint in the limit-state design philosophy of IS 800:2007 and IS 800:2023).

According to **IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.2.4.3 / Cl. 10.2.6)**, a simple (flexible) connection is one where the rotational stiffness is low enough that the beam end may be assumed to rotate freely under gravity loads. The connection is therefore designed for **shear alone**, and the moment arising from the eccentricity of the connector group is treated as a secondary effect (accounted for via the design eccentricity rule of **Cl. 10.4.7** of IS 800:2007).

### Common Types of Simple Beam End Connectors

| S.No. | Connector Type | Field Application | Typical Usage |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Web Cleat (Double Angle Cleat)** | Beam web to column flange | Most common; field-bolted shop-welded |
| 2 | **Fin Plate (Shear Tab)** | Plate welded to column, bolted to beam | Economy for light framing |
| 3 | **Flexible End Plate** | Partial depth plate, bolted both sides | Light to medium loads |
| 4 | **Top & Seat Angle (with Web Cleat)** | Old-style flexible connection | Pre-engineered buildings |
| 5 | **Single Angle Cleat** | One angle on one side only | Light secondary beams |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme OESFT722 syllabus, the emphasis is on the **safety checks** required at ultimate and serviceability limit states, NOT on the geometric detailing of bolts/cleats. The student must master failure modes and the **partial safety factor (γm)** framework.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Door Hinge vs. The Welded Frame**

Imagine a beam supported on a column. Now picture two ways to attach it:

- **Rigid (Moment) Connection** → Like a beam **welded rigidly** to the column. If you push down on the beam end, the column also bends — there is moment transfer. (Example: A bookshelf bracket bolted to a wall with 4 large screws.)
- **Simple (Shear-Only) Connection** → Like a beam **resting on a door hinge pin**. The hinge allows the beam to rotate freely but holds it up against gravity. No moment is transferred; only the **weight (shear)** is carried. (Example: A shelf sitting on L-brackets — push down → shelf stays; twist the shelf → it freely rotates.)

A **simple beam end connector** is the engineering equivalent of that "hinge pin + L-bracket". Its safety must be verified for:
1. The **weight it carries** (shear demand)
2. The **friction between the pin and the hole** (bolt slip / bearing)
3. The **metal of the bracket itself not tearing** (plate shear, block shear)

The connector is intentionally designed to be **flexible** so that gravity loads in the beam do not produce secondary moments in the column — this simplifies analysis drastically and is the **default assumption in modern multi-storey steel design**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Partial Safety Factor for Material (Steel)**: **γ_m0 = 1.10** (yielding), **γ_m1 = 1.25** (ultimate/rupture), **γ_mb = 1.25** (bolt)
- **Ultimate Strength to Yield Strength Ratio (fu/fy)** typically **1.18 to 1.40** depending on steel grade
- **Bearing Factor kb** (IS 800 Cl. 10.3.4): minimum of **(e/(3d₀)), (p/(3d₀) − 0.25), fu/ffu, 1.0**
- **Effective shear area for plate in shear** = **Lv × t** (gross), or **(Lv − ndh × t)** (net)
- **Bolt hole diameter d₀ = d + clearance** (clearance = **2 mm** for d ≤ 24 mm, **3 mm** for d > 24 mm per IS 800)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy of a Simple Connection

A simple beam end connector must satisfy **three independent safety conditions** at the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)**:

### Step 1 — Load Path Identification

The vertical reaction from the beam (Vu) must travel through:
> Beam web → **Bolts in shear & bearing** → **Connector plate / cleat** → **Fillet weld or bolts** → **Column flange / web**.

If **any single link** in this chain is weaker than the demand, the connection fails. Each link is designed independently and the **governing (lowest) capacity** dictates the connection design capacity.

### Step 2 — Failure Mode Cataloging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A bolt in a simple connector can fail in any of the following modes. The student must check each:

1. **Bolt Shear Failure** — bolt shank sheared along the shear plane(s).
2. **Bolt Bearing Failure** — hole edge crushes the plate.
3. **Plate Shear Yielding (Gross Section)** — gross shear area yields.
4. **Plate Shear Rupture (Net Section)** — net shear area ruptures.
5. **Plate Tensile / Block Shear Failure** — a "block" of material behind the bolt group shears out.
6. **Weld Failure** — throat of fillet weld cracks.
7. **Serviceability — Slip in Slip-Critical Connections** (only if specified).

### Step 3 — Design Eccentricity Rule

Because the **line of action of the shear** does **not pass through the centroid of the bolt group**, an **eccentricity `e`** exists. The bolt group must therefore be checked for the **combined effect of direct shear + torsional shear** using the elastic method (IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7).

- **Resultant shear on the critical bolt** $R = \sqrt{V^2 + T^2}$
- **Torsional shear** $T = \dfrac{V_u \cdot e \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2}$
- **Direct shear** $V_d = \dfrac{V_u}{n}$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the table below. Every KTU exam question on Simple Beam End Connectors will hinge (pun intended) on at least three of these formulas.

| # | Check / Failure Mode | Design Strength Formula (IS 800:2007) | γ_m |
|---|----------------------|----------------------------------------|-----|
| 1 | Bolt single shear (rivet analogy) | $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot (n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb})$ | γ_mb = **1.25** |
| 2 | Bolt bearing on plate | $V_{dpb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u$ | γ_mb = **1.25** |
| 3 | Plate gross shear yielding | $V_{dy} = \dfrac{0.6 \, f_y \, A_v}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | γ_m0 = **1.10** |
| 4 | Plate net shear rupture | $V_{dn} = \dfrac{0.6 \, f_u \, A_{vn}}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | γ_m1 = **1.25** |
| 5 | Block shear strength | $V_{db} = \dfrac{0.6 \, f_u \, A_{vn}}{\gamma_{m1}} + \dfrac{f_y \, A_{tg}}{\gamma_{m0}}$ (governing case) | — |
| 6 | Effective net area | $A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_0 \cdot t$ | — |
| 7 | Bearing factor $k_b$ | $\min\!\left(\dfrac{e}{3d_0},\ \dfrac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25,\ \dfrac{f_u}{f_{uf}},\ 1.0\right)$ | — |
| 8 | Fillet weld strength | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}$ ; $R_w = 0.7 s \cdot f_{wd} \cdot L_w$ | γ_mw = **1.25 / 1.50** |
| 9 | Critical bolt resultant | $R = \sqrt{V_d^2 + T^2} \le V_{dsb}$ (governing bolt) | — |
| 10 | Minimum edge / pitch | $e_{\min} = 1.5 d_0$ ; $p_{\min} = 2.5 d$ | — |

### Symbol Legend

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $f_u, f_y$ | Ultimate & yield stress of plate | MPa (= N/mm²) |
| $f_{ub}$ | Ultimate stress of bolt | MPa |
| $d, d_0$ | Bolt nominal dia / hole dia | mm |
| $t$ | Thickness of thinner plate | mm |
| $A_{nb}$ | Net tensile area of bolt shank | mm² |
| $A_{sb}$ | Shank area (for threads excluded) | mm² |
| $n_n, n_s$ | Number of shear planes (threads / shank) | — |
| $e$ | Eccentricity from column face to CG of bolt group | mm |
| $r_i$ | Radial distance of i-th bolt from CG of group | mm |
| $L_w, s$ | Weld length & size | mm |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where simple beam end connectors are used in practice:**
> 1. **Industrial PEB (Pre-Engineered Buildings)** — primary rafter-to-column connections are predominantly fin plates.
> 2. **Multi-storey commercial steel frames** — secondary beams connect to primary beams using web cleats; primary-to-column via fin plates.
> 3. **Pipe racks in refineries** — most connections are simple to allow for thermal expansion.
> 4. **Roof purlins and girts** — sleeve-type simple connections are universal.

The **safety advantage** of simple connectors is the **predictability of the load path** — the designer does not need to model complex moment-rotation (M-θ) behavior, dramatically reducing design time and **eliminating brittle failure modes** associated with fully rigid connections under seismic loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Block Shear Strength (IS 800 Cl. 6.4.1)

Block shear is a **combined shear + tension failure** along the perimeter of a bolt group. IS 800 prescribes two resisting components:

- **Shear resistance along the shear planes** (using $f_u$)
- **Tension resistance along the tension planes** (using $f_y$, since yielding governs the tearing face)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block Shear Capacity } V_{db} &= \min\!\left[\dfrac{f_y A_{tg}}{\gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.6 f_u A_{vn}}{\gamma_{m1}},\ \dfrac{f_u A_{tn}}{\gamma_{m1}} + \dfrac{0.6 f_y A_{vg}}{\gamma_{m0}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. The "block" is the rectangular chunk of material surrounded by the bolt lines, the edge, and the shear path.
2. Two faces of the block are **in shear** (parallel to load) → use $f_u$ with $0.6$ factor (von Mises equivalent).
3. Two faces of the block are **in tension** (perpendicular to load) → use $f_y$ (yield governs) or $f_u$ (rupture governs), whichever combination yields the minimum.
4. IS 800 gives **two formulas** to bracket the actual failure — we take the lesser.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Bolt Force under Eccentric Shear

For an `n`-bolt group subjected to an in-plane shear `Vu` at eccentricity `e` from the centroid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct shear per bolt: } V_d &= \frac{V_u}{n} \\[6pt]
\text{Torsional moment: } M &= V_u \cdot e \\[6pt]
\text{Torsional shear on bolt } i: T_i &= \frac{M \cdot r_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j^2} \\[6pt]
\text{Resultant on bolt } i: R_i &= \sqrt{V_d^2 + T_i^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. The vertical load `Vu` is shared **equally** by all bolts → component `Vd` acts downward on every bolt.
2. The same load creates a **moment `M = Vu·e`** about the CG, which is resisted by **bolt reactions proportional to their distance from the CG**.
3. The torsional shear `Ti` on bolt `i` is **perpendicular to the radius `ri`** (acts tangentially, in the direction of rotation).
4. The critical bolt is the one with the **largest `ri`** (typically the corner bolt).
5. For that bolt, `V_d` (vertical) and `T_max` (perpendicular) combine vectorially to give the **resultant `R`**.
6. Safety check: $R \le V_{dsb}$ (bolt shear capacity) and $R \le V_{dpb}$ (bearing capacity).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Model for KTU Exam)

**Problem:** A secondary beam ISMB 300 transfers a factored end reaction $V_u = 180$ kN to the flange of a column via a **double angle web cleat (2 × 100 × 75 × 8 mm)** with **4 numbers of 16 mm diameter bolts (Property Class 8.8)** in **double shear**.

Given: $f_y = 250$ MPa (plate), $f_u = 410$ MPa (plate), $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa (bolt), $d_0 = 18$ mm. The eccentricity $e = 60$ mm. Bolt layout (vertical column): 2 bolts × 2 bolts, pitch = 60 mm, gauge from beam top = 50 mm. Compute (a) bolt shear capacity, (b) bearing capacity, (c) plate shear capacity, (d) critical bolt resultant.

---

### (a) Bolt Shear Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{nb} &= 0.78 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 16^2 = 156.7 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (net tensile area, threaded)} \\[4pt]
A_{sb} &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 16^2 = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (shank area)} \\[4pt]
V_{dsb} &= \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mb}} \left( n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb} \right) \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{800}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} \left( 2 \cdot 156.7 + 2 \cdot 201.06 \right) \\[4pt]
&= 369.5 \times (313.4 + 402.12) \\[4pt]
&= 369.5 \times 715.5 = 264{,}376 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{264.4 \text{ kN}} \text{ (per bolt)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Comment: This is the design strength **per bolt in double shear**.

---

### (b) Bearing Capacity (Plate is thinner at 8 mm vs column flange which is much thicker; hence plate governs)

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_b &= \min\!\left(\dfrac{e}{3d_0},\ \dfrac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25,\ \dfrac{f_u}{f_u},\ 1.0\right) \\[4pt]
&= \min\!\left(\dfrac{50}{3 \cdot 18},\ \dfrac{60}{3 \cdot 18} - 0.25,\ 1.0,\ 1.0\right) \\[4pt]
&= \min(0.926,\ 0.861,\ 1.0,\ 1.0) = 0.861 \\[4pt]
V_{dpb} &= \dfrac{2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u}{\gamma_{mb}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2.5 \cdot 0.861 \cdot 16 \cdot 8 \cdot 410}{1.25} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2.5 \cdot 0.861 \cdot 16 \cdot 8 \cdot 410}{1.25} = 90{,}419 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{90.4 \text{ kN}} \text{ (per bolt)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### (c) Plate Shear Capacity (Net Shear Rupture)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net shear length } L_v &= (2 \cdot 60) - 1.5 \cdot 18 = 120 - 27 = 93 \text{ mm} \\
A_{vn} &= L_v \cdot t = 93 \cdot 8 = 744 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
V_{dn} &= \dfrac{0.6 \, f_u \, A_{vn}}{\gamma_{m1}} = \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 410 \cdot 744}{1.25} = \mathbf{146{,}419 \text{ N}} \approx 146.4 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For two angles:** $V_{dn,\text{total}} = 2 \times 146.4 = \mathbf{292.8 \text{ kN}}$.

---

### (d) Critical Bolt Resultant (Corner Bolt)

For 2 × 2 bolt pattern, CG is at the geometric center. Radial distances:
- Bolts at corners: $r = \sqrt{30^2 + 30^2} = 42.43$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum r_i^2 &= 4 \times (30^2 + 30^2) = 4 \times 1800 = 7200 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
T_{\max} &= \dfrac{V_u \cdot e \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2} = \dfrac{180{,}000 \cdot 60 \cdot 42.43}{7200} = 63{,}642 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
V_d &= \dfrac{V_u}{n} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{4} = 45{,}000 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
R_{\max} &= \sqrt{45{,}000^2 + 63{,}642^2} = \sqrt{2.025 \times 10^9 + 4.050 \times 10^9} = 77{,}942 \text{ N} \approx 77.9 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Safety Check:** $R_{\max} = 77.9$ kN $\le V_{dsb} = 264.4$ kN ✓
> $R_{\max} = 77.9$ kN $\le V_{dpb} = 90.4$ kN ✓
> **Bearing governs the bolt-side check** for this configuration.

> **Connection is SAFE.** ✔️

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **force flow** through a typical double-angle web cleat simple connection, showing the hierarchy of safety checks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Factored Beam End Reaction Vu] --> B[Vertical Shear Demand]
    B --> C{Bolt Group}
    C --> D[Bolt Shear Check Vdsb]
    C --> E[Bolt Bearing Check Vdpb]
    C --> F[Eccentric Torsional Demand Tmax]
    F --> G[Critical Bolt Resultant Rmax]
    G --> D
    G --> E
    C --> H[Connector Plate Cleat]
    H --> I[Gross Shear Yield Vdy]
    H --> J[Net Shear Rupture Vdn]
    H --> K[Block Shear Vdb]
    H --> L[Fillet Weld to Column]
    L --> M[Weld Throat Stress fwd]
    L --> N[Weld Length Check Lw]
    M --> O[Connection SAFE OR UNSAFE]
    D --> O
    E --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    N --> O
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic Mapping — Force Path through a Double-Angle Cleat

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Beam[Beam Web carries Vu] --> CleatTop[Top Angle Leg]
    Beam --> CleatBot[Bottom Angle Leg]
    CleatTop --> Bolts1[Bolts in Double Shear]
    CleatBot --> Bolts2[Bolts in Double Shear]
    Bolts1 --> ColFlange[Column Flange via Bearing and Friction]
    Bolts2 --> ColFlange
    ColFlange --> Col[Column transfers load to Foundation]
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Given Vu, e, Bolt Layout] --> S1[Compute Direct Shear Vd per bolt]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Torsional Shear Ti for each bolt]
    S2 --> S3[Identify Critical Bolt at max radius]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Resultant Rmax]
    S4 --> S5{Rmax less than Vdsb?}
    S5 -- No --> F1[UNSAFE: Increase bolt grade or diameter]
    S5 -- Yes --> S6{Rmax less than Vdpb?}
    S6 -- No --> F2[UNSAFE: Increase plate thickness or edge distance]
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Check Plate Gross Shear Vdy]
    S7 --> S8{Vdy greater than Vu?}
    S8 -- No --> F3[UNSAFE: Increase plate thickness]
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Check Plate Net Shear Vdn]
    S9 --> S10{Vdn greater than Vu?}
    S10 -- No --> F4[UNSAFE: Increase plate depth or reduce bolt holes]
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Check Block Shear Vdb]
    S11 --> S12{Vdb greater than Vu?}
    S12 -- No --> F5[UNSAFE: Redesign bolt spacing]
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[Check Weld fwd and Lw]
    S13 --> S14{All checks pass?}
    S14 -- Yes --> SAFE[CONNECTION SAFE]
    S14 -- No --> F6[UNSAFE: Increase weld size or length]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a "simple connection" as per IS 800:2007. Mention two examples of simple beam end connectors.**

**Model Answer:**
A simple connection, as defined in IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.2.4.3), is a connection that transmits end shear only and is capable of accommodating rotation of the beam end without developing significant moments. It is also called a "flexible" or "pinned" connection.

> Two examples:
> 1. **Web cleat (double angle)** connection
> 2. **Fin plate (shear tab)** connection

*(For full 3 marks: definition 1 mark, two examples 2 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is "block shear" failure? How is it checked in the design of a simple beam end connector?**

**Model Answer:**
Block shear is a **combined shear-tension failure** in which a "block" of plate material, bounded by bolt lines and plate edges, tears out along two shear planes and one tension plane.

**Check:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{db} &= \min\!\left[\dfrac{f_y A_{tg}}{\gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.6 f_u A_{vn}}{\gamma_{m1}},\ \dfrac{f_u A_{tn}}{\gamma_{m1}} + \dfrac{0.6 f_y A_{vg}}{\gamma_{m0}}\right] \\
&\ge V_u
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for formula and application)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

A simply supported secondary beam transfers a **factored end reaction $V_u = 250$ kN** to the flange of an ISMB 450 column using a **double angle web cleat (2 × 100 × 75 × 10 mm)** with **6 numbers of 20 mm diameter bolts (Property Class 8.8)**. The bolt layout is **3 bolts × 2 bolts** (3 vertical rows × 2 columns), with pitch = 55 mm and gauge = 55 mm. The eccentricity from the column face to the centroid of bolt group is **70 mm**. Take $f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa for the cleat angle, $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa for the bolt, and $d_0 = 22$ mm. The thickness of the column flange is **16 mm**.

**Perform the following checks:**
- (a) **Check the bolt shear and bearing capacities** for the connection (7 marks).
- (b) **Check the cleat plate for gross shear yielding, net shear rupture, and block shear** (7 marks).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1: Bolt Shear Capacity (Double Shear)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{nb} &= 0.78 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 20^2 = 244.97 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sb} &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 20^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2 \\
V_{dsb} &= \dfrac{800}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} \left( 2 \cdot 244.97 + 2 \cdot 314.16 \right) \\
&= 369.5 \times (489.94 + 628.32) \\
&= 369.5 \times 1118.26 = 413{,}209 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{413.2 \text{ kN per bolt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Bearing Capacity**

The thinner plate governs; cleat thickness $t = 10$ mm, column flange is 16 mm. Since both are in contact, the bearing capacity is governed by the **cleat angle (t = 10 mm)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_b &= \min\!\left(\dfrac{e}{3d_0},\ \dfrac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25,\ 1.0,\ 1.0\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\dfrac{55}{3 \cdot 22},\ \dfrac{55}{3 \cdot 22} - 0.25,\ 1.0,\ 1.0\right) \\
&= \min(0.833,\ 0.583,\ 1.0,\ 1.0) = 0.583 \\
V_{dpb} &= \dfrac{2.5 \cdot 0.583 \cdot 20 \cdot 10 \cdot 410}{1.25} = \dfrac{2.5 \cdot 0.583 \cdot 20 \cdot 10 \cdot 410}{1.25} \\
&= 95{,}615 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{95.6 \text{ kN per bolt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Critical Bolt Force**

For a 3 × 2 pattern with pitch p = 55 mm (vertical) and gauge g = 55 mm (horizontal), the CG of the bolt group is at the centroid. The **farthest bolts** are at the four corners.

Distance from CG to corner bolt: $r = \sqrt{27.5^2 + 55^2} = \sqrt{756.25 + 3025} = 61.43$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum r_i^2 &= 2 \times (27.5^2 + 55^2) \times 2 \quad \text{(4 corner bolts)} \\
&= 4 \times (756.25 + 3025) = 4 \times 3781.25 = 15{,}125 \text{ mm}^2 \\
V_d &= \dfrac{250{,}000}{6} = 41{,}667 \text{ N} \\
T_{\max} &= \dfrac{250{,}000 \cdot 70 \cdot 61.43}{15{,}125} = 71{,}066 \text{ N} \\
R_{\max} &= \sqrt{41{,}667^2 + 71{,}066^2} = \sqrt{1.736 \times 10^9 + 5.050 \times 10^9} = 82{,}386 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{82.4 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Safety Checks**

- $R_{\max} = 82.4$ kN $\le V_{dsb} = 413.2$ kN ✓
- $R_{\max} = 82.4$ kN $\le V_{dpb} = 95.6$ kN ✓

> **Bolts are SAFE.** ✔️

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Step 1: Gross Shear Yielding of Cleat Plate**

The shear plane is along the vertical edge of the bolt column. Total shear length (gross) $L_g = 2 \times 55 + 30 = 140$ mm (assuming edge distance 30 mm; note: 2 pitches between 3 bolts gives $2p + e$ for vertical extent — careful, here the angle leg attached to column has $L_g = 2 \cdot 55 + 30 = 140$ mm vertically per angle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= L_g \cdot t = 140 \cdot 10 = 1400 \text{ mm}^2 \\
V_{dy,\text{one angle}} &= \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 250 \cdot 1400}{1.10} = \mathbf{190{,}909 \text{ N}} \approx 190.9 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

For two angles: $V_{dy,\text{total}} = 2 \times 190.9 = \mathbf{381.8 \text{ kN}} \ge V_u = 250$ kN ✓

**Step 2: Net Shear Rupture**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{vn} &= (L_g - 1.5 d_0) \cdot t = (140 - 1.5 \cdot 22) \cdot 10 = (140 - 33) \cdot 10 = 1070 \text{ mm}^2 \\
V_{dn,\text{one angle}} &= \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 410 \cdot 1070}{1.25} = \mathbf{210{,}624 \text{ N}} \approx 210.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

For two angles: $V_{dn,\text{total}} = 2 \times 210.6 = \mathbf{421.2 \text{ kN}} \ge V_u = 250$ kN ✓

**Step 3: Block Shear (Conservative Estimate)**

For an angle leg with 3 bolts in a single line:
- $A_{vn} = 2 \times (140 - 33) \times 10 / 2 = 1070 \text{ mm}^2$ per angle (shear plane)
- $A_{tg} = 50 \times 10 = 500 \text{ mm}^2$ per angle (tension plane, edge distance ~ 50 mm)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{db,\text{one angle}} &= \dfrac{f_y A_{tg}}{\gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.6 f_u A_{vn}}{\gamma_{m1}} \\
&= \dfrac{250 \cdot 500}{1.10} + \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 410 \cdot 1070}{1.25} \\
&= 113{,}636 + 210{,}624 = 324{,}260 \text{ N} \approx 324.3 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

For two angles: $V_{db,\text{total}} = 2 \times 324.3 = \mathbf{648.5 \text{ kN}} \ge V_u = 250$ kN ✓

> **Plate is SAFE in all three checks.** ✔️

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A **fin plate (shear tab) connection** is used to connect a secondary beam (ISMB 250) to a column flange. The fin plate is **150 mm deep × 8 mm thick** and is welded to the column with **two vertical fillet welds of size 6 mm**, one on each side of the plate. The plate is bolted to the beam web using **3 numbers of 16 mm diameter bolts (Grade 8.8)** in a **single vertical line** with a pitch of 50 mm. The factored end reaction is $V_u = 120$ kN. Eccentricity $e = 50$ mm. Material: $f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa, $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa, $d_0 = 18$ mm.

**Perform the following:**
- (a) **Check the fin plate for net shear rupture, gross shear yielding, and block shear** (7 marks).
- (b) **Check the strength of the fillet weld connecting the fin plate to the column** (7 marks).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1: Geometry**
- Plate depth = 150 mm, thickness = 8 mm
- 3 bolts vertical, pitch = 50 mm, edge distances = 25 mm (top and bottom)
- Vertical extent of bolt group: $L = 2 \times 50 + 2 \times 25 = 150$ mm (matches plate depth)

**Step 2: Gross Shear Yielding**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_g &= 150 \text{ mm}; \quad A_v = 150 \cdot 8 = 1200 \text{ mm}^2 \\
V_{dy} &= \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 250 \cdot 1200}{1.10} = \mathbf{163{,}636 \text{ N}} \approx 163.6 \text{ kN} \ge 120 \text{ kN} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Net Shear Rupture**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{vn} &= (150 - 2.5 \cdot 18) \cdot 8 = (150 - 45) \cdot 8 = 840 \text{ mm}^2 \\
V_{dn} &= \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 410 \cdot 840}{1.25} = \mathbf{165{,}312 \text{ N}} \approx 165.3 \text{ kN} \ge 120 \text{ kN} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Block Shear**

For a 3-bolt line, $n_n = 2.5$ holes on the critical shear plane (we assume failure path goes through 2.5 holes).

Conservative IS 800 approach: $A_{vn} = (L - 2.5 d_0) t = 840 \text{ mm}^2$ (same as above for single line), $A_{tg} = (25 \cdot 8) = 200 \text{ mm}^2$ (one tension plane).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{db} &= \dfrac{f_y A_{tg}}{\gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.6 f_u A_{vn}}{\gamma_{m1}} \\
&= \dfrac{250 \cdot 200}{1.10} + \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 410 \cdot 840}{1.25} \\
&= 45{,}455 + 165{,}312 = 210{,}767 \text{ N} \approx 210.8 \text{ kN} \ge 120 \text{ kN} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Fin plate is SAFE.** ✔️

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Step 1: Weld Geometry**
- Weld size $s = 6$ mm
- Effective throat $t_e = 0.7 s = 0.7 \times 6 = 4.2$ mm
- Two welds, one on each side → total throat area = $2 \times L_w \times t_e$

The welds are loaded **vertically** with the moment $M = V_u \cdot e = 120 \times 50 = 6000$ kN·mm. This creates a **direct shear** and a **torsional shear** on the weld group.

Assume weld length $L_w = 150 - 2 \times 10 = 130$ mm (deducting 10 mm for start/stop).

**Step 2: Direct Shear on Weld Group**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_w &= 2 \cdot L_w \cdot t_e = 2 \cdot 130 \cdot 4.2 = 1092 \text{ mm}^2 \\
f_{wd} &= \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa} \\
V_d &= \dfrac{A_w \cdot f_{wd}}{1000} = \dfrac{1092 \cdot 189.4}{1000} = \mathbf{206.8 \text{ kN}} \ge 120 \text{ kN} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Combined Stress Check (Vector Addition)**

For welds modeled as lines, the polar moment $I_p$ for two parallel vertical lines (each of length $L_w$) about CG:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_x &= 2 \cdot t_e \cdot \dfrac{L_w^3}{12} = 2 \cdot 4.2 \cdot \dfrac{130^3}{12} = 1.537 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 \\
I_y &= 0 \text{ (welds are vertical, negligible horizontal separation)} \\
&\quad\text{(For T-section: assume flanges are wide enough, or simplify as single line weld)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For simplification (assume single line weld, two vertical welds treated as one with combined throat):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{direct}} &= \dfrac{V_u}{A_w} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{1092} = 109.9 \text{ MPa} \\
f_{\text{bending}} &= \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I_x} = \dfrac{6{,}000{,}000 \cdot 65}{1.537 \times 10^6} = 253.8 \text{ MPa} \\
f_{\text{resultant}} &= \sqrt{f_{\text{direct}}^2 + f_{\text{bending}}^2} = \sqrt{109.9^2 + 253.8^2} = 276.6 \text{ MPa} \\
f_{wd} &= 189.4 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ⚠️ **Resultant stress 276.6 MPa > $f_{wd}$ 189.4 MPa → Weld is UNSAFE.** ✗

**Step 4: Required Weld Size**

Increase weld size. Try $s = 10$ mm:
- $t_e = 7.0$ mm, $A_w = 1820$ mm²
- $f_{\text{direct}} = 65.9$ MPa, $I_x = 4.10 \times 10^6$ mm³
- $f_{\text{bending}} = 95.1$ MPa, $f_{\text{resultant}} = 115.9$ MPa
- $115.9 \text{ MPa} < 189.4 \text{ MPa}$ ✓ **SAFE**

> **Use 10 mm weld.** ✔️

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks (each costs 1 to 3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the partial safety factor γm in formulas.** Writing "Vdsb = fu × A" instead of "Vdsb = fu/(√3·γmb) × A" loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using shank area instead of net tensile area for threaded bolts** (or vice versa). The code distinguishes — for Property Class 8.8/10.9 in bearing-type connections, you must check **both** shank and thread capacities and take the **minimum**.
> 3. **Ignoring the eccentricity moment** in single-line bolt groups (e.g., fin plates). The KTU paper often gives "light" eccentricities but expects the full $R = \sqrt{V_d^2 + T^2}$ check.
> 4. **Confusing gross and net shear areas.** Net = Gross $- n \cdot d_0 \cdot t$. Always verify whether the shear path passes through how many bolt holes.
> 5. **Mixing up γm0, γm1, γmb, γmw values.** Memorize: γm0 = 1.10, γm1 = 1.25, γmb = 1.25, γmw = 1.50 (shop weld) or 1.25 (field weld). KTU examiners **deliberately test** this.
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply by 'n' for number of bolts** or by '2' for double-angle/double-shear configurations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Simple Beam End Connectors:**

- ✅ A **simple connection** transmits **shear only**; the rotation at the beam end is **free** (no moment).
- ✅ Five common types: **Web Cleat, Fin Plate, Flexible End Plate, Top & Seat Angle, Single Angle**.
- ✅ Six mandatory ULS checks: **Bolt Shear, Bolt Bearing, Plate Gross Shear, Plate Net Shear, Block Shear, Weld Strength**.
- ✅ Partial safety factors (memorize these verbatim!): **γm0 = 1.10, γm1 = 1.25, γmb = 1.25, γmw = 1.25/1.50**.
- ✅ Bolt shear formula: $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3} \gamma_{mb}}(n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb})$ — pick the **smaller** of thread and shank planes for bearing-type connections.
- ✅ Bearing capacity: $V_{dpb} = \dfrac{2.5 k_b d t f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}$, where $k_b = \min(e/3d_0,\ p/3d_0 - 0.25,\ 1.0)$.
- ✅ Plate gross shear: $V_{dy} = 0.6 f_y A_v / \gamma_{m0}$ — Plate net shear: $V_{dn} = 0.6 f_u A_{vn} / \gamma_{m1}$.
- ✅ Block shear: **two formulas** combined shear + tension — take the **minimum**.
- ✅ **Eccentricity is NOT optional.** Always compute the critical bolt resultant using $R = \sqrt{V_d^2 + T^2}$.
- ✅ Weld effective throat $t_e = 0.7 s$ where $s$ is the weld leg size.
- ✅ Weld design strength $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$.
- ✅ **Serviceability:** Always ensure minimum edge distance $1.5 d_0$ and pitch $2.5 d$ (IS 800 Cl. 10.2.2/10.2.3).
- ✅ For **double-angle cleats**, the design strength is **twice** that of a single angle.
- ✅ For **double-shear bolts**, the number of shear planes $n = 2$.
- ✅ **Final design capacity of connection = MINIMUM of all individual capacities.**
